20 Chapter 1: Febrile Illness Protocols I. ACUTE ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (DELERIUM TREMENS)- See “Neurologic Protocols, Acute Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome” II. ANTHRAX- A bacterial infection caused by Bacillus anthracis (gram positive bacilli) that releases damaging spores and toxins. This bacterium is very stable in the environment and has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 5-7 days 2. Three different clinical syndromes a. Cutaneous anthrax i. Evolving lesions that start as pruritic papules, enlarge to develop central vesicle that ruptures, leaving a painless, necrotic black eschar ii. Sores will have extensive surrounding erythema and lymphadenopathy b. Inhalation anthrax i. May come from weaponization or from breathing spores from contaminated wool or hides ii. Evolving respiratory syndrome that begins with 4-5 day prodrome of myalgia, fever, malaise iii. Rapidly progresses to hemoptysis, severe dyspnea/hypoxia and shock c. Gastrointestinal anthrax i. Comes from ingestion of undercooked meat contaminated with spores ii. Patient develops ulcers in gut with extensive edema iii. Illness starts with prodrome of fever, flushing and conju

inal anthrax i. Comes from ingestion of undercooked meat contaminated with spores ii. Patient develops ulcers in gut with extensive edema iii. Illness starts with prodrome of fever, flushing and conjunctivitis followed by nausea, vomiting and abdominal pain a few days later iv. Rapidly progresses to ascites, hypovolemia and shock B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Patient must receive diagnostic testing and antibiotics b. Arrange hospital admission c. Discuss antibiotic therapy, such as oral or IV ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) or doxycycline (Vibramycin®) 2. INITIATE OUTBREAK INVESTIGATION a. Anthrax is primarily spread through exposure to the bacteria or spores, not person-to-person b. Identify source of outbreak c. Consider antibiotic prophylaxis for all potentially exposed personnel such as ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) or doxycycline (Vibramycin®) III. APPENDICITIS, RUPTURED APPENDIX- See Gastrointestinal Protocols, Appendicitis”

21 IV. COMMUNITY ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA, BACTERIAL- See “Cardiopulmonary Protocols, Community Acquired Pneumonia, Bacterial” V. FUNGAL PNEUMONIAS, MUCORMYCOSIS- A devastatingly invasive fungal infection in patients with immunocompromise, trauma, or malnutrition. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Inhaled spores cause fever, sinusitis, nasal congestion, purulent nasal discharge and sinus pain 2. Symptoms quickly progress to orbital areas, palate and brain. Symptoms include periorbital edema, proptosis, and blindness 3. Black eschar of necrotic tissue may be visible on nasal or palatine mucosa B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. This disorder is rapidly progressive b. Administer Amphotericin B (Amphocin®) if available c. Monitor vitals hourly until evacuation d. Administer oxygen and IV as required VI. ENCEPHALITIS- See “Neurologic Protocols, Encephalitis” VII. GASTROENTERITIS, YERSINIA ENTEROCOLITICA- See “Gastrointestinal Protocols, Gastroenteritis, Yersinia Enterocolitica” VIII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, AMEBIASIS (“AMOEBIC DYSENTERY”)- See “Gastrointestinal Parasites, Amebiasis” IX. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, SCHISTOSOMIASIS (“KATAYAMA FEVER”)- A blood fluke

litica” VIII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, AMEBIASIS (“AMOEBIC DYSENTERY”)- See “Gastrointestinal Parasites, Amebiasis” IX. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, SCHISTOSOMIASIS (“KATAYAMA FEVER”)- A blood fluke (worm) that inhabits the blood vessels and digestive tract of the host. Schistosomes invade directly through the skin when the host exposes skin to freshwater that is also inhabited by snails (the other host of the schistosome life cycle). Schistosomiasis occurs around the world in tropical and semi-arid areas including Iran, Iraq, the Nile River Valley, Japan, Philippines, Brazil, Puerto Rico, and much of Africa. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Erythemic, pruritic papular rash, usually on feet or lower legs at the site of schistosome entry that occurs within one day of exposure 2. “Katayama fever” develops 2-8 weeks later with fever, malaise, dry cough, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss and headache 3. Primary findings on exam are lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly 4. Urinary frequency, dysuria, and hematuria if schistosomes invade the urinary system B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid and electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Definitive treatment should be

dysuria, and hematuria if schistosomes invade the urinary system B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid and electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Definitive treatment should be administered only after positive identification of the parasite b. Consider evacuation for further evaluation and management C. Prevention

22 1. Do not allow personnel to wade in fresh water 2. Counsel personnel to avoid washing or splashing in fresh water X. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, TRICHINOSIS- Parasitic roundworm infection affecting humans. Infection occurs by ingesting contaminated raw or undercooked pork. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of eating raw or undercooked, smoked, or dried meat 2. Usually comes from pig, bear, walrus, boar, horse or wild carnivore meat 3. Incubation 5-15 days and lasts 5 weeks 4. Diarrhea, abdominal cramps, malaise, nausea and vomiting 5. Fever (low to high grade) 6. Muscle tenderness, especially with breathing, chewing or using large muscle groups 7. Edema and spasms 8. Periorbital edema, sweating, photophobia, fatigue 9. Dysphagia, dyspnea, coughing and mucous membrane hemorrhages 10. May display signs of meningitis, encephalitis, myocarditis, pneumonitis, nephritis and CN disorders (depending on where the worm travels) B. Treatment 1. Establish IV access and correct fluid/electrolyte balance if indicated 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If evacuation will be delayed and history includes eating undercooked meat in the past 2 weeks, consider antihelminthic me

balance if indicated 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If evacuation will be delayed and history includes eating undercooked meat in the past 2 weeks, consider antihelminthic medication and steroids b. Bed rest with bathroom privileges 3. Identify contaminated meat source and destroy/eliminate it C. Prevention 1. Ensure all pork and wild meat is always cooked “well done” 2. Smoking, salting, or drying wild meat WILL NOT kill the organism XI. HEAT STROKE- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, Heat Stroke” XII. HEMORRHAGIC FEVERS (EBOLA AND MARBURG)- Filoviruses are the most deadly pathogens in humans. Most infections come from unintentional exposure to an infected animal or physical contact with the body fluids of a sick patient. A major public health concern is that these viruses have the potential to be weaponized and the disease has the potential to be transmitted before diagnosis is made. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Abrupt onset of illness with high fever, bradycardia, severe malaise 2. Muscle and joint pains 3. Petechiae in the conjunctivae, easy bruising 4. Confusion rapidly progressing to coma 5. Skin flushing, nonpruritic maculopapular rash on the upper bo

rdia, severe malaise 2. Muscle and joint pains 3. Petechiae in the conjunctivae, easy bruising 4. Confusion rapidly progressing to coma 5. Skin flushing, nonpruritic maculopapular rash on the upper body is very characteristic filoviruses CLINICAL NOTE: Consider schistosomiasis in all GI disorders refractory to treatment if the disease is endemic. Schistosomiasis presents much like bacillary or amoebic dysentery.

23 6. Spontaneous hemorrhaging with rapid progression to hypotension, shock, multiorgan failure, and death B. Treatment 1. Airway, breathing, O2, monitor, intubate if necessary 2. Initiate two large-bore IV’s with Lactated Ringer’s and bolus as necessary 3. “Spacesuit” isolation is not necessary; however strict respiratory, droplet, and contact precautions should be followed a. Private room, keep stethoscope with patient b. Restrict access to patient to as few people as possible and also keep them isolated as much as possible c. Gloves, gowns, face shields, surgical masks, eye protection, N-95 masks; remove all disposable gear while exiting the patient room and decontaminate shoes outside of room d. Dispose of sharps with extreme care 4. Investigate source of contamination/outbreak XIII. HEPATITIS (VIRAL)- Liver inflammation caused by specific viruses. On exam, liver can be tender, be enlarged, or be small with nodules on it. Careful physical exam is critical. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period varies between viruses a. Hepatitis A: 15-15 days b. Hepatitis B: 40-180 days (second most common) c. Hepatitis C: 20-120 days (transmitted by blood) d. Hepatitis D: 30-180 days e. H

Incubation period varies between viruses a. Hepatitis A: 15-15 days b. Hepatitis B: 40-180 days (second most common) c. Hepatitis C: 20-120 days (transmitted by blood) d. Hepatitis D: 30-180 days e. Hepatitis E: 14-60 days 2. The prodromal (pre-icteric) Phase CLINICAL NOTE: Many viruses are implicated in the production of acute hepatitis. The more common of these are hepatitis A, B, C (non-A, non-B), D (delta agent) and E (enteric hepatitis). Hepatitis A is primarily spread through fecal-oral contamination, but may be food borne. Hepatitis B/C are typically transmitted through contact with blood, semen, or vaginal fluids. Hepatitis C (non-A, non-B) is responsible for most transfusion related cases of hepatitis. Hepatitis D is seen in conjunction with hepatitis B (never alone) and frequently exacerbates chronic hepatitis B infections. Hepatitis E (enteric hepatitis) is has been identified in waterborne outbreaks in many countries—including India, Burma, Afghanistan, Algeria and Mexico. ACTION ALERT: Hepatitis A is extremely contagious. Strict isolation is not necessary; however standard contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) are required to prevent its spread.

ACTION ALERT: Hepatitis A is extremely contagious. Strict isolation is not necessary; however standard contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) are required to prevent its spread. Thorough hand washing by caregivers handling contaminated utensils bedding or clothing is essential. Extreme caution must be exercised when using and disposing of sharps and other biohazards.

24 a. Anorexia, malaise, nausea, vomiting and fever or right upper quadrant abdominal pain b. Dark urine 3. Icteric or Jaundice Phase (Acute) a. Patient appears yellow but other symptoms begin to resolve b. Enlarged and tender liver c. Jaundice (yellow skin) and icterus (yellowing of the sclera) peaks in 1- 2 weeks then fades 4. Recovery (convalescent) Phase a. Appetite returns b. Acute viral hepatitis usually resolves spontaneously after 4-8 weeks B. Treatment 1. Wear gloves when handling utensils, soiled facial tissue (Kleenex®), feces, urine or any of the seven specified fluids requiring precautions 2. Use only single-use disposable syringes and needles 3. Good personal hygiene and controlled sanitation methods are a must 4. Patient should be assigned separate bathroom facilities 5. Provide tissue and paper bags for patient to use-dispose of used tissue in sealed plastic bags 6. Bed rest during the acute phase 7. No strenuous physical exertion, alcohol or hepatotoxic agents [i.e. sedatives, morphine, or acetaminophen (Tylenol®)] during the acute phase 8. All medications must be cleared by Preceptor 9. Provide liquid, soft, or bland nutritional diet, as tolerated 10. Patients or

datives, morphine, or acetaminophen (Tylenol®)] during the acute phase 8. All medications must be cleared by Preceptor 9. Provide liquid, soft, or bland nutritional diet, as tolerated 10. Patients or those in close contact should not be permitted to prepare food for others for a period of at least 2 weeks after onset of jaundice to preclude spreading the disease 11. IV normal saline bolus and/or maintenance if nausea and vomiting are pronounced or if oral intake is substantially reduced 12. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. Although the IDMT would have the skills necessary to manage an uncomplicated case of acute viral hepatitis, it is unrealistic for the IDMT to do so given the lack of private facilities when deployed and the risk of transmission to the rest of the unit b. Patient should also receive lab workup and have lab available during recovery 13. If evacuation is delayed or if patient has any mental status changes, consider restricting protein in diet until clinical improvement CLINICAL NOTE: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR HEPATITIS PROPHYLAXIS Hepatitis A immunization for all active duty personnel. Hepatitis A Immune globulin (IG) should be routinely given to all close persona

provement CLINICAL NOTE: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR HEPATITIS PROPHYLAXIS Hepatitis A immunization for all active duty personnel. Hepatitis A Immune globulin (IG) should be routinely given to all close personal contacts of patients with hepatitis A. Recommended dose: 0.2 ml/kg IM. Hepatitis B immunization for health care personnel (including IDMT’s) is also required. Hepatitis B immune globulin is administered for accidental needle stick exposure to Hep B infected blood, and for sexual partners of infected patients (within two weeks of sexual contact). Document in PHS, Immunization Record. Hepatitis C: No prophylactic treatment is available.

25 XIV. HIGH ALTITUDE PULMONARY EDEMA (HAPE)- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, High Altitude Pulmonary Edema” XV. INFLUENZA- Rapidly mutating virus that causes acute respiratory illness in epidemics worldwide, primarily during the winter and early spring. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Fevers to 102°, malaise, weakness/fatigue, myalgias, night sweats, headache 2. Sneezing, nonproductive cough, sore throat, nasal discharge 3. Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea; may lose up to 5 lbs 4. In rare cases can progress to encephalitis, pericarditis or Guillain-Barré 5. Flushing of the face, throat, ear canals 6. Consider nasal washing for influenza and adenovirus, if available B. Treatment 1. Supportive therapy 2. Increase fluid intake enough to urinate every 2 hours 3. Administer IV NS until able to tolerate PO or if patient is orthostatic 4. Control nausea with IV or PO diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) or ondansetron (Zofran®) 5. Use Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) PO or PR PRN for control of fever and myalgias 6. Avoid use of aspirin due to risk of potentially deadly Reye’s Syndrome 7. Consider zanamivir (Relenza®) or oseltamivir (Tamiflu®) if available within first 36 hours of symptoms C. Prevention 1. Ensure

void use of aspirin due to risk of potentially deadly Reye’s Syndrome 7. Consider zanamivir (Relenza®) or oseltamivir (Tamiflu®) if available within first 36 hours of symptoms C. Prevention 1. Ensure annual vaccination in active duty troops and high-risk populations 2. Respiratory isolation for anyone who is ill with above symptoms to prevent spread to healthy individuals 3. Avoid markets where live birds are present 4. Do not eat or handle birds or bird meat in countries where avian flu is endemic 5. Regular briefings on handwashing, coughing into sleeve, etc. XVI. HUMAN IMMUNODEFICIENCY VIRUS (HIV) SEROCONVERSION- A world pandemic, Human immunodeficiency virus is the causative agent of Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS). Highest risk populations in the US include IV drug users, anal sex participants, individuals who have acquired other sexually transmitted infections, and healthcare workers with needle stick injury. According to the Center for Disease Control and Prevention, several African countries now have more than 25% of their population affected. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period usually 2-4 weeks, but can be much longer 2. Fever- usually low grade but may

several African countries now have more than 25% of their population affected. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period usually 2-4 weeks, but can be much longer 2. Fever- usually low grade but may reach 104°F CLINICAL NOTE: Acute HIV seroconversion mimics influenza and other viral syndromes and is often missed due to not considering it a possible diagnosis for viral syndrome.

26 3. Nontender lymphadenopathy, pharyngitis, myalgias, arthralgias 4. Headache which can be severe with photophobia and stiff neck, or milder and diffuse, usually located retro-orbitally and worse with eye movements 5. Shallow oral ulcers and/or genital and/or anal ulcers that indicate route of exposure 6. Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea 7. May have maculopapular rash on upper chest, collar area, and face that can also include scalp, palms and soles 8. Often missed or confused with “The Flu” B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Supportive therapy b. Increase fluid intake enough to urinate every 2 hours c. Administer IV NS until able to tolerate PO or if patient is orthostatic d. Control nausea with IV or PO diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) or ondansetron (Zofran®) e. Use Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) PO or PR PRN for control of fever and myalgias 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. Evacuation for further evaluation and testing is indicated if HIV seroconversion is suspected b. Use proper personal protective equipment when handling blood and body fluids c. Saliva, tears, and urine are at low risk for transmission d. Having a high index of suspicion is key. Collect a thorough history includ

onal protective equipment when handling blood and body fluids c. Saliva, tears, and urine are at low risk for transmission d. Having a high index of suspicion is key. Collect a thorough history including sexual contacts and sexual trauma C. Prevention 1. Many individuals will come to clinic seeking sexually transmitted infection screening after a high-risk sexual encounter; screening HIV blood test commonly performed in the military will not turn positive until after seroconversion 2. Patients seeking sexually transmitted infection screening need to return to clinic for HIV test 6 months after exposure 3. Counsel patients to avoid high risk sexual exposure 4. Encourage use of condoms every time, including with anal and oral sex 5. As a healthcare worker, avoid needle stick injury by proper use and disposal of sharps and by wearing all appropriate personal protective equipment XVII. KALA AZAR- See “Integumentary Protocols, Leishmaniasis” XVIII. LYME DISEASE- Spirochete infection caused by Borrelia burgdorferi transmitted by Ixodes ticks. Disease has three clinical phases: early localized, early disseminated, and late persistent. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Stage 1 (early localized) a.

by Borrelia burgdorferi transmitted by Ixodes ticks. Disease has three clinical phases: early localized, early disseminated, and late persistent. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Stage 1 (early localized) a. Flat or slightly raised red lesion at site that starts about 1 week after tick bite and expands over several days

27 b. Red lesion may have a typical “bull’s eye” (“target”) appearance called “Erythema Migrans” due to central clearing, but may also have central intensification or a homogeneous c. “Target” lesion commonly seen in the groin, legs, or axillae d. Skin lesion may be associated with viral-like illness (myalgias, arthralgias, headache, fever, fatigue) e. Skin lesions resolve in 3-4 weeks 2. Stage 2 (early disseminated) a. Occurs within weeks of original infection and is marked by multiple skin lesions, malaise, fatigue, fever, headache, neck pain, and generalized aching b. May develop cardiac abnormalities such as myocarditis or heart blocks c. May develop neurologic abnormalities such as signs of meningitis or facial nerve palsy 3. Stage 3 (late persistent) a. Months to years after initial infection marked by single or multiple large joint pains and encephalopathy b. If disease wasn’t diagnosed in stage 1 or 2 it is frequently missed in stage 3; have a high index of suspicion for any chronic large joint pains B. Treatment 1. If stage I, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Treat Stage 1 (erythema migrans) with doxycycline (Vibramycin®) b. No labs required if erythema migrans i

ronic large joint pains B. Treatment 1. If stage I, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Treat Stage 1 (erythema migrans) with doxycycline (Vibramycin®) b. No labs required if erythema migrans is seen 2. If Stage 2 or 3, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. Will require further evaluation and possible specialist consultation b. Consider starting doxycycline if evacuation will be delayed XIX. MALARIA- Relapsing, febrile illness caused by multiple different species of Plasmodium parasites that are transmitted through the bite of an infected Anopheles mosquito. The parasites infect the liver and blood cells. Malaria is highly infective and should be considered in the differentials of anyone who has a fever and has been in or to a malaria- endemic region within the past year. CLINICAL NOTE: Tick must feed for at least 36 hours to transmit Borrelia burgdorferi. Prevent Lyme Disease by checking for and removing ticks nightly. - Emphasize prevention-cover exposed skin with long sleeve shirts, pants tucked into socks, light colored clothing with repellant. Inspect for ticks nightly in regions where exposure is possible. - Remove tick as soon as possible. Use fine tipped tweezer

eeve shirts, pants tucked into socks, light colored clothing with repellant. Inspect for ticks nightly in regions where exposure is possible. - Remove tick as soon as possible. Use fine tipped tweezers at its mouthparts or gloved fingers until it releases its hold (do not pull or squeeze on body). Pull straight up and firmly. Do not jerk or twist. If mouthparts remain in the skin, leave them alone and they will be expelled naturally. - Do not use petroleum jelly, hot match, fingernail polish, or isopropyl alcohol. These will irritate the tick and cause further regurgitation of tick contents into host. - Disinfect the skin with soap and water after tick has been removed.

28 A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Feverish paroxysms occur at irregular intervals 2. Tachycardia, tachypnea, chills, malaise, fatigue, diaphoresis, cough 3. Nausea, anorexia, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea 4. Headache, arthralgias, myalgias 5. Palpable, tender, enlarged spleen 6. May have mild jaundice 7. May progress to “complicated malaria” where blood cells get stuck causing extensive circulatory, respiratory, and neurologic destruction leading to death 8. Diagnosis made based on peripheral blood smear or rapid diagnostic testing B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. Anyone having relapsing fevers and the symptoms above should be evacuated for further evaluation b. Supportive therapy until evacuation including IV fluids if necessary 2. Medications are tailored to the specific region where the illness was acquired due to the high incidence of resistant strains 3. Treatment often requires two or more medications C. Prevention 1. Wear long sleeves and pants tucked into socks/boots to reduce amount of skin exposure 2. Avoid outdoor activity between dusk and dawn (when mosquitoes feed) 3. Wear insect repellent; 50% DEET is effective for 3-6 hours 4. Sleep under bed

to socks/boots to reduce amount of skin exposure 2. Avoid outdoor activity between dusk and dawn (when mosquitoes feed) 3. Wear insect repellent; 50% DEET is effective for 3-6 hours 4. Sleep under bed nets treated with permethrin 5. Follow local guidance regarding prophylactic medications 6. Eliminate standing water areas in camp XX. MENINGITIS- See “Neurologic Protocols, Meningitis” XXI. PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)- Acute infection of the uterus and/or ovaries caused by an untreated or incompletely treated sexually transmitted infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bilateral lower abdominal pain worse during intercourse or after menses; pain with jarring movements, may have abnormal spotting or bleeding 2. Risk factors include prior history of STIs, intercourse without condoms and douching 3. Diffuse tenderness worse in lower quadrants, rebound tenderness and decreased bowel sounds 4. Severe cervical motion tenderness and/or tenderness around the ovaries; may have purulent endocervical discharge (due to untreated STI) 5. Fever 6. Lab: Urine HCG for pregnancy B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access and give fluid bolus if indicated b. Administer analgesic such as

ical discharge (due to untreated STI) 5. Fever 6. Lab: Urine HCG for pregnancy B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access and give fluid bolus if indicated b. Administer analgesic such as aspirin or acetaminophen (Tylenol®) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION

29 a. PID will present with a clinical syndrome similar to acute appendicitis or other surgical abdomen and therefore immediate further evaluation is required b. If patient is febrile and evacuation is delayed, consider cefoxetin (Mefoxin®) and doxycycline (Vibromyin®) until patient is afebrile or transferred c. If patient is afebrile initially, or has been afebrile for 24 hours and evacuation is delayed, consider PO therapy of levofloxacin (Levaquin®) and metronidazole (Flagyl®) XXII. PLAGUE- A potentially fatal infection caused by the gram negative coccobacillus, Yersinia pestis. The infection has been responsible for many pandemics through the centuries, including the “Black Death” of the 14th century, during which 1 out of every 3 citizens in England and some countries in mainland Europe died of the infection. It is transmitted through the bite of infected fleas. In this country deaths from plague still sometimes occur in the Southwestern United States, where some prariedog populations still carry infected fleas. This organism has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bubonic Plague- sudden onset fever, chills, weakness, headache, followed by intense pain an

ions still carry infected fleas. This organism has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bubonic Plague- sudden onset fever, chills, weakness, headache, followed by intense pain and swelling of the lymph nodes and redness of the overlying skin (called buboes, most commonly in the inguinal lymph nodes; over several days buboes progress to septic shock and death 2. Septicemic plague- very rare; same initial presentation but progresses directly to septic shock and death without bubo formation 3. Pneumonic plague- Acquired by inhalation of respiratory secretions or aerosolized droplets from infected animals; can also come from contact with blood from an infected bubo; presents with sudden onset dyspnea, high fever, pleuritic chest pain, cough with grossly bloody sputum that progresses rapidly to death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, circulation, O2 monitor b. Strict isolation and droplet precautions c. Limit number of individuals having contact with patient 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If known contact with rodent population or recent flea bites and the above symptoms occur, do not delay treatment with antibiotics b.

th patient 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If known contact with rodent population or recent flea bites and the above symptoms occur, do not delay treatment with antibiotics b. Consider gentamicin (Garamycin®) if available; may use doxycline (Vibramycin®) C. Prevention 1. Avoid contact with animal carcasses or skins 2. Maintain rodent control and flea control in worksites and living quarters ACTION ALERT: It is essential to rule out ectopic pregnancy (and other causes of a surgical abdomen) on all female patients with severe abdominal pain and elevated temperature.

30 3. Promote appropriate insect repellant measures such as proper washing of ABU uniform and application of topical insect repellants XXIII. PNEUMONIA, MYCOPLASMA (ATYPICAL PNEUMONIA)- See “Cardiopulmonary Protocols, Pneumonia, Mycoplasma” XXIV. MONONUCLEOSIS (EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS)- Self limiting viral illness characterized by a triad of fever, sore throat, and lymphadenopathy. It is often difficult to distinguish from strep throat in the absence of throat culture or blood tests. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Malaise, headache, sore throat 2. Low grade fever for 1-3 days prior to onset of sore throat, and lymphadenopathy 3. Profound fatigue suggestive of diagnosis 4. May have white, gray-green or necrotic exudates in posterior oropharynx 5. Splenomegaly is not always present, but if present, is suggestive of diagnosis B. Treatment 1. Quarters (up to several weeks) may be necessary when severe fatigue is present 2. If splenomegaly is present or if blood test is positive, limit physical activities that will impact the spleen for a minimum of 1 month. Activities include, but not limited to, contact sports, bumpy rides, load bearing, heavy lifting, etc. 3. Increase fluid intake 4. Bland or l

ties that will impact the spleen for a minimum of 1 month. Activities include, but not limited to, contact sports, bumpy rides, load bearing, heavy lifting, etc. 3. Increase fluid intake 4. Bland or liquid diet. Advance as tolerated 5. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or NSAIDs for fever, malaise and body aches C. Prevention 1. Hand washing education 2. Frequent decontamination of shared spaces, doorknobs, equipment, etc. 3. Avoid shared eating/drinking utensils XXV. PROSTATITIS (ACUTE)- An inflammatory condition of the prostate that may be acute or chronic. Often occurs with gonorrhea or chlamydial infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Urinary retention/hesitation, dysuria, perineal or suprapubic pain 2. Fever, chills and myalgias 3. Nausea, vomiting, anorexia CLINICAL NOTE: Splenomegaly is present in 50-60% of cases, but not diagnostic of Epstein- Barr Virus. Spleen must be checked in every patient that has EBV. Enlarged spleens have high incidence of life-threatening splenic rupture. Due to the overlap of symptoms with strep throat, antibiotics are often mistakenly given to patients with EBV, which results in the development of an urticarial, maculopapular rash in 70-90% of cases. CLINICA

he overlap of symptoms with strep throat, antibiotics are often mistakenly given to patients with EBV, which results in the development of an urticarial, maculopapular rash in 70-90% of cases. CLINICAL NOTE: EBV is often shed from asymptomatic patients for several months after symptoms resolve.

31 4. Warm, tender prostate 5. Lab: Macroscopic UA positive for WBCs, nitrites and RBCs B. Treatment 1. Control pain with aspirin (Bayer®) 2. Sitz baths 2-3 times/day (for 1 hour) may relieve pain and spasm (See “Gastrointestinal Protocols, Hemorrhoids”) 3. Ensure adequacy of hydration, but do not force fluids 4. Observe for urinary retention due to swelling of prostatic tissue 5. Suspect obstructed urethra if patient does not void for 6 hrs or if they complain of increasing bladder discomfort with inability to void 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. If over 35 years, consider Trimethroprim-Sulfamethoxazole (Septra®), Ciprofloxacin (Cipro®), or tetracycline (Tetra®) b. Treat with antibiotics for 1 month if symptoms have lasted longer than one month c. If under 35 years old, consider Ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) and Doxycycline (Vibramycin®) d. If allergic, call preceptor for alternative regimen 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if symptoms persist XXVI. PYELONEPHRITIS, ACUTE- An infectious inflammatory disease of the kidney usually caused by the spread of a urinary tract infection. Usually caused by Escherichia coli (gram negative bacilli). A. Signs and Symptoms 1.

ACUTE- An infectious inflammatory disease of the kidney usually caused by the spread of a urinary tract infection. Usually caused by Escherichia coli (gram negative bacilli). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dysuria, urgency and frequency 2. Fever, flank pain, shaking chills 3. Pronounced CVA tenderness 4. Associated with nausea, vomiting and diarrhea 5. Lab: Macroscopic UA positive for WBCs, leukocyte esterase, bacteria and possibly RBCs B. Treatment 1. Maintain hydration: 1-2 Liters Lactated Ringer’s followed by normal saline if not able to tolerate adequate PO intake 2. Administer acetaminophen (Tylenol®) PRN for fever CLINICAL NOTE: Symptoms of prostatitis resemble UTI’s. UTI’s are uncommon in men and symptoms are almost always caused by prostatitis. ACTION ALERT: If urinary obstruction is suspected, do not catheterize! Refer the patient to a higher level of care immediately. ACTION ALERT: Avoid excessive manipulation of the prostate during exam. Septicemia may result.

32 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION a. May treat as outpatient with preceptor approval b. Consider levofloxacin (Levaquin®), ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Septra®) c. Administer analgesics for relief of severe pain, meperidine (Demerol®), as needed d. If not tolerating PO, consider immediate transfer 4. Reevaluate patient daily to ensure clinical improvement 5. Evacuate and arrange for hospital admission if febrile or fever not controlled with medications 6. Monitor and record urine output 7. Consider promethazine (Phenergan®) if needed for nausea and vomiting 8. If patient appears unstable and immediate transfer is not possible, consider administering ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) + anti-pseudomonal penicillin such as ticarcillin (Ticar®) or pipercillin (Pipracil®) as directed by preceptor XXVII. RABIES- Rabies is a potentially fatal virus that is transmitted when the patient is bitten by an infected dog, raccoon, bat, skunk, fox, or coyote. The virus travels along nerves to the brain and spinal cord; once clinical manifestations occur it will universally progress to neurologic deficits, encephalitis, and death. Assume any animal tha

ote. The virus travels along nerves to the brain and spinal cord; once clinical manifestations occur it will universally progress to neurologic deficits, encephalitis, and death. Assume any animal that bites has rabies unless it is a pet and its vaccination status is well documented. A. Signs and Symptoms of Rabies 1. Incubation period- lasts days to months after bite or exposure; bites close to the head will have shorter incubation periods than bites on the extremities 2. Prodromal period- flu-like symptoms lasting up to a week that include nausea, malaise, anorexia, sore throat, headache, irritability, and low grade fever; percussion of muscular areas will cause mounding up of tissue at the percussion site 3. Encephalitic rabies- fear of water, (hydrophobia) painful pharyngeal spasms upon encountering a draft of air, excessive salivation, fluctuating consciousness, cardiac arrhythmias lasting 2-7 days 4. Paralytic rabies- less common than encephalitic rabies, it resembles Guillain- Barré syndrome with weakness progressing to flaccid paralysis and respiratory failure; it is different than Guillain-Barré in that it is accompanied by persistent fever, intact sensation, and bladder d

arré syndrome with weakness progressing to flaccid paralysis and respiratory failure; it is different than Guillain-Barré in that it is accompanied by persistent fever, intact sensation, and bladder dysfunction 5. Coma- both encephalitic and paralytic rabies progress to coma, flaccid paralysis, and respiratory and vascular collapse B. Treatment 1. Apply direct pressure to control bleeding 2. Cleanse superficial wounds with soap and water; if tissue damage is extensive inject local anesthesia into wound prior to cleansing and clean with gently pressurized sterile water; remove any devitalized tissue prior to closure ACTION ALERT: Clinical improvement does not necessarily indicate cure of infection. Continue to monitor closely.

33 3. The IDMT should not close bite wounds from cats or humans, puncture wounds, bites > 12 hours old, bites associated with crush injury, or bites on the hands or feet 4. Inject Rabies Immune Globulin (RIG) into wound if available 5. Survival from rabies is dependent on being vaccinated prior to the appearance of symptoms; therefore it is critical that patient and animal be evaluated for rabies whenever an animal bite from a potential carrier occurs 6. Every effort should be made to capture/kill the animal so that it can be evaluated for presence of rabies virus 7. In the absence of the animal, the patient may be required to undergo lengthy and painful treatments such as HDCV (Human Diploid Cell Vaccine) 8. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION for further evaluation and management XXVIII. ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER- Potentially lethal infection occurring 2-14 days after bite from an infected tick transmitting the gram-negative bacteria Rickettsia Rickettsii. Infected ticks are found throughout the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Central and South America. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. About 1 week after tick bite patient will develop fever, chills, headache, n

ected ticks are found throughout the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Central and South America. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. About 1 week after tick bite patient will develop fever, chills, headache, nausea, vomiting, myalgias, restlessness, insomnia and irritability 2. Faint macules that progress to maculopapules and then petechiae appear between 2nd and 6th days after bite 3. This characteristic rash appears first on wrists and ankles and spreads centrally to arms/legs/trunk over 2-3 days following onset of fever and malaise 4. Rash includes palms and soles 5. Be alert in dark-skinned individuals because rash may not be visible or may be very difficult to see 6. Up to 10% of patients do not develop rash or petichiae 7. Advanced disease includes abdominal pain, cough, bleeding, confusion, seizures, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish large-bore IV and rehydrate with normal saline if indicated b. Treat nausea with promethazine (Phenergan®) IM or ondansetron (Zofran®) IV 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Doxycycline (Vibramycin®) orally or IV is the mainstay of treatment CLINICAL NOTE: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever has a high li

IV 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Doxycycline (Vibramycin®) orally or IV is the mainstay of treatment CLINICAL NOTE: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever has a high likelihood of successful treatment if it is diagnosed in the first week after tick bite. According to the Center for Disease Control data, approximately 25% of patients die when treatment is delayed until the second week. Don’t forget to include infectious diseases in your differentials!

34 b. Do not use any other antibiotics unless directed by preceptor; sulfa drugs may worsen infection and most other antibiotics increase mortality compared to doxycylcine (Vibramycin®) C. Prevention 1. Wear protective clothing and insect repellants 2. For successful removal of ticks, see “Lyme Disease” clinical note on tick removal procedures XXIX. SECONDARY SYPHILIS- See “Genitourinary Protocols, Syphilis” XXX. TOXIC SHOCK SYNDROME- Shock caused by infection with Staphylococcus aureus (gram positive coccus) that has entered the bloodstream and released toxins. Often caused by use of super-absorbent tampons or retained vaginal foreign bodies. In males, other staphylococcal infections may be the cause. This toxin has the potential to be weaponized. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Rapidly progressive syndrome occurring in 1-3 days 2. Fever, orthostatic hypotension, and characteristic diffuse, red rash resembling sunburn that includes palms and soles of feet 3. Profuse, watery diarrhea, shock, decreased urine output, cyanosis, edema of the extremities 4. Neurologic symptoms that include confusion, agitation, irritability, hallucinations, decreased level of consciousness 5. Skin desquamation

decreased urine output, cyanosis, edema of the extremities 4. Neurologic symptoms that include confusion, agitation, irritability, hallucinations, decreased level of consciousness 5. Skin desquamation (blistering and sloughing skin) and seizures are late and ominous findings 6. Lab: Urine HCG B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Remove retained foreign object (nasal packing can also cause TSS) b. Initiate IV with Lactated Ringer’s and bolus as indicated c. Patient may require 10-20 Liters of fluid per day-discuss with preceptor d. Monitor vital signs 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider antibiotic treatment with clindamycin (Cleocin®) b. Seizure precautions

35 Chapter 2: Emergency Medicine Protocols I. ANAPHYLAXIS- Potentially fatal reaction to bite/sting, medication, or food due to massive immune response. Occurs within minutes to several hours following exposure. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Skin/mucosa- hives, itching, flushing, swelling of lips, tongue, uvula, periorbital edema (swelling around eyes) 2. Respiratory- wheeze, dyspnea, tachypnea, hypoxemia, reduced peak flow, sensation of throat closure, tightness, or itching 3. Cardiovascular- hypotension, syncope, dizziness, tachycardia, may have incontinence 4. Gastrointestinal- nausea, vomiting, cramping abdominal pain B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, circulation, O2, monitor, expose patient b. Place patient supine with legs elevated c. Epinephrine 1:1000 aqueous solution (which is the same as epinephrine 1 mg/mL) give 0.5 mL IM; repeat every 5 minutes until symptoms improve d. Obtain 2 large-bore IVs with normal saline at maintenance rate if no hypotension; if hypotension is present administer 1-2 L normal saline bolus over 1 hour e. If no improvement in anaphylactic symptoms after 3 doses of IM epinephrine, prepare an epinephrine infusion i. Using 1:10,000 epi

tension is present administer 1-2 L normal saline bolus over 1 hour e. If no improvement in anaphylactic symptoms after 3 doses of IM epinephrine, prepare an epinephrine infusion i. Using 1:10,000 epinephrine solution give 2 mL per minute IV/IO ii. Titrate to response f. If patient develops stridor or complains of difficulty getting air or sensation of throat closure, consider immediate intubation prior to the development of laryngeal edema g. If unable to insert endotracheal tube, perform emergency cricothyroidotomy (may also use ET tube for epinephrine if no other route available) h. If wheezing is present but airway is still intact, administer albuterol 5 mg diluted in 3 mL normal saline inhaled PO via continuous nebulizer; may repeat every 10 minutes as needed for maximum of 3 doses (if ACTION ALERT: The intramuscular administration of epinephrine is the single most important action in the treatment of an anaphylactic reaction. 000 is not available, may dilute epinephrine 1:1000 for use in IV. 1. Put 0.1 mL of 1:1000 epinephrine into 10 mL of normal saline; this solution contains 0.1 mg of epinephrine 2. Give 2 mL per minute IV or IO, titrated to response

36 nebulizer is not available, administer 2 puffs albuterol (Ventolin®) PO via metered dose inhaler or HFA inhaler with spacer; repeat every 5 minutes as needed for maximum of 3 doses [six puffs total]) i. If itching, flushing, or rash is present, consider diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) 25-50 mg IV single dose j. For gastrointestinal symptoms, consider ranitidine (Zantac®) 50 mg IV or famotidine (Pepcid®) 20 mg IV single dose k. Monitor for hypothermia and keep patient warm 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Reassess vital, temperature, and breath sounds every 5 minutes b. If BP < 90/50 initiate rapid volume expansion with normal saline or preceptor preference; titrate to keep BP > 90/50 (rapid expansion of intravascular fluid volume is necessary due to large amounts of fluid escaping to the extra-vascular spaces) c. If not responding to epinephrine, consider dopamine (Intropin®) continuous infusion IV per Preceptor d. Consider methylprednisolone (Medrol®) 125 mg IV or dexamethasone (Decadron®) 20 mg IV per Preceptor 3. If evacuation is delayed, most cases of anaphylaxis will resolve within 12 hours with appropriate treatment a. Once acute symptoms have resolved,

r dexamethasone (Decadron®) 20 mg IV per Preceptor 3. If evacuation is delayed, most cases of anaphylaxis will resolve within 12 hours with appropriate treatment a. Once acute symptoms have resolved, administer diphenhydramine hydrochloride (Benadryl®) 25 mg PO every six hours for one week b. Monitor for “biphasic reaction”, or new onset anaphylaxis within 72 hours of initial episode c. Even if symptoms have fully resolved, patient should still be evaluated by physician II. UNCONSCIOUS PATIENT UNKNOWN CAUSE- In general, consciousness requires three main components: intact neural pathways, oxygen reaching the brain, and sugar reaching the brain. Neural pathways can be disrupted by head trauma, electrical injury, swelling of brain or meninges, infection, toxins, metabolic abnormalities, environmental temperature extremes, fever, seizures, or drugs/medications. Oxygen delivery to the brain can be disrupted by dehydration/hypotension, shock, cardiac abnormalities, inadequate oxygen intake (bronchospasm, decreased respiratory drive due to sedation or chest wall pain, reduced oxygen levels at altitude, pneumothorax, etc), inadequate perfusion of the lungs (chest trauma, pulmonary embolis

(bronchospasm, decreased respiratory drive due to sedation or chest wall pain, reduced oxygen levels at altitude, pneumothorax, etc), inadequate perfusion of the lungs (chest trauma, pulmonary embolism), or disruption in the oxygen exchange barrier (pneumonia, pulmonary edema, acute respiratory distress syndrome, etc). Inadequate glucose delivery to the brain can be caused by dehydration/hypotension, shock, inadequate glucose intake (nausea/vomiting, not eating enough to meet body’s energy demands, etc) or inadequate absorption (diabetes, gastroenteritis, diarrhea, weight loss supplements, laxatives, etc). These are just a few examples and not meant to be an exhaustive list. Use clues in patient’s environment to help determine cause. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bystander discovers unresponsive patient 2. Be alert to interventions bystanders may have performed B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish unresponsiveness

37 b. Initiate C-spine precautions c. Airway, breathing, circulation, O2, monitor; initiate CPR per ACLS guidelines if indicated d. Establish baseline vitals signs and treat any abnormalities found e. Estimate Glasgow Coma score; consider intubation if score <8 f. Initiate two large-bore IV lines, bolus 1 Liter normal saline; if IV access can not be obtained may use IO access g. Obtain baseline glucose reading if possible; administer 50 mL of 50% glucose solution in IV if glucose reading not available or if glucose < 200 mg/dL h. If no response to above measures, administer naloxone hydrochloride (Narcan®) 2 mg IV/IM/subcutaneous i. Obtain venous blood sample for portable blood analyzer (iSTAT®) to evaluate for electrolyte abnormalities (CHEM 7) if available j. Obtain arterial blood gas sample for portable blood analyzer (iSTAT®) to check for respiratory and metabolic abnormalities (ABG) if available k. Expose patient; perform a thorough secondary survey l. When evaluating patient’s eyes, if resistance or response to passive eye opening occurs, patient is not unconscious 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION CLINICAL NOTE: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is used to asse

es, if resistance or response to passive eye opening occurs, patient is not unconscious 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION CLINICAL NOTE: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is used to assess level of neurologic disability. Consider intubation for GCS < 8. A score of 15 (max score) indicates patient is awake, interactive, and oriented. The minimum score is 3. I. Eye opening (max score = 4) a. Spontaneous eye opening = 4 b. Eyes open when someone speaks = 3 c. Eyes open to pain = 2 d. No response = 1 II. Verbal response (max score = 5) a. Oriented = 5 b. Confused = 4 c. Inappropriate = 3 d. Incomprehensible = 2 e. No response = 1 III. Motor response (max score = 6) a. Obeys commands = 6 b. Localizes to pain = 5 c. Withdraws from pain = 4 d. Flexion to pain = 3 e. Extension to pain = 2 f. No response = 1

38 C. Initial Investigation to Determine Cause (complete once patient is stabilized) 1. Gather information from patient’s peers a. Anything abnormal in behavior, movements, or mood? b. Any complaints in past 24 hours? c. How long ago since was patient last observed to be normal? d. Any recent medical visits? e. Any chronic medical conditions? f. Does the patient normally take any medications, herbs or supplements? g. What were they doing before they became unconscious? 2. Gather information from patient’s location a. Any environmental hazards? (electricity, chemicals, fall risks, etc) b. Any fumes in environment? c. Anyone else sick in the unit? 3. Gather information from patient’s living area (requires Commander’s approval) a. Any herbs, supplements, or medication bottles? b. Any chance of accidental or intentional overdose? c. Any illegal drugs or paraphernalia? d. Any evidence of severe depression or discontent? III. CARDIAC EMERGENCIES- Includes myocardial infarction, symptomatic bradycardia, tachycardias, acute coronary syndrome, and arrhythmias. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Chest pain, palpitations, “fluttering” in chest, uncomfortable chest pressure, fullness, squeezing sensatio

radycardia, tachycardias, acute coronary syndrome, and arrhythmias. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Chest pain, palpitations, “fluttering” in chest, uncomfortable chest pressure, fullness, squeezing sensations in center of chest 2. Pressure or pain may radiate to shoulders, neck, arms, jaw, or back 3. May or may not have pain; may also manifest as lightheadedness, dizziness, fainting, sweating, nausea, or vomiting 4. Unexplained sudden shortness of breath 5. Diaphoresis (sweating), cyanosis B. Treatment 1. Follow American Heart Association’s current Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) treatment algorithms 2. Healthy active duty patients usually have a stressor that triggered their cardiac abnormality 3. Consider “H’s and T’s”- hypovolemia, hypothermia, hypoxia, hydrogen ion (acidosis), hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia, hypokalemia, toxins, trauma, cardiac tamponade, tension pneumothorax, thrombosis (cardiac or pulmonary) C. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION ACTION ALERT: A conscious cardiac patient can rapidly become an unconscious cardiac patient. It is critical to have quick access to the following equipment throughout patient care and evacuation: AED or monitor that can deli

s cardiac patient can rapidly become an unconscious cardiac patient. It is critical to have quick access to the following equipment throughout patient care and evacuation: AED or monitor that can deliver shocks, advanced airway equipment and supplies, and suction equipment and supplies.

39 IV. STROKE- Blood clot or hemorrhage causing sudden loss of focal brain functioning while other brain functions remain intact. Generally, focal neurologic deficits WITH headache or nausea/vomiting are more likely to be hemorrhage, trauma, or increased intracranial pressure. Focal neurologic deficits without headache or nausea/vomiting are more likely to be ischemic stroke. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Asymmetrical facial, arm, or leg weakness 2. Speech difficulties such as word finding or slurring 3. Visual field defects 4. Listen for carotid bruits (avoid palpation of the neck since this may dislodge further B. Treatment 1. Follow current ACLS treatment guidelines 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Complete baseline full neurologic exam 4. Repeat neurologic exam every 30 minutes to identify improvement or deterioration in functioning Trauma V. MULTIPLE ORGAN SYSTEM TRAUMAS- The vast majority of deaths and disabilities in active duty military personnel are caused by accidental or battlefield traumas. Trauma is the leading cause of death in the United States for all people under the age of 45. It is critical for the IDMT to have a systematic approach to handling

accidental or battlefield traumas. Trauma is the leading cause of death in the United States for all people under the age of 45. It is critical for the IDMT to have a systematic approach to handling trauma. Initial response to multiple organ system traumas is addressed here, following by specific considerations for individual organ systems. The treatment described below is best accomplished in controlled, protected environments. A. Indicators of Severe Trauma 1. Fall from greater than twice the patient’s height 2. Extrication of patient took longer than 20 minutes 3. A death occurred in the same vehicle 4. Patient was ejected from vehicle B. Initial Assessment 1. Airway- assess airway; secure airway (oropharyngeal, nasopharyngeal) provide simultaneous manual protection of C-Spine 2. Breathing- Check rate, efficiency, effort, administer high flow oxygen using nonrebreather mask if needed, assist ventilation if needed; check for symmetrical vs paradoxical chest movement ACTION ALERT: The Automated External Defibrillator (AED) should never be applied to a conscious patient unless the AED has cardiac monitoring capability. An AED is only indicated for the pulseless unconscious patient

e Automated External Defibrillator (AED) should never be applied to a conscious patient unless the AED has cardiac monitoring capability. An AED is only indicated for the pulseless unconscious patient.

40 3. Circulation- Check pulse, capillary refill, and blood pressure; start CPR if indicated, control life-threatening bleeding 4. Disability- assess level of consciousness using AVPU (A = alert/awake, V= responds to voice, P = responds to pain, U = unresponsive) or Glasgow Coma scale 5. Expose patient- Examine whole patient, including patient’s back and buttocks; complete rectal exam for occult blood, rectal wall tenderness/bony fragments, and decreased sphincter tone; check genital region for blood, incontinence, and spontaneous ejaculation; complete full set of vital signs including temperature C. Additional Treatments 1. Airway- reassess airway; secure permanent airway (Combitube®, endotracheal intubation, cricothyroidotomy, etc), secure protection of C- Spine 2. Breathing- Reassess rate, efficiency, effort, assist ventilation if needed, auscultate lung sounds; if diminished on one side perform needle decompression for presumed pneumothorax (See “Tension Pneumothorax” below); palpate for crepitus; percuss lungs for tympany, dullness, or hyper- resonance 3. Circulation- Check pulse, capillary refill, and blood pressure; control obvious bleeding; initiate two large bore IV/IO lin

te for crepitus; percuss lungs for tympany, dullness, or hyper- resonance 3. Circulation- Check pulse, capillary refill, and blood pressure; control obvious bleeding; initiate two large bore IV/IO lines and administer up to 2 L Normal Saline bolused 4. Disability- reassess patient’s level of disability; perform rapid inspection, auscultation, percussion, and palpation of abdomen; consider urinary catheter insertion; DO NOT insert urinary catheter in male patients with signs of lower abdominal/genitourinary trauma or if there is blood at the urethra; strictly monitor fluid intake and output 5. Cover patient- repeat vitals signs including temperature, cover above and below patient to help prevent hypothermia D. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 1. Discuss fluid resuscitation; preceptor may advise switch to Lactated Ringers, ½ normal saline, D5 ½ normal saline, or hypertonic saline depending on injuries; after first two Liters of Normal Saline reduce rate per preceptor’s guidelines 2. Reassess critical patient’s vital signs every 5 minutes; reassess stable trauma patients every 15 minutes 3. The two most common causes of preventable death in multi-organ system trauma p

uidelines 2. Reassess critical patient’s vital signs every 5 minutes; reassess stable trauma patients every 15 minutes 3. The two most common causes of preventable death in multi-organ system trauma patients are failure to maintain patent airway and failure to recognize life-threatening internal hemorrhage 4. The most common cause of poor response to fluid therapy is continuing hemorrhage 5. If evacuation is delayed longer than 24 hours, maintain urine output > 30 ml/hour E. Treat non-life threatening injuries 1. SAMPLE history (signs/symptoms, allergies, medications, past medical history, last meal/medications, events leading up to emergency) 2. Immobilize fractures 3. Dress and bandage all wounds

41 4. Package patient appropriately for weather conditions VI. SHOCK- Inadequate tissue perfusion, leading to inadequate oxygen delivery to tissues, which results in end-organ damage. Causes include trauma/hemorrhage, diarrhea/vomiting, severe infection, heat stroke, burns, toxins, anaphylaxis, spinal cord injury, and cardiac failure. Trauma-induced hypovolemia is discussed here. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pre-shock- patient is still able to compensate; may already have lost up to 10% blood volume; may have mild increase or decrease in blood pressure and mildly elevated pulse 2. Shock- compensatory system is overwhelmed and symptoms of organ dysfunction appear including dyspnea, tachypnea, tachycardia, restlessness, cool clammy skin/diaphoresis, and decreased urine output; may have lost up to 25% of blood volume in this stage; may have respiratory rate less than 10 or more than 30, capillary refill longer than 2 seconds, thready pulse 3. End organ damage/failure- absent urine output, agitation, lethargy, coma, death B. Treatment 1. Pre-shock- Establish IV access with Lactated Ringer’s and provide high-flow oxygen as needed 2. Shock- Rapid volume resuscitation with Lactated Ringer’s;

tation, lethargy, coma, death B. Treatment 1. Pre-shock- Establish IV access with Lactated Ringer’s and provide high-flow oxygen as needed 2. Shock- Rapid volume resuscitation with Lactated Ringer’s; high flow oxygen to offset blood loss, may require blood transfusion 3. End-organ damage- loss of life imminent, continue Lactated Ringer’s, plan for transfusion and surgical intervention VII. CERVICAL SPINE TRAUMA- Always suspect C-Spine trauma in patients with decreased mental status, with history or evidence of blunt or penetrating trauma above the clavicle, or with mechanisms of severe trauma (see “Multiple Organ System Trauma” above) A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Mechanism suggests neck trauma 2. Neck pain or spasm 3. Changes in sensory or motor function 4. Decreased respiratory drive (“C3, C4, C5 keep the diaphragm alive”) B. Treatment 1. Protect cervical spine from flexion-extension-rotation-bending with semi- rigid collar backboard, and head immobilization devices 2. Use jaw thrust/chin lift maneuver to open the airway 3. Insert airway adjunct (oropharyngeal airway or nasopharyngeal airway) if indicated 4. Consider advanced airway placement- endotracheal intubation is extremely diff

lift maneuver to open the airway 3. Insert airway adjunct (oropharyngeal airway or nasopharyngeal airway) if indicated 4. Consider advanced airway placement- endotracheal intubation is extremely difficult to perform while maintaining cervical spine in neutral position; consider other appropriate airway such as Combitube®, LMA®, or cricothyroidotomy C. Field Clearance of Cervical Spine 1. Cervical spine precautions do not need to be maintained if all of the following conditions are met a. Mechanism was not a “severe trauma” as defined above b. No midline cervical spine tenderness

42 c. Patient has normal level of alertness d. The patient is not intoxicated e. There are no abnormal neurologic findings f. There are no other injuries severe enough (painful enough) to distract patient from identifying additional injuries g. Patient is able to communicate (no foreign language barrier, not a child) 2. If patient meets all of the above criteria, remove cervical collar and ask patient to actively rotate neck left and right a. Patient should be able to rotate at least 45 degrees in each direction; if so, patient does not require C-Spine immobilization or x-rays b. Patient may have pain with this maneuver; clearance is based on ability to actively rotate neck; can still be cleared even if pain is present c. If unable to rotate neck at least 45 degrees in each direction, replace C-collar and continue patient care with patient immobilized VIII. HEAD TRAUMA- Approximately 50% of trauma deaths are associated with head injury. A. Signs and Symptoms (Severe Head Trauma) 1. Altered mental status 2. Glasgow Coma Scale Score less than 8 or change of 2 points or more while under observation 3. Persistence of changes in levels of consciousness lasting longer than 2 minutes 4. D

ered mental status 2. Glasgow Coma Scale Score less than 8 or change of 2 points or more while under observation 3. Persistence of changes in levels of consciousness lasting longer than 2 minutes 4. Decreased heart rate/respirations 5. Bradycardia suggests increased intracranial pressure 6. Unequal pupils suggests unilateral head trauma 7. Fixed, dilated pupils may be due to brain ischemia, pain, drug overdose, or drug withdrawal 8. Unilateral sensory or motor changes 9. Drainage of clear, bubbly, or bloody discharge from ears or nose may indicate basilar skull fracture 10. Open injuries, especially with presence of suspected brain tissue or bony fragments B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Protect cervical spine b. Follow “Multiple Organ System Trauma” protocols above c. Patient requires continuous observation; do not leave patient unattended 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consult physician preceptor regarding type and amount of IV fluids for resuscitation b. Do not administer any medications unless directed by a preceptor c. Continually reassess for changes in neurologic status CLINICAL NOTE: Narcotics and sedatives should not be administered to a patien

Do not administer any medications unless directed by a preceptor c. Continually reassess for changes in neurologic status CLINICAL NOTE: Narcotics and sedatives should not be administered to a patient with a head injury because these medications will alter future neurologic evaluations and may worsen neurologic complications (decreased respiratory drive, hypothermia, etc).

43 IX. TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX- Collection of air between the lung and the chest wall prevents the lung from expanding properly during inhalation. As the collection of air increases, lung volume decreases. Common causes include rib fracture and over-ventilation during resuscitation efforts. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Initial presentation includes dyspnea, pain with deep inspiration or inability to take a deep breath, guarding or splinting the chest wall on affected side 2. Progresses to respiratory distress/tachypnea, hypotension, cyanosis 3. Unilaterally absent breath sounds, hyper-resonance with chest wall percussion on affected side, distended neck veins B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Perform immediate needle decompression on affected side b. If unsure whether or not pneumothorax may be present, perform needle decompression anyway! Rush of air will be diagnostic as well as therapeutic. If no rush of air, consider alternative diagnoses 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Needle decompression may need to be repeated multiple times while awaiting transport b. Definitive management is insertion of a chest tube with suction device X. OPEN PNEUMOTHORAX (“SUCKING CH

TION a. Needle decompression may need to be repeated multiple times while awaiting transport b. Definitive management is insertion of a chest tube with suction device X. OPEN PNEUMOTHORAX (“SUCKING CHEST WOUND”)- Chest wall penetrating injury causes air to collect between lung and chest wall, preventing adequate lung expansion. As patient attempts to breathe, air is pulled straight through opening in chest wall, rapidly causing lung on affected side to collapse. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Open chest wall wound 2. May have audible gurgling or sucking sounds from wound 3. May have frothy blood or bubbling at puncture sites B. Treatment 1. Follow “Multiple Organ System Trauma” protocols, above 2. Apply occlusive dressing to wound and attach securely 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION ACTION ALERT: Performing a needle decompression: 1. Select insertion site- 2nd intercostal space at mid-clavicular line on same side as hyper-resonance and diminished breath sounds 2. Cleanse site with povidone-iodine (Betadine®) or alcohol 3. Select 10-14 gauge needle over the catheter device at least 2 inches in length; use 10 gauge and longer needles for patients with increased bulk in

site with povidone-iodine (Betadine®) or alcohol 3. Select 10-14 gauge needle over the catheter device at least 2 inches in length; use 10 gauge and longer needles for patients with increased bulk in chest 4. Insert needle in a “Z” pattern going just over top of lower rib; nerves and blood vessels run along the inferior surface of each rib and so these areas should be avoided 5. Aspirate during insertion until air breaks vacuum 6. Remove needle and syringe and leave catheter attached to flutter valve or 3-way stopcock

44 XI. FLAIL CHEST- Blunt trauma to the chest wall causes a segment of ribs to break free from the rest of the chest wall. Usually 3 or more ribs are broken in two places each, creating the flail segment. Flail chest causes decreased respiratory ability on its own, but due to the force of the trauma there is also usually underlying organ damage. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Crepitus of the chest wall, asymmetrical chest wall motion, paradoxical respirations (chest wall seems to contract with inspiration and expand with exhalation) 2. Decreased respiratory rate, splinting or guarding on affected side, shallow breaths, chest wall pain 3. Contusions on chest wall B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Follow “Multiple Organ System Trauma” protocols, above b. Stabilize flail segment by applying direct pressure over injury site; this will relieve pain and improve ventilation c. Assume spinal trauma; transport with C-collar, backboard, and head immobilization d. Transport with affected side slightly elevated, if possible e. Shock is likely, but hydrate cautiously; use Lactated Ringer’s solution and administer just enough to keep systolic blood pressure greater than 90 mmHg f. Aggressive fluid r

y elevated, if possible e. Shock is likely, but hydrate cautiously; use Lactated Ringer’s solution and administer just enough to keep systolic blood pressure greater than 90 mmHg f. Aggressive fluid resuscitation can lead to pulmonary edema and death 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Do not administer narcotics unless directed by preceptor (narcotics may be necessary for pain control but will worsen respiratory depression) b. Discuss fluid resuscitation to include type and rate of administration XII. ABDOMINAL TRAUMA- Abdominal traumas are at increased risk of multiple life- threatening injuries including aortic dissection, spleen or liver laceration; shearing forces on kidney, liver, or spleen causing blood vessel rupture, or open wounds such as CLINICAL NOTE: Taping an occlusive dressing on 3 sides vs 4 sides for open pneumothorax remains controversial. Advantages of taping on 3 sides is that air has a way to escape from the pleural cavity but not to get in, where it can cause further damage. Disadvantages of taping on three sides are that it is more difficult to secure the occlusive dressing and that it can increase the risk of infection. Four-sided occlusi

re it can cause further damage. Disadvantages of taping on three sides are that it is more difficult to secure the occlusive dressing and that it can increase the risk of infection. Four-sided occlusive dressings reduce the risk of infection, but increase the risk of tension pneumothorax from a buildup of air in the pleural space. This will require needle decompression. Four-sided taping is most common for transport times under 30 minutes, because these wounds are unlikely to have time to develop a tension pneumothorax. For the IDMT, transport times will usually be longer than 30 minutes, and therefore 3-sided taping is recommended although both are permitted. In either case, continuously reassess patient and “burp” dressing or perform needle decompression at any sign of tension pneumothorax.

45 evisceration. Due to the high concentration of bacteria and debris inside the GI tract, abdominal wounds are at high risk for chronic infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Abdominal pain, guarding, nausea/vomiting, rebound tenderness, pelvic instability 2. Abdominal distention, abdominal wall ecchymosis, decreased bowel sounds B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Follow “Multiple Organ System Trauma” protocols, above b. Assume spinal trauma; transport with C-collar, backboard, and head immobilization c. Cover evisceration with moist (saline) dressing; however exposed abdominal organs can rapidly lower the body’s core temperature. Avoid moist dressings unless core body temperature can be maintained d. Reassess bowel sounds every 15 minutes in stable patients, every 5 minutes in unstable patients e. FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma) if available and trained 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XIII. PREGNANT TRAUMA PATIENT- Ask every female of childbearing age if there is a possibility of pregnancy. Special consideration must be made for the pregnant trauma patient. A. Anatomical Changes in the Pregnant Patient 1. Cardiovascular- increased heart rat

ing age if there is a possibility of pregnancy. Special consideration must be made for the pregnant trauma patient. A. Anatomical Changes in the Pregnant Patient 1. Cardiovascular- increased heart rate, changes in blood pressure, increased vascularity, increased plasma volume, EKG changes may resemble myocardial infarction, decreased red blood cell count, increased clotting in 3rd trimester 2. Pulmonary- Decreased lung volume, increased respiratory rate 3. Gastrointestinal- Decreased stomach volume, increased risk of aspiration; much greater risk of abdominal injury but more difficult to detect because guarding, rebound tenderness, and decreased bowel sounds are difficult to assess or not present despite severity of injury 4. Renal- Normal reduced renal function of pregnancy increases risk of kidney hypo-perfusion and injury during trauma B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Follow “Multiple Organ System Trauma” protocols, above unless otherwise specified CLINICAL NOTE: Diminished bowel sounds will be one of the first physical exam findings of severe abdominal injury. Take the time to listen to each quadrant for one full minute; as soon as bowel sounds are heard, continue with the r

sounds will be one of the first physical exam findings of severe abdominal injury. Take the time to listen to each quadrant for one full minute; as soon as bowel sounds are heard, continue with the remainder of exam and reassess again after 15 minutes. If no bowel sounds are heard following major trauma, assume patient is bleeding into abdominal cavity. Prepare for rapid decline in patient’s status progressing to shock.

46 b. Pregnant patient will require more oxygen than non-pregnant patient with same injuries c. CPR is more difficult in pregnant patients due to decreased chest wall flexibility d. Ask patient to report all fetal movements; check fetal heart tones with Doppler device if available (auscultation of fetal heart tones with stethoscope difficult until after 20 weeks) e. Keep patient in left lateral recumbent position to reduce compression of vena cava; this is very difficult if performing CPR f. Pregnant patients will have increased fluid requirements compared to non-pregnant patients with same injuries, however due to their decreased quantity of red blood cells, they will have more difficulty maintaining tissue oxygenation. Stop all bleeding as soon as possible to avoid loss of red blood cells! 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION

47 Chapter 3: Eye Protocols I. CENTRAL RETINAL ARTERY OCCLUSION (CRAO)- Blockages of the central retinal artery producing sudden, unilateral blindness. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Poor pupillary response with direct light, but brisk response with consensual light 2. Pale retina with “cherry red” macula 3. Affected field of vision corresponds with size of emboli and affected arterial branches B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Immediate treatment by an ophthalmologist is essential 3. If evacuation is delayed, consider digital massage over the closed eye 4. Perform cardiac exam for arrhythmias, murmurs, and bruit II. CHALAZION- a chronic enlargement of a meibomian gland followed by inflammation of the gland or surrounding tissue. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Difficult to distinguish from hordeolum 2. Lid edema, swelling and irritation are common 3. History of a slowly-growing, mobile, indurated mass in the lid 4. Red or grey mass visible on conjunctival side of eye lid B. Treatment 1. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm compresses to eyes for 15 minutes QID x 2 weeks then recheck CLINICAL NOTE: Differential Diagno

eye lid B. Treatment 1. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm compresses to eyes for 15 minutes QID x 2 weeks then recheck CLINICAL NOTE: Differential Diagnosis of “RED EYE”—Ruling out vision-threatening causes of red eye is critical in the management of all cases of red eye. Most “red eyes” that are seen in the clinical setting will be caused by allergic, bacterial or viral conjunctivitis. Prior to evaluating these etiologies, the following vision-threatening etiologies should be considered. Red eye accompanied by headache, nausea and vomiting is suggestive of glaucoma. Acute onset of red eye suggests trauma or irritation of the conjunctiva. Bilateral itching and watery discharge suggests allergic conjunctivitis. Scratchiness or foreign body sensation suggests corneal abrasion. A deep ocular ache accompanies scleritis. Photophobia suggests corneal abrasion, corneal ulcer or uveitis. Decreased visual acuity suggests severe pathology including glaucoma, corneal ulcer, hyphema and uveitis. Discharge corresponds to allergic, viral or bacterial infection. Unilateral exophthalmos suggests retro-bulbar pathology such as an abscess or tumor. Irre

glaucoma, corneal ulcer, hyphema and uveitis. Discharge corresponds to allergic, viral or bacterial infection. Unilateral exophthalmos suggests retro-bulbar pathology such as an abscess or tumor. Irregular corneal light reflex occurs with corneal edema. Redness around the cornea suggests uveitis, glaucoma or keratitis. Increased intraocular pressure is diagnostic for glaucoma. Pupil irregularity may be due to trauma, uveitis, or glaucoma. CLINICAL NOTE: digital massage can help dislodge the clot and allow it to enter a smaller branch of the artery and therefore limit the area of retinal ischemia.

48 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Typically most chalazion clear spontaneously in weeks to months b. Symptomatic patients with recalcitrant lesions can be referred to an ophthalmologist for incision and curettage or direct steroid injection 4. If no resolution after six weeks, refer to physician preceptor for further evaluation III. OCULAR BURNS, CHEMICAL- Can be caused by either acidic or basic solutions. Use pH paper to determine when the eye has returned to its normal pH. Use high volume but low pressure when irrigating. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of splash or powder exposure to the face 2. Severe pain and sensation of burning 3. May have photophobia, sensation of foreign body, vision changes 4. Copious lacrimation and conjunctival injection are the prominent symptoms 5. Coexisting lid edema and burns are common B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. IRRIGATE! Continuous irrigation is best chance to preserve vision b. Instill proparacaine hydrochloride (Alcaine®) ophthalmic solution, 1- 2 gtts. Prior to irrigation if blepharospasm or pain prevents irrigation c. Direct lavage of cornea with continuous sterile solution to dislodge particles d. Swab superior and

ophthalmic solution, 1- 2 gtts. Prior to irrigation if blepharospasm or pain prevents irrigation c. Direct lavage of cornea with continuous sterile solution to dislodge particles d. Swab superior and inferior cul-de-sac to remove particles. Dilate with a cycloplegic mydriatics drug (i.e. 2% homatropine 1 gtt BID) e. Administer NSAID and/or narcotic analgesic PRN such as CLINICAL NOTE: A stye appearing near the lower inner canthus must be differentiated between dacryrostenosis (blockage of the nasolacrimal duct causing excessive tearing). ACTION ALERT: The most important treatment of chemical burns is extensive immediate irrigation. Sterile higher osmotic solutions such as amphoteric solution (Diphoterine) or buffered solutions (BSS or Lactated Ringers) are ideal. If not available, sterile isotonic saline is an appropriate irrigant. DO NOT USE hypotonic solutions, such as water, because these result in deeper penetration of corrosive material into the corneal structures due to the cornea's higher osmotic gradient. The duration and amount of irrigation is determined by the eye pH. Continue irrigation until the pH remains at normal level for 30 minutes. Use of a Morgan lens or other

ea's higher osmotic gradient. The duration and amount of irrigation is determined by the eye pH. Continue irrigation until the pH remains at normal level for 30 minutes. Use of a Morgan lens or other eye irrigation system can minimize interference from blepharospasm, which can often be severe. If these are unavailable, the lid can be retracted manually with a Desmarres retractor, lid speculum, or bent paperclip. The end of intravenous tubing can direct the stream of sterile fluid across the eye. In addition, use a cotton swab to remove any particulate matter that may be retained in the fornices (corners).

Meperidine hydrochloride (Demerol®) 25 mg IV/IO every 3 -4 hrs PRN for pain v. Be prepared to manage respiratory depression any time IV/IO/IM narcotics are used f. Evaluate and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons g. CONSULT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION i. Needs urgent referral to ophthalmologist ii. All ocular burns require topical antibiotics, tetanus immunization, cycloplegics and mydriatics IV. OCULAR BURNS, RADIATION- Mild cases often go unrecognized in military population.
