Tearing, sensation of foreign body, photophobia 2. May have coexisting “sunburn” on eyelids and orbits B. Treatment 1. Mirrors treatment for corneal abrasion 2. Evaluate and

. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Tearing, sensation of foreign body, photophobia 2. May have coexisting “sunburn” on eyelids and orbits B. Treatment 1. Mirrors treatment for corneal abrasion 2. Evaluate and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 3. Symptoms should resolve within 24 hours with conservative treatment V. CONJUNCTIVITIS, BACTERIAL- inflammation of the bulbar and palpebral conjunctivae commonly caused by Staphylococcus or Streptococcus species, but may also be caused by exposure to genital secretions containing gonorrhea or chlamydia. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Injection of the conjunctivae 2. Itching 3. White/yellow/green mucoid discharge best seen along lid margins and in corners 4. Must ask thorough sexual history for possible STI exposure 5. Regardless of history, suspect gonorrhea or chlamydia if discharge is thick, purulent, or any vision changes are present (floaters, halos, etc) B. Treatment 1. Evaluate and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm compresses to eyes for 15 minutes every 3-4 hours 3. Remove crusts and debris 4. Usually self-limiting lasting 10 - 14 days 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Instill

arisons 2. Apply warm compresses to eyes for 15 minutes every 3-4 hours 3. Remove crusts and debris 4. Usually self-limiting lasting 10 - 14 days 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Instill antibiotic erythromycin (E-mycin®) ophthalmic ointment TID until asymptomatic

50 b. Alternatives are polymixin/ trimethroprim (Polytrim®) gtts or bacitracin (Eacticin®) ointment c. If patient wears contact lenses, stop lens wear and consider ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) gtts. d. If gonorrhea or chlamydia are suspected, patient requires further evaluation by a physician and oral anitibiotics i. If evacuation is delayed, begin systemic antibiotics ii. Treat patient for both gonorrhea and chlamydia until culture is available iii. Refer to Genitourinary protocols for further information VI. CONJUNCTIVITIS, VIRAL- Highly contagious infection of the conjunctivae caused by multiple different viruses. Usually presents without systemic symptoms. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Self-limited course lasting 2-4 weeks (typically lasts longer than bacterial conjunctivitis) 2. Pre-auricular lymphadenopathy suggests viral etiology 3. Watery discharge with scant mucous 4. May have history of recent URI or coexisting URI 5. May complain of burning, sandy or gritty feeling with “eye matted closed” especially upon waking 6. Second eye typically displays symptoms 24-48 hours after the first eye B. Treatment 1. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm co

sed” especially upon waking 6. Second eye typically displays symptoms 24-48 hours after the first eye B. Treatment 1. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm compresses to eyes for 15 minutes every 3 or 4 hours 3. Remove crusts and debris 4. Usually self limiting and resolves over 2-3 weeks 5. No medications indicated; discontinue eye makeup and throw away current contact lenses 6. Use glasses until 2 days after symptoms have completely resolve VII. CONJUNCTIVITIS, ALLERGIC- Injection of the conjunctivae associated with immune response to an irritant. Improves or resolves when changing environments or if irritant can be identified and removed. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Itchiness is the primary distinguishing symptom 2. Copious, bilateral, watery discharge ACTION ALERT: Gonoccocal conjunctivitis is a vision-threatening infection and characterized by copious, mucoid discharge, pain, periorbital swelling/tenderness to palpation. IMMEDIATE EAVCUATION to an ophthalmologist is required. ACTION ALERT: Herpetic infections of the eye can be serious and characterized by grouped vesicles over an erythematous base with serosanguinous crusts. IMMEDIATE EVA

to an ophthalmologist is required. ACTION ALERT: Herpetic infections of the eye can be serious and characterized by grouped vesicles over an erythematous base with serosanguinous crusts. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION to an ophthalmologist is required. Do not use steroids.

51 3. Periorbital edema 4. Additional allergic symptoms such as runny nose or sneezing B. Treatment 1. Visine AC® eye drops (for a short period of time) AND 2. Systemic antihistamines such as diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) or loratadine (Claritin®) 3. See “Ear, Nose, and Throat Protocols- Allergic Rhinitis” for more information VIII. CORNEAL ABRASION- Conjunctival and corneal abrasions are common and occur as the result of foreign bodies in the eye, dirt, debris, or contact use. Differential diagnosis should include foreign body, corneal ulcer and penetrating trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Foreign body sensation 2. Irritation and increased lacrimation 3. May be accompanied by photophobia 4. Flourescein stain highlights abrasion B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons b. If patient’s pain prevents an adequate exam, contact physician preceptor and instill topical ophthalmic anesthetic such as 1 gtt of proparacaine hydrochloride (Ophthaine®) c. Check cornea and conjunctiva with the Wood's lamp after instilling fluorescein stain into the eye to rule out foreign body and diagnose abrasion 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICA

(Ophthaine®) c. Check cornea and conjunctiva with the Wood's lamp after instilling fluorescein stain into the eye to rule out foreign body and diagnose abrasion 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Instill antibiotic, polymyxin B-bacitracin-neomycin (Neosporin®) ophthalmic ointment, into involved eye TID to QID b. For significant abrasion, instill 1-2 gtts of 5% homatropine (Isopto Homatropine®) to prevent a secondary iritis and help with discomfort c. Administer PO analgesics such as ibuprofen (Motrin®) or acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol#3®) 3. Remove patient from duties requiring eyestrain or intense visual focus ACTION ALERT: Warning signs of a more serious problem that should prompt evaluation by an ophthalmologist include: 1. Reduction or change in visual acuity 2. Ciliary flush: A pattern of injection in which the redness is most pronounced in a ring at the limbus (the limbus is the transition zone between the cornea and the sclera) 3. Photophobia 4. Severe foreign body sensation that prevents the patient from keeping the eye open 5. Corneal opacity 6. Fixed pupil 7. Severe headache with nausea

Repeat treatment if not completely re-epithelized and contact physician preceptor to consider referral to ophthalmologist C. Prevention 1. Ensure all work areas have eye protection sufficient for everyone 2. Ensure workout facilities have appropriate eye protection for activities at high risk of eye injury (Squash, racquetball, etc) 3. Check hobby locations to make sure eye protection is available (wood shop, framing shop, etc) IX. CORNEAL ULCER- Necrosis of corneal tissue due to infection by bacteria, fungus, virus or other microorganism is often due to extended contact lens wear. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Ache and foreign body sensation 2. Photophobia and increased lacrimation 3. Dull, grayish, circumscribed superficial opacity with an excavated center 4. Lesion will stain green with fluorescein 5. Erythema and corneal neovascularization occurs with long-standing ulcer 6. Pus may appear in the anterior chamber B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons b. Check cornea and conjunctiva using Wood's lamp or o

r 6. Pus may appear in the anterior chamber B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons b. Check cornea and conjunctiva using Wood's lamp or ophthalmoscope to rule out foreign body 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Administer optic antibiotics: ciprofloxacin (Cipro®), tobramycin (Tobradex®) or ofloxacin (Ocuflox®) gtts every 1-2 hours until seen by an ophthalmologist b. Administer PO analgesics ibuprophen (Motrin®) or acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) PRN for pain C. Prevention CLINICAL NOTE: Corneal ulcers are frequently the result of prolonged contact lens wear or inadequate cleansing of lenses between uses. Additionally, individuals will frequently use disposable lenses past their functional use- for example wearing 2-week lenses for 5 or 6 weeks. Even with proper cleansing and care, breakdown of the disposable lens material can cause infections to begin. ACTION ALERT: Do not use topical ophthalmic corticosteroids. They will make the condition worse because they tend to promote fungal growth. Be sure to use only medications designed for ophthalmologic use.

DACROCYSTITIS- An acute inflammation of the nasolacrimal duct, causing excessive tearing. Dacryocystitis can be complicated by orbital cellulitis, sepsis, or meningitis. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Conjunctival irritation B. Treatment 1. Massage lacrimal duct 3-4 times per day by applying gentle, downward pressure on the lacrimal duct, pressing towards the nasal fold 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. If infection is mild, prescribe clindamycin (covers MRSA), ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin (Levaquin®) b. DETACHED RETINA- Separation of the retina from the underlying epithelium. It can be caused by trauma, or as a result of chronic disease such as diabetes. Nearsightedness is a risk factor for detached retina. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Retinal detachment is painless 2. Vitreous floaters 3. Patient reports Hx of seeing flashes or blurred/darkened vision 4. If the macula is involved, central visual acuity fails 5. Fundoscopic exam may show irregularities of the retina such as loss of red reflex and darkened blood vessels B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Must be treated immediately by an ophthalmologist XII. FOREIGN BODY- Conjunctival and corneal foreign bodies are the most common eye injuries. Differential diagnosis of ocular penetration should be ruled out first. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Foreign body sensation 2. Irritation/increased lacrimation 3. May be accompanied by photophobia 4. ion 2. Irritation/increased lacrimation 3. May be accompanied by photophobia 4. Fluorescein stain highlights abrasions and foreign bodies 5. Multiple vertical lacerations indicate foreign body is trapped under the upper lid; lacerations are caused when the patient blinks and drags the foreign body across the conjunctiva B. Treatment

Use a wet cotton swab to remove the foreign body 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. buprofen (Motrin®) or acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol#3®) 4. Recheck patient every 24 hours until signs and symptoms resolve a. Check for secondary infection of conjunctiva with symptoms such as redness or discharge b. Injury has high likelihood of developing corneal ulcer 5. Follow up should be performed by an ophthalmologist within 1-2 days after injury if possible XIII. GLAUCOMA, PRIMARY OPEN-ANGLE- Increased intraocular pressure (IOP) associated with an open anterior chamber angle. This is the most common form of glaucoma. Both eyes are usually affected, but not equally. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. No early warning symptoms 2. Peripheral vision loss occurs first and usually not noticed by the patient 3. Patients may complain of difficulty reading, driving or performing coordinated activities 4. Intraocular pressure may be elevated (>21mmHg) 5. Risk factors include age, heritage (African American), family history, myopia (nearsightedness) and diabetes 6. HORDEOLUM- An acute, localized infection of the glands of Zeis or Moll (external hordeolum) or occasionally of the Meibomian glands (internal hordeolum or stye) A. Signs and Symptoms 1. External hordeolum a. Pain, redness and tenderness of the lid margin initially, followed by a point of induration or pustule b. Photophobia, lacrimation and foreign body sensation c. Localized edema, but may be diffuse d. Symptoms are relieved if the abscess ruptures, releasing purulent discharge 2. Internal hordeolum a. More severe and occur with less frequency than external styes b. Small, elevated, yellow punctal lesion at the site of the infected gland c. Abscess may come to a point through the conjunctiva or occasionally through the anterior side of the eye lid d. Spontaneous rupture does not usually occur B. Treatment 1. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm, moist compresses to eyes for 10 minutes 3-4 times per day until spontaneous rupture
Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 2. Apply warm, moist compresses to eyes for 10 minutes 3-4 times per day until spontaneous rupture

56 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Topical antibiotics may be needed to treat internal hordeola b. Consider erythromycin (E-Mycin®) ophthalmic 4. Reevaluate patient in 24 hours 5. If stable, continue treatment for 5 days 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION if symptoms last longer than 1 week or if at any point vision is affected XVI. HYPHEMA (ANTERIOR CHAMBER HEMORRHAGE)- Bleeding into the anterior chamber of the eye may follow blunt trauma. Hyphemas are an emergency requiring immediate attention from an ophthalmologist. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Blood pooling in the anterior chamber of the eye with obvious blood-fluid level 2. Caused by blunt trauma to the globe or orbit 3. May have distorted pupil 4. May be followed by recurrent bleeding, glaucoma, and staining of the cornea B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons b. Place patient at bed rest c. Patient will remain at bed rest – (Fowler’s position) 45° - for 6 to 7 days d. Do not allow patient to lie flat. Keep the patient’s head elevated at 45° or greater e. Patch affected eye without causing additional pressure on the affected eye 2. CONTACT PHYSI

- for 6 to 7 days d. Do not allow patient to lie flat. Keep the patient’s head elevated at 45° or greater e. Patch affected eye without causing additional pressure on the affected eye 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If evacuation is delayed, consider a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor such as one acetazolamide (Diamox®) tablet every 4 hours b. Do not administer NSAIDs or aspirin- may increase bleeding c. Give acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain relief d. Consider acetaminophen with oxycodone (Percocet®) or acetaminophen with hydrocodone (Vicodin®) for severe pain e. For nausea, consider ondansetron (Zofran®) f. Do not give mydriatic drugs or stain the eye unless directed by an ophthalmologist XVII. ORBITAL CELLULITIS- Inflammation of the tissues surrounding the orbit caused by an infection that extends from any tissue in the face, mouth or sinuses. Cellulitis can also be caused by bacteria introduced by direct trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms ACTION ALERT: Trauma affecting the eye should be treated by an ophthalmologist. Primary concerns are increased IOP (intraocular pressure) and globe rupture. Aggressive pain/nausea management is essential to avoid increases in IO

ng the eye should be treated by an ophthalmologist. Primary concerns are increased IOP (intraocular pressure) and globe rupture. Aggressive pain/nausea management is essential to avoid increases in IOP.

57 1. Trauma to the eyelid and infections of the sinus cavity are the most common causes 2. Pain, eyelid redness, swelling, hyperemic mucous membranes 3. Exophthalmos, decreased mobility of the eye 4. Fever 5. Double vision 6. Complications include blindness, meningitis and encephalitis B. Treatment 1. Find the primary cause of the infection 2. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Start antibiotic therapy b. Consider cefuroxime (Ceftin®) or cefoxitin (Mefoxin®) PO/IV 4. Reevaluate daily 5. If antibiotics are not effective, refer to an ophthalmologist XVIII. PINGUECULA/PTERYGIUM- A pinguecula is a benign neoplasm of the conjunctiva. A pterygium is a corneal degeneration. Both are yellow, bumpy surface changes on the eye related to increased UV/sun exposure. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pinguecula- raised, yellow-white mass on the bulbar conjunctiva adjacent to the cornea at the 3 or 9 o’clock position 2. Pterygium- wedge-shaped growth extending onto the cornea with visible intrusion over the iris 3. Both pinguecula and pterygium are painless but may be accompanied by foreign body sensation, irritation or localize

edge-shaped growth extending onto the cornea with visible intrusion over the iris 3. Both pinguecula and pterygium are painless but may be accompanied by foreign body sensation, irritation or localized erythema B. Treatment 1. Does not directly effect eye’s ability to see, but can eventually limit field of vision by direct extension across the cornea 2. No treatment required in the acute setting 3. May use OTC ophthalmologic lubricants (Artificial Tears® or Clear Eyes CLR®) to reduce irritation if needed XIX. TRAUMA AND CONTUSIONS INVOLVING THE EYE- Direct trauma to the face or nose can manifest as “black eye”, even if the orbit did not sustain trauma itself. Swelling around the orbits can make it difficult to examine the structures of the globe; if globe is unable to be assessed due to surrounding edema, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION is required to ensure orbital fracture, hyphema, or vision-threatening problems have not occurred. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Lid Contusions a. “Black Eye” b. Periorbital ecchymosis and edema ACTION ALERT: Any decreased mobility of the eye or history of double vision accompanied by fever is highly suggestive of meningeal spread or sepsis. Emer

Black Eye” b. Periorbital ecchymosis and edema ACTION ALERT: Any decreased mobility of the eye or history of double vision accompanied by fever is highly suggestive of meningeal spread or sepsis. Emergency evacuation is required.

58 2. Lid Lacerations- minor a. Horizontal b. Not full thickness 3. Lid Lacerations- extensive (any of the following) a. Vertical b. Involving nasal or tear duct areas c. Full thickness B. Treatment 1. Lid contusions a. Apply ice packs to the region for the first 48 hours b. Apply warm compresses to the region for the next 48 hours c. Ensure trauma is limited to lid and does not involve the globe d. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION i. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is required if any of the extraoccular movements or pupillary reflexes are abnormal ii. Other treatments at discretion of preceptor 2. Lid Lacerations- minor a. Cleanse and suture with 6-0 silk b. Many minor upper lid horizontal lacerations do not require sutures c. Administer tetanus toxoid 0.5 ml or immunoglobulin as indicated d. Apply sterile dressing and keep dry 3. Lid Lacerations- extensive a. CONSULT PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION i. Cleanse ii. Apply sterile dressing with wound margins approximated if possible iii. Administer tetanus toxoid 0.5 ml or immunoglobulin as indicated iv. IDMT’s should not suture due to the wound’s increased complexity XX. GLOBE TRAUMA- Blunt trauma affecting the continuity of the conju

dminister tetanus toxoid 0.5 ml or immunoglobulin as indicated iv. IDMT’s should not suture due to the wound’s increased complexity XX. GLOBE TRAUMA- Blunt trauma affecting the continuity of the conjunctiva or sclera, causing an opening of either of the chambers of the eye and distorting the globe. Includes laceration of the iris, dislocated lens, hemorrhage into the globe, and hemorrhage or rupture of the eyeball. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma 2. May have irregular, teardrop-shaped or nonreactive pupil 3. Hyphema 4. Extruded vitreous humor 5. Unable to open eye ACTION ALERT: NEVER SHAVE THE EYEBROW. It may not grow back. Do not use ophthalmic ointments when a penetrating wound of the eye is present. They will cause permanent eye floaters. Never attempt to suture a vertical laceration of the eyelid or laceration involving the lid margin as an associated penetrating injury to the eye must be ruled out.

59 6. Do not use fluorescein in patients with suspected globe trauma; however if fluorescein staining was done and “streaming” fluorescein is seen, this is diagnostic of an open chamber a. “Streaming” is caused by leaking aqueous humor b. Avoid fluorescein backwashing into wound B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Administer meperidine HCl (Demerol®) IV for pain b. Apply sterile protective dressing to both eyes c. Penicillin PO/IM or other systemic antibiotic as directed d. If patient IS allergic to penicillin, give erythromycin (E-mycin®) PO until evacuation e. Administer tetanus toxoid, 0.5 ml, IM, or immunoglobulin as indicated 2. Evacuate to a medical facility with ophthalmologic and maxillofacial surgery as soon as possible XXI. TRAUMA, PENETRATING WOUND OF THE EYE- Occurs when a foreign body has passed through the thickness of the conjunctiva and cornea or sclera causing an opening into one of the chambers of the eye. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. May or may not be able to visualize foreign body depending on the location 2. History of foreign object sensation or trauma 3. May have irregular, teardrop-shaped or nonreactive pupil 4. Hyphema 5. Extrud

or may not be able to visualize foreign body depending on the location 2. History of foreign object sensation or trauma 3. May have irregular, teardrop-shaped or nonreactive pupil 4. Hyphema 5. Extruded vitreous humor 6. Unable to open eye 7. Do not use fluorescein in patients with suspected penetration of the globe; however if fluorescein staining was done and “streaming” flourescein is seen, this is diagnostic of an open chamber a. “Streaming” is caused by leaking aqueous humor b. Avoid fluorescein backwashing into wound B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Place patient on his/her back b. Keep manipulation of the eye to an absolute minimum c. Do not clean wounds of the globe d. Reduce visual stimulation i. Turn off lights ii. Apply sterile, protective dressing to both eyes e. Examine head and other areas of the body for additional injuries 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Administer meperidine hydrochloride (Demerol®), 50 mg, IM, every 3 to 4 hours, as necessary, for pain b. Evacuate to a medical facility with ophthalmologic and maxillofacial surgery as soon as possible
rs, as necessary, for pain b. Evacuate to a medical facility with ophthalmologic and maxillofacial surgery as soon as possible

60 XXII. SUBCONJUNCTIVAL HEMORRHAGE- A wedge-shaped flat collection of blood between the sclera and conjunctiva, occurring suddenly after a transient increase in intraocular pressure such as crouching, sneezing or vomiting, but can also occur secondary to blunt trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Usually painless and asymptomatic 2. Focal collection of bright red blood between sclera and conjunctiva B. Treatment 1. None 2. Defect will resolve spontaneously over 1-3 weeks XXIII. UVEITIS- Inflammation of the iris, cilliary body, or chorioid. Distinguished from conjunctivitis because redness usually at or crossing the iris border, whereas in conjunctivitis the injection usually spares the iris. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain, redness, photophobia, and decreased vision 2. Patient may complain of “floaters” 3. Redness worst adjacent to the cornea radiating from the iris B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Test and record visual acuity as a baseline for later comparisons b. Instill fluorescein stain into conjunctival sac and examine eye to rule out a corneal lesion c. Warm compresses will decrease pain 2. CONTACT PHYISICAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Antimicrobials are used to treat i

tain into conjunctival sac and examine eye to rule out a corneal lesion c. Warm compresses will decrease pain 2. CONTACT PHYISICAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Antimicrobials are used to treat infectious uveitis; consult preceptor b. Consider instilling 1 drop of scopolamine ophthalmic solution (Isopto Hyoscine®) (0.25%) into involved eye every 12 hours, until asymptomatic if directed by preceptor c. Administer non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug or narcotic/NSAID combination PO as needed for pain relief ACTION ALERT: If size or shape of the blood spot is changing, or if symptoms are accompanied by pain or foreign body sensation, consider trauma or foreign body. CLINICAL NOTE: This pattern of redness is different from that seen with viral, allergic, and bacterial conjunctivitis because in those cases, the injection usually spares the area around the cornea. In the case of uveitis, the injection usually appears like a “sunburst” radiating from the iris or cornea.

61 Chapter 4: Ear, Nose and Throat Protocols Ear I. OTITIS EXTERNA, ACUTE- An inflammation of the external auditory canal. May be precipitated by other ENT infections, allergies or flaking of the skin. Trauma from cotton- tipped applicator use is the most common source of infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Itching, pain, and drainage of the affected ear 2. History of Q-tip® use or other manipulation of the auditory canal 3. History of wet environment (i.e. swimming, headphones, ear plugs etc.) 4. Inflammation of external auditory canal 5. Tenderness to tragus/pinna with manipulation 6. Visible debris or discharge in externl auditory canal B. Treatment 1. Remove visible debris from canal 2. Administer NSAIDs or acetaminophen for pain 3. Apply heat to the affected ear for comfort 4. Advise patient to keep the ear dry and avoid earplugs, headphones, etc. until the infection has completely cleared 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Apply ciprofloxacin otic (Cipro HC®), Cortisporin Otic® or other acidic combination of steroid + antiseptic + antibiotic drops into the ear canal b. If otic antibiotics are not available, apply undiluted acetic acid (vinegar) or alcohol TID c. Us

rin Otic® or other acidic combination of steroid + antiseptic + antibiotic drops into the ear canal b. If otic antibiotics are not available, apply undiluted acetic acid (vinegar) or alcohol TID c. Use ear wick placed in canal to administer drops if canal is too swollen to apply drops directly d. Culture ear discharge for pseudomonas if greenish appearance or failure to respond to above measures e. Administer appropriate systemic antimicrobial therapy if indicated by fever, or involvement of surrounding tissues f. Patient requires emergency evacuation if systemic symptoms occur II. OTITIS MEDIA, ACUTE PURULENT- “Ear infection” marked by ear pain and bulging red tympanic membrane. The EAC can also be erythematous in severe cases with erythema extending away from the tympanic membrane. If the canal is inflamed and inflammation is not worse adjacent to the TM, then a diagnosis of external otitis should be considered. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Ear pain 2. Decreased hearing 3. Possible fever 4. May be accompanied by history of recent upper respiratory infection 5. Red, bulging TM 6. Purulent discharge in external auditory canal if TM has ruptured 7. Conductive hearing loss (Weber/Rinné t

4. May be accompanied by history of recent upper respiratory infection 5. Red, bulging TM 6. Purulent discharge in external auditory canal if TM has ruptured 7. Conductive hearing loss (Weber/Rinné test with 512 tuning fork: sound lateralizes to affected ear and BC>AC)

62 8. Anterior and posterior auricular lymphadenopathy B. Treatment 1. Administer analgesics as needed for pain; acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or ibuprofen (Motrin®) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Administer appropriate antimicrobial therapy b. If patient IS NOT allergic to penicillin-give amoxicillin (Trimox®), OR amoxicillin clavulanate (Augmentin®) c. Alternatives are clarithromycin (Cleocin®) or azithromycin (Zithromax®) III. BAROTRAUMA, EAR- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, Barotrauma, Ear” IV. FOREIGN BODY IN EXTERNAL AUDITORY CANAL- Ear foreign body in adults is typically caused by crawling insects, but can also be related to environmental hazards, foreign objects used to clean or scratch the ear canal, or ear protection/headphone malfunction. Removal strategies are based on cause. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain to the affected ear 2. Bleeding 3. Change in hearing or sensation of movement in the ear canal (insect) 4. Visible obstruction or trauma 5. Hearing changes B. Treatment 1. If the object is easily grasped (the exterior ½ of the canal) remove slowly with hemostats or alligator forceps 2. For plastic objects, irrigate with warm water using 30-50 cc syringe

B. Treatment 1. If the object is easily grasped (the exterior ½ of the canal) remove slowly with hemostats or alligator forceps 2. For plastic objects, irrigate with warm water using 30-50 cc syringe and butterfly catheter 3. For removal of insects that are deeper than external ½ of canal, attempt to entice insect out with at light source at external meatus ; large insects will not be able to turn and light will not help them 4. If the TM is verified as intact, and if the insect cannot or will not be enticed out, immobilize the insect before manually removing it by filling the external auditory canal with lidocaine, mineral oil, or alcohol 5. If the TM can be verified to be intact, you may flush the external auditory canal with mineral oil and then remove the foreign object manually as it gets closer to the auditory meatus CLINICAL NOTE: Mastoid bone tenderness and uncontrolled fevers are emergencies due to the potential spread of the infection to the deep structures of the brain and sinuses. Contact physician preceptor for evacuation consideration. ACTION ALERT: Do not use irrigation for batteries or chemicals. If unable to remove the object, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATIO

inuses. Contact physician preceptor for evacuation consideration. ACTION ALERT: Do not use irrigation for batteries or chemicals. If unable to remove the object, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION.

63 6. If foreign body and signs of infection are present at the same time, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 7. Reassess hearing after foreign body is removed 8. Reevaluate patient in 24 hours for infection and to recheck hearing V. RUPTURED OR PERFORATED EARDRUM- Ruptured tympanic membrane may be found incidentally on exam or may be related to external otitis, otitis media, or trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe escalating ear pain followed by abrupt improvement 2. Drainage from the ear 3. History of ear surgeries/tubes/frequent ear infections, hearing loss 4. May complain of sensation of air in the ear or “rushing” air sounds 5. Visible hole in TM (may enable clear view of ossicles) 6. Drainage may be evident in canal 7. Potentially abnormal Weber, Rinné or Whisper tests B. Treatment 1. Keep ear absolutely dry- use cotton balls in shower, etc. 2. Treat underlying condition if rupture is due to otitis media or other infection 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Do not irrigate or use curette in ear to remove debris unless instructed to do so b. Follow up daily for reexamination and reevaluation of hearing c. Administer analgesics for pain, as needed

ATION a. Do not irrigate or use curette in ear to remove debris unless instructed to do so b. Follow up daily for reexamination and reevaluation of hearing c. Administer analgesics for pain, as needed i. Ibuprofen (Motrin®) PRN for pain, or ii. Acetaminophen with codeine sulfate (Tylenol #3®) with preceptor approval d. Surgical intervention may be required VI. OTITIS MEDIA, SEROUS - Blockage of the eustacean tube causing fluid buildup behind the tympanic membrane and inability to clear ears. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Feeling of fullness 2. Hearing change 3. Often associated with recent URI, allergies or air travel 4. Retracted TM 5. Thick or amber colored TM 6. Bubbles or fluid line visible behind the TM 7. Conductive hearing loss (Weber/Rinné test with 512 tuning fork: sound lateralizes to affected ear and BC>AC) B. Treatment 1. Treat underlying cause 2. Administer oral decongestants such as pseudoephedrine hydrochloride (Sudafed®) 3. Administer oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin®) nose drops for treatment of associated URI. Do not use for more than 3 days 4. Perform self-insuflation of the ear using Valsalva maneuver after each instillation of nose drops ACTION ALERT: Patients mus

e drops for treatment of associated URI. Do not use for more than 3 days 4. Perform self-insuflation of the ear using Valsalva maneuver after each instillation of nose drops ACTION ALERT: Patients must be able to clear their ears (perform Valsalva maneuver) before being allowed to fly. Flying with serous otitis media predisposes patients to tympanic membrane rupture since they are unable to equalize pressure on both sides of the membrane.

64 VII. VERTIGO - Symptom related to several disorders affecting the inner ear. A false sensation of movement causing deficits in proprioception, balance and gait. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. “Dizziness”, imbalance, or sensation of spinning or tilting made worse by moving or tilting the head 2. May be accompanied by severe nausea or vomiting 3. Patient may appear to be having a “panic attack”- often the vertigo is so scary they get sensation of panic with tachycardia and dyspnea 4. Nystagmus, gait instability, and falling to side while attempting Romberg test can all be present B. Treatment 1. Attempt to determine the underlying cause of the vertigo 2. Treat the underlying cause when applicable 3. Administer diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) PO, or if symptoms are severe, administer diazepam (Valium®) PO 4. If patient is still nauseous 30 minutes after diphenhydramine administration, consider ondansetron (Zofran®) 5. Check hearing 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. If tinnitus or hearing changes are present, evacuate for further evaluation b. If vertigo follows otitis media, meningitis, or signs of other infectious process, antibiotics are indicated. Discuss with preceptor. 7.

earing changes are present, evacuate for further evaluation b. If vertigo follows otitis media, meningitis, or signs of other infectious process, antibiotics are indicated. Discuss with preceptor. 7. Place patient at bedrest, preferably in a darkened room, until severe symptoms subside 8. For mild symptoms or motion sickness prophylaxis or treatment, give dimenhydrinate (Dramamine®), or meclizine (Antivert®) PO Nose VIII. RHINOSINUSITIS, ACUTE VIRAL/”UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTION”- A “cold”. An inflammation and infection of the paranasal sinuses caused by a variety of communicable viruses. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Nasal congestion/obstruction 2. Loss of sense of smell 3. Rhinorrhea (may be purulent) 4. Ear pressure/pressure when leaning forward 5. Symptoms are typically less than 10 days 6. Swollen, erythematous turbinates 7. Maxillary pressure with palpation and percussion B. Treatment 1. Symptoms should improve or resolve within 10 days with/without therapy 2. If cough is present, check pulse oximetry and peak flows 3. Consider nasal washes for influenza and adenovirus
k pulse oximetry and peak flows 3. Consider nasal washes for influenza and adenovirus

65 4. Mechanical flushing of the nasal cavity with salt solution reduces symptoms and prevents bacterial superinfection a. Use syringe or netipot to irrigate nasal passageways b. Mix 1 Tbsp Kosher salt (no iodine) with 8 oz warm water c. Encourage patient that although initially unpleasant, once mastered this technique will dramatically improve symptoms and pressure 5. If ear pressure is also a symptom, administer oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin ®) for no more than 3 days 6. Provide supportive therapy and encourage PO fluid intake >3 L per day or enough for patient to urinate every 2 hours while awake IX. RHINITIS, ALLERGIC- Differentiated from viral and bacterial rhinosinusitis due to multiple factors. Symptoms of allergic rhinitis are intermittent and change based on season or environment. Usually symptoms all start at the same time and do not evolve. Nasal symptoms tend to occur before sore throat. This is in contrast to a “cold”, which often starts as malaise and sore throat and progresses to cough. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Thin watery, “dripping faucet” nasal secretions; watery eyes and tearing 2. Absence of pain 3. Itching of the nose, throat, eyes, or face 4. Sneezing 5.

progresses to cough. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Thin watery, “dripping faucet” nasal secretions; watery eyes and tearing 2. Absence of pain 3. Itching of the nose, throat, eyes, or face 4. Sneezing 5. All symptoms start at once and leave at once without progression of symptoms 6. Pale grey, white or blue swollen turbinates which may appear to have dangling masses or polyps 7. Visible post nasal drip in posterior oropharynx or pharyngeal erythema 8. Associated symptoms include: increased lacrimation, conjunctival injection/cobblestoning, and eyelid edema B. Treatment 1. If cough is present, check pulse oximetry and peak flows 2. Avoid allergens if known 3. 1st line treatment- nasal glucocorticoids such as fluticasone (Flonase®) or mometasone (Nasonex®) 4. If symptoms are severe, consider adding 1st /2nd generation antihistamine such as hydroxyzine (Atarax®) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) CLINICAL NOTE: Saline spray, zinc, and pseudoephedrine (Sudafed®) are all widely used but lack clinical evidence sufficient to recommend their use. ACTION ALERT: Avoid using oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin ®) nose drops for more than 3 consecutive days. Rebound congestion may occur with prolonged

al evidence sufficient to recommend their use. ACTION ALERT: Avoid using oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin ®) nose drops for more than 3 consecutive days. Rebound congestion may occur with prolonged or excessive use. The above drugs must be used with caution with patients having a cardiac history. Consult with preceptor prior to administering if such history is present.

66 X. BACTERIAL SINUSITIS- An acute inflammation of the paranasal sinuses. Caused by multiple different strains of bacteria. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Nasal congestion/obstruction, purulent nasal discharge, fever, loss of sense of smell, ear pressure, halitosis, decreased appetite, and headache all possible 2. Symptoms >10 days duration 3. Positive head tilt, positive tooth rake 4. Swollen, erythematous turbinates 5. Maxillary sinus tenderness (especially unilateral) with palpation/percussion 6. Initial improvement followed by worsening symptoms B. Treatment 1. If fever < 101° and pain is mild, preferred treatment is close observation and nasal saline washes (See “Acute Viral Sinusitis”) 2. If cough is present, check pulse oximetry and peak flows 3. Consider nasal washings for influenza and adenovirus 4. If mucous is thick, may use mucolytics such as guaifenesin (Mucinex®) 5. If nasal swelling, congestion, or sinus pressure is a predominant symptom, give nasal glucocoricoid such as Flonase® or Nasonex® 6. Administer analgesics/antipyretics such as acetaminophen (Tylenol®) if required for relief of pain and fever 7. If ear pressure is also a symptom, administer oxymetazoline hydrochl

ase® or Nasonex® 6. Administer analgesics/antipyretics such as acetaminophen (Tylenol®) if required for relief of pain and fever 7. If ear pressure is also a symptom, administer oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin®) nasally for no more than 3 days 8. Provide supportive therapy and encourage PO fluid intake >3 L per day or enough for patient to urinate every 2 hours while awake 9. Zinc preparations, systemic decongestants such as pseudoephedrine hydrochloride (Sudafed®), and antihistamines have little efficacy compared to side effect profile and should be avoided, although patient may be adamant that they help 10. If fever >101° or severe pain, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. 1st line- amoxicillin, trimethoprim-sulfamethaxazole (Septra®), or erythromcyin b. Do not use amoxicillin in penicillin-allergic patients CLINICAL NOTE: 1st line treatment, or if planning to treat with a single agent, use a nasal glucocorticoid such as fluticasone (Flonase®) or mometasone (Nasonex®). Additionally, may add 1st generation antihistamine as above. Loratidine (Claritin®), cetirizine (Zyrtec®) and fexofenadine (Allegra®) may be used instead of 1st generation antihistamine due to their decre

tionally, may add 1st generation antihistamine as above. Loratidine (Claritin®), cetirizine (Zyrtec®) and fexofenadine (Allegra®) may be used instead of 1st generation antihistamine due to their decreased side effect of drowsiness, but they have been shown to be less effective than the first generation antihistamines. Decongestants such as pseudoephedrine (Sudafed®) do not demonstrate significant improvement when compared to nasal glucocorticoids. ACTION ALERT: If patient has fever> 103°F, visual changes, periorbital edema, or severe headache, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION. Consider evacuation for further evaluation and management.

67 XI. BAROTRAUMA, SINUS- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, Barotrauma, Sinus” XII. EPISTAXIS- Bloody nose” incidence is increased in winter weather due to decreased humidity in the air and drying of the nasal passages. May result from facial trauma or arise spontaneously. Other factors that predispose patients to epistaxis include upper respiratory infection, excessive alcohol intake, allergic rhinitis, digital trauma and ingesting medications that interfere with clotting (i.e. NSAIDS and aspirin), and nasal steroid use (Flonase®). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bleeding from the nose, usually unilateral, often brisk 2. History of one or more of the above predisposing factors 3. Evaluate for possible foreign body during history and physical 4. Determine site of bleed (have patient blow their nose and attempt examination) B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Monitor the airway b. Keep patient in a sitting position; do not lean patient forward or backward; keep patient’s head in a neutral position c. Instruct patient to blow their nose and spray 2 sprays of oxymetazoline (Afrin®) in affected nare d. Instruct patient to apply pressure by pinching the soft lobular portion of the nose (pre

osition c. Instruct patient to blow their nose and spray 2 sprays of oxymetazoline (Afrin®) in affected nare d. Instruct patient to apply pressure by pinching the soft lobular portion of the nose (pressure should be maintained for 5 to 10 minutes) e. This is often sufficient; however, if bleeding continues: i. Pack the bleeding nostril with a cotton ball or tampon that has been saturated with a vasoconstrictor such as oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin®) or lidocaine with epinephrine (place a small amount of Bacitracin® on the tip of the tampon or cotton ball for ease of insertion) ii. After insertion, inject approximately 10 cc of saline into the tampon to cause tampon to expand iii. Leave tampon in place for >15 minutes iv. If bleeding continues and originates from a visible, bare blood vessel in the anterior portion of the nostril, use a silver nitrate stick to cauterize the bleeding site v. If the above measures are insufficient, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION and possible evacuation CLINICAL NOTE: Antibiotics are not recommended if pain is mild and temperature is less than 101° even if symptoms have lasted >10 days. Spontaneous resolution is likely. Follow viral rhi

le evacuation CLINICAL NOTE: Antibiotics are not recommended if pain is mild and temperature is less than 101° even if symptoms have lasted >10 days. Spontaneous resolution is likely. Follow viral rhinosinusitis guidelines. ACTION ALERT: If bleeding is severe or in the posterior area and uncontrollable by the above treatments, use the postnasal pack procedure to control bleeding. The balloon of a Foley catheter may be used to obstruct the choana (After consulting with preceptor).

68 SINUSITIS, MUCORMYCOSIS (FUNGAL)- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Fungal Pneumonias, Mucormycosis” Throat XIII. STREP THROAT- For empirical treatment with antibiotics, patients should meet 3 of 4 Centor criteria. If patient does not meet Centor criteria but with severe sore throat, close followup is recommended. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Centor criteria a. Erythematous, exudative tonsils and posterior oropharynx b. Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy c. Fever by history or exam d. No cough 2. May also have halitosis, hematuria, rashes, vision changes, joint pain and orthostatic hypotension B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Monitor vital signs b. Initiate IV fluids if needed to correct orthostasis c. Advise patient to rest and eat a light diet. Encourage fluid intake d. Advise patient to decrease or stop smoking e. Administer analgesics/antipyretics as required. May give up to 1 gram of acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or 800mg of ibuprofen (Motrin®) if fever >101°F and no hematuria f. Perform rapid strep test if available i. If rapid test is negative, perform throat culture and sensitivity tests to identify causative organism ii. If rapid strep test is positive, throat culture

a f. Perform rapid strep test if available i. If rapid test is negative, perform throat culture and sensitivity tests to identify causative organism ii. If rapid strep test is positive, throat culture is not necessary g. Consider nasal washes for influenza and adenovirus h. Administer warm (not hot) saline gargles every 2 hours i. Quarters until afebrile for 24 hours j. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR-MEDICATION CLINICAL NOTE: For all sore throats, consider possible life threats of peritonsillar abscess and rheumatic fever (a complication of strep throat). Sore throats can be broken down into categories of bacterial vs viral vs other. This section reviews major causes of sore throat and diagnosis and treatment for each category. Subjective data should include the Centor criteria, if rash is present, and whether or not the patient is able to swallow saliva. CLINICAL NOTE: For rheumatic fever, check cardio for S3 sounds, murmur, tachycardia, and rubs; check skin for rash and check joints for pain/inflammation. Check lungs for rales/crackles at bases. If splenomegaly is present, diagnosis is more-likely EBV.
pain/inflammation. Check lungs for rales/crackles at bases. If splenomegaly is present, diagnosis is more-likely EBV.

69 i. Administer antibiotics if 3 or more Centor criteria are met and/or rapid strep test is positive ii. If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin, give phenoxymethyl penicillin (Pen Vee K®) or IM equivalent. iii. If patient IS allergic to penicillin, give clarithromycin (Biaxin®), azithromycin (Zithromax®), or erythromycin (E- mycin®) XIV. VIRAL PHARYNGITIS- Likely etiology when patient meets <3 of the Centor criteria. Etiologic agents can include Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), influenza, rhinovirus, adenovirus, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and HIV. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Review signs and symptoms for peritonsillar abscess and strep throat 2. Will not meet Centor criteria 3. May display the following: a. Low grade or no fever, rhinitis, cough/hoarse voice, sinusitis, headache and body aches and decreased appetite b. Erythema of the posterior oropharynx, normal to slightly enlarged tonsils without exudates c. Mild, nontender lymphadenopathy or absence of palpable lymph nodes B. Treatment 1. Supportive care a. Lozenges or numbing sprays may provide symptomatic relief b. Encourage fluid intake to >3 L per day c. Soft or liquid diet; advance as tolerated d. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®)

ment 1. Supportive care a. Lozenges or numbing sprays may provide symptomatic relief b. Encourage fluid intake to >3 L per day c. Soft or liquid diet; advance as tolerated d. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain, body aches and fever XV. MONONUCLEOSIS (EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS)- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Mononucleosis” XVI. MUCOCUTANEOUS LEISHMANIASIS- See “Integumentary Protocols, Leishmaniasis” XVII. PERITONSILLAR ABSCESS - An acute infection/abscess of pharyngeal structures common in young adults. This disorder can progress rapidly and may pose a significant threat to the patient’s airway. A. Signs and Symptoms CLINICAL NOTE: Ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) may be given for PCN sensitivity such as a rash, but not for PCN anaphylaxis). ACTION ALERT: Strep throat can have serious or even deadly consequences such as poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis (marked by hematuria and other urinary changes) and acute rheumatic fever (fine pink sandpaper-like rash, vision changes, joint pain, and valvular heart disease). To reduce the chance of complications such as rheumatic fever or nephritis, treatment of pharyngitis should be continued for the full course.

70 1. Difficulty swallowing, progressing to a. Sensation of airway closure when lying supine, progressing to b. Inability to swallow saliva with frequent spitting/drooling, progressing to c. Airway collapse requiring surgical airway management 2. Unilateral swollen tonsil 3. Deviation of uvula to unaffected side 4. Tonsillar, cervical and submandibular lymphadenopathy 5. Snoring sounds from upper airway obstruction 6. Orthostatic hypotension B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Evaluate and monitor airway b. Be prepared for intubation and crichothyroidotomy for airway management c. If patient has orthostatic hypotension, initiate IV access, give 1 liter fluid bolus and then titrate until orthostasis is corrected 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Administer antimicrobial therapy i. If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin (Pen Vee K®), initiate IV ampicillin-sublactam (Unasyn®) or IV clindamycin (Cleocin®). ii. PO antibiotics are not as effective, but may be used if IV antibiotics are not available. Consider ampicillin (Omnipen®) or clindamycin (Cleocin®)PO b. Administer analgesics for relief of pain, acetaminophen or ibuprofen (Motrin®) as necessary. If unable

IV antibiotics are not available. Consider ampicillin (Omnipen®) or clindamycin (Cleocin®)PO b. Administer analgesics for relief of pain, acetaminophen or ibuprofen (Motrin®) as necessary. If unable to tolerate PO pain medicine, consider low dose IV narcotic such as morphine c. Ensure adequate fluid intake IV or PO until evacuation- approximately 3 L per day or enough to urinate every two hours while awake XVIII. LARYNGITIS- Inflammation of the larynx due to a virus or overuse. May also be caused by gastroesophageal reflux or chronic bronchitis. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Change of voice 2. Hoarseness and raw sensation of the pharynx 3. Constant urge to clear throat may occur B. Treatment 1. Advise patient to rest voice (use a note pad and pencil to communicate; whispering will also stress the voice) 2. Advise patient to stop smoking 3. Administer steam inhalations 4. Administer analgesics, as needed, for relief of pain, acetaminophen (Tylenol®) 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Administer antimicrobial therapy if patient meets Centor criteria, see “Strep Throat” protocols

71 Chapter 5: Dental Protocols I. ACUTE NECROTIZING ULCERATIVE GINGIVITIS (“ANUG”)- Infectious disease of the marginal gingival and attachment apparatus of the tooth. Synonyms include “trench mouth” and “Vincent’s Infection.” A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Factors associated with ANUG are: smoking, stress and poor oral hygiene 2. Acute pain 3. Halitosis 4. Interproximal necrosis resulting in characteristic, “punched out” appearance to the papillae B. Treatment 1. Administer analgesics PO as necessary for pain. Consider acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or ibuprofen (Motrin®) 2. Manually remove as much plaque as possible with a toothbrush 3. Benzocaine 20% (Hurricane® Topical Anesthetic gel) may be placed on the gingiva before brushing (tissue will be very sensitive with or without the gel) 4. Counsel the patient on a. The need for good nutrition, oral hygiene and plenty of rest b. The need to stop smoking c. The use of dental floss and thorough brushing several times a day is a must! 5. Have patient swish with 1 capful of Peridex® for 30 seconds and expectorate, twice daily for 7 days 6. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider antibiotic therapy such as Penicillin (Pen VK®) if

swish with 1 capful of Peridex® for 30 seconds and expectorate, twice daily for 7 days 6. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider antibiotic therapy such as Penicillin (Pen VK®) if symptoms do not improve b. Provide further analgesia as necessary; consider acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) II. ALVEOLAR OSTEITIS (“DRY SOCKET”)- Post-extraction alveolitis is characterized by pain emanating from the mandible or maxilla. It occurs when the clot that had formed in the removed tooth’s socket becomes dislodged. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. More common in smokers and oral contraceptive users 2. Associated with post-extraction of the 3rd molars 3. Pain begins 2-3 days after tooth extraction and radiates to the ear B. Treatment 1. Use sterile H2O or saline to gently irrigate the socket and remove necrotic debris 2. Apply a palliative medication: Gel Foam® slightly moistened with Eugenol® placed in the socket for 24 hours (should relieve the intense ache within 30 - 40 minutes) 3. Continue changing dressing every 24 hours for 3 days, gently irrigating extraction site with sterile saline before replacing dressing
es) 3. Continue changing dressing every 24 hours for 3 days, gently irrigating extraction site with sterile saline before replacing dressing

72 4. Administer analgesics, PO as necessary for pain. Consider acetaminophen (Tylenol®), ibuprofen (Motrin®) or acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) 5. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR– MEDICATION a. Antibiotics are not generally warranted, but each case should be discussed with a dental preceptor i. Penicillin (Pen-Vee K®) if patient is not penicillin allergic ii. Erythromycin (E-Mycin®) if patient is penicillin is allergic b. Notify dental clinic of any persistent symptoms and arrange for patient to be seen as soon as possible III. APHTHOUS ULCER- Recurrent, round, painful lesions of the oral mucosa with an unknown etiology. Other causes of oral ulcers must be considered and ruled out including herpes, ANUG, candidiasis, hand-foot-mouth disease, gonorrhea and syphilis. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of recurrence since childhood 2. Stress 3. History of similar lesions 1-2 times per year 4. Pain or burning for 1-2 days preceding ulcers 5. Pain disproportional to the size of the lesion 6. Lesions are non-grouped, well defined, shallow, and round with a necrotic center over an erythematous base B. Treatment 1. Administer topical anesthetic, such as viscous lidocaine, 1 tablespoon, 4

6. Lesions are non-grouped, well defined, shallow, and round with a necrotic center over an erythematous base B. Treatment 1. Administer topical anesthetic, such as viscous lidocaine, 1 tablespoon, 4 times a day (before meals and at bedtime) or apply topical anesthetic gel Benzocaine 20% (Hurricane Gel®) to provide short-term relief and to facilitate eating if patient has multiple ulcers. Have patient swish medication in mouth for one to two minutes and expectorate 2. Apply a protective dental paste (Orabase®) to individual ulcers 4 times a day (after meals and at bedtime) to prevent irritation by the teeth and oral fluids 3. Have patient remain on soft bland diet until resolution 4. Notify dental preceptor if condition worsens or does not resolve in 7-10 days IV. TRAUMATIC ULCER- Ulcerations associated with chemical burns, cheek or tongue biting secondary to mastication, abrasions from fractured teeth or a thermal burn from hot food. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Round Painful lesion in close proximity to a fractured tooth, near the biting plane on buccal mucosa, near the palate as a result of hot food entry into the oral cavity, or at the placement site for improper use of aspirin or

ose proximity to a fractured tooth, near the biting plane on buccal mucosa, near the palate as a result of hot food entry into the oral cavity, or at the placement site for improper use of aspirin or other analgesics as patient’s self-treatment for a tooth ache B. Treatment 1. Administer topical anesthetic, such as viscous lidocaine, 1 tablespoon, 4 times a day (before meals and at bedtime) or apply topical anesthetic gel Benzocaine 20% (Hurricane Gel®) to provide short-term relief and to facilitate eating if patient has multiple ulcers. Have patient swish medication in mouth for one to two minutes and expectorate 2. Apply a protective dental paste (Orabase®) to individual ulcers 4 times a day (after meals and at bedtime) to prevent irritation by the teeth and oral fluids

73 3. Have Patient remain on soft bland diet until resolution 4. Notify dental preceptor if condition worsens or does not resolve in 7-10 days V. AVULSED TOOTH- Missing tooth following trauma to the face. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Missing tooth 2. Gum trauma B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Examine socket area and gums for any obvious bone fragment or deformity (remove any deformity) b. Place tooth in Save A Tooth® solution (Hank’s Balanced Salt Solution®) for 20 min c. Reimplant tooth in socket site; if unable to reimplant leave it in solution d. Place a small amount of wax on the avulsed tooth and adjacent teeth to help stabilize tooth e. Administer analgesics- PO for pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®), acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain 1. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION AND MEDICATION a. If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin, administer penicillin (Pen-VK®) b. If patient IS allergic to penicillin, administer clindamycin (Cleocin®) c. Administer tetanus toxoid 0.5 ml or immunoglobulin as indicated d. VI. BAROTRAUMA, DENTAL- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, Barotrauma, Dental” VII. DENTAL CARIES

inister clindamycin (Cleocin®) c. Administer tetanus toxoid 0.5 ml or immunoglobulin as indicated d. VI. BAROTRAUMA, DENTAL- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, Barotrauma, Dental” VII. DENTAL CARIES- Tooth decay known as “cavities.” A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Tender, painful teeth 2. Initially produces no symptoms 3. Pain with exposure to hot, cold or sweet foods/beverages 4. May have pain with chewing or percussion (tooth rake/tooth sleuth) B. Treatment 1. Remove any gross debris if visible with saline irrigation or floss 2. Administer analgesics--PO for pain as required. Consider acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin®), acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®). acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain 3. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. If symptoms are relieved, make a routine scheduled appointment b. If symptoms are not relieved with analgesics, notify dental preceptor for evacuation CLINICAL NOTE: Do not scrape tooth. If Save A Tooth® solution is not available, other storage solution options include the following (in order of preference): milk, saline, saliva, or sterile water.
ble, other storage solution options include the following (in order of preference): milk, saline, saliva, or sterile water.

74 c. If evacuation is delayed, place a temporary filling using IRM® or other restorative material VIII. DISPLACED TOOTH- Misaligned tooth following trauma to the face. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Visible malalignment of the tooth 2. Trauma to the gums and mouth 3. External facial trauma and edema often visible B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Attempt to reposition tooth in socket with finger pressure and stabilize with wax if tooth is very loose b. If unable to move tooth into original position, place gauze between posterior teeth as a jaw rest c. Administer analgesics--PO for pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain 2. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION IX. FRACTURED TOOTH OR CROWN- A fracture of a tooth resulting from blunt-force trauma or overuse. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Visible fracture or missing portion of a tooth 2. Pain, temperature sensitivity 3. May be asymptomatic B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Mix glass ionomer restorative material such as Fuji 9®, IRM® or Vitrabond® b. Cover exposed area with restorative material c. Smooth surfaces of material applied d. Have

atment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Mix glass ionomer restorative material such as Fuji 9®, IRM® or Vitrabond® b. Cover exposed area with restorative material c. Smooth surfaces of material applied d. Have patient bite down gently to check occlusion of teeth e. Remove any excess material f. Administer analgesics--PO for pain as required. Consider acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin®), acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain g. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION CLINICAL NOTE: If symptomatic after treatment, notify dental clinic and arrange for patient to be seen as soon as possible (ASAP). If asymptomatic after treatment, make a routine scheduled appointment. CLINICAL NOTE: If tooth is asymptomatic, immediate treatment is not required. Schedule a routine appointment.

75 X. GINGIVITIS- Inflammation of the gingiva usually caused by poor oral hygiene. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Non-painful inflammation of the marginal gingiva 2. Swelling and easy bleeding B. Treatment 1. Advise patient to maintain good oral hygiene 2. Have patient swish with 1 capful of Peridex® for 30 sec and expectorate, twice daily for 7 days 3. Administer analgesics--PO for pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); may use acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain 4. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Arrange for patient to make a routine scheduled appointment b. Recheck patient in 1 week to make sure symptoms are improving XI. ODONTOGENIC SPACE INFECTION- Abscesses or cellulitis involving the buccal or vestibular spaces progressing to bilateral submandibular and sublingual space infections known as Ludwig’s Angina. This is an instance in which a toothache may develop into a life- threatening condition. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Firm or fluctuant, painful soft tissue swelling associated with necrotic tooth 2. Systemic signs of an infection include lymphadenopathy, fever and malaise 3. Trismus (can’t open jaw all th

ymptoms 1. Firm or fluctuant, painful soft tissue swelling associated with necrotic tooth 2. Systemic signs of an infection include lymphadenopathy, fever and malaise 3. Trismus (can’t open jaw all the way) B. Treatment 1. Monitor patient’s airway and vital signs 2. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Abscesses i. With the concurrence of the Dental Preceptor: incise and drain the abscess at the point of fluctuance either intra- or extra- orally ii. Administer analgesics--PO for pain as required. Consider acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin®), acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain b. Cellulitis i. Discuss case with Dental Preceptor a) If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin, administer penicillin (Pen Vee K®) b) If patient IS allergic to penicillin, administer clindamycin (Cleocin®) c. Definitive treatment involves draining the abscess and may involve a root canal or extraction XII. PERIAPICAL ABSCESS- Inflammatory changes of the apex of the tooth root where the pulp canal contents connect with the periodontium. Usually occurs after the death of the dental pulp due to bacterial infection, traumatic ischemi

atory changes of the apex of the tooth root where the pulp canal contents connect with the periodontium. Usually occurs after the death of the dental pulp due to bacterial infection, traumatic ischemia or pulpal necrosis. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sensitivity to percussion (tooth rake/tooth sleuth)

76 2. Pain may be acute, spontaneous, continuous or intermittent and often positional 3. Affected teeth usually unresponsive to cold, but may respond to heat 4. May develop a granuloma or cyst at the buccal side of the tooth apex 5. May develop a sinus tract with purulent discharge (gum boil) B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. If obvious superficial fluctuant swelling is present, induce drainage with #11 blade or explorer b. Administer analgesics--PO for pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) for severe pain 2. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Definitive treatment may involve a root canal or extraction b. If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin, administer penicillin (Pen Vee K®) c. If patient IS allergic to penicillin, administer clindamycin (Cleocin®) XIII. PERICORNITIS- Inflammation or infection of tissues covering or adjacent to non-erupted or partially erupted teeth—usually the 3rd molars. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Trauma to the covering tissues of the erupting tooth caused by the opposing tooth. Usually, it’s an upper third molar (upper wisdom tooth) opposing an erupting

he 3rd molars. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Trauma to the covering tissues of the erupting tooth caused by the opposing tooth. Usually, it’s an upper third molar (upper wisdom tooth) opposing an erupting mandibular third molar (lower wisdom tooth) 2. Foreign body sensation under the covering tissues (operculum) 3. May develop trismus in masticatory muscles (unable to open mouth wide) B. Treatment 1. If possible, remove obvious plaque buildup by irrigation of the area using large amounts of saline and an irrigation syringe (be gentle because this area will be very tender) 2. Stress to the patient the need for good oral hygiene to improve the condition of the gum in spite of the pain or bleeding 3. Have patient swish with 1 capful of Peridex® for 30 sec and expectorate, BID x 7 days 4. Dispense an irrigation syringe to patient and show them how to irrigate area four times a day with saline solution 5. Administer analgesics, PO for pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol # 3®), for severe pain 6. If available, consider giving the patient a maxillary mouthguard to prevent further irritation to the lower tooth

or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol # 3®), for severe pain 6. If available, consider giving the patient a maxillary mouthguard to prevent further irritation to the lower tooth/gum tissue 7. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION 8. If relief is not evident in 4 to 8 hours, administer appropriate antimicrobial therapy and notify dental clinic of any persistent symptoms a. If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin, administer phenoxymethyl penicillin (Pen-Vee K®) b. If patient IS allergic to penicillin, administer clindamycin (Cleocin®)

77 9. If relief is not evident in 4 to 8 hours, administer appropriate antimicrobial therapy and notify dental clinic of any persistent symptoms XIV. PERIODONTAL ABSCESS- An abscess involving the infection of connective tissue surrounding teeth. Foreign body, often calculus or food debris forms a nidus for development of a localized infection with a periodontal pocket 2. Sensitivity to palpation of the tooth with tooth mobility is diagnostic B. Treatment 1. If possible, remove obvious plaque buildup by irrigation of the area using large amounts of saline and an irrigation syringe. Be gentle because this area will be very tender 2. Stress to the patient the need for good oral hygiene to improve the condition of the gum in spite of the pain or bleeding 3. Have patient swish with 1 capful of Peridex® for 30 sec and expectorate, BID x 7 days 4. Administer analgesics, PO for pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®); acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3® ), for severe pain 5. CONTA

CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR-EVACUATION a. If patient is NOT allergic to penicillin, administer penicillin (PenVee K®) b. If patient IS allergic to penicillin, administer Clindamycin (Cleocin®) XV. REVERSIBLE PULPITIS- Pulpalgia and pulpitis describe pain and inflammatory changes possible in an affected tooth. Pulpitis can be described as reversible or irreversible. Diagnosis is based on symptoms. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Hypersensitive reaction precipitated by thermal stimuli, usually cold; pain is acute and lasts less than 5 seconds after application of stimulus 2. No sensitivity to palpation/percussion (tooth rake/tooth sleuth) 3. History of defective filling or recent dental work B. Treatment 1. Administer analgesics, PO for mild or moderate pain as required. Consider aspirin, acetaminophen (Tylenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®), acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol#3®), for severe pain 2. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR XVI. IRREVERSIBLE PULPITIS- Pulpalgia and pulpitis describe pain and inflammatory

lenol®), or ibuprofen (Motrin®), acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol#3®), for severe pain 2. CONTACT DENTAL PRECEPTOR XVI. IRREVERSIBLE PULPITIS- Pulpalgia and pulpitis describe pain and inflammatory changes possible in an affected tooth. Pulpitis can be described as reversible or irreversible. Diagnosis is based on symptoms. A. Signs and Symptoms CLINICAL NOTE: if symptoms do not improve in 24 hours, notify dental clinic and arrange for patient to make a routine scheduled appointment.

78 1. May or may not be symptomatic 2. Spontaneous pain and prolonged painful response to thermal stimuli are diagnostic 3. Causes include caries, defective restorations, cracked teeth, or barodentalgia 4. May be sensitive to biting pressure B. Treatment 1. Administer analgesics, PO for mild or moderate pain as required.
