79 Chapter 6: Neurologic Protocols I. ALCOHOL INTOXICATION- Approximately 10% of American adults abuse alcohol- it is implicated as the leading factor in traffic accidents, domestic violence, homicide, and suicide nationwide. Military personnel frequently avoid medical attention for alcohol- related trauma due to fear of career repercussions. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Symptoms based on blood alcohol content percentage: a. 0.01-0.10%: euphoria, mild deficits in coordination, attention, and cognition b. 0.11-0.20%: deficits in coordination, decreased attention, slurred speech, uncoordinated walk, impaired judgment, mood volatility c. 0.21-0.30%: incoherence, nausea, vomiting d. >0.30%: stupor, loss of consciousness, respiratory depression, coma, death 2. At any of these levels, patient may be malodorous and unkempt B. Treatment 1. Monitor hydration status, encourage fluid intake 2. “Hangovers” typically caused by acute dehydration following diuresis from alcohol 3. Do not release patient until clinical symptoms of intoxication have passed 4. Do not release patient alone- release into care of competent adult 5. In patients with alcohol intoxication and nausea/vomiting, respiratory depr

until clinical symptoms of intoxication have passed 4. Do not release patient alone- release into care of competent adult 5. In patients with alcohol intoxication and nausea/vomiting, respiratory depression and loss of airway is a serious concern a. Initiate IV rehydration with Ringer’s Lactate and reassess vitals every 15 minutes until stabilized 6. For patients with decreased level of consciousness, follow above measures AND administer thiamine IV 7. May place an NG tube if patient is nauseous/vomiting, but gastric lavage and/or charcoal are generally not indicated due to rapid rate of alcohol absorption II. ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (DELERIUM TREMENS)- The “DT’s”. Abrupt cessation of alcohol use in someone who abuses alcohol. Symptoms begin 6-12 hours after the last drink. High risk categories include individuals who consume alcohol daily and “binge drinkers” that have consumed more than five drinks per sitting with three or more binges over the past two weeks. A. Signs and Symptoms CLINICAL NOTE: Alcohol breakdown is linear, meaning only a certain amount can be broken down per hour regardless of how much is ingested. It is impossible to determine clinically if the patient is

ms CLINICAL NOTE: Alcohol breakdown is linear, meaning only a certain amount can be broken down per hour regardless of how much is ingested. It is impossible to determine clinically if the patient is still absorbing ingested alcohol or if the patient has stopped absorbing and is now breaking the alcohol down. Depending on the severity of the intoxication or the reported amount ingested, be prepared for patient to worsen before getting better. Plan to observe all patients with acute intoxication brought to medical attention for several hours. Perform vitals and brief history and physical hourly until released.

80 1. Based on time since last drink and follow a predictable pattern a. Stage 1: 6-24 hours after last drink i. Agitation, anxiety, insomnia, tremors, diaphoresis, palpitations, mild hypertension, tachycardia less than 120 bpm, nausea ii. Mostly coherent at this stage iii. Stage 1 may resolve within 72 hours or proceed into severe withdrawal b. Stage 2: 24-72 hours after last drink i. Similar symptoms as stage 1 but more severe ii. Marked agitation, nausea/vomiting/ diarrhea, severe tremors, tachycardia, escalating hypertension, marked diaphoresis iii. May have seizures at this stage c. Stage 3- “Delirium Tremens”: 72-96 hours after last drink i. Hyperactivity, fever, severe tachycardia and hypertension ii. Agitation, drenching sweats, hallucinations, disorientation iii. May have seizures at this stage 2. Patients can progress rapidly or unpredictably through the stages B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Patient likely to be combative- protect self first 3. Avoid being alone with patient 4. Monitor vitals and neurologic status every 30 minutes 5. Consider diazepam (Valium®) or lorazepam (Ativan®) for severe agitation or seizures III. SPINAL CORD T

rst 3. Avoid being alone with patient 4. Monitor vitals and neurologic status every 30 minutes 5. Consider diazepam (Valium®) or lorazepam (Ativan®) for severe agitation or seizures III. SPINAL CORD TRAUMA- Trauma that causes direct or indirect pressure or damage to the spinal cord. Common etiologies include motor vehicle accidents, falls, gunshot wounds, and high-velocity sporting accidents (bull riding, boating accidents, etc). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Transient or permanent sensation/motor function loss below level of trauma 2. Trauma to cervical spine causes quadriplegia and full-body flaccid paralysis 3. Priapism and urinary retention are common with lumbar spine injuries 4. Pain at the vertebrae or nerve roots associated with the trauma 5. Cervical spine injuries cause quadriplegia and can be fatal a. The diaphragm is innervated by cervical nerves at the C3-C5 spinal level b. Trauma at or above this area will lead to difficulty breathing in addition to flaccid paralysis of the body B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Move patient as little as possible 3. Logroll technique, C-Spine precautions, backboard 4. Do not backboard if immobilization ca

dy B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Move patient as little as possible 3. Logroll technique, C-Spine precautions, backboard 4. Do not backboard if immobilization causes deterioration in sensation or motor function 5. See “trauma” in emergency treatment protocols for further information

81 IV. CEREBROVASCULAR ACCIDENT- See “Emergency Medicine Protocols- Stroke” and follow current ACLS guidelines V. GULLAIN-BARRÉ SYNDROME- Progressive ascending paralysis that follows routine infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Patient initially complains of weakness of the lower extremities which may be accompanied by back or leg pain 2. May report sensory abnormalities in hands and feet but minimal or no sensory abnormalities found on exam 3. Deep tendon reflexes are absent 4. Symptoms progress over next 2-4 weeks with ascending paralysis, respiratory depression, and potentially death from asphyxiation 5. Mental status is usually preserved but patient is unable to move or respond (“Locked in”) B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Transport any patient with ascending weakness 3. Be prepared for advanced airway management and mechanical ventilation if evacuation is delayed VI. HEADACHE, MIGRAINE- Benign headache that is often confused with acute neurologic deficiency due to transient sensory or motor changes. Patients typically try to stay as still as possible. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Chronic, episodic headache 2. Symptoms may last 4-72 hours 3. Char

c deficiency due to transient sensory or motor changes. Patients typically try to stay as still as possible. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Chronic, episodic headache 2. Symptoms may last 4-72 hours 3. Character usually throbbing or pulsatile moderate to severe pain 4. Typically improves or resolves with sleep 5. Pain may be unilateral or bilateral 6. Associated symptoms include nausea/vomiting, photophobia, and phonophobia 7. Patient may have difficulty concentrating during attacks 8. Headache may be proceeded by one or more prodromal symptoms: a. Visual symptoms- bright zigzags, scotomata, scintillating lights b. Paresthesias, numbness, or tingling c. Speech disturbances CLINICAL NOTE: Patients with significant mechanism for spinal cord injury who do not present with paralysis MUST BE OBSERVED for a minimum of six hours due to the possibility of swelling causing compression of the cord. CLINICAL NOTE: Frequent opioid use can contribute to development of chronic daily headache and may interfere with other preventive therapies. Regular users of NSAIDs or acetaminophen are also likely to experience rebound headache.
fere with other preventive therapies. Regular users of NSAIDs or acetaminophen are also likely to experience rebound headache.

82 B. Treatment 1. Place patient in dark, quiet room 2. Manage mild to moderate pain with NSAIDS or acetaminophen (Tylenol®), but not if they have had either of these in the past 24 hours 3. For incapacitating pain a. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION i. Consider administration of zolmitriptan (Zomig®) or sumitriptan (Imitrex®) ii. Alternative therapies include acetaminophen + aspirin + caffeine (Excedrin®) iii. For severe pain, consider triple therapy of 1L IV NS, metoclopramide (Phenergan®) IV/IM, and either zolmitriptan (Zomig®) or sumitriptan (Imitrex®) (This therapy works best for patients with migraines and nausea, but is very effective for most migraines) C. Prevention 1. Reduce stress, regulate sleep cycle, eat regular meals, hydrate enough to urinate every two hours 2. Discontinue caffeine, herbs, supplements, tobacco 3. Avoid triggers if possible (consider emotional stress, mensturation/hormonal irregularities, fasting or inadequate oral intake, nitrates/food additives, weather changes, sleep disturbances, aspartame, and drinking wine are all common triggers) 4. Daily preventive medication indicated if headaches are frequent, long-lasting, or if causing significant

weather changes, sleep disturbances, aspartame, and drinking wine are all common triggers) 4. Daily preventive medication indicated if headaches are frequent, long-lasting, or if causing significant disability 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. New repetitive headaches require further workup b. Physician may consider verapamil (Verelan®), nifedipine (Procardia®), amyltriptiline (Elavil®) or antidepressive therapy VII. HEADACHE, CLUSTER- Benign, severe, incapacitating headache that occurs in recurring patterns. Patients typically very agitated with behavior such as pacing, yelling, or thrashing. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain typically unilateral in temporal-orbital area 2. Attacks often usually occur at the same time each day or in predictable pattern occurring in groups or “clusters” 3. Pain may wake patient from sleep 4. Pain is excruciating, but peaks within minutes and usually resolves within an hour 5. Patients usually restless and agitated 6. May experience nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, lacrimation, facial flushing and Horner’s syndrome (ptosis, miosis, enophtalmos, and anhidrosis) on the ipsilateral (same) side 7. Attacks have a frequency from one every other day

stion, rhinorrhea, lacrimation, facial flushing and Horner’s syndrome (ptosis, miosis, enophtalmos, and anhidrosis) on the ipsilateral (same) side 7. Attacks have a frequency from one every other day to eight per day B. Treatment 1. Inhalation of 100% oxygen, 7 L per min for 15 min 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EMERGENCY EVACUATION

83 VIII. HEADACHE, TENSION- Benign headache associated with emotional stress and muscular fatigue. Symptoms usually recur until underlying physical and emotional stressors are addressed. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Mild, generalized pain generally without the incapacity, nausea, or photophobia seen with migraine 2. Pain typically bilateral, described as “vice-like”, non-throbbing 3. Often precipitated by mental stress, or physical stress of sitting still for long periods 4. Normal physical exam or muscle tenderness in the head, neck, or shoulders B. Treatment 1. Manage pain with ibuprofen (Motrin®), naproxen (Aleve®), acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or combination analgesics such as acetaminophen, aspirin and caffeine (Excederin®) but avoid taking any of these medications regularly for headache since rebound headache may result 2. Muscle relaxants are not recommended C. Prevention 1. Identify and treat potential triggers a. Sleep irregularities/disorders b. Poor posture c. Emotional stressors d. TMJ dysfunction e. Neck pain f. Eyestrain 2. Increase exercise, regulate sleep schedule, eat healthy diet, hydrate enough to urinate every two hours, avoid herbs, supplements, tobacco, alcohol IX. HE

TMJ dysfunction e. Neck pain f. Eyestrain 2. Increase exercise, regulate sleep schedule, eat healthy diet, hydrate enough to urinate every two hours, avoid herbs, supplements, tobacco, alcohol IX. HEAD INJURIES- See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Head Trauma”. X. HERNIATED NUCLEUS PULPOSIS (HNP) WITH RADICULOPATHY- “Herniated disc” or “slipped disc”. A traumatic or degenerative rupture of one of the cushioning discs between the vertebrae; may be asymptomatic or present clinically due to compression of nearby nerve roots. A. Signs and Symptoms (if nerve roots have been compressed) 1. Unilateral sciatica, which is pain or burning sensation down posterior or lateral leg past the knee and worse with specific motions 2. Straight leg test may cause sciatica but should not be bilateral 3. Palpation of the vertebrae and discs causes pain (in musculoskeletal low back pain, palpation on the spinous processes should not be tender) 4. Extension of the spine causes pain and may cause unilateral or bilateral weakness if herniation is severe (Musculoskeletal low back pain typically has lateral back pain and spasms with flexion of spine, but extension of spine is usually not as painful) B. Treatm

bilateral weakness if herniation is severe (Musculoskeletal low back pain typically has lateral back pain and spasms with flexion of spine, but extension of spine is usually not as painful) B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Do not allow patient to lift, push, or pull more than 5 lbs 3. May sit or lay in position of comfort

84 XI. ENCEPHALITIS- Swelling of the brain itself; often associated with viral infections. Differentiated from meningitis because in meningitis, brain functionality is preserved, whereas in encephalitis, thinking, motor, sensory, and speech can be impaired. These viruses have the potential to be used as biological weapons. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Altered mental status 2. Motor and sensory deficits 3. Altered behavior 4. Personality changes 5. Speech disorders 6. Movement disorders 7. Seizures 8. Often caused by viruses such as West Nile or rabies, or following viral infection such as rubella, mumps, measles, herpes, Epstein-Barr, or influenza B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Requires lumbar puncture for further evaluation 3. Give Acyclovir (Zovirax®) IV. Do not delay antiviral treatment if encephalitis is suspected, even without lumbar puncture confirmation 4. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for fever/pain XII. MENINGITIS- Swelling of the fibrous layers (meninges) that protect the brain and spinal cord. Often caused by coexisting bacterial or viral infections. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Unlike encephalitis, patients with meningitis do not generally have

eninges) that protect the brain and spinal cord. Often caused by coexisting bacterial or viral infections. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Unlike encephalitis, patients with meningitis do not generally have focal neurologic findings or altered behavior on exam until very late in course, although they may have decreased level of consciousness earlier 2. Patients may have fever and/or complain of headache or neck stiffness a. Bruzinski’s sign- passive flexion of the neck causes flexion at the hips in the supine patient b. Kernig’s sign- Flex hip to 90° in the supine patient and attempt to extend the knee. Kernig’s sign is when patient is reluctant to allow knee extension CLINICAL NOTE: If severe herniated disc, patient may have cauda equina syndrome, which is bowel, bladder, or penile dysfunction and may be associated with numbness in the genitals or inner thighs (ie “saddle anesthesia”). These patients may also have bilateral sciatica or weakness of the lower extremities. Any of these symptoms predict potential for paralysis and therefore require emergency evacuation. Put patient on strict bed rest in position of greatest comfort. CLINICAL NOTE: Rabies can cause encephalitis. If you suspec

predict potential for paralysis and therefore require emergency evacuation. Put patient on strict bed rest in position of greatest comfort. CLINICAL NOTE: Rabies can cause encephalitis. If you suspect rabies, start treatment for rabies with RIG and HDCV. Discuss with preceptor prior to initiating therapy. See “Rabies” in “Febrile Illnesses Protocols, Rabies”.

85 3. Papilledema on fundoscopic exam (blurring of cup and disc margins) with photophobia 4. Petichiae/purpura (if bacterial) B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Requires lumbar puncture for further evaluation 3. IV access, correct fluid and electrolyte disturbances 4. Do not delay initiation of antibiotics if meningitis is possible a. Consider IV vancomycin (Vancocin®) plus IV ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) b. Consider IV dexamethasone (Decadron®) as adjunctive therapy c. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for fever/pain d. Monitor and record vital signs every 30 minutes e. Monitor and maintain an adequate airway XIII. SEIZURES- Brain electrical activity disorder in which whole body spasms and convulses followed by a “postictal state” in which memory and cognition are impaired. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Seizure typically begins as abrupt loss of consciousness, possibly with scream or shriek 2. Full body goes rigid and patient may become cyanotic for approximately a minute (“tonic phase”) 3. This is followed by the violent “clonic” phase with bilateral muscle jerking lasting 1-2 minutes. Patient may bite tongue, froth at mouth, or become incontinent during this phase

te (“tonic phase”) 3. This is followed by the violent “clonic” phase with bilateral muscle jerking lasting 1-2 minutes. Patient may bite tongue, froth at mouth, or become incontinent during this phase 4. Seizure is followed by a postictal state lasting up to several hours during which patient has altered level of consciousness, deep breathing, and confusion 5. As patient becomes more alert they generally complain of headache 6. Etiologies in healthy active duty population include acute intoxications, poisonings, alcohol intoxication or withdrawal, head trauma, infections, hypoxia, and hypoglycemia 7. Primary seizures (ie epilepsy) usually triggered by environmental or physiological stressor such as flashing lights, strong emotion, intense exercise, or loud music ACTION ALERT: Meningitis is a lethal and very contagious condition. Contact preceptor immediately to begin prioritizing evacuation. Treat all meningitis as bacterial until proven otherwise. ACTION ALERT: Close contacts should receive Rifampin 600 mg PO BID x 4 doses for meningococcal prophylaxis. Consult with preceptor.
eceive Rifampin 600 mg PO BID x 4 doses for meningococcal prophylaxis. Consult with preceptor.

86 B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish and maintain an adequate airway using an airway adjunct (OP/NP) unless it induces gagging, vomiting or trismus is present b. Protect the patient from injury c. During seizure do not try to restrain patient d. Clear area so that patient’s violent jerkings cause as little trauma as possible e. Do not attempt to force patient’s mouth open f. May administer lorazepam (Ativan®) IV (preferred) or IM during seizure g. Place patient on side with head down and suction if necessary h. Administer 100% O2 i. Treat underlying cause, if known and examine for associated injuries and treat as required j. Be alert for possible for muscle damage from severe contractions leading to kidney damage (see “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Rhabdomyolysis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Establish IV access after initial episode 4. Treat any trauma caused by incident 5. Seizure precautions- bedrest with bathroom privileges, pad bedrails, place cot on floor XIV. SLEEP DEPRIVATION, “JET LAG”, AND SHIFT WORK- “Sleep deprivation” exists when quantity or quality of sleep is insufficient to support adequate alertness, functioning, and health. Shi

cot on floor XIV. SLEEP DEPRIVATION, “JET LAG”, AND SHIFT WORK- “Sleep deprivation” exists when quantity or quality of sleep is insufficient to support adequate alertness, functioning, and health. Shift work frequently disrupts circadian rhythms required to maintain adequate sleep cycles. “Jet lag” refers to disruption of sleep-wake cycles due to change of multiple time zones. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Daytime sleepiness, inattention/distraction, yawning 2. Mood disorders, outbursts, frustration, “short fuse”, stress 3. Increased accidents or mistakes 4. Headaches, neck pain, fatigue, malaise, gastrointestinal upset B. Treatment/Prevention 1. For chronic poor sleep a. Set same bedtime and wakeup routine at same time every day b. Use bed for sleep only- no TV, eating, reading, etc. CLINICAL NOTE: syncope can mimic seizures with unilateral or bilateral muscle contractions, but syncopal patients will be able to remember events preceding the syncopal episode and will not have a postictal phase. ACTION ALERT: Do not force objects between teeth as damage to tongue and/or teeth could result. DO NOT PUT YOUR FINGERS IN THE PATIENT'S MOUTH.
ase. ACTION ALERT: Do not force objects between teeth as damage to tongue and/or teeth could result. DO NOT PUT YOUR FINGERS IN THE PATIENT'S MOUTH.

87 c. Avoid bright light or screens (TV, computer) within 1 hour of bedtime d. Perform daily exercise with at least one period of sustained maximal exertion several hours prior to bedtime e. Avoid caffeine, especially after lunch f. Avoid alcohol g. Avoid nicotine, especially after lunch h. If worries are a contributing factor, journal with lower ambient light immediately prior to bedtime i. Avoid big meals right before bed j. If awake in the middle of the night, get out of bed and perform soothing activity in low light; avoid bright screens and eating 2. For jet lag a. Maximize time exposed to sunlight in new location b. Eat only at normal mealtimes (if normal routine is to eat supper at 6 pm, then eat at 6 pm at new location) c. Exercise (especially outdoors) will increase ability to fall asleep d. Avoid alcohol and stimulants to regulate sleep cycle if possible e. Melatonin (herbal preparation) has supporting data but is not FDA approved; advise patient of risks 3. For shift workers a. Avoid rotating shifts or picking up extra shifts b. Maintain same sleep schedule on days off c. For managers in charge of rotating the shift schedule i. Recommend keeping same schedule for longer

a. Avoid rotating shifts or picking up extra shifts b. Maintain same sleep schedule on days off c. For managers in charge of rotating the shift schedule i. Recommend keeping same schedule for longer intervals (3 months or more) ii. When rotating shifts, rotate each group later (afternoon shift moves to evening shift, etc) XV. TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY (“CONCUSSION”)- Caused by direct head trauma or acceleration-deceleration forces on the brain. Syndromes are complicated and a significant cause of morbidity in personnel in combat zones. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Constellation of symptoms that follow predictable progression a. Within minutes- initially patient is confused, befuddled, with amnesia of event with or without loss of consciousness b. Within minutes to hours- dizziness, vertigo, headache, nausea/vomiting, lack of awareness of surroundings c. Within hours to days- cognitive or mood disturbances, sensitivity to light or noise 2. Consider traumatic brain injury whenever any of the following are identified a. Slurred speech b. Delay in answering questions c. Vacant stare d. Emotions out of proportion to stimulus e. Confusion, incomprehensible statements f. Disorientation (not awa

ollowing are identified a. Slurred speech b. Delay in answering questions c. Vacant stare d. Emotions out of proportion to stimulus e. Confusion, incomprehensible statements f. Disorientation (not aware of person/place/time) g. Inability to focus attention h. Gross incoordination (unable to walk straight line)

88 i. Memory deficits or unable to remember 3 items at 5 minutes j. Any loss of consciousness, however brief B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Every hour assess vitals, neurologic status, speech, coordination, and ability to remember 3 objects at 5 minutes 3. Seizure precautions- bed rest with bathroom privileges, pad bedrails, place cot on floor 4. Don’t leave patient alone 5. Fall precautions 6. Consider Military Acute Concussion Evaluation

89 Chapter 7: Cardiopulmonary Protocols I. ASTHMA, ACUTE EXACERBATION/ASTHMA “ATTACK”- Acute reaction affecting the airway caused by exposure to allergens or irritants resulting in bronchoconstriction. Diagnosis is based on history, physical exam and pulmonary function testing or spirometry. Asthma is classified by degree of severity. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dyspnea, chest tightness, non-productive weak cough and wheezing 2. Respiratory distress 3. Possible cyanosis 4. Exam with prolonged expiratory phase and diminished air movement B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Check pulse oximetry and peak flows; normal peak flow is greater than 80% predicted value b. O2 via non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM c. Administer albuterol via metered-dose inhaler using a spacer or use albuterol nebulizer d. Continue O2 therapy until respiratory rate is <20, then slowly wean e. Patient must be able to maintain O2 saturation above 97% with walking and ADLs f. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR -CONSULTATION 2. If patient unable to maintain O2 saturation above 97% without supplemental oxygen, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION a. Consider administration of epinephrine IM (EpiPen®, Adrenalin

patient unable to maintain O2 saturation above 97% without supplemental oxygen, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION a. Consider administration of epinephrine IM (EpiPen®, Adrenalin®) b. Consider systemic corticosteroid such as prednisolone (Prelone®), prednisone (Deltasone®) or methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol®) II. ASTHMA, MILD INTERMITTENT- Intermittent affecting the airway caused by exposure to allergens or irritants resulting in bronchoconstriction. Diagnosis is based on history, physical exam and pulmonary function testing or spirometry. Asthma is classified by degree of severity. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Weak irritating cough especially at night and with exertion. May have reversible feelings of chest “tightness” or wheeze 2. Patient may have history of allergic rhinitis and show symptoms of allergies to include pale, boggy nasal mucosa, nasal polyps, itchy, injected, watery eyes, and clear rhinorrhea 3. Lung exam typically normal or have occasional wheeze at the end of expiration 4. Examiner may note frequent throat-clearing or weak, dry or minimally productive cough B. Treatment 1. Check Peak flows if available. Normal is greater than 80% predicted value
he end of expiration 4. Examiner may note frequent throat-clearing or weak, dry or minimally productive cough B. Treatment 1. Check Peak flows if available. Normal is greater than 80% predicted value

90 2. Prescribe an albuterol MDI with spacer as needed; give first dose in clinic and repeat peak flows. Patient should demonstrate some improvement 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider evacuation if symptoms worsen or no improvement seen b. If stable, patient will still eventually require further evaluation to include spirometry or pulmonary function testing when treatment facilities are available 4. Attempt to identify and control triggers 5. Treat coexisting allergic rhinitis aggressively with both intranasal corticosteroid and systemic antihistamine (see allergic rhinitis) III. ANTHRAX, INHALATIONAL- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Anthrax” IV. BRONCHITIS, ACUTE- An inflammation of the upper airway usually following an upper respiratory infection. Bronchitis is usually caused by a virus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Nonproductive cough for more than 5 days with or following URI symptoms 2. Chest pain with deep inspiration 3. No hypoxia 4. May have scattered rhonchi and wheezing; often has normal lung exam 5. Sputum is minimal compared to pneumonia, but may be clear, purulent or blood tinged B. Treatment 1. Check pulse oximetry and peak flow 2. Acetaminophen (Tyl

and wheezing; often has normal lung exam 5. Sputum is minimal compared to pneumonia, but may be clear, purulent or blood tinged B. Treatment 1. Check pulse oximetry and peak flow 2. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain and fever 3. Avoid antitussives such as benzonate (Tessalon Perles®) or Dextromethorphan combinations (Robitussin®, Triaminic®) unless required to aid sleep 4. If wheezing, consider albuterol MDI with spacer 5. If pneumonia is suspected, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR and consider antibiotic therapy only in patients with cough, fever, dyspnea and purulent sputum V. ACUTE RESPRATORY DISTRESS SYNDROME (ARDS) IN THE ADULT- Inflammatory disorder of the lung caused by sepsis, pneumonia, trauma or aspiration. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Develops within 24-48 hours after initial injury or illness 2. Dyspnea, cough and chest pain 3. Tachypnea and tachycardia 4. Mottled/cyanotic skin 5. Abnormal breath sounds that includes crackles, rhonchi and wheezes 6. Respiratory failure 7. Diffuse, bilateral infiltrates on chest x-ray B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Apply 100% oxygen via non-rebreather at 15 LPM b. Establish large bore IV access with normal saline at maintenance rate c. Do no

ilateral infiltrates on chest x-ray B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Apply 100% oxygen via non-rebreather at 15 LPM b. Establish large bore IV access with normal saline at maintenance rate c. Do not administer additional fluids unless directed to do so

91 d. Condition almost always requires endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation e. If needed, administer oxygen by positive pressure (AMBU bag or ventilator)- use the lowest level of PEEP or pressure that produces adequate oxygenation 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Attempt to identify and treat underlying cause b. Initiate broad-spectrum antibiotics via IV treatment if infection is known or suspected VI. BAROTRAUMA, PULMONARY- See “Envornmental Injury Protocols, Barotrauma, Pulmonary” VII. COSTOCHONDRITIS- See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Costochondritis” VIII. DIPHERHERIA- An acute pharyngeal infection caused by Corynebacterium diphtheria (gram positive bacilli), which produces exotoxin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation 2-4 days with prodromal period of 12-24 hours 2. Respiratory diphtheria characterized by visibly swollen neck, pharynx and tonsils 3. Hoarseness, stridor and dyspnea 4. Sore throat, dysphagia, low grade fever and tachycardia 5. Nausea, emesis, chills, headache 6. Dirty grey fibrous pharyngeal membrane that bleeds easily 7. May develop cardiac dysfunction, cranial nerve abnormalities, or renal failure 8. Cutaneous diphtheria char

ea, emesis, chills, headache 6. Dirty grey fibrous pharyngeal membrane that bleeds easily 7. May develop cardiac dysfunction, cranial nerve abnormalities, or renal failure 8. Cutaneous diphtheria characterized by chronic, non-healing sores or shallow ulcers covered with a dirty grey membrane B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. If evacuation will be delayed, consider initiating antibiotic therapy with IV penicillin or erythromycin IX. FLAIL CHEST- See “Emergency Protocols, Flail Chest” X. HUMAN IMMUNODEFICIENCY VIRUS (HIV) SEROCONVERSION- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) Seroconversion” XI. HYPERTENSION- chronically elevated systolic or diastolic blood pressure that increases lifetime risk of heart attack or stroke. A. Definitions 1. Hypertension- defined as systolic blood pressure greater than or equal to 140 or diastolic blood pressure greater than or equal to 90 on 3-6 blood pressure measurements not taken on the same day 2. Prehypertension- systolic blood pressure 120-139 or diastolic blood pressure 80-89 on 3-6 measurements not taken on the same day 3. Normal blood pressure- less than 120 systolic and less than

day 2. Prehypertension- systolic blood pressure 120-139 or diastolic blood pressure 80-89 on 3-6 measurements not taken on the same day 3. Normal blood pressure- less than 120 systolic and less than 80 diastolic

92 4. Isolated elevated blood pressure- any elevated blood pressure reading that does not meet the definition of “normal blood pressure” in a patient who has not undergone evaluation to determine if they meet the definitions of hypertension or prehypertension 5. Malignant hypertension- diastolic blood pressure greater than 100 accompanied by retinal hemorrhages, retinal exudates, papilledema, heart attack, or stroke (end organ damage) B. Signs and Symptoms 1. Usually asymptomatic and elevated blood pressure is found incidentally during routine exam for other purposes 2. Blood pressure may be elevated due to pain, abnormal hydration status, nervousness, or increased activity level; blood pressure readings taken under these circumstances should not be used towards diagnosis of hypertension 3. Gather history of possible end organ damage to include questions regarding vision, cardiac, and renal function 4. Perform thorough secondary history to include diet history, exercise history, medications/supplements, and family history C. Treatment 1. For cases of isolated elevated blood pressure, counsel the patient on any of the following, tailored to their diet and exercise history a. Reduce

tions/supplements, and family history C. Treatment 1. For cases of isolated elevated blood pressure, counsel the patient on any of the following, tailored to their diet and exercise history a. Reduce salt intake b. Increase aerobic exercise c. Stop all chronic NSAID and supplement use d. Lose weight if indicated. Even a 5-10 pound weight loss can make substantial improvements in a patient’s blood pressure e. Increase fruit and vegetable intake f. Limit alcohol intake g. Stop smoking and using tobacco products 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. If chronic hypertension or prehypertension are suspected, contact preceptor for consultation regarding non-emergent blood pressure evaluation b. If malignant hypertension is suspected and in all cases of blood pressure greater than 160 systolic or greater than 100 diastolic, consult preceptor for further direction XII. HYPERTROPHIC OBSTRUCTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY- A genetic disease that frequently goes undiagnosed even when joining the military. It often is discovered postmortem, with a typical scenario being an athlete that is performing well and suddenly dies on the field. It is caused by cardiac muscle fibers that are all going dif

litary. It often is discovered postmortem, with a typical scenario being an athlete that is performing well and suddenly dies on the field. It is caused by cardiac muscle fibers that are all going different directions; as they contract they fail to squeeze the ventricle closed. As a result, the heart increases in size, further narrowing the size of the ventricles until they are no longer effective. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Usually asymptomatic, but initial presentation may include exertional dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, palpitations, lightheadedness with standing, and fatigue

93 2. Physical exam in an asymptomatic patient may include 4th parasternal heart sound, heaves, harsh murmur after S1 at the apex and lower left sternal border that radiates to the axilla 3. Murmur gets worse if the sitting patient suddenly stands or if the patient Valsalvas; murmur gets better if patient squats B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway breathing, O2, monitor b. Keep patient in position of comfort 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XIII. PERICARDITIS- Acute inflammation of the pericardial sac, often following viral illness. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sharp, pleuritic chest pain that is relieved by sitting up and leaning forward 2. Pericardial friction rub- a superficial scratching or squeaking sound heard with the diaphragm of the stethoscope at the left sterna border occurring with each heartbeat 3. Recent or resolving viral respiratory or gastrointestinal illness 4. May have shortness of breath 5. Chest pain may improve with sitting up and leaning forward B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, monitor b. Keep patient in position of comfort 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Many patients may be managed wit

rward B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, monitor b. Keep patient in position of comfort 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Many patients may be managed with NSAIDs as an outpatient, but additional diagnostic testing is necessary b. Keep patient warm XIV. PERTUSSIS (“WHOOPING COUGH”)- A respiratory infection caused by Bordetella Pertussis (gram negative bacilli) common in children and adolescents characterized by a high- pitched, crowing inspiration. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 7-14 days 2. Begins with URI symptoms of sneezing, lacrimation and signs similar to a common cold 3. Cough begins with paroxysms of rapid cough and characteristic “whoop”ing sound 4. Cough is productive, with thick mucous that may cause vomiting or choking B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Initiate respiratory isolation (gloves, gown, mask, private room) b. Provide supportive care with O2 and suction as indicated c. Obtain IV access and administer IV fluids if indicated 2. CONTACT PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider azitrhomycin (Zithromax®) and erythromycin (E-mycin®) depending on the stage of the disease b. Fevers may get very high; im

V fluids if indicated 2. CONTACT PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider azitrhomycin (Zithromax®) and erythromycin (E-mycin®) depending on the stage of the disease b. Fevers may get very high; implement seizure precautions

94 XV. COMMUNITY ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA (CAP), BACTERIAL- Inflammation of the lungs caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae. X-rays show a solid white consolidation in the affected lobe with air-fluid levels. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Acute onset of malaise, cough, dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain 2. Cough initially dry, but becomes productive, thick, and sometimes painful 3. Dyspnea, especially with exertion 4. Fever, (100.4-105° F), tachypnea and tachycardia 5. Crackles, bronchial breath sounds, bronchophony, egophony and whispered pectoriloquy can be scattered throughout but are worse in affected lobe(s) 6. Scattered inspiratory and/or expiratory wheezes may be present 7. Tactile fremitus and dullness to percussion in the affected lobe(s) B. Treatment 1. Place patient on bed rest and keep warm 2. Administer oxygen: 2 to 4 liters per minute via nasal cannula or 8-10 liters per minute by mask 3. Continue to increase oxygen as needed to keep SpO2 >95 % or until respiratory rate is less than 20 per minute 4. If dyspneic, consider albuterol inhaler (Ventolin®) with spacer or albuterol nebulizer if available 5. Maintain adequate hydration for patient to urinate every 2 hours using IV fluids if

er minute 4. If dyspneic, consider albuterol inhaler (Ventolin®) with spacer or albuterol nebulizer if available 5. Maintain adequate hydration for patient to urinate every 2 hours using IV fluids if necessary due to sore throat 6. If fever >100.4, administer acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or ibuprofen (Motrin®) and observe for improvement 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider azithromycin (Zithromax®) or doxycycline (Vibramycin®) for typical presentation and levofloxacin (Levaquin®) if patient has been on antibiotics for any reason in the past 60 days b. Arrange for medical evacuation and hospital admission if any of the following conditions occur i. Patient is dyspneic>20 breaths per minute after albuterol treatment ii. Patient requires O2 due to sensation of air hunger or to maintain O2 saturation greater than 95% iii. Fever does not go lower than 100.4° F after first doses of acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and ibuprofen (Motrin®) iv. Patient is confused or excessively sleepy v. Patient has been on antibiotics within the past 60 days 8. If patient does not meet criteria for evacuation, patient should be placed on quarters and checked every 4 hours by nonmedical assist

eepy v. Patient has been on antibiotics within the past 60 days 8. If patient does not meet criteria for evacuation, patient should be placed on quarters and checked every 4 hours by nonmedical assistant 9. Mandatory medical followups every 24 hours until patient is afebrile for a full 24 hours XVI. PNEUMONIA, VIRAL- “Walking pneumonia.” Approximately 40% of adult cases of pneumonia are caused by viruses. Most commonly caused by influenza A/B, adenovirus, and parinfluenza. X-rays show “patchy infiltrates” or fluffy markings in one or more lobes. A. Signs and Symptoms

95 1. Gradual onset of signs and symptoms 2. Signs and symptoms are the similar to bacterial pneumonia, but the patient does not appear as ill 3. Cough producing white-yellow sputum 4. Bilateral, diffuse crackles, bronchial breath sounds, bronchophony, egophony and whispered pectoriloquy that do not localize to a specific lobe 5. Scattered wheezes may be present 6. Tactile fremitus and percussion findings, if present at all, do not localize to a specific lobe B. Treatment 1. Maintain adequate hydration for patient to urinate every 2 hours using IV fluids if necessary; consider normal saline unless otherwise directed 2. If fever >100.4, administer acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or ibuprofen (Motrin®) and observe for improvement 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION a. Consider antiviral therapy (if available) if viral pneumonia is suspected and it is within 48 hours of onset of symptoms b. Antibiotics are not indicated for suspected viral pneumonia; however if diagnosis is not clear between viral pneumonia and bacterial pneumonia and no lab or x-ray services are available, discuss possibility of antibiotics with preceptor 4. Arrange for medical evacuation and hos

sis is not clear between viral pneumonia and bacterial pneumonia and no lab or x-ray services are available, discuss possibility of antibiotics with preceptor 4. Arrange for medical evacuation and hospital admission if any of the following conditions occur a. Patient is dyspneic>20 breaths per minute after albuterol treatment b. Patient requires O2 due to sensation of air hunger or to maintain O2 saturation greater than 95% c. Fever does not go lower than 100.4° F after first doses of acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and ibuprofen (Motrin®) d. Patient is confused or excessively sleepy e. Patient has been on antibiotics within the past 60 days 5. If patient does not meet criteria for evacuation, patient should be placed on quarters and checked every 4 hours by nonmedical assistant 6. Mandatory medical followups every 24 hours until patient is afebrile for a full 24 hours XVII. PNEUMONIA, MYCOPLASMA (ATYPICAL PNEUMONIA)- Mycoplasma is the smallest free- living organism. It is a short bacillus that does not accept gram staining and so is invisible under the microscope. It infects approximately 1% of the population per year, predominantly in fall and winter months, and is very common in school

at does not accept gram staining and so is invisible under the microscope. It infects approximately 1% of the population per year, predominantly in fall and winter months, and is very common in schoolchildren, colleges, and military recruits. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Common in adults 20-40 years of age 2. Gradual onset of signs and symptoms that begin with malaise, sore throat and non-productive cough 3. Incessant paroxisms of cough with sputum becoming blood streaked and mucopurulant as the disease progresses 4. Unlike bacterial or viral pneumonia, mycoplasma does not usually cause pain with deep inspiration

96 5. Chest soreness from persistent coughing is common 6. No dullness to percussion and no increased tactile fremitus 7. Maculopapular rash occurs in 10-20% of patients B. Treatment 1. Continue to increase oxygen as needed to keep SpO2 >95 % or until respiratory rate is less than 20 per minute 4. If dyspneic, consider albuterol Metered Dose Inhaler with spacer or albuterol nebulizer if available 5. Maintain adequate hydration for patient to urinate every 2 hours using IV fluids if necessary due to sore throat 6. Consider azithromycin (Zithromax®) or doxycycline (Vibramycin®) for typical presentation and levofloxacin (Levaquin®) if patient has been on antibiotics for any reason in the past 60 days b. Arrange for medical evacuation and hospital admission if any of the following conditions occur i. Patient is dyspneic >20 breaths per minute after albuterol treatment ii. Patient requires O2

0 days b. Arrange for medical evacuation and hospital admission if any of the following conditions occur i. Patient is dyspneic >20 breaths per minute after albuterol treatment ii. Patient has been on antibiotics within the past 60 days 8. If patient does not meet criteria for evacuation, patient should be placed on quarters and checked every 4 hours by nonmedical assistant 9.
