PNEUMONIAS, FUNGAL- ASPERGILLOSIS- An acute or chronic illness caused by aspergillus species of molds typically found in compost piles, burn piles, damp hay and stored grains. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Typical presentation includes fever, chest pain, dyspnea, cough and hemoptysis 2. Suspect fungal infection if patient fails to respond to antibiotics 3. If fungi are ingested instead of inhaled, patients may develop abdominal pain and GI bleeding 4. Fungi can inva

and hemoptysis 2. Suspect fungal infection if patient fails to respond to antibiotics 3. If fungi are ingested instead of inhaled, patients may develop abdominal pain and GI bleeding 4. Fungi can invade from gut into local tissues then the bloodstream B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 2. Attempt to determine source of infection and eliminate risk to remainder of unit

97 XIX. PNEUMONIAS, FUNGAL- HISTOPLASMOSIS- Pulmonary fungal infection endemic to Central America, and Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Typical presentation similar to bacterial pneumonia, but patient does not respond to antibiotic treatment 2. Lymph nodes around the heart and lungs may get markedly enlarged 3. Difficulty swallowing due to esophageal narrowing from big lymph nodes 4. Congestive heart failure from poor venous return because big lymph nodes obstruct the vessels B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSLTATION AND MEDICATION a. Consider antifungal therapy such as itraconazole (Sporanox®) b. Most infections are self limiting and require only supportive therapy XX. PNEUMONIAS, FUNGAL- COCCIDIOMYCOSIS (“VALLEY FEVER”)- a pulmonary fungal infection endemic to Southwestern US and desert climates of Central and South America. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Fever, chest pain, cough and hemoptysis 2. May have skin manifestations of erythema nodosum (red or violet nodules that develop on the tibia) and erythema multiforme (target lesions often accompanied by bullae) 3. Subclinical presentation includes chronic arthralgias known as “desert rheumati

red or violet nodules that develop on the tibia) and erythema multiforme (target lesions often accompanied by bullae) 3. Subclinical presentation includes chronic arthralgias known as “desert rheumatism” and fatigue B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Frequently no treatment is required b. Evacuate and arrange for hospital admission if any of the following conditions occur i. Losing weight since symptoms began ii. Night sweats iii. Inability to work iv. Hemopysis XXI. PNEUMONIAS, FUNGAL- MUCORMYCOSIS- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Fungal Pneumonias, Mucormycosis” XXII. OTHER PNEUMONIAS A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Klebsiella pneumonia- acquired by immunosuppressed patients, trauma patients, or healthy individuals exposed to the papermill industry. Signs and symptoms are similar to other bacterial pneumonias, but characterized by thick, bloody sputum production that looks like “currant jelly” 2. Legionella- acquired by travelers exposed to contaminated water aerosolized in international hotels or on cruise ships; more common in smokers. Signs and symptoms are similar to other bacterial pneumonias, but frequently with lethargy and stupor
olized in international hotels or on cruise ships; more common in smokers. Signs and symptoms are similar to other bacterial pneumonias, but frequently with lethargy and stupor

98 3. Acute HIV seroconversion- May resemble acute viral or bacterial pneumonia; See “Febrile Illness Protocols, HIV” B. Treatment 1. Contact preceptor if any of the above organisms are suspected 2. Perform occupational hazard assessment if klebsiella is suspected 3. Perform water sampling if legionella is suspected 4. Locate, identify, and treat swamp coolers (evaporative air coolers that use fans blowing over static water; often get refilled without cleaning the water container leading to contamination) and window air conditioners XXIII. SPONTANEOUS PNEUMOTHORAX- Air in the pleural space resulting in partial or complete lung collapse caused by rupture of gaps in the subpleural space known as “blebs”. Blebs are congenital (inherited). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, anxiety or altered mental status 2. Sudden or gradual onset depending on severity and rate of development 3. Patients may have underlying pulmonary disease, typically tall, thin, males in teens or 20s 4. Smoking dramatically increases risk 5. Absent tactile fremitus, hyperresonance to percussion and decreased breath sounds on the affected side B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer suppl

0s 4. Smoking dramatically increases risk 5. Absent tactile fremitus, hyperresonance to percussion and decreased breath sounds on the affected side B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer supplemental O2 to maintain O2 saturation >95% b. Establish IV access but do not administer fluid bolus unless directed to do so c. If signs and symptoms are severe, to include tachycardia, hypotension, dyspnea, or cyanosis, perform a needle decompression of the involved lung 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXIV. TRAUMATIC PNEUMOTHORAX/TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX- Air in the pleural space resulting in partial or complete lung collapse caused by penetrating or blunt trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma 2. Dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain and anxiety or altered mental status 3. Absent tactile femitus, hyperresonance to percussion and decreased breath sounds on the affected side 4. May have subcutaneous emphysema (feels like “Rice Krispies®” under the skin) B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 at >95% b. Establish IV access and treat for shock (See Emergency Protocols, Shock) c. If signs and symptoms are severe, perform a need

IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 at >95% b. Establish IV access and treat for shock (See Emergency Protocols, Shock) c. If signs and symptoms are severe, perform a needle decompression of the involved lung; leave catheter in place and cover d. Cover open wounds with occlusive dressing

99 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Monitor for coexisting cardiac tamponade (space around the heart is reduced so heart can’t beat effectively; check for diminished or absent pulses) 4. Recheck heart sounds, lung sounds, and pulses every 5 minutes 5. Repeat needle decompression as necessary 6. May require intubation and ventilation, but repeating needle decompressions will help delay need for advanced airway XXV. PULMONARY EDEMA- Acute left ventricular failure causing blood to back up into the lungs. Can be caused by inadequate acclimatization to high altitude and narcotic overdose in otherwise healthy adults. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dyspnea, restlessness and anxiety with sense of suffocation 2. Cough producing blood-tinged or pink frothy sputum 3. Pallor and cyanosis 4. Signs and symptoms of shock 5. Rapid/thready pulse 6. Fine crackles bilaterally both anterior/posteriorly 7. S3 characteristic, or may have S4 heart sound B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. O2 15 LPM via non-rebreather; be prepared for advanced airway management and mechanical ventilation b. Position patient upright c. Initiate IV with normal saline at maintenance rate d. Do not administer

15 LPM via non-rebreather; be prepared for advanced airway management and mechanical ventilation b. Position patient upright c. Initiate IV with normal saline at maintenance rate d. Do not administer fluid bolus unless directed to do so 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider administration of furosemide (Lasix) at 1mg/kg IV push b. Consider administration of nitroglycerine (Nitrostat®) 0.4mg sublingually every 5 minutes c. Consider morphine 5mg IV push for breathlessness d. Maintain Systolic BP>80-90mmHg; mandatory discussion of all fluid therapy with preceptor XXVI. PULMONARY EMBOLI- The blockage of one or more pulmonary arteries by a thrombus, tumor, air collection, or fat collection that originates elsewhere in the body. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sudden onset of dyspnea (within a few seconds) with pleuritic chest pain 2. Tachypnea, tachycardia, hypotension 3. Amplified S2 heart sound 4. May cause patient to go into cardiac arrest 5. Risk factors include; underlying cardiovascular disease, family history, sedentary life style, smoking, history of immobilization and oral contraceptive use 6. May result from massive trauma 7. For patients with PE caused by

derlying cardiovascular disease, family history, sedentary life style, smoking, history of immobilization and oral contraceptive use 6. May result from massive trauma 7. For patients with PE caused by thrombus, check for signs and symptoms of a deep venous thrombosis (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Deep Venous Thrombosis”)

100 B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. O2 15 LPM via non-rebreather b. Be prepared for advanced airway management and mechanical ventilation c. Initiate IV, NS and maintain BP>80-90 Systolic d. May sit or lay in position of comfort 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Monitor for life-threatening complications while awaiting evacuation b. Strict bed rest; minimize patient’s movements c. Avoid jostling the patient as much as possible XXVII. STABLE ANGINA- Reversible chest pain or discomfort that occurs with the oxygen demand of the heart muscle exceeds its oxygen supply. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Chest discomfort/pain occurs at a predictable level of exertion and is relieved with rest up to 15 minutes 2. Pain can not be reproduced with palpation or deep breathing 3. May have associated neck, jaw, shoulder, or arm discomfort or pain 4. Vitals may show increased heart rate or increased blood pressure 5. Cardiac exam may have new extra sounds (S3 or S4) or new murmur B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer oxygen: 4 to 6 liters per minute, by nasal catheter or cannula b. Increase to 8-10 liters per minute by mask if signs of hypoxia c. Keep patient calm and re

Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer oxygen: 4 to 6 liters per minute, by nasal catheter or cannula b. Increase to 8-10 liters per minute by mask if signs of hypoxia c. Keep patient calm and relaxed. Rule out emergent cardiac conditions (see “Acute Coronary Syndrome” in Emergency Protocols) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION a. Consider nitroglycerin (Nitrostat®)sublingually b. Do not give nitroglycerin (Nitrostat®) if patient has taken sildenafil (Viagra®) or other sexual performance enhancers in the past 72 hours XXVIII. TUBERCULOSIS- A chronic infection of the lungs caused by a mycobacterium and is the leading infectious cause of illness and death in adults worldwide. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Vague symptoms and general malaise 2. Productive cough of yellow or green sputum, usually in the morning; may have hemoptysis 3. Night sweats, weight loss, fatigue 4. Exam findings include diminished breath sounds, dullness to percussion with DECREASED tactile fremitus (compared to increased fremitus with bacterial pneumonia) 5. Rales (crackles) on inspiration or after short coughs (post-tussive rales) 6. Positive TB skin test 7. Upper lobe cavitation lesions

emitus (compared to increased fremitus with bacterial pneumonia) 5. Rales (crackles) on inspiration or after short coughs (post-tussive rales) 6. Positive TB skin test 7. Upper lobe cavitation lesions visible on chest x-ray B. Treatment
101 1. Respiratory isolation if TB is suspected (private room, gown, mask, gloves) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION 3. Monitor other members of unit for similar symptoms

102 Chapter 8: Gastrointestinal Protocols I. ABDOMINAL TRAUMA- “See Emergency Medicine Protocols, Abdominal Trauma” II. ANAL FISSURES- A tear in the anal lining due to anal trauma, usually from the passage of a large hard stool. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sensation of tearing during passage of stool followed by small amount of blood in toilet or with wiping 2. Exam reveals a fresh laceration extending in or through anal sphincter 3. Patients generally will not tolerate digital rectal exam or anoscopy due to pain B. Treatment 1. Treatment has three components a. Warm Sitz baths (see treatment for hemorrhoids) for 10-15 minutes after passage of stool PLUS b. Increasing dietary fiber PLUS c. Topical lidocaine (Xylocaine®) or witch hazel 2. Advise patient that all three are necessary for lesion to heal 3. Consider adding docusate sodium (Colace®) to soften stools 4. Do not give stimulant laxatives such as Ex-Lax®, Senecot®, or Senna® because these will increase pressure around the sphincter III. ANTHRAX, GASTROINTESTINAL- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Anthrax” IV. APPENDICITIS- Inflammation of the vermiform appendix. The most common cause for an attack of severe, acute abdominal pain t

III. ANTHRAX, GASTROINTESTINAL- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Anthrax” IV. APPENDICITIS- Inflammation of the vermiform appendix. The most common cause for an attack of severe, acute abdominal pain that requires surgical intervention. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sudden onset epigastric or periumbilical pain followed by nausea and vomiting several hours later 2. Pain shifts to the right lower quadrant 3. Direct tenderness and rebound tenderness in the right lower quadrant at McBurney’s Point. 4. Low grade fever of 100-101°F and leukocytosis of 12-15,000/µL are characteristic 5. Positive Rovsing’s, Psoas and Obturator signs and rectal tenderness suggest appendicitis B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access b. Give fluid bolus if indicated c. Consider narcotics for pain d. Consider promethazine (Phenergan®) or ondansetron (Zofran®) for nausea e. Keep patient NPO, insert NG tube and monitor I&Os 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION

103 a. Consider antibiotics if directed by a preceptor b. Ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) IV PLUS metronidazole (Flagyl®) IV is effective V. BAROTRAUMA, GI- See “Environmental Injury Protocols, Barotrauma, GI” VI. CHOLECYSTITIS- Acute inflammation of the gallbladder usually resulting from an obstruction caused by a gall stone. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Recurrent colicky pain, progressing to severe and constant pain, localized right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right lower scapula 2. Pain is aggravated by ingesting a high fat meal 3. Nausea and vomiting are common 4. Gall bladder is palpable in 50% of cases 5. Patients attempt to splint the abdomen during deep inspiration 6. Positive Murphy’s sign 7. Low grade fever, tachycardia B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access and bolus fluids if indicated b. Manage pain with meperidine (Demorol®) or IM ketoralac (Toradol®) as necessary c. Keep patient NPO d. Monitor I&Os 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. High fever, high white blood cell count, and absent bowel sounds suggest gangrenous gall bladder or rupture b. If signs and symptoms of rupture are present (severe pain, rebound tenderness, signs of shoc

h fever, high white blood cell count, and absent bowel sounds suggest gangrenous gall bladder or rupture b. If signs and symptoms of rupture are present (severe pain, rebound tenderness, signs of shock), consider antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) VII. CONSTIPATION- Difficult or infrequent passage of stool. In deployed populations, constipation is commonly caused by reduced fiber in diet. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. No passage of stool or inability to pass stool or infrequent stools that require straining to pass 2. Patient may see blood streaks on stool 3. Abdominal exam usually mild to moderate tenderness, areas of dullness to percussion especially left lower quadrant 4. Tense, tympanic abdomen indicates mechanical obstruction CLINICAL NOTE: Abdominal exam demonstrating a tense, tympanic abdomen with diminished or absent bowel sounds suggests something is mechanically obstructing the gut (ovarian tumor pinches off a segment of gut, for example). If these symptoms are occurring, IMMEDIATE EVACUATION IS REQUIRED.
ing, IMMEDIATE EVACUATION IS REQUIRED.

104 5. Causes of constipation can be numerous. Collect comprehensive history to include a life-long bowel history, changes in diet, exercise, sleep, activity, stress, hydration, herbals/supplements/medications B. Treatment 1. Treat underlying cause if known 2. Increase dietary fiber to 35 mg/day 3. Add citrus fruits, prunes or prune juice to diet if available 4. Consider Docusate sodium (Colace®) as a daily stool regulating agent 5. Avoid stimulant laxatives unless directed by physician preceptor 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if recurrent, associated with a fever, or if initial treatment does not work VIII. GASTROENTERITIS, GENERAL- An inflammation of the stomach and intestinal tract primarily caused by an infection, but may be caused by ingested poisons and toxins. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Decreased appetite 2. Nausea/vomiting 3. Diarrhea 4. Abdominal cramping B. Treatment 1. Provide symptomatic relief 2. Maintain fluid and electrolyte balance 3. Initiate IV support if necessary and transition to oral rehydration solutions that contain water, salt, and sugar as soon as patient is able to tolerate PO 4. Start patient on dry cereal, crackers, or BRAT diet after tolera

necessary and transition to oral rehydration solutions that contain water, salt, and sugar as soon as patient is able to tolerate PO 4. Start patient on dry cereal, crackers, or BRAT diet after tolerating PO fluids for 2 or more hours 5. Bed rest with bathroom privileges 6. If any of the following conditions are met, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION: a. Hypovolemia does not correct with initial treatments b. Stools contain blood c. Temp>101°F d. Severe abdominal pain e. History of antibiotic use in past month 7. Treatment of diarrhea with antibiotics may be initiated if none of the above criteria are met AND the patient is having more than four unformed stools per day, fever, and correctable hypovolemia CLINICAL NOTE: The World Health Organization Solution 3.5 grams of sodium chloride (salt) 2.5 grams sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) 1.5 grams potassium chloride (salt substitute) 40 grams sucrose (table sugar) 1 Liter of water OR ½ tsp salt ½ tsp baking soda 4 Tbsp table sugar 1 Liter of water
Liter of water

105 IX. GASTROENTERITIS, VIRAL- The majority of cases of acute gastroenteritis are caused by viruses such as norovirus, rotavirus and adenovirus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Differentiate between bacterial causes 2. Symptoms start with vomiting 3. Incubation period of 24-48 hours 4. Illness lasts 12-60 hours B. Treatment 1. Control nausea with IM or PO promethazine (Phenergan®) OR IV or PO ondansetron (Zofran®) 2. Support with IV therapy if patient is orthostatic and nauseous 3. Bed rest/quarters 4. Clear liquids for 12 hours, advance to BRAT diet and then advance as tolerated 5. No antibiotics indicated X. GASTROENTERITIS, VIBRIO CHOLERAE (CHOLERA)- (Gram negative, comma-shaped bacillus) Grows in the intestinal tract and becomes endemic when stools from infected persons contaminate water supply. This organism has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation of 24-72 hours 2. Abrupt onset of watery emesis and/or liquid “rice water” diarrhea 3. Stools have “fishy” odor 4. Fever is uncommon with cholera, but fast, severe dehydration can be fatal 5. Found in developing countries in Asia, Middle East, Africa and South/Central America B. Treatment 1. Aggressive mana

dor 4. Fever is uncommon with cholera, but fast, severe dehydration can be fatal 5. Found in developing countries in Asia, Middle East, Africa and South/Central America B. Treatment 1. Aggressive management and replacement of fluids and electrolytes via IV 2. Switch to aggressive PO hydration with WHO rehydration solution or watered down Gatorade® as soon as PO can be tolerated 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider antibiotic therapy with single-dose doxycycline (Vibramycin®) as soon as PO is tolerated b. Patient may require extreme amounts of IV fluids (20 or more liters) 4. Investigate for contaminated water supply XI. GASTROENTERITIS, CLOSTRIDIUM BOTULINUM (BOTULISM)- (Gram positive, spore- forming bacillus) Grows in anaerobic foods (canned or processed) and produces a powerful neurotoxin. The bacteria and the toxin have the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 24-96 hours 2. Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dry mouth and sore throat followed by a. Diplopia, dysphagia, dysphonia, and respiratory compromise if untreated b. Descending paralysis, no fever
th and sore throat followed by a. Diplopia, dysphagia, dysphonia, and respiratory compromise if untreated b. Descending paralysis, no fever

106 c. Patient remains aware but unable to move or communicate (“Locked in”) B. Treatment 1. Aggressive airway management and ventilation 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Administer antitoxin if available XII. GASTROENTERITIS, ESCHERICHIA COLI (“TRAVELLER’S DIARRHEA”)- (Gram negative bacillus) E. coli is normal intestinal flora that can overgrow or individuals can be exposed to a different strain. E. Coli produce toxins and invades epithelial surfaces of the GI tract. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 24-72 hours 2. Abrupt onset of diarrhea/rare vomiting 3. History of drinking untreated, foreign water B. Treatment 1. Usually self-limiting and resolves within 3 days 2. Maintain hydration status 3. Consider loperimide (Lomotil®, Imodium®) if no fever 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION if a. Blood is present b. Symptoms are severe and incapacitating c. Consider antibiotic therapy with ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®) XIII. GASTROENTERITIS, CLOSTRIDIUM DIFFICILE (“C-DIFF”)- (Gram positive, spore-forming rod) Normal flora in the intestinal tract. Infection occurs when patient takes antibiotics th

hoxazole (Bactrim®) XIII. GASTROENTERITIS, CLOSTRIDIUM DIFFICILE (“C-DIFF”)- (Gram positive, spore-forming rod) Normal flora in the intestinal tract. Infection occurs when patient takes antibiotics that kill off the helpful bacteria in the GI ecosystem and C-diff becomes opportunistic. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Onset corresponds with administration of antibiotic therapy, but varies with individuals 2. Abrupt, foul smelling, bloody, watery diarrhea and low grade fever B. Treatment 1. Stop any current antibiotic therapy unless patient already has a more dangerous infection 2. Maintain hydration and electrolyte status for patient 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Consider treatment with metronidazole (Flagyl®) b. Do not administer loperamide (Lomotil®, Imodium®) unless directed by preceptor c. Illness is spread fecal-orally, but should not be a risk to healthy individuals CLINICAL NOTE: May develop life-threatening “toxic megacolon” with severe abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and slowing of stools due to intestinal muscles slowing. This is suggestive of impending colon rupture. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION IS REQUIRED.
ion, and slowing of stools due to intestinal muscles slowing. This is suggestive of impending colon rupture. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION IS REQUIRED.

107 d. Exercise good hand washing and consider contact precautions (gown, gloves) if feasible XIV. GASTROENTERITIS, STAPHYLOCOCCAL ENTERITIS- (gram positive cocci) Diarrhea caused by enterotoxin from ingested staphylococcal bacteria. Staph grows in meats, dairy, eggs, and bakery products. This bacteria has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Contaminated foods are left at room temperature and then consumed after a significant amount of toxin was produced 2. Incubation period of 1-6 hours after ingestion 3. Abrupt onset of vomiting lasting approximately 24 hours 4. Nausea and vomiting are usually severe, but diarrhea is less intense or absent 5. Occurs in persons eating the same food B. Treatment 1. Recovery is spontaneous without antibiotics in 1-2 days 2. May give promethazine (Phenergan®) or ondansatron (Zofran®) IV or PO to reduce nausea 3. Enforce hydration 4. Initiate IV if orthostatic and nauseous XV. GASTROENTERITIS, CLOSTRIDIUM PERFRINGENS- (gram positive bacillus) Normal intestinal flora. Infection results by eating contaminated food (typically rewarmed meat- containing foods). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 8-16 hours 2. Abrupt onset o

bacillus) Normal intestinal flora. Infection results by eating contaminated food (typically rewarmed meat- containing foods). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 8-16 hours 2. Abrupt onset of profuse, foul-smelling diarrhea and vomiting B. Treatment 1. Recovery spontaneous without antibiotics within 4 days 2. May give promethazine (Phenergan®) or ondansatron (Zofran®) IV or PO to reduce nausea 3. Enforce hydration 4. Initiate IV support if orthostatic and nauseous 5. If symptoms are severe, consider antibiotic therapy and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND CONSULTATION XVI. GASTROENTERITIS, SHIGELLA (“BACCILLARY DYSENTERY” OR “SHIGELLOSIS”)- (Gram negative bacillus) Common, self-limiting disease found in conjunction with poor sanitary conditions. It grows in the intestinal epithelium and produces a toxin. This bacteria has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 24-72 hours 2. Acute onset of bloody or mucous-containing diarrhea, lower abdominal cramps and tenesmus (rectal dry heaves) 3. Fever of 104°F, chills, malaise, decrease appetite and headache 4. Weakness and dehydration 5. Abdominal tenderness B. Treatment
lower abdominal cramps and tenesmus (rectal dry heaves) 3. Fever of 104°F, chills, malaise, decrease appetite and headache 4. Weakness and dehydration 5. Abdominal tenderness B. Treatment

108 1. Maintain fluid/electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Consider oral or IV ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) or ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) IM b. Strict contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves, etc) 3. Progress to liquid diet and advance as tolerated 4. Other bacteria produce a similar toxin: consider amoebic dysentery, salmonella, E. coli, and ulcerative colitis XVII. SALMONELLA- (gram negative bacillus) “Typhoid fever” is a severe systemic illness with stepwise progression caused by Salmonella typhi and is transmitted through contaminated water/food. This bacteria has the potential for weaponization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period is 5-14 days 2. Follows a predictable, sequential pattern a. First week of illness i. Fevers to 104°F, chills and sweats ii. May be misdiagnosed as malaria or dengue fever iii. Abdominal pain, vomiting and diarrhea iv. Diarrhea described as “Pea Soup” in nature v. Maximum temperature each day is higher than the previous day’s temp and stabilizes in 1 week b. Second week of illness i. Fever decreases in a stair-step fashion by the 10th day ii. Bradycardia iii. Faint salmon-colored macules appear on the t

the previous day’s temp and stabilizes in 1 week b. Second week of illness i. Fever decreases in a stair-step fashion by the 10th day ii. Bradycardia iii. Faint salmon-colored macules appear on the trunk and abdomen iv. Malaise, headache and cough c. Third week of illness i. Hepatosplenomegaly, intestinal bleeding, peritonitis, intestinal perforation and septic shock ii. May have changes in mentation or psychoses d. Observe for “typhoid state”—patient appears exhausted, motionless and unresponsive, but able to follow simple commands B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid/electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Consider oral or IV ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) or ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) IM b. Strict contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) 3. Progress to liquid diet and advance as tolerated 4. Other bacteria produce a similar toxin: consider amoebic dysentery, salmonella, E. coli, and ulcerative colitis XVIII. GASTROENTERITIS, CAMPYLOBACTER JEJUNI- (Gram negative spiral bacillus) Grows in the jejunum and ilium and transmitted in contaminated drinking water and unpasteurized milk.
negative spiral bacillus) Grows in the jejunum and ilium and transmitted in contaminated drinking water and unpasteurized milk.

109 A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period 1-7 days (mean=3 days) after ingestion 2. Abrupt onset of periumbilical cramping and abdominal pain (mimicking acute appendicitis) and diarrhea that may be accompanied by high fever with rigors, body aches, dizziness and delirium 3. Bloody stools may develop on the second or third day of diarrhea B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid/electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Consider Erythromycin (E-Mycin®) or ciprofloxacin (Cipro®) b. Strict contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) 3. Progress to liquid diet and advance as tolerated 4. Other bacteria produce a similar toxin: consider amoebic dysentery, salmonella, E. coli, and ulcerative colitis XIX. GASTROENTERITIS, YERSINIA ENTEROCOLITICA- (gram negative coccobacillus) Yersinia Enterocolitica is transmitted from contaminated food (raw vegetables or salad ingredients contaminated with human manure) or a water supply contaminated with human feces. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period 1-11 days (mean = 4 days) 2. Severe abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fever often misdiagnosed as acute appendicitis 3. Nausea and vomiting are less common 4. Blood

Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period 1-11 days (mean = 4 days) 2. Severe abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fever often misdiagnosed as acute appendicitis 3. Nausea and vomiting are less common 4. Blood in stool B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid/electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Antibiotics are not indicated unless severe disease, but symptoms are often indistinguishable from other causes of acute gastroenteritis that do require antibiotics b. Find source of contaminated food or water 3. Progress to liquid diet and advance as tolerated XX. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, AMEBIASIS (“AMOEBIC DYSENTERY”)- Amoebic dysentery is caused by entamoeba histolytica, which is a protozoan form of parasite. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Diarrhea is the most common presentation; between 5-10 episodes per day 2. Incubation period of several weeks with insidious onset of symptoms 3. Weight loss and abdominal cramping may be accompanied by vomiting 4. Fever; may be as high as 105°F 5. Recurrent episodes of diarrhea and abdominal cramps alternating between periods of constipation is also common 6. May have blood and mucous in stool 7. Tender, enlarged liver 8. Difficult to diagno

5. Recurrent episodes of diarrhea and abdominal cramps alternating between periods of constipation is also common 6. May have blood and mucous in stool 7. Tender, enlarged liver 8. Difficult to diagnose. Requires repeated stool samples and identification of the organism or cysts in the stool. Frequently misdiagnosed as other conditions

110 B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid/electrolyte balance 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Mandatory evacuation for any patient with bloody diarrhea b. Consider evacuation in other cases for definitive diagnosis XXI. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, SCHISTOSOMIASIS (“KATAYAMA FEVER”)- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Gastrointestinal Parasites, Schistosomiasis” XXII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, GIARDIASIS- Giardia lamblia (protozoan) caused by ingestion of contaminated water supply, is endemic worldwide. In the US, the disorder is common among hikers drinking inadequately filtered/treated water and in daycare centers (fecal-oral transmission). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 7-14 days 2. 50% of infections are asymptomatic 3. Acute/chronic diarrhea alternating with constipation 4. Excessive foul flatulence 5. Diarrhea is foul and excessively fatty or greasy 6. Blood/mucous in stool and mild abdominal cramps 7. Nausea, vomiting, low grade fever and headache 8. Weight loss (up to 10% of body weight in a month) and malabsorption symptoms 9. Mimics peptic ulcer disease and cholecystitis B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid and electrolyte balance 2. Strict contact precauti

loss (up to 10% of body weight in a month) and malabsorption symptoms 9. Mimics peptic ulcer disease and cholecystitis B. Treatment 1. Maintain fluid and electrolyte balance 2. Strict contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Medication of choice is metronidazole (Flagyl®), however may not be able to differentiate giardia from other more serious conditions without lab capability b. If bloody stool is present, IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is required 4. Attempt to localize source of contamination XXIII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, TAPE WORMS- General classification of several worms that affect humans. Distribution is worldwide. Worms vary in size from 1 cm to 300cm. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of eating infected, undercooked or raw beef, pork, fish or seafood 2. May see worm segments in stool/toilet or may feel worm movement around anus 3. Heavy infestations cause weight loss, diarrhea, abdominal pain 4. Larvae travel throughout the body and are capable of invading all tissue types 5. CNS infections manifest with seizures, CNS depression, personality disturbances and hydrocephalus B. Treatment 1. Diagnosis is made by f

ughout the body and are capable of invading all tissue types 5. CNS infections manifest with seizures, CNS depression, personality disturbances and hydrocephalus B. Treatment 1. Diagnosis is made by finding worm segments in clothing, bedding or stool or by identification of ova on the perianal skin

111 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider evacuation for diagnostic testing and treatment b. If illness is not severe and worm segments are identified, drug of choice is praziquantel (Biltricide®) XXIV. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, PINWORMS (ENTEROBIASIS)- An intestinal infestation by Enterobius vermicularis that lives in tropical and temperate environments. Worms live in intestinal tract and lay eggs around the anus. Infection is by ingestion of eggs dislodged from clothing/bedding or by eating food prepared by persons with contaminated hands. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Presenting complaint is often intense, perianal pruritus 2. Perianal excoriations from scratching 3. Transferred via fecal oral route or by fomites 4. Diagnosis is made by observing the female worm on the surface of the anus 1-2 hours after patient goes to bed or by identification of ova on the perianal skin B. Treatment 1. Indicated if patient is symptomatic 2. Strict contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Administer a single dose of mebendazole (Vermox®) b. Repeat mebendazole (Vermox®) dose in 2 weeks 4. Evaluate all close contac

and bathroom, gown, gloves) 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Administer a single dose of mebendazole (Vermox®) b. Repeat mebendazole (Vermox®) dose in 2 weeks 4. Evaluate all close contacts 5. All bedding and clothing must be washed in hot water and hot dryer to destroy eggs 6. Clip infected patient’s fingernails close to skin XXV. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, TRICHINOSIS- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Gastrointestinal Parasites, Trichinosis” XXVI. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, TRICHURIASIS (WHIPWORMS)- Small, skinny worms 30- 50 mm in length found worldwide primarily in the tropics and warm, humid areas. Infection comes from ingestion of soil, such as eating inadequately washed raw vegetables or fruits. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Usually asymptomatic, but most commonly presents with rectal prolapse 2. Abdominal pain, tenesmus (rectal dry heaves), diarrhea, abdominal distention, flatulence, nausea, vomiting and weight loss 3. May exhibit pica (eating things that aren’t food such as dirt, ice, or chalk) 4. Finger clubbing also occurs 5. Barrel shaped eggs in stool visible with microscope B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Mebendazole (Vermox®) is th

uch as dirt, ice, or chalk) 4. Finger clubbing also occurs 5. Barrel shaped eggs in stool visible with microscope B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Mebendazole (Vermox®) is the drug of choice b. If meds are unavailable, consider soapsuds enema 2. Identify source of contamination and eliminate

112 XXVII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, ASCARIASIS- The most common intestinal worm. Worldwide distribution, primarily in warm, wet areas or during rainy seasons. Infection caused by fecal-oral route and contaminated water or food (especially raw fruits and vegetables). Adult worms are 20-40 cm long, mature in the intestines and migrate to the lungs. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Fever, cough, hemoptysis, rales and evidence of lung involvement 2. Heavy infections present with abdominal pain and colic 3. History of coughing up a worm, or a worm passing through the nose B. Treatment 1. Diagnosis is made by finding the worms or larvae in stool or sputum 2. Maintain fluid and electrolyte balance 3. Strict contact precautions (private room and bathroom, gown, gloves) 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. If evacuation will be delayed, consider antihelminth treatment with mebendazole (Vermox®) b. Patient may be agitated if worms were visualized or patient has sensation of moving worms. Consider anxiolytic such as diazepam (Valium®) XXVIII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, HOOKWORM- Widespread in the tropics; transmitted by wearing open footwear while walking on contaminated soil or wading i

sider anxiolytic such as diazepam (Valium®) XXVIII. GASTROINTESTINAL PARASITES, HOOKWORM- Widespread in the tropics; transmitted by wearing open footwear while walking on contaminated soil or wading in contaminated water. Worms invade directly through the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Itching at site of penetration 2. May see the larval track underneath the skin (cutaneous larva migrans) 3. 8-21 days later- hookworms invade pulmonary tissue, climb the pulmonary tree and are swallowed 4. May be asymptomatic or have mild cough 5. Intestinal infection demonstrates nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, increased flatulence, and abdominal pain that is worse postprandially (after eating) 6. Chronic infection causes nutritional deficiencies and anemia B. Treatment 1. Diagnosis is made based on seeing larval tracks in the skin or coughed/vomited worms visualized 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Albendazole (Albenza®) single dose is preferred treatment; mebendazole (Vermox®) is an alternative b. Iron supplements (Ferrous Sulfate®) if anemic C. Prevention 1. Do not allow personnel deployed to warm, humid areas to go barefoot or wear open shoes 2. Do not allow deployed personnel to recrea

ve b. Iron supplements (Ferrous Sulfate®) if anemic C. Prevention 1. Do not allow personnel deployed to warm, humid areas to go barefoot or wear open shoes 2. Do not allow deployed personnel to recreationally wade in fresh water XXIX. GASTRITIS- Inflammation of the gastric mucosa caused by infections, NSAID use, alcohol, and stress. Gastritis can be classified as wither erosive or non-erosive, acute or chronic. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Erosive Gastritis

113 a. Caused by NSAIDs, alcohol stress, viral infection or trauma b. May complain of heartburn, dyspepsia, nausea or vomiting c. May have hematemesis or melena d. Symptoms begin within 2-5 days of the inciting event e. Bleeding is mild or moderate 2. Non-erosive gastritis a. Mainly the result of H. pylori infection b. May complain of mild dyspepsia or vague abdominal symptoms, nausea or epigastric pain B. Treatment 1. If bleeding is present a. IMMEDIATE ACTION i. Initiate IV access and administer fluid bolus if indicated ii. Stop all medications, herbals, and supplements iii. If alcohol is possible etiology, prepare for possible seizures and Delirium Tremens (see “Neurological Protocols- Acute Alcohol Withdrawal”) b. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION a) Consider IV acid suppression therapy, such as rinitidine (Zantac®) or famotidine (Pepcid®) b) Keep patient NPO unless otherwise directed by Preceptor 2. If pain is less severe and bleeding is absent a. Determine the cause and correct it b. Administer PO acid suppressants such as rinitidine (Zantac®) or famotidine (Pepcid®) c. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION i. Consider evacuation for H. Pylori serum t

e cause and correct it b. Administer PO acid suppressants such as rinitidine (Zantac®) or famotidine (Pepcid®) c. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION i. Consider evacuation for H. Pylori serum test or urea breath test ii. If positive, treat for peptic ulcer disease (PUD) d. Reevaluate in a few days to make sure symptoms have resolved XXX. DYSPEPSIA (“HEARTBURN”)- The sensation of epigastric discomfort caused by peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux, or drugs (NSAIDs and some antibiotics). May be caused by pyloric dysfunction, motility problems, gastritis or malignancy. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Indigestion, gassiness, postprandial (after eating) fullness, gnawing and burning behind sternum 2. If patient complains of chest pain, see “Emergency Protocols, Acute Coronary Syndrome” B. Treatment 1. For severe discomfort a. Give “GI cocktail” of 30 mL Maalox®/Mylanta® + 10 mL viscous lidocaine + 10 mL Donnatal® mixed together and drunk b. If no improvement in 1 hr, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION 2. For mild discomfort
CIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION 2. For mild discomfort

114 a. Treat symptoms with an H2 blocker such as cimetidine (Tagamet®) or ranitidine (Zantac®), or proton pump inhibitor such as omeprazole (Prilosec®) b. Test for fecal occult blood and arrange for routine medical evacuation XXXI. HEMORRHOIDS- Dilated veins in the tissue in and around the anus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Chief complaint of blood with bowel movement or with wiping, pruritus, or palpable mass around the anus 2. Risk factors include prolonged sitting, pregnancy, repetitive straining (ie weightlifters), and chronic constipation 3. Mass will be visible and/or palpable on anal exam 4. Consider anoscopy for better visualization B. Treatment 1. Treat risk factors, if known 2. Increase fiber content of diet to reduce discomfort of bowel movements 3. Fiber will also help when constipation is a contributing factor 4. Sitz baths- submerge the anal area in 2-3 inches of warm (not hot) water perform BID-TID will markedly improve discomfort and itching 5. Hydrocortisone cream applied topically or via suppository twice daily for maximum of 7 days will also reduce itching, but may irritate and damage the anal mucosa 6. Witch hazel will cool area and decrease discomfort XXXII. HEPATI

topically or via suppository twice daily for maximum of 7 days will also reduce itching, but may irritate and damage the anal mucosa 6. Witch hazel will cool area and decrease discomfort XXXII. HEPATITIS (VIRAL)- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Hepatitis” XXXIII. INGUINAL AND FEMORAL HERNIAS- An internal defect in muscle or fascia that allows abdominal contents to move where they don’t belong. Umbilical hernias are when abdominal contents protrude through the anterior abdominal wall, creating a visible outpouching near the umbilicus (belly button) with increased abdominal pressure. Direct inguinal hernias can be felt externally along the inguinal creasewith increased abdominal CLINICAL NOTE: Patients may present with acute severe anal pain and palpable hard mass near the anus. This is a thrombosed hemorrhoid. Although very uncomfortable, these patients can be managed with rest and the above treatments plus Acetaminophen and/or NSAIDs PLUS topical lidocaine or witch hazel with probable resolution of symptoms within 5-7 days. If these measures are insufficient, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION for possible surgical correction. CLINICAL NOTE: All patients with rectal bleeding

resolution of symptoms within 5-7 days. If these measures are insufficient, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION for possible surgical correction. CLINICAL NOTE: All patients with rectal bleeding should be evaluated by endoscopy to make sure the patient doesn’t have a more serious disease such as cancer, ulcerative colitis, or Crohn’s Disease. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION for all patients with rectal bleeding, even if source of bleeding is clearly identifiable.

115 pressure. Indirect inguinal hernias occur when abdominal contents slide down through the inguinal canal and may be noticeable inside the scrotum; they can be palpated within the inguinal canal. Femoral hernias occur more often in women and are when abdominal contents slide along the anterior/medial thigh. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sensation of fullness, dull ache, or discomfort that is more pronounced when patient is lifting, coughing, straining, or increasing abdominal pressure 2. Discomfort and pressure typically improve when the lifting or straining is stopped (ie sitting or resting) due to the intestinal contents moving back into the abdominal cavity 3. Severe pain or pain and pressure that do not resolve spontaneously should raise suspicion that the hernia is “incarcerated”, which means it is trapped and unable to move back into the abdominal cavity spontaneously 4. While patient is standing facing the seated examiner, have patient cough, strain, or Valsalva and observe and palpate for visible bulge. Complete “hernia check” in males for indirect inguinal hernias B. Treatment 1. For uncomplicated, spontaneously reducing hernias, patient will require surgery for repair 2. CON

pate for visible bulge. Complete “hernia check” in males for indirect inguinal hernias B. Treatment 1. For uncomplicated, spontaneously reducing hernias, patient will require surgery for repair 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION a. Limited duty profile to restrict lifting and gear to no more than 10 lbs b. Rest, ice, NSAIDs, and stretching exercises such as yoga will all help reduce symptoms 3. If incarcerated hernia is suspected a. Apply gentle direct pressure on visible bulge, manipulating it back toward abdominal cavity b. If this is insufficient, place patient flat with head down and feet up (Trendelenburg position) c. Administer 5-10 mg diazepam (Valium®) IM and attempt to reduce hernia d. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXXIV. MOTION SICKNESS- A syndrome caused by repetitive vertical and horizontal acceleration and deceleration. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Nausea, vomiting, vague abdominal discomfort 2. Preceded by salivation, pallor, sweating and sleepiness 3. May have headache, fatigue, weakness and dizziness B. Treatment 1. Patients prone to motion sickness should premedicate with scopolamine hydrobromide (Scopace®) 2. An antihistamine such as diphen

s 3. May have headache, fatigue, weakness and dizziness B. Treatment 1. Patients prone to motion sickness should premedicate with scopolamine hydrobromide (Scopace®) 2. An antihistamine such as diphenhydramine hydrochloride (Benadryl®) taken 1 hour before anticipated movement is also effective ACTION ALERT: Observe closely for signs of impending or actual strangulation such as pain and tenderness to palpation in the region of the hernial swelling, discoloration of tissue over swelling site, nausea and vomiting, low grade fever and malaise, or absence of bowel movements or bowel sounds.

116 3. Manage vomiting by administration of promethazine (Phenergan®) IM, PO, or PR as long as patient did not premedicate with an antihistamine 4. Avoid rear-facing seating on aircraft and seat patients over the wings to minimize motion XXXV. PANCREATITIS- Inflammation of the pancreas. Pancreatitis may be acute or chronic. Alcoholism and biliary tract disease accounts for over 80% of cases of acute pancreatitis. The remaining 20% are attributed to drugs (hormones and diuretics) associated with some disease processes. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe epigastric abdominal pain radiating straight through to the back 2. Pain may radiate to the abdomen 3. Pain described as severe, steady, boring and without relief persisting for hours or days and often worsened by ingesting a heavy meal 4. Patient appears restless, anxious or agitated 5. Bending or leaning forward alleviates pain 6. Pain is aggravated by coughing, movement or deep breathing 7. Nausea and vomiting also occur 8. Abdomen may appear distended 9. Tachycardia- pulse may be over 100 BPM, transient hypertension and postural hypotension are common 10. Epigastric abdominal tenderness always occurs 11. Bowel sounds may be hypoacti

ppear distended 9. Tachycardia- pulse may be over 100 BPM, transient hypertension and postural hypotension are common 10. Epigastric abdominal tenderness always occurs 11. Bowel sounds may be hypoactive B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Keep patient completely NPO- no pills, fluids, etc. b. Establish IV access with Ringer’s Lactate unless otherwise directed by preceptor c. Monitor I&Os and aggressively hydrate unless ascites, edema or pulmonary findings are present d. Pain management with meperidine (Demerol®) 50-100mg IV, especially during transport (morphine IV may be used as an alternative) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXXVI. PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE (“PUD”)- An erosion in a segment of the GI mucosa, typically in the stomach or the first 5 cm of the duodenum. Nearly all ulcers are caused by Helicobacter pylori or NSAID use. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Burning epigastric pain relieved by food or antacids 2. Pain described as burning or gnawing and usually chronic or recurrent 3. May have postprandial epigastric discomfort, early satiety, or nausea 4. PUD complications include hemorrhage, gastric obstruction and perforation B. Treatment 1. Therapy is often start

r recurrent 3. May have postprandial epigastric discomfort, early satiety, or nausea 4. PUD complications include hemorrhage, gastric obstruction and perforation B. Treatment 1. Therapy is often started without definitive diagnosis 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider evacuation to test for H. pylori

117 b. If H. pylori is present, treatment consists of “triple therapy” with omeprazole (Prilosec®) or rabeprazole (Aciphex®) PLUS amoxicillin (Amoxil®) PLUS clindamycin (Cleocin®) 3. Discontinue tobacco and alcohol use 4. Gastric acidity can be treated by use of proton pump inhibitors such as omeprazole (Prilosec®) or rabeprazole (Aciphex®), H2 blockers such as ranitidine (Zantac®) or cimetidine (Tagamet®) and/or antacids such as aluminum hydroxide or calcium carbonate (Tums®, Rolaids®) XXXVII. PERITONITIS- Inflammation of the peritoneal cavity. Peritonitis can be either primary or secondary. Primary peritonitis occurs following an infection in the blood stream. Secondary peritonitis (most common) usually occurs as a result of perforations of the GI tract through a variety of pathology and trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma or systemic disease 2. Sudden onset abdominal pain can either be localized or diffuse depending on the cause of the peritonitis 3. Fever, large volume ascites with abdominal distention, absent bowel sounds and altered mental status occurs in over 50% of patients 4. Vomiting and high fever >103°F not uncommon B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. In

me ascites with abdominal distention, absent bowel sounds and altered mental status occurs in over 50% of patients 4. Vomiting and high fever >103°F not uncommon B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Initiate IV and provide fluid and electrolyte replacement as needed b. Keep patient NPO c. Insert NG tube d. Monitor I&Os 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If evacuation will be delayed, consider ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) IV b. Repeat vitals at least hourly until evacuation XXXVIII. ULCERATIVE COLITIS AND CROHN’S DISEASE (INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASES)- Chronic recurring episodes of inflammation of the lining of the bowel that usually starts when patient is in their 20’s or 30’s. Symptoms often start as bright red blood in bowel movements and in underwear. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Patient has symptoms indistinguishable from infectious causes of diarrhea to include frequent loose stools, cramping abdominal pain especially before passage of stool, low grade fever, and abdominal pain 2. History is suggestive of an inflammatory bowel disease over an infectious condition if patient has had similar episodes of bloody stool that resolved spontaneously, if bleeding occurs as br

al pain 2. 118 addition to stools and/or wiping, if exposure to infectious causes are unlikely, and if nausea is not a presenting symptom 3. CANDIDIASIS (CANDIDAL VULVOVAGINITIS)- an overgrowth of normal vaginal yeast flora that may occur spontaneously or after taking broad-spectrum antibiotics. Yeast infections may affect men as well as women. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Vulvar pruritus 2. Labial soreness, irritation, and/or redness 3. If discharge is present, it may be increased typical (“normal”) discharge or thick and “cottage cheese-like” discharge without odor 4. May have dyspareunia (pain with intercourse) or dysuria (pain with urination) 5. Bright red “beefy” erythema around the labia and/or vulva and possibly excoriations 6. Vaginal vault may have increased or “cottage cheese” discharge 7. Cervical motion tenderness should be absent 8. Lab: Swab taken from vaginal vault and mounted on a slide with KOH shows branching yeast hyphae B. Treatment 1. Prescribe oral fluconazole (Diflucan®) single dose or topical clotrimazole (Lotrimin®) or miconazole (Monistat®) 2. Instruct patient to avoid tight-fitting clothing, pantyliners, and pantyhose 3. If patient is pregnant or has recurrent episodes (>2 episodes in 3 months or 3 or more episodes per year), CONTACT PHYSICIA

truct patient to avoid tight-fitting clothing, pantyliners, and pantyhose 3. If patient is pregnant or has recurrent episodes (>2 episodes in 3 months or 3 or more episodes per year), CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION 4. For males with candidal infection around scrotum, cleanse genitalia with soap and water and apply nystatin (Mycostatin®) cream twice daily II. CHANCROID- A Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) caused by Haemophilus ducreyi (gram negative bacillus). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period up to 1 month, but typically 4-10 days after exposure 2. Initially manifests as 1-2cm, painful, soft, ragged ulcer with necrotic base and surrounding erythema on penis, anus or genitalia 3. Progresses to multiple, localized lesions and painful, tender, unilateral lymphadenopathy 4. Lymph nodes may liquefy and rupture 5. Fever, chills and malaise are usually present with lymph node involvement CLINICAL NOTE: Although popularly recommended, the oral or vaginal use of active yogurt cultures has no demonstrated benefit. ACTION ALERT: Persistent Candidiasis may be a result of diabetes or HIV. Consider causes of immunocompromise in all recurrent cases.
gurt cultures has no demonstrated benefit. ACTION ALERT: Persistent Candidiasis may be a result of diabetes or HIV. Consider causes of immunocompromise in all recurrent cases.

CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION 2. Consider Azithromycin (Zithromax®) PO, erythromycin (E-Mycin®) PO or ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) IM 3. Perform needle aspiration or I&D on any swollen, tender, fluctuant lymph nodes 4. All STIs must be reported through military public health. CHLAMYDIA (NONGONOCCOCAL URETHRITIS OR “NGU”)- An acute or chronic inflammation of the urethra not caused by gonorrhea. NGU is usually caused by Chlamydia trachomatis but may be caused by mycoplasma or other bacteria. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Urethral discharge most often seen in the morning 2. Itching, dysuria, urgency and frequency, or may be asymptomatic 3. Irritated meatus with visible discharge 4. Males may have prostatitis or epididymitis 5. Prolonged infection in females may lead to infertility/sterility or pelvic inflammatory disease (see “Febrile Illness Protocols, Pelvic Inflammatory Disease”) 6. Lab: Check STI

tis or epididymitis 5. Prolonged infection in females may lead to infertility/sterility or pelvic inflammatory disease (see “Febrile Illness Protocols, Pelvic Inflammatory Disease”) 6. All STIs must be reported through military public health. Assist the public health office with partner contact/tracking and treatment as directed by the HMTF public health officer IV. ECTOPIC PREGNANCY- A fertilized egg that failed to implant in the uterus. May be located in the fallopian tube or in the abdominal cavity. Fetus can not survive due to poor placental support, but can grow into second trimester and pose a life-threatening risk to the mother as a result of internal damage and hemorrhage. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Symptoms typically develop 6-8 weeks after last menstrual period 2. Abdominal pain, rebound tenderness 3. Amenorrhea and vaginal bleeding 4. May mimic normal pregnancy such as frequent

and Symptoms 1. Symptoms typically develop 6-8 weeks after last menstrual period 2. Abdominal pain, rebound tenderness 3. Amenorrhea and vaginal bleeding 4. May mimic normal pregnancy such as frequent urination, nausea and fatigue 5. Unilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain with cervical motion tenderness 6. Lab: Check urine HCG for pregnancy B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Keep patient NPO

esions occur 3. Lesions appear as grouped clear vesicles over an erythematous base 4. Lesions crust and heal in 1-2 weeks ACTION ALERT: If unable to rule out testicular torsion or if patient appears septic, IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is warranted.

GENITAL WARTS (CONDYLOMA ACUMINATA)- A sexually transmitted infection caused by the human papilloma virus (HPV) that causes growths on the skin in the genital region. Patient frequently minimizes them or dismisses them as “normal” because they are nontender and grow slowly. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period 2-18 months after exposure 2. Single or multiple, soft, fleshy skin growths in the genital and perianal region 3. May be small or layered and cauliflower

ly. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period 2-18 months after exposure 2. Single or multiple, soft, fleshy skin growths in the genital and perianal region 3. May be small or layered and cauliflower-like 4. Pruritus may be present 5. Lab: Rule out condyloma lata of syphilis. RPR is indicated. Check STI panel (HIV, Hepatitis panel, GC, KOH and wet prep) and bacterial and viral cultures of any open wounds; check HCG in females if indicated B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider evacuation if severe and limiting ability to perform duties b. Consider cryotherapy, podofilox (Condylox®) or imiquimod (Aldara®) topical treatments 2. Examine and treat sexual partners 3. RPR/VDRL initially and at 3 months 4. Follow-up every 2 weeks. Refer for further evaluation if no improvement 5. Observe for secondary infection 6. All STIs must be reported through military public health. Assist the public health office with partner contact/tracking and treatment as directed by the HMTF public health officer VIII. GLOMERULONEPHRITIS, ACUTE- Glomerulonephritis is an acute kidney injury involving inflammation of the filtering unit of the nephron. In young, Active Duty populati

the HMTF public health officer VIII. GLOMERULONEPHRITIS, ACUTE- Glomerulonephritis is an acute kidney injury involving inflammation of the filtering unit of the nephron. In young, Active Duty population, the disorder is most often caused by an untreated streptococcal infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Hypertension, edema, and decreased urine output 2. Red to brown colored urine (hematuria) 3. Malaise, anorexia and headache

123 4. Lab: Macroscopic UA shows RBCs, protein, and possibly WBCs. Sediment often visible in sample. If a Basic Metabolic Panel (CHEM 7) is available, creatinine will most-likely be elevated B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Patient is at risk of permanent kidney failure b. Measure all I&O’s; goal output is >30cc/hr c. Establish IV access but do not administer fluid bolus unless directed by Preceptor 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If evacuation will be delayed, discuss food and fluid restrictions b. Consider diuretics such as furosemide (Lasix®) if patient is developing volume overload affecting respiration c. Monitor vitals at least every 30 minutes IX. GONORRHEA- A sexually transmitted infection caused by Neisseria gonorrheae (gram negative diplococcus). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period 2-8 days 2. Common cause of pelvic pain and sterility in females 3. May be asymptomatic in either sex 4. Men—burning on urination, serous or yellow, milky penile discharge; may have tender epididymis or prostate 5. Women—Vaginal itching, thick mucoid discharge; may present as painful urination, urinary frequency and urgency; inflamed cervix and increased vagina

ischarge; may have tender epididymis or prostate 5. Women—Vaginal itching, thick mucoid discharge; may present as painful urination, urinary frequency and urgency; inflamed cervix and increased vaginal discharge 6. Rectal infection can occur from anal sex or directly spread from vagina 7. Lab: Urethral/cervical swab testing and urine testing is available. Draw STI panel (HIV, RPR, Hepatitis panel, GC, KOH and wet prep); check HCG in females B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Consider ceftriaxone (Rocephin®) IM or cefixime (Suprax®) PO b. If allergic, consider high dose azithromycin c. Consider empirically treating for Chlamydia, since infections are clinically indistinguishable and co-infection is common 2. Contact and treat any partners suspected of exposure 3. All positive gonorrhea screens must be reported through military public health. Assist the public health office with partner contact/tracking and treatment as directed by the HMTF public health officer ACTION ALERT: If laboratory is not available or patient is a flight risk and clinical suspicion for gonorrhea is high, treat patient in clinic for both gonorrhea and Chlamydia.

124 X. HIV INFECTION- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Human Immunodeficiency Virus” XI. HYDROCELE- A fluid-filled sac near or surrounding a testicle that results in swelling of the scrotum. Can arise spontaneously or be related to trauma or infection. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Painless swelling of the scrotum 2. Mass can be transilluminated (bright glowing fluid-filled sac) with otoscope 3. May be painful if associated with epididymitis 4. Lab: Macroscopic UA if infection/STI is suspected B. Treatment 1. Stable, painless hydrocele does not require evacuation 2. Treat any coexisting genital infections 3. Increased scrotal support (cotton-lined athletic support) may reduce pull on swollen area 4. Persistent, symptomatic hydrocele requires surgery 5. If hydrocele is tense, it may compromise the circulation; IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is required 6. If there is an associated hernia, testicular mass or epididymitis, CONSULT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION for evaluation and treatment XII. MENSTRUAL IRREGULARITY/OLIGOMENORRHEA- Infrequent or missed periods may be caused by a myriad of conditions in the healthy active duty female population. A. General Considerations/Differentials 1. Pregnancy—C

L IRREGULARITY/OLIGOMENORRHEA- Infrequent or missed periods may be caused by a myriad of conditions in the healthy active duty female population. A. General Considerations/Differentials 1. Pregnancy—Check urine HCG; All patients with missed periods are presumed pregnant until proven otherwise 2. Ectopic pregnancy 3. Contraceptive use (especially Depo-Provera®, IUD, Implanon®, or Seasonale®) 4. Anorexia nervosa—Irregular menses, bradycardia, hypotension, arrhythmias, dry, cracked skin (especially around corners of the mouth), brittle hair and hair loss 5. Female athlete triad—Irregular menses, disordered eating, and stress fractures; most commonly found in gymnastics, ballet, figure skating and runners 6. Malabsorption syndromes—Celiac disease, ulcerative colitis, or Crohn’s disease 7. Bulimia nervosa 8. Thyroid Disease B. Treatment 1. Determine underlying cause 2. Determine weight gain or loss history through chart review if possible 3. Take actual weight and monitor regularly 4. Oligomenorrhea is common in Active Duty females, especially with increased exercise; however, oligomenorrhea and amenorrhea are suggestive of CLINICAL NOTE: Atypical sites of primary gonorrhea infection in

menorrhea is common in Active Duty females, especially with increased exercise; however, oligomenorrhea and amenorrhea are suggestive of CLINICAL NOTE: Atypical sites of primary gonorrhea infection include pharynx, eyes, septicemia, arthritis, meningitis or pneumonia.

Increase iron, calcium and fiber intake c. Take a daily multivitamin 6. Reevaluate patient’s weight, dietary history, and menstrual history in 1 month XIII. NEPHROTIC SYNDROME- Kidney damage resulting in leaking large amounts of protein from the blood into the urine. Defined as the triad of high protein in the urine, low albumin in the blood, and edema. Causes include allergy, diabetes, NSAID use, heroin abuse, HIV, obesity, lupus, hepatitis B/C, malignancy, and many more. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Peripheral edema due to loss of protein and low colloid osmotic pressure 2. Dyspnea from pulmonary edema, pleural effusions, and ascites 3. Lab: Macroscopic UA positive for protein. If chemistry analysis (iSTAT) is available, the comprehensive metabolic panel (CHEM 14) will show low albumin, low protein and high cholesterol/LDL/HDL B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Determine underlying cause, if possible b. Strict I&O’s c. Establish IV access; do not bolus patient unless directed to do so 2. C

tein and high cholesterol/LDL/HDL B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Determine underlying cause, if possible b. Strict I&O’s c. Establish IV access; do not bolus patient unless directed to do so 2. ORCHITIS- An inflammation of one or both testicles most commonly associated with the virus that causes mumps, but may also be caused by bacterial infections or STIs. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Testicle(s) will be diffusely tender with possible erythema and edema in or around the testicles 5. Lab: obtain macroscopic UA and STI panel (HIV, RPR, Hepatitis panel, GC, KOH and wet prep) B. Apply cold/hot compresses (cold compresses if due to mumps/warm compresses if due to bacterial infection) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Must rule out testicular torsion b. PREGNANCY- Defined as the period fom conception to birth when females carry developing offspring within the body. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Fatigue, nausea, and increased frequency of urination 2. Associated with amenorrhea and breast tenderness 3. May have bloating, lightheadedness, food cravings or aversions, mood changes, low back pain and heartburn 4. Lab: Urine HCG. If positive, or lower abdominal tenderness is present, evaluate for ectopic pregnancy B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION 2. Limited duty profile to avoid exposures to toxins and radiation 3. May continue to PT during first trimester, but if unsure of trimester, restrict PT and heavy lifting XVII. RENAL FAILURE, ACUTE- A sudden decrease in kidney function, resulting in an inability to maintain acid-base, fluid a

XVIII. PYELONEPHRITIS, ACUTE- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Pyelonephritis” XIX. RENAL FAILURE, ACUTE- A sudden decrease in kidney function, resulting in an inability to maintain acid-base, fluid and electrolyte balance and ability to excrete nitrogenous wastes. In the young, healthy active duty population is frequently caused by severe dehydration leading to rhabdomyolysis, but many other causes are possible such as medications, supplements, alcohol, severe infection and abnormal eating and drinking habits. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Decreased or absent urine output 2. Nausea, vomiting and malaise 3. Altered mental status 4. Abnormal blood pressure; in general, if patient is hypotensive the kidney damage is a result; if patient is hypertensive, the kidney malfunction is likely the cause 5. May have cardiac abnormalities (murmurs, gallops, rubs, or arrhythmias) 6. Peripheral edema CLINICAL NOTE: Patients may be unreliable historians (either intentionally deceptive or in denial about the reality of their condition). May mistake cramping and spotting associated with early pregnancy for menses, and fail to recognize a missed cycle. Consider sexual trauma in active duty women.

RHABDOMYOLYSIS- Severe muscle injury such as crush injury, extreme physical exertion or heat stress that causes release of muscle enzymes and myoglobin. Myoglobin is toxic to kidney tubules and can cause acute renal failure. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Muscle cramping and pain 2. Dark, red-brown urine 3. History of trauma or extreme physical exertion 4. Severe muscle injury can cause release of calcium which may cause seizures 5. Lab: Macroscopic UA appears bloody, but microscopic UA is negative for RBC’s. Serum creatine kinase (CK) if available, will be extremely elevated. If basic metabolic panel (CHEM 7) is available, the serum creatinine and BUN will identify the severity of renal injury B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish 2 large bore IV’s; Administer 2 Liters normal saline bolused followed by two more liters over 1 hour do not administer Lactated Ringer’s or any other types of fluid unless directed by a Preceptor b. Treat shock (See “Emergency Protocols

Painless papule that develops into an ulcer with clear base and firm margins b. Regional, painless, lymphadenopathy c. Painless ulcer usually located in genitalia, but may be in the pharynx d. Ulcer heals spontaneously without treatment 3. Secondary Syphilis a. Non

irm margins b. Regional, painless, lymphadenopathy c. Painless ulcer usually located in genitalia, but may be in the pharynx d. Ulcer heals spontaneously without treatment 3. Secondary Syphilis a. Nonpruritic maculopapular rash that includes the palms and soles b. Large gray/white lesions on mucous membranes (condyloma lata) c. Fever, headache, anorexia, sore throat and weight loss d. May occur any time after secondary syphilis and can involve any tissue in the body b. Most common sites for infection are skin, mucous membranes, skeletal system, eyes, respiratory system, GI, cardiovascular system and nervous system (neurosyphilis) c. rmed at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months or until not reactive 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Administer penicillin IM (Bicillin®) or PO (Pen-Vee K®) b. Painless enlargement of a testis or unilateral mass on the testis 2. Mass does not transilluminate, but may be accompanied by a hydrocele 3. Masses are assumed to be cancerous until proven otherwise B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION a. Acute onset of unilateral, severe diffuse testicular pain and tenderness 2. “High-riding” testicle (testicle is pulled up into the inguinal canal) 3. Swelling in the scrotal sac 4. Light headedness, nausea and vomiting 5. May present as severe lower abdominal pain B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE

Signs and Symptoms 1. Purulent, malodorous, thin discharge associated with vulvar burning, itching, dysuria, urinary frequency and dyspareunia 2. Erythema of the vulva and vaginal mucosa 3. Greenish-yellow frothy discharge may be seen 4. Punctate hemorrhages may be visible on the vaginal walls or cervix (aka “strawberry cervix”) 5. Males are frequently asymptomatic carriers 6. Lab: Swab taken of vaginal discharge and mounted on a slide with saline shows swimming organisms (do not confuse with sperm, which may be present up to 5 days after intercourse) 7.
