Administer single dose of metronidazole (Flagyl®) or tinidazole (Tindamax®) for patient and known sexual partners. Males continue treatment for 7 days. Repeat infections should be treated for 7 days b. Identify and treat all possible partners and refer patients to public health 2. All STIs must be repo

l partners. Males continue treatment for 7 days. Repeat infections should be treated for 7 days b. Identify and treat all possible partners and refer patients to public health 2. All STIs must be reported through military public health. Assist the public health office with partner contact/tracking and treatment as directed by the HMTF public health officer 3. Patients should avoid intercourse until 7 days after patient and partners have completed antibiotic therapy CLINICAL NOTES: Good pain management will be needed in order to attempt reduction. Consider administering Diazepam (Valium®) IV or IM in addition to narcotics to aid in relaxation of the spermatic cord. Be prepared to provide respiratory support when using combined narcotics and benzodiazepines. To reduce a right-sided torsion - gently rotate testicle counterclockwise one turn. To reduce a left-sided torsion - gently rotate testicle clockwise one turn. (Whether right or left torsion, if pain is increased by attempt at reduction, rotate in opposite direction). Testicular torsion is a true urological emergency. Immediate surgical evaluation is required. If pain has subsided (indicating a torsion/spontaneous de-torsion), ev

ion, rotate in opposite direction). Testicular torsion is a true urological emergency. Immediate surgical evaluation is required. If pain has subsided (indicating a torsion/spontaneous de-torsion), evacuation and immediate urological consultation is still required. ACTION ALERT: Warn patient about “Antabuse® effect” while taking Metronidazole (Flagyl®). Mixing alcohol with this medication can cause profound vomiting. Alcohol should not be consumed for at least 48 hours after completion of treatment

131 XXVI. URINARY TRACT CALCULI (“KIDNEY STONES”)- Crystals forming in the urinary system dislodge and move into the ureter causing obstruction and severe pain. Stones are caused by super-saturation of the urine with specific salts. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe unilateral flank pain 2. Difficulty staying still and staying quiet due to pain 3. Pain associated with nausea and vomiting 4. Episodic pain, radiating anteriorly to the abdomen. As the stone progresses down the ureter, pain may refer to the ipsilateral (same side) testicle or labia 5. Urinary urgency and frequency 6. Lab: Macroscopic UA showing RBCs and may show crystals; stones containing calcium may be visible on X-Ray ordered as “KUB” for “kidneys, ureters, and bladder” 7. Conclusive diagnosis is usually made with CT scan B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Monitor and record vital signs every 10 minutes while patient appears unstable b. Ensure adequate hydration. Start IV hydration if patient unable to tolerate PO fluids c. Provide analgesia: Acetaminophen (Tylenol®), ibuprofen (Motrin®) or Tylenol with codeine (Tylenol #3®) d. Provide treatment for nausea: Promethazine (Phenergan®) 25mg orally every 6 hours as neede

c. Provide analgesia: Acetaminophen (Tylenol®), ibuprofen (Motrin®) or Tylenol with codeine (Tylenol #3®) d. Provide treatment for nausea: Promethazine (Phenergan®) 25mg orally every 6 hours as needed e. If pain symptoms are mild and patient is tolerating oral intake patient may be monitored as an outpatient f. Strain all urine in an effort to recover stone for laboratory analysis g. Activity as tolerated h. Lab: Check Macroscopic UA; Check urine HCG if female 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. If patient is not tolerating oral intake, or is dehydrated, arrange for medical evacuation b. Preceptor may choose to force large volumes of fluids and will advise which fluids to use; otherwise Lactated Ringer’s, normal, or half normal saline may be used to ensure hydration at routine daily rate c. Monitor I&Os d. If unable to control pain with oral medications consider meperidine (Demerol®) or morphine IV e. If fever or signs of infection, initiate antimicrobial therapy. Consider trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®), or gentamycin (Garamycin®) CLINICAL NOTE: Suspect pyelonephritis in any patient with symptoms of a UTI accompanied with fever, flank pain, costovertebral ang

trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®), or gentamycin (Garamycin®) CLINICAL NOTE: Suspect pyelonephritis in any patient with symptoms of a UTI accompanied with fever, flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness or nausea/vomiting. CLINICAL NOTE: Many STIs also present with voiding symptoms as a chief complaint. Perform thorough sexual history and consider a pelvic exam in any female patient with dysuria. Consider prostate exam in male patients with dysuria.

132 XXVII. URINARY TRACT INFECTION/CYSTITIS- A bacterial infection of the lower urinary tract (below the level of the kidneys) to include the bladder and urethra. “Cystitis” specifically refers to irritation of the bladder. Note: painful urination can have other causes, but only urinary infection is addressed here. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dysuria, pyuria, urinary urgency and increased frequency 2. Sensation of incomplete void; nocturia 3. Absence of CVA tenderness (if present, see “Acute Febrile Illness Protocols, Pyelonephritis”) 4. Pyelonephritis and cystitis can both be present at the same time (upper and lower tract disease) 5. Lab: Macroscopic UA positive for WBCs, leukocyte esterase, bacteria and possibly RBCs B. Treatment 1. Increase PO fluid intake; patient should complete a full-bladder void every 1- 2 hours 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION a. Consider trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (“TMP-SMX”, Bactrim DS®, Septra®) or nitrofurantoin (Macrobid®) to treat infection b. Consider phenazopyridine (Pyridium®) for severe dysuria i. Warn patient that Pyridium® can stain all body fluids orange, including tears ii. patient should not wear contacts while ta

reat infection b. Consider phenazopyridine (Pyridium®) for severe dysuria i. Warn patient that Pyridium® can stain all body fluids orange, including tears ii. patient should not wear contacts while taking this medication due to potential staining effects c. Cranberry juice is popularly recommended, but this has only been shown to improve symptoms if it is the pure juice concentrate with no added sugar (only available at food specialty stores) and should not be recommended by the IDMT XXVIII. BACTERIAL VAGINOSIS- An overgrowth of normal vaginal anaerobic (gram negative rod) bacteria due to a decrease in vaginal lactobacilli. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. “Fishy smelling”, off-white, thin discharge most noticeable after coitus 2. Risk factors include multiple or new sexual partners (although the condition is not an STI), douching, cigarette smoking and sexual contact with other females 3. BV increases risk of acquiring STIs due to change in vaginal pH 4. Normal labia and vulva; vault with thin, homogenous “fishy smelling” grey discharge 5. Lab: Swab taken from vaginal wall cells (not discharge) and mounted on a slide with saline shows “clue cells” (vaginal wall cells studded with bacteria

n, homogenous “fishy smelling” grey discharge 5. Lab: Swab taken from vaginal wall cells (not discharge) and mounted on a slide with saline shows “clue cells” (vaginal wall cells studded with bacteria) B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR – MEDICATION a. Consider metronidazole (Flagyl®) PO or PV, clindamycin (Cleocin®) PO or PV b. If symptoms recur, treat sexual partners concurrently

133 c. Instruct patient to avoid tight-fitting clothing, pantyliners, and pantyhose d. If symptoms fail to resolve with antibiotics, consider diagnosis of trichomonas e. If symptoms recur more than once in a 6 month period, refer to HMTF for further evaluation XXIX. VARICOCELE- A dilation of the blood vessels of the spermatic cord supplying a testis. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pulling or aching unilaterally in the groin. Worse after prolonged standing 2. Patient may complain of “feeling something” inside the scrotal sac 3. Examination of the scrotal sac reveals a tortuous, dilated blood vessel commonly described as “bag of worms” B. Treatment 1. No treatment necessary unless pain is severe 2. Avoid “boxers” undergarments 3. May wear athletic support to decrease pulling sensation CLINICAL NOTE: Although popularly recommended, the oral or vaginal use of live yogurt strains has no demonstrated benefit.

134 Chapter 10: Endocrine Protocols XXIV. DIABETES MELLITUS- Impaired insulin secretion or sensitivitiy leading to hyperglycemia. There are two main types of diabetes mellitus, type 1 and type 2. Type one is referred to as juvenile onset and is characterized by the autoimmune destruction of beta islet cells in the pancreas leading to insulin deficiency. Type 2, or adult-onset diabetes describes a condition where insulin secretion is present, but cells are resistant to insulin effects. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Hyperglycemia (normal blood glucose is 60-100mg/dl; patients in diabetic ketoacidosis may have glucose readings over 500mg/dl) 2. Urinary frequency, polyuria, polyphagia, and polydipsia that progress to orthostatic hypotension and dehydration with weight loss 3. As dehydration worsens, weakness, fatigue and mental status changes occur 4. Type 2 diabetics may have symptoms of hyperglycemia, but more commonly are asymptomatic and the condition is discovered on routine physical exam B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Initiate IV and administer saline bolus if indicated b. Lab: check fingerstick glucose; check urine for glucose and ketones; check iSTAT Basic Metabolic Panel (CHEM

B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Initiate IV and administer saline bolus if indicated b. Lab: check fingerstick glucose; check urine for glucose and ketones; check iSTAT Basic Metabolic Panel (CHEM 7) if available c. Strict I&O’s 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXV. DIABETIC KETOACIDIOSIS (DKA)- Acute complication of diabetes characterized by hyperglycemia, hyperketonemia and metabolic acidosis. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Nausea, Vomiting, abdominal pain 2. Hyperglycemia 3. pH <7.3 (iSTAT) 4. All signs and symptoms of DM 5. Hypotension/tachycardia 6. Kussmaul’s respirations (air hunger, gasping breaths) B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access and administer infusion of 1-3L normal saline rapidly b. Check vital signs every 15 minutes while resuscitating c. Check blood glucose every 60 minutes while resuscitating d. After initial bolus, administer additional 3 L of NS over 1st 5 hours 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXVI. HYPOGLYCEMIA- Low blood glucose (<60mg/dL) that causes CNS dysfunction. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sweating, nausea, warmth, anxiety, palpitations, tremulousness, hunger and paresthesias 2. Headache, blurred v

CEMIA- Low blood glucose (<60mg/dL) that causes CNS dysfunction. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sweating, nausea, warmth, anxiety, palpitations, tremulousness, hunger and paresthesias 2. Headache, blurred vision, confusion, seizures and coma 3. BG<60mg/dL B. Treatment

135 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Administer oral glucose, juice, soda, or candy if conscious b. If patient is unable to drink or eat, administer glucagon 1mg, IM. OR c. 50-100 mL IV bolus of 50% glucose 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXVII. HYPOTHYROIDISM- A condition in which the thyroid gland does not make enough thyroid hormone, affecting patient’s homeostasis and metabolism. Treatment is based on correcting the underlying cause and/or replacing inadequate thyroid hormone. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Weight gain, fatigue, cold intolerance, arthralgias, myalgias, depression, menorrhagia, amenorrhea, hoarseness 2. Fingers or toes changing color and cramping when exposed to the cold (Raynaud’s syndrome) 3. Physical findings of dry skin, brittle hair, bradycardia, delayed deep tendon reflexes, thin/brittle nails, thinning hair (especially the outer half of eyebrows), peripheral edema, puffy face and eyelids, skin pallor or jaundice 4. Palpably enlarged thyroid (goiter) may be present 5. Note: All patients being evaluated for depression should have their thyroid function assessed prior to starting antidepressant therapy B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSUL

be present 5. Note: All patients being evaluated for depression should have their thyroid function assessed prior to starting antidepressant therapy B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION a. Patient requires lab evaluation of thyroid stimulating hormone b. Also needs to be evaluated for possible coexisting conditions XXVIII. HYPERTHYROIDISM- Caused by the thyroid gland secreting too much T3 and T4 hormone, affecting a patient’s homeostasis and metabolism. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Jitteriness, anxiety, palpitations, weakness, fatigue, weight loss, heat intolerance, loose stools, sweating nervousness, menstrual irregularities 2. Physical findings of warm moist skin, tachycardia, lid lag (whites are visible above iris as patient looks down), bulging eyes (exophthalmos), tremors 3. May have palpably enlarged thyroid B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYISICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND ROUTINE EVACUATION 2. Treatment typically involve treatments to suppress or destroy the thyroid gland or surgical removal 3. After destructive treatment, patient will require lifelong daily oral replacement of thyroid hormone CLINICAL NOTE: Life threatening hypothyroidism, calle

troy the thyroid gland or surgical removal 3. After destructive treatment, patient will require lifelong daily oral replacement of thyroid hormone CLINICAL NOTE: Life threatening hypothyroidism, called myxedema crisis, can occur. These patients have impaired cognition, confusion, or coma and peripheral edema. As with any cause of altered mental status, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION.

136 Chapter 11: Integumentary Protocols I. ACNE- A common inflammatory skin disease of unknown etiology with onset in puberty or delayed until 3rd or 4th decade of life. Factors include plugging of follicles, retention of sebum, overgrowth of Propionibacterium acnes and staphylococcal bacteria, irritation by accumulated fatty acids, and inflammatory reaction to extrafollicular sebum. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Soreness or pain from open or closed comedones (hallmark of the disease) 2. Papules, pustules, cysts or nodules 3. May occur over face, neck, upper chest, back or shoulders B. Treatment 1. Do not pick or manipulate lesions, it will result in scarring 2. Use only mild soaps (Ivory® or Cetaphil®, for example) on acne-prone area 3. Do not use “scrubbing” creams, “microbeads” or any other abrasive action on the acne 4. Do not wash face more than twice daily (including showering) 5. Do not use astringent washes on face unless they are part of prescribed regimen 6. If skin drying or flaking occurs, add hypo-allergenic facial moisturizer developed for acne prone skin 7. Apply sunscreen every day before leaving the house; use only mild hypo- allergenic preparations 8. Medications used

ing occurs, add hypo-allergenic facial moisturizer developed for acne prone skin 7. Apply sunscreen every day before leaving the house; use only mild hypo- allergenic preparations 8. Medications used for acne are based on the severity of the condition a. Comedonal Acne: Topical retinoids, start with tretinoin 0.25% cream (Retin-A®, Avita®)) topically applying a pea-sized amount over clean skin twice per week and slowly increasing until using nightly. If patient is unable to tolerate, may use adapalene gel 0.1% (Differin®) topically nightly. If no response to retinoid alone, may add Benzoyl peroxide 2.5%-5% daily b. If pustules, papules and/or nodules present and/or if acne is prominent chest and back, CONTACT PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION CLINICAL NOTE: The management of integumentary disorders will basically be the same in isolated environments as in other spheres. A good patient history is vital to determining cause and accurate diagnosis. ACTION ALERT: General eruptions often accompany or precede an anaphylactic reaction. A Timely yet complete evaluation of the patent is essential. Be prepared to take emergent actions for managing an anaphylactic episode. If patient becomes short of bre

recede an anaphylactic reaction. A Timely yet complete evaluation of the patent is essential. Be prepared to take emergent actions for managing an anaphylactic episode. If patient becomes short of breath or displays other signs of anaphylaxis, refer to “Emergency Protocols, Anaphylaxis” CLINICAL NOTE: Due to the nature of integumentary problems, they often lend themselves to telemedicine or emailing of digital photographs. Always ensure patient privacy and ensure that telemedicine consultation procedures are reviewed/approved by the host MTF.

137 early in the course of treatment for addition of topical and/or oral antibiotics II. CORNS- Corns are thickened outer layers of skin caused by friction and pressure (usually on the feet), and include a painful hyperkeratotic core. A. Signs and Symtpoms 1. Thickened skin at a pressure point; if patient has worn ill-fitting shoes to the clinic, may see an area of redness around the thickened skin 2. Painful growth or “corn” deep to the outermost skin layer 3. Overlying skin is smooth, unlike warts which have a bumpy, cauliflower-like texture; skin lines will be preserved B. Treatment 1. Debulk the corn with scalpel by keeping blade parallel to skin surface and gently scraping away successive layers of thickened skin (if black spots are visible in the inner layers of the lesion, consider diagnosis of warts) 2. Apply salacylic acid plaster 40%, cut to the size of the lesion 3. Leave plaster in place for 48-72 hours; keep plaster dry 4. Remove plaster, pare down whitened skin, and repeat 5. Patient may pare own skin and replace plaster every 24 hours until lesion has resolved C. Prevention 1. Avoid tight or ill-fitting footwear! 2. May use moleskin cut in ring to pad the areas aroun

t 5. Patient may pare own skin and replace plaster every 24 hours until lesion has resolved C. Prevention 1. Avoid tight or ill-fitting footwear! 2. May use moleskin cut in ring to pad the areas around the lesion to disperse the pressure applied to the corn 3. Keep feet clean and dry III. ECZEMA (ATOPIC DERMATITIS)- “Dry skin”. Starts as itchy vesicles and progresses to dry flaky eruption on the face, neck, trunk, and flexor surfaces of wrists, hands, and antecubital and popliteal fossae. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pruritic, rough, red plaques with thick flaky scale 2. Commonly affects the flexor surfaces of knees and elbows 3. If chronic, the skin is dry, leathery, and lichenified; in dark-skinned individuals, the affected area may be hypopigmented 4. During acute flares, may have widespread redness with weeping plaques 5. Severe cases also include cracking, fissuring and bleeding around plaques B. Treatment 1. Patient education is key! Medications are of limited value, patient must control the illness with their own behavior CLINICAL NOTE: Results of acne therapy may not be apparent for 6-12 weeks after initiating new treatment regimen. The condition often worsens for 1-4 weeks bef

trol the illness with their own behavior CLINICAL NOTE: Results of acne therapy may not be apparent for 6-12 weeks after initiating new treatment regimen. The condition often worsens for 1-4 weeks before improving. Every time changes are made, wait at least 8 weeks before altering therapy again. Patient must be counseled that developing a successful treatment takes several visits and several months and must keep their expectations realistic.

138 2. Start skin rehabilitation with bathing routine—immediately after showering or bathing, towel-dry skin and while it is still warm, apply copious amounts of Vaseline® to all affected areas 3. If bathing at night, recommend dedicating a pair of pajamas to wear after ointment treatments 4. Do not bathe more than once daily and handwashing should be minimized 5. Avoid exfoliating products such as washcloths and scrub brushes 6. Discontinue all deodorant or antimicrobial soaps; use only mild body saps such as Keri Soap® or Ivory® 7. Keep a portable tube of ointment in pocket; every time hands are washed (after using the restroom, after washing dishes, etc) immediately apply ointment to towel-dried hands. May also carry handkerchief to wipe off excess 8. If groin, trunk, or buttocks are affected, recommend cotton underwear 9. Avoid nylon or synthetic clothing 10. Use only mild laundry soaps without softeners or bleaches 11. For severe cracking of hands, recommend washing hands, towel drying, copious ointment, and then placing hands inside plastic gloves and placing gloved hands inside mittens or wool socks. The warmth and occlusion will enable the Vaseline® to deeply penetrate and

rying, copious ointment, and then placing hands inside plastic gloves and placing gloved hands inside mittens or wool socks. The warmth and occlusion will enable the Vaseline® to deeply penetrate and moisturize the skin, but patient should be warned that ointment will get runny and potentially make a mess 12. Counsel patients that lotions are water-based and will ultimately dehydrate the skin 13. Creams are more penetrating and effective than lotions and ointments are more penetrating and effective than creams; therefore ointments are treatment of choice 14. For pruritic (itching) lesions, apply small amount of topical corticosteroid cream or ointment such as hydrocortisone 1% or triamcinolone 0.1% twice daily; counsel patient to slowly taper off steroid cream but continue aggressive Vaseline® treatment 15. For acute, weeping lesions apply Burow’s solution (Domeboro®, Campho- Phenique®) solution or colloidal oatmeal dressings for 10-30 minutes 2-4 times per day; these treatments will soothe the skin and they act as an astringent to speed healing 16. Fingernails should be kept short to prevent excoriations and secondary infection 17. If patient has open breaks in the skin that will

oothe the skin and they act as an astringent to speed healing 16. Fingernails should be kept short to prevent excoriations and secondary infection 17. If patient has open breaks in the skin that will effect ability to perform duties (healthcare workers, food service workers, etc), consider duty profile 18. Secondary infections should be treated with systemic antibiotics such as dicloxacillin (Dynapen®) CLINICAL NOTE: Topical corticosteroids must be used with caution. Use only as long as needed and taper down. Only low potency steroids should be used on the face. Avoid steroids in folds of the skin and around the eyes. CLINICAL NOTE: Smallpox Vaccine is usually contraindicated in patients with eczema/atopic dermatitis. Consult your physician preceptor if smallpox vaccine is indicated in such an individual.

139 IV. CELLULITIS- Diffuse spreading infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Begins as small tender erythematous, edematous patch 2. Patch expands over several hours to days, with increased swelling, erythema and pain 3. Erythematous “streaks” away from lesion and/or regional lymphadenopathy are possible 4. As lesion spreads patient may develop fever, chills, malaise B. Treatment 1. If patient is hypotensive, start a large bore IV of NS or Ringer’s Lactate and consider diagnosis of septic shock (See “Emergency Protocols- Septic Shock”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. For mild infections: consider dicloxacillin (Dynapen®) or cephalexin (Keflex®) b. If “spider bite” appearance or possible MRSA-consider trimethoprim- sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®) or clindamycin (Cleocin®) c. For severe infections, consider IV antibiotics V. CHICKENPOX- A pox virus that produces characteristic rash largely eliminated in the United States due to vaccination. Spreads through contact with the fluid from direct contact with vesicles or from aerosolized nasal secretions from an infected individual. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period up to two weeks 2. P

reads through contact with the fluid from direct contact with vesicles or from aerosolized nasal secretions from an infected individual. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period up to two weeks 2. Prodrome of fever, malaise, pharyngitis, and loss of appetite occurring 24 hours before the appearance of the rash 3. Pruritic lesions start as macules, then progress to vesicles, that turn into pustules that crust 4. Vesicles have a very characteristic “dew drop on rosepetal” appearance with the vesicle in the center of the macule 5. Lesions typically are in different stages on the face, trunk, and extremities B. Treatment 1. If discomfort from itching is severe, administer oral antihistamines such as hydroxyzine hydrochloride (Atarax®) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) CLINICAL NOTE: Cellulitis is common on the lower legs, especially in patients that have chronic lower leg swelling due to venous insufficiency. Infection usually caused by Staphylococcal and Streptococcal infections (organisms that normally live harmlessly on the skin but can invade when the skin is weakened). ACTION ALERT: If systemic symptoms such as fever, lymphadenopathy, erythematous streaking from the wound, wound is

that normally live harmlessly on the skin but can invade when the skin is weakened). ACTION ALERT: If systemic symptoms such as fever, lymphadenopathy, erythematous streaking from the wound, wound is progressing rapidly, or patient appears ill, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION. If hypotension or other signs of sepsis are present, see “Emergency Protocols- Septic Shock”, start large bore IV, discuss IV antibiotics with Physician Preceptor, and arrange immediate evacuation.

140 2. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for fever 3. Do not give aspirin-containing analgesics, since these have been linked to potentially fatal Reye’s Syndrome 4. Cut fingernails very close to avoid potential for bacterial superinfection of lesions 5. Consider treatment with oral acyclovir (Zovirax®) within 24 hours of rash onset VI. CONTACT DERMATITIS- Acute or chronic dermatitis that results from direct skin contact with chemicals or allergens. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of exposure to allergen or irritating substance such as poison ivy/oak/sumac, topical medications, hair care products, nickel in jewelry, fragrances in perfumes or cosmetics, deodorants, preservatives, latex, rubber, adhesive tape, or detergents 2. Identifying the cause is key to prevent recurrence; history should include changes in soaps and detergents, location of lesions, occupation, and hobbies 3. Eruption of intensely pruritic erythematous popular or vesicular rash on the skin that was contacted; usually occurs hours after the exposure 4. Vesicles and bullae may rupture and cause oozing of serous fluid 5. Lesions due to direct contact with plant resins frequently occur on the hands, forearms, or face in line

hours after the exposure 4. Vesicles and bullae may rupture and cause oozing of serous fluid 5. Lesions due to direct contact with plant resins frequently occur on the hands, forearms, or face in linear lesions from brushing contact with stems or leaves 6. Plant resins on the hands may be transferred to other skin sites directly or by transferring the resin to fomites (touching towels, sheets, etc) 7. Patients may believe that oozing lesions are “spreading”, or causing new lesions to occur, though this usually does not happen. Instead, patients are probably touching a contaminated fomite with clean skin, causing new lesions B. Treatment 1. Remove all contaminated fomites and offending agents, if still present 2. Wash the contaminated area of skin thoroughly with mild detergent within 30 minutes of exposure 3. Immediately remove any contaminated clothing and wash thoroughly 4. If the affected area is small (less than 5% of body surface area), prescribe a topical corticosteroid such as Triamcinolone 0.1% cream (Kenalog® cream) applied sparingly to affected area 5. If wheals or itching are prominent, administer oral antihistamines such as hydroxyzine hydrochloride (Atarax®) or diphenh

riamcinolone 0.1% cream (Kenalog® cream) applied sparingly to affected area 5. If wheals or itching are prominent, administer oral antihistamines such as hydroxyzine hydrochloride (Atarax®) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) 6. If topical agents and antihistamines and are not effective or a larger area of skin is involved, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION and consider oral steroids such as prednisone (Deltasone®) CLINICAL NOTE: Warn patient about drowsiness when first taking Atarax® or Benadryl® and complete profile restricting from driving/hazardous duty if patient intends to take Atarax® during waking hours.

141 VII. DERMATITIS MEDICAMENTOSA- Skin rash caused by medication. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Vary based on medication; may be fine maculopapular, urticarial, or exfoliative 2. Usually occurs in a central or a sun-exposed distribution 3. Simple- occurs during 2nd week of therapy, no constitutional signs 4. Complex- occurs during 3rd week of therapy, has constitutional signs B. Treatment 1. Supportive therapy; treatment based on symptoms 2. Discontinue offending drug 3. Consider diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) or hydroxyzine (Atarax®) for urticarial lesions 4. If lesions are blistering and extensive, CONSULT PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION because patient may require inpatient burn unit care VIII. FOLLICULITIS- A superficial bacterial skin infection of the hair follicles that does not involve the deeper tissues. Folliculitis is caused by follicle inflammation and production of purulent material. Folliculitis is a superficial skin infection, whereas abscesses and furuncles extend into the deeper layers of the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Clusters of multiple small, raised, pruritic or burning erythematous papules less than 5 mm in diameter 2. Pustules may be present in the centers of the lesions 3

of the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Clusters of multiple small, raised, pruritic or burning erythematous papules less than 5 mm in diameter 2. Pustules may be present in the centers of the lesions 3. Folliculitis is often observed in areas that are hairy or frequently shaved 4. Differentiated from lesions of pseudofolliculitis, which are pustules caused by loose ends of shaved hair curling back to puncture and irritate the skin 5. Pseudomonas is one causative agent—patients with folliculitis caused by pseudomonas may have sharply demarcated areas of erythema following exposure to inadequately chlorinated hot tubs, whirlpools, or swimming pools B. Treatment 1. Warm compresses TID will usually resolve lesions regardless of causative organism 2. Avoid shaving in affected areas 3. Make sure all water sources that may be possible sources of pseudomonas are adequately treated 4. Avoid tanning booths, local pedicure providers, local saunas and swimming at local watering holes 5. For severe cases, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR to consider topical antibiotics such as erythromycin (E-Mycin®) IX. HERPES SIMPLEX VIRUS- Viral vesicular/ulcerative lesion of orolabial and genital areas caused by

cases, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR to consider topical antibiotics such as erythromycin (E-Mycin®) IX. HERPES SIMPLEX VIRUS- Viral vesicular/ulcerative lesion of orolabial and genital areas caused by herpesvirus (a member of the poxvirus family). CLINICAL NOTE: Medicamentosa is an adverse reaction to a medication but is not an “allergic reaction”. Avoid re-exposure. Common medications include NSAIDs, sulfa drugs, narcotics, and neuropsychiatric medications.

142 A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Burning and stinging of orolabial or genital area followed by eruption of small grouped vesicles on an erythematous base 2. Vesicles usually rupture and heal within 1 week 3. May have local lymphadenopathy B. Treatment 1. For orolabial herpes, oral medications usually are not required 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Genital herpes- For first clinical episode acyclovir (Zovirax®) or valacyclovir (Valtrex®) x 7-10 days; Recurrent genital herpes- valacyclovir (Valtrex®) twice daily x 3 days, or acyclovir (Zovirax®) 5 times daily x 5 days b. Orolabial herpes- For first clinical episode can give acyclovir (Zovirax®) or valacyclovir (Valtrex®) x 7-10 days X. HERPES ZOSTER- Acute viral vesicular eruption caused by reactivation of latent chickenpox infection. Usually occurs when patient is stressed, pregnant, or immunocompromised. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain along a dermatome followed by unilateral grouped vesicles along the same dermatome 3-7 days later 2. Most commonly on face and trunk 3. Vesicles are exquisitely tender, even to light touch such as sheets or clothing 4. Vesicles may rupture and ooze clear fluid 5. Up to 20 lesions may be foun

later 2. Most commonly on face and trunk 3. Vesicles are exquisitely tender, even to light touch such as sheets or clothing 4. Vesicles may rupture and ooze clear fluid 5. Up to 20 lesions may be found outside the affected dermatome 6. May have regional lymphadenopathy B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Use systemic antivirals in patients >age 50 or with moderate to severe pain or rash b. Goal of antivirals is not to cure the outbreak; the goal is to reduce the severity of nerve damage and chronic pain (postherpetic neuralgia) c. Acyclovir (Zovirax®) or valacyclovir (Valtrex®) x 7 days d. Consider oral steroids such as prednisone for 3 weeks with taper e. Treat pain with appropriate analgesic for level of pain (may require narcotics); preceptor may prefer to treat with neuropathic pain medications (tricyclic antidepressants or gabapenting, for example) f. If lesions resolve and patient still has pain in the area, patient probably has postherpetic neuralgia; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION for appropriate pain control 2. Symptoms usually lasts 2-3 weeks and does not recur CLINICAL NOTE: Compliance is better with valacyclovir (Valtrex®) because fewer d

ACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION for appropriate pain control 2. Symptoms usually lasts 2-3 weeks and does not recur CLINICAL NOTE: Compliance is better with valacyclovir (Valtrex®) because fewer doses are required per day. To be most effective treatment needs to be started at first onset of symptoms (during burning or stinging, before lesions even appear). Topically applied medicines are not very effective. Encourage patient to use sunscreen, which may prevent sun-induced recurrences.

143 3. May consider Calamine® lotion topically 4. If lesions are on forehead, eyelid, or anterior scalp, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION for Ophthalmology consult 5. Prevention-Vaccine (age 60 and up) XI. IMPETIGO- A superficial skin infection caused by broken skin in the presence of colonizing gram positive cocci such as Staphylococcus or Streptococcus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Clusters of vesicles, bullae, or pustules that rupture and develop thick, adherent, honey-colored granular crusts over the lesion 2. Lesions are typically fluid-filled and surrounded by erythema 3. Vesicular fluid typically starts as clear yellow and becomes darker and more turbid as lesions approach rupturing 4. Risk factors include warm humid conditions with overcrowding and poor hygiene B. Treatment 1. If lesions are limited and without bullae, consider mupirocin (Bactroban®) topically 2. If lesions are bullous or more widespread, consider oral erythromycin (E- Mycin®) 3. Do not delay treatment since lesions can progress into cellulitis, lymphangitis, furunculosis, hyper/hypopigmentation, and scarring 4. Emphasize handwashing due to easy transmission from contaminated hands 5. If lesion

ment since lesions can progress into cellulitis, lymphangitis, furunculosis, hyper/hypopigmentation, and scarring 4. Emphasize handwashing due to easy transmission from contaminated hands 5. If lesions develop abscesses, I&D (incise and drain) the lesion and consider clindamycin (Cleocin®) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®) due to possible superinfection with MRSA XII. INGROWN TOENAIL- Curving in or impingement of a nail border into its adjacent nail fold, causing pain and swelling. Area may become infected. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Causes include tight shoes, abnormal gait, bulbous toe shape, and cutting nails too short or too rounded 2. Pain occurs at the corner of the nail fold or along the lateral margin 3. Exam shows swollen, tender posterior or lateral nail fold that may also be draining or collecting purulent fluid and/or granulation tissue CLINICAL NOTE: This is a recurring disorder, and the antivirals work better the earlier the prescription is started. For these reasons it is advisable to give the patient a 5 day supply of acyclovir (Zovirax®) or valacyclovir (Valtrex®) after symptoms resolve to be initiated at the first sign of recurrence. This is particularly i

ons it is advisable to give the patient a 5 day supply of acyclovir (Zovirax®) or valacyclovir (Valtrex®) after symptoms resolve to be initiated at the first sign of recurrence. This is particularly important if the patient is traveling to a remote location. CLINICAL NOTE: Even though impetigo skin disease is relatively minor, patients are at risk for poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis leading to kidney failure, and also rheumatic fever leading to fevers, cardiac problems, blindness, and death. Have high suspicion for these conditions in any patient that presents with malaise following impetigo infection.

144 B. Treatment 1. Treatment is based on the extent of the disease; attempt nonsurgical methods first (choose one or more of the below treatments): a. Warm water soaks 10-20 minutes with or without Epsom salts three times a day Instruct patient to push the lateral nail fold gently away from nail plate after each soak. Perform first treatment in clinic b. Insert small amount of cotton removed from a cotton ball or cotton- tipped applicator (Q-Tip®) or dental floss between the ingrown nail and the nail fold. If this is too painful, place cotton under the unaffected portion of the distal nail to help prop up the ingrowing corner c. If pustule is present, consider incision and drainage (see “Abscess” for procedure description) 2. Most ingrown nails will resolve with the above treatments and patience XIII. LEISHMANIASIS- Skin or systemic infection caused by Leishmania, a protozoan group of parasites transmitted by sandfly bites. Commonly seen in Iraq, Afghanistan, and other arid desert regions. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Cutaneous: Single or multiple cutaneous swellings that start as small papules and develop into nonulcerated dry plaques or large encrusted ulcers a. Ulcers are well dema

ions. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Cutaneous: Single or multiple cutaneous swellings that start as small papules and develop into nonulcerated dry plaques or large encrusted ulcers a. Ulcers are well demarcated raised and indurated margins b. Lesions are painless unless secondarily infected c. Localized lymphadenopathy is common, but systemic symptoms are uncommon d. Healing occurs spontaneously in months to a few years with significant scarring 2. Mucocutaneous (espundia): Nasal congestion followed by ulceration of mouth, lips, palate, pharynx and larynx. a. Occurs months or years after resolved cutaneous lesions b. Fatality >90% if left untreated, primarily from hemorrhage and superinfection with other organisms 3. Visceral (kala azar): Nonulcerating nodule at site of bite may precede systemic symptoms but is usually not apparent a. Fever, chills, sweats, weakness, anorexia, weight loss, cough and diarrhea b. Splenomegaly, +/- Liver enlargement and lymphadenopathy c. Hyperpigmentation of skin d. Skin lesions, petechiae, gingival bleeding, jaundice, edema and ascites e. Progression causes wasting and malnutrition and death due to secondary infections B. Treatment CLINICAL NOTE: For se

skin d. Skin lesions, petechiae, gingival bleeding, jaundice, edema and ascites e. Progression causes wasting and malnutrition and death due to secondary infections B. Treatment CLINICAL NOTE: For severe nails refractory to the measures above, consider partial or full excision of the nail. Oral antibiotics typically not indicated, even if pus is present. Perform local I&D instead.

Evaluation requires skin biopsies and specialist consultation with Dermatologist and/or Infectious Disease specialist; treatment includes IV medications XIV. LYMPHANGITIS- Inflammation of the lymphatic channels or regional lymph nodes caused by infection with bacteria, mycobacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites. Often accompanied by lymphadenitis, or painful swollen regional lymph nodes. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Throbbing pain in area of cellulitis with red streak in skin extending toward regional lymph nodes 2. Local lymphadenopathy 3. Malaise, anorexia, sweating, chills, and fever may develop rapidly 4. Lab: CBC-Shows elevated WBC count; get blood cultures if available B. Treatment 1. Use moist heat compresses, elevation, and immobilize infected area 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION a. Patient may require IV antibiotics and inpatient admission b. Consider coverage for MRSA such as trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®) or clindamycin (Cleocin®) XV. LYME DISEASE- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Lyme Disease” XVI. for MRSA such as trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®) or clindamycin (Cleocin®) XV. LYME DISEASE- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Lyme Disease” XVI. MOLLUSCUM CONTAGEOSUM- Papular skin condition of groin, inner thighs, and lower abdomen caused by a poxvirus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Single or multiple small, smooth, umbilicated papules on penis, pubis, inner thighs, face, or lower abdomen 2. Lesions have characteristic dome shape with central ubilication (looks like a papule with a belly button) CLINICAL NOTE: Prevention is KEY! Use insect repellants, fine insect netting, long sleeves and pants. Continuously revaluate disease control measures where leishmaniasis is endemic. Consider large-scale prevention measures such as destruction of animal reservoir, mass treatment for personnel in disease-prevalent areas, pesticide spraying in dwellings, etc. Counsel personnel to avoid contact with dogs and other domesticated animals in high-risk areas. ACTION ALERT: REASONS TO CONTACT PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE CONSULTATION: 1. Masses with associated soft tissue infection will likely require systemic antibiotics 2. Multiple new lesions associated with systemic signs and symptoms will likely re

TOR- IMMEDIATE CONSULTATION: 1. Masses with associated soft tissue infection will likely require systemic antibiotics 2. Multiple new lesions associated with systemic signs and symptoms will likely require systemic antibiotics 3. 146 3. Lesions may have surrounding erythema, scale, and pruritus 4. Transmitted by skin-to-skin contact in wrestlers or during intimacy 5. Wrestlers may have lesions on defensive areas such as forearms and thighs 6. Can continuously auto-inoculate (patient spreads it to other areas themselves) through direct contact of lesions with nearby areas of moistened or sweaty skin 7. Expose the whole patient and identify/treat all lesions B. Treatment 1. Two treatment options: a. Apply liquid nitrogen to each lesion for 5-10 seconds being careful not to contact any of the surrounding normal skin b. Using a curettage instrument, cauterize each lesion being careful not to contact any of the surrounding normal skin 2. Regardless of treatment method chosen, be sure to treat all lesions to avoid autoinoculation XVII. May be asymptomatic or have intense pruritus 2. Careful examination includes gentle combing wet hair with a fine-toothed comb near the crown and observing for insects 3. Entire head or area should be combed twice 4. Nits (la

ave intense pruritus 2. Careful examination includes gentle combing wet hair with a fine-toothed comb near the crown and observing for insects 3. Entire head or area should be combed twice 4. Nits (larvae) fluoresce pale blue with Wood’s lamp 5. Body lice infestations not likely to be found by examining the skin—instead check in seams of clothing 6. Insects can survive on fomites such as headphones, towels, and bedding for days or longer B. Treatment 1. Pediculosis Capitis a. Permethrin 1% (Nix®) cream, apply to head/scalp and leave on for 10 minutes, then rinse off b. Comb out nits c. Eyelashes treated with petrolatum jelly (Vaseline®) twice a day x 8 days and then nits plucked off d. Dispose of infested hats and headgear 2. Pediculosis Corporis a. Dispose of infested clothing b. If nits are seen on hair shafts treat with Permethrin cream 5% (Nix®) leave on for 8 hours, then rinse off 3. Pediculosis Pubis a. Apply Permethrin 1% cream (Nix®) to affected area for 10 minutes and then rinse off b. Sexual contacts should also be treated ACTION ALERT: If there is any evidence of secondary or systemic infection, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE CONSULTATION.

147 4. For all forms of pediculosis, wash all towels and bedding in boiling water and dry with high heat (see below) XVIII. PSEUDOFOLLICULITIS BARBAE- Inflammation around a hair follicle where a curly hair shaft folds over and re-enters the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Inflammation around the embedded hairs results in firm, skin colored or erythematous papules that become irritated from shaving 2. Differentiated from acne by noting that lesions are adjacent to hair follicle, not arising from the follicle 3. Pustules develop as disease progresses B. Treatment 1. Grow beard (requires profile to allow growth of 1/8”) 2. Instruct patient to clip/shave in direction of hair growth, not against it 3. Encourage patient to use warm cloths before shaving to open skin pores 4. May use buff puff pad in circular motions to release trapped hairs prior to clipping 5. If no improvement, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION about possible treatments with retinoids, topical steroids or topical antibiotics XIX. PSORIASIS- A benign, chronic inflammatory skin condition that may also involve the nails and joints. A. Signs and Symptoms CLINICAL NOTE: Do not allow patient to wash usi

s or topical antibiotics XIX. PSORIASIS- A benign, chronic inflammatory skin condition that may also involve the nails and joints. A. Signs and Symptoms CLINICAL NOTE: Do not allow patient to wash using other shampoos or conditioners prior to treatment because they may reduce the efficacy of medications. CLINICAL NOTE: Wash and dry all clothes and bedding at high temperatures (ie boiling water and high heat dryer). If it is not possible to wash and dry with high heat, then store all bedding and clothes in a plastic bag for two weeks and this should be long enough to starve all lice and nits. Examine all household contacts and treat as necessary. ACTION ALERT: If signs or symptoms of systemic infection are present contact physician preceptor. CLINICAL NOTE: Educating the patient is critical regarding proper release of hair shaft. Advise patient not to pluck/tweeze ingrown hairs. Use only sharp razors. May use depilatories (Magic Shave®) but these often cause extensive irritation. Education regarding depilatories: do use depilatory within 36 hours after shaving, use sensitive skin formulation for initial use, and apply only to small skin area for first use to monitor for irritation.

ion regarding depilatories: do use depilatory within 36 hours after shaving, use sensitive skin formulation for initial use, and apply only to small skin area for first use to monitor for irritation. Discontinue use if skin irritation occurs.

148 1. Silvery scales on bright red, well demarcated plaques with varying degrees of pruritus 2. Usually located on extensor surfaces of elbows, knees, sacral region and scalp and occasionally on palms and soles 3. Examine patient’s nails; if nail pitting is present it is suggestive of psoriasis 4. Ask about joint pain, joint stiffness, and complete a full review of systems questioning about systemic disease B. Treatment 1. If scalp is involved, use coal tar shampoo (Tarsum®) daily 2. Exposing involved skin to sunlight is effective, but be careful to avoid sunburn because skin is already damaged 3. If lesions are present on more than 10% body surface area, if symptoms persist more than a month despite treatment, or if any systemic or joint involvement is present, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION XX. ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever” XXI. SCABIES- A skin infection caused by the insect Sarcoptes scabiei. Causes intense pruritus, which comes from deposition of feces and eggs from mites travelling underneath the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Small erythematous nondescript papule that are usually excoriated and tipped w

uritus, which comes from deposition of feces and eggs from mites travelling underneath the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Small erythematous nondescript papule that are usually excoriated and tipped with hemorrhagic crusts 2. Small vesicles or pustules may be present 3. “Burrows” may be seen- thin, grey, red, or brown lines several mm long; burrows are frequently obscured by excoriation 4. Lesions are typically located in web spaces, heels of palms, wrist flexors, elbows, axillae, waistline, and groin 5. Patient typically describes intense itching worse at night out of proportion to exam findings 6. Transmission is from skin-to-skin direct contact with infected individual; in young adults transmission is usually through sexual contact B. Treatment 1. Consider topical permethrin cream (Nix®) or oral ivermectin (Stromectol®) 2. Itching typically persists several weeks after eradication of infection; nighttime itching can be controlled by 1st generation antihistamines such as diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) or hydroxyzine (Atarax®); daytime itching ACTION ALERT: if joint pain and skin findings are both present at the same time, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION. Patient requires wor

ine (Benadryl®) or hydroxyzine (Atarax®); daytime itching ACTION ALERT: if joint pain and skin findings are both present at the same time, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION. Patient requires workup for psoriatic arthritis and prompt treatment to prevent progression and possibly permanent joint destruction. CLNICAL NOTE: This disorder carries significant psychosocial impact; patients are often very self-conscious about lesions, especially on the scalp and face. Medications that exacerbate lesions include β-blockers, anti-malarial antibiotics, statins, lithium and oral steroids.

149 can be controlled by 2nd generation antihistamines such as loratadine (Claritin®) or cetirizine (Zyrtec®), but do not give more than one antihistamine 3. Localized itching can be treated with topical hydrocortisone cream (Westcort®) 4. Mites cannot survive more than 3 days away from skin; wash all clothing and bedding that has been used in the past 3 days by machine washing in hot water and drying in hot dryer. If that is not possible, fabrics may also be stored in a tightly sealed plastic bag for 14 days and then washed by whatever means available XXII. SEBORRHEIC DERMATITIS- Dry scaling skin with underlying erythema involving the head, scalp, or face. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dry scales or oily yellowish scales commonly on the scalp, face, nasolabial folds, between the eyes, and on the eyebrows and eyelid margins 2. Can also occur on the chest, back, umbilicus and body folds 3. Has varying degrees of pruritus (itchiness) B. Treatment- based on location 1. Scalp a. Treat with shampoos containing zinc (Denorex®) or selenium (Head and Shoulders®, Selsun Blue®) used daily b. Two days a week, treat with ketoconazole shampoo (Nizoral®) left in for at least 5 minutes instead of the

poos containing zinc (Denorex®) or selenium (Head and Shoulders®, Selsun Blue®) used daily b. Two days a week, treat with ketoconazole shampoo (Nizoral®) left in for at least 5 minutes instead of the zinc or selenium c. Rotating shampoos is often helpful 2. Face a. Hydrocortisone 1% used intermittently (small amount every other day and avoid area around eyes) b. If no improvement add ketoconazole 2% cream (Nizoral®) twice daily 3. Eyelids a. Gentle cleansing nightly with warm compresses b. May also use diluted baby shampoo applied with a cotton swab and rinsed thoroughly 4. Intertriginous areas (skin folds) a. Hydrocortisone 1% applied twice daily for 5-7 days b. Once improved, can drop down to once weekly for maintenance XXIII. SKIN ABSCESSES AND FURUNCLES- “Abscess” describes any collection of pus within the dermis and deeper skin tissues. “Furuncles” are related to hair follicles and are a deep- CLINICAL NOTE: Excoriated lesions are at high risk for superinfection including cellulitis, impetigo, etc. Suspect superinfection if lesions have surrounding erythema, edema, or are weeping (scabies lesions are typically dry with excoriations that have bleeding and scabbing). is, impetigo, etc. Suspect superinfection if lesions have surrounding erythema, edema, or are weeping (scabies lesions are typically dry with excoriations that have bleeding and scabbing). Also a high risk of spread to household; treat all family members/contacts.

150 seated pustular infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus involving the entire hair follicle and adjacent subcutaneous tissue. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Common on hairy skin exposed to irritation, friction, pressure or moisture 2. Pain, erythema, warm skin surrounding rounded or conical abscess 3. Gradually enlarges, becomes fluctuant and then softens 4. May spontaneously open and drain serosanguinous or pustular fluid 5. Regional lymphadenopathy and streaking away from wound may occur 6. Lab: Culture fluid draining from abscess if possible B. Treatment 1. Antibiotics will not penetrate a closed abscess; incision and drainage is required 2. Incision and drainage procedure: a. Obtain written consent b. Clean area with topical chlorhexidine (Hibiclens®) if available or povidone-iodine (Betadine®) preparation and allow to dry c. With shallow approach, inject wound just underneath top layer of skin with 1% lidocaine (Xylocaine®) with epinephrine (do not use epinephrine on fingers, toes, ears, nose, or near genitalia or mucous membranes). Insert needle through the ulceration if lesion is open. Inject lidocaine at least 5 mm past furthest edge of erythema in all directions d. Cleanse

ears, nose, or near genitalia or mucous membranes). Insert needle through the ulceration if lesion is open. Inject lidocaine at least 5 mm past furthest edge of erythema in all directions d. Cleanse area again with povidone iodine (Betadine®) preparation e. Drape area with sterile drape or dressing, if available f. Check anesthetized area with something sharp; patient may feel pressure but should not feel sharp or pricking pain g. Use sterile blade to create an incision approximately ½ the diameter of the pustular portion of the lesion h. Apply firm pressure to margins of lesion and attempt to express pus and debris i. Explore inside of lesion with blunt probe and break up any septations (walls) j. Pack lesion with wick material, leaving at least 1 inch of packing outside the skin k. Draw line with permanent marker around margin of erythema l. Cover open wound and wick with sterile dressing m. Follow patient daily for wound management/repacking and to redraw line around the erythema to make sure it is not advancing n. May discontinue packing when minimal to no drainage occurs over 24 hour interval and cavity is minimized CLINICAL NOTE: If using an analgesic that includes epinephri

o make sure it is not advancing n. May discontinue packing when minimal to no drainage occurs over 24 hour interval and cavity is minimized CLINICAL NOTE: If using an analgesic that includes epinephrine, the numbing effect will be anywhere that is blanched by the epinephrine in the solution. This makes it easy to see visually where the patient will be numb. Unforutnately, pain control will be sub-optimal in infected areas due to neutralizing effect of pus on lidocaine.

151 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION a. Treat patient with antibiotic that kills MRSA b. Consider clindamycin (Cleocin®) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim®) XXIV. SMALLPOX- A viral illness caused by variola, a poxvirus. The virus is very hardy and can survive for long periods away from a host. Although it was globally eradicated in nature, it remains a concern since it has the potential to be weaponized. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Incubation period of 10-14 days; followed by sudden onset high fever, severe headache, backache, malaise, and vomiting 2. After 2-4 days, symptoms progress to tiny red spots on the tongue and palate ACTION ALERT- CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is required if erythema extends past permanent marker line, there are new erythema streaks spreading away from wound, if patient develops fevers, or if any signs of hemodynamic instability/shock. CLINICAL NOTE: How to complete informed consent for incision and drainage: 1. Type of procedure: incision and drainage of skin abscess 2. Why procedure is necessary: remove infected debris from wound 3. Risks of procedure: pain, bleeding, reaction to anesthesia, infection may not resolve,

edure: incision and drainage of skin abscess 2. Why procedure is necessary: remove infected debris from wound 3. Risks of procedure: pain, bleeding, reaction to anesthesia, infection may not resolve, risk of repeat or additional procedures, poor cosmetic outcome (scarring) 4. Risk of not doing the procedure: spread of infection in skin or into the blood 5. Type of anesthesia to be used (including strength and with or without epinephrine. Example: “2 mL of 1% lidocaine with epinephrine” 6. Patient’s statement of his known allergies and that he/she does not have a known allergy to latex, iodine, lidocaine, or the medications you plan to prescribe. Example: “Patient denies food, drug, and contact allergies” or “Patient denies known allergies to latex, lidocaine, and clindamycin; has received procaine (Novocaine®) for dental procedures without adverse reaction” 7. Your signature/date 8. Patient signature/date 9. Witness signature/date CLINICAL NOTE: Example procedure note for incision and drainage: “Written consent obtained and placed in patient chart. Area was cleansed with betadine solution. Abscess was injected in all directions with 2 mL of 1% lidocaine with epinephrine. Area of an

drainage: “Written consent obtained and placed in patient chart. Area was cleansed with betadine solution. Abscess was injected in all directions with 2 mL of 1% lidocaine with epinephrine. Area of anesthesia extended 5 mm past visible erythema. Area was prepped and draped in sterile fashion. A 3-mm incision was made directly over pustule. Applied pressure yielded 2 mL of thick debris. Wound was explored with blunt probe and septations were broken. 5 inches of iodoform (Invacare®) gauze was packed into wound, leaving a 1-inch wick. Wound was dressed with sterile dressing. Patient tolerated procedure well. No complications. Estimated blood loss: less than 5 mL.”

152 3. Approximately 24 hours after mucosal rash appears, the oral lesions evolve into papules and vesicles and the skin develops a maculopapular red rash 4. As the rash evolves, areas develop papules, then vesicles, then pustules; the pustules eventually rupture and crust 5. Smallpox lesions typically all evolve at the same rate; this can be differentiated from Chickenpox since in Chickenpox there are usually separate “crops” of lesions and older crusted lesions will be present while new vesicles and pustules arise 6. Lesions can also occur on the palms and soles B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing,circulation, O2, monitor b. Immediate isolation including respiratory and contact precautions c. Limit number of persons exposed to the patient d. Establish IV access and provide maintenance fluids if patient is unable to tolerate PO 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. There are no FDA approved treatments for smallpox 4. Provide supportive care as indicated XXV. SUBUNGUAL HEMATOMA- Blood under fingernail/toenail from trauma to distal phalanx caused by bleeding from nail bed. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Throbbing pain 2. Blue-black discoloration under t

ated XXV. SUBUNGUAL HEMATOMA- Blood under fingernail/toenail from trauma to distal phalanx caused by bleeding from nail bed. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Throbbing pain 2. Blue-black discoloration under the nail as the hematoma progresses 3. Pain is from pressure in confined space and pressing against nerve fibers B. Treatment 1. Pain is relieved immediately for most patients by making a hole in the nail 2. Procedure a. Obtain written consent b. Restrain digit as needed c. Clean the nail with povidone iodine (Betadine®) swabs using sterile technique d. Isopropyl alcohol is highly flammable and should be avoided if electrocautery is used e. Puncture the nail with a hot metal wire (an electrocautery device, for example) i. The heated device is pressed against the nail in the center of the hematoma while avoiding the lunula and its associated nail matrix ii. Care should be taken to prevent contact with the nail bed f. The hole should be large enough (three to four millimeters) for continued drainage that may occur for 24 to 36 hours after the injury 3. Alternative procedure a. Twirl a needle on the nail until blood is expressed through the hole in the nail
rainage that may occur for 24 to 36 hours after the injury 3. Alternative procedure a. Twirl a needle on the nail until blood is expressed through the hole in the nail

153 b. If a small bore needle (23 gauge) is used, then multiple holes should be placed to reduce the chance of plugging and hematoma re- accumulation c. If a large bore needle (18 gauge) is used, then a single three to four millimeter hole should suffice XXVI. TINEA CAPITIS/CORPORIS/PUBIS/PEDIS- Fungal infection of head, body, groin, or feet. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Tinea Capitis a. Three distinctly different forms, "gray patch," "black dot" and “favus” b. Most common is black dot, which is an erythematous, scaling patch on the scalp. It slowly enlarges, usually unnoticed until alopecia (hair loss) develops c. Hair shafts break even with skin giving black dot appearance d. Without treatment tinea capitis may result in permanent alopecia 2. Tinea Corporis (“ring worm”) a. Ring shaped plaque with advancing scaly border and central clearing b. Lesions are usually on exposed areas of body (face or arms) 3. Tinea Cruris (“jock itch”) a. Erythematous patch in intertriginous areas b. Scaly, spreading, and sharply demarcated border with centrally clearing c. Marked pruritus on inner thighs and along groin lines d. Lesions usually confined to groin and gluteal cleft 4. Tinea Pedis a. Itchi

spreading, and sharply demarcated border with centrally clearing c. Marked pruritus on inner thighs and along groin lines d. Lesions usually confined to groin and gluteal cleft 4. Tinea Pedis a. Itching, burning, stinging of interdigital webs between toes b. Scaling and cracking of soles (or palms—in tinea manuum) CLINICAL NOTE: Do not put a hole in the nail if you suspect a distal phalanx fracture. If fracture is present, then by putting a hole in the nail you have created an open fracture. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if you suspect any fractures in the hand. CLINICAL NOTE: How to complete informed consent for subungual hematoma drainage: 1. Type of procedure: drainage of subungual hematoma 2. Why procedure is necessary: to decrease pressure under nail and decrease pain 3. Risks of procedure: infection, pain, scarring, bleeding, risk of repeat or additional procedures, causing a closed fracture to become an open fracture, risk of reaction to anesthesia 4. Risk of not doing the procedure: increase in pain, nail may fall off, permanent damage to nail bed may occur, pressure on nerves may cause permanent nerve damage 5. Patient’s statement of his known allergies and tha

ng the procedure: increase in pain, nail may fall off, permanent damage to nail bed may occur, pressure on nerves may cause permanent nerve damage 5. Patient’s statement of his known allergies and that he/she does not have a known allergy to latex or iodine 6. Your signature 7. Patient signature 8. Witness signature CLINICAL NOTE: Example procedure note for drainage of a subungual hematoma: “Nail and folds were cleansed with povidone iodine (Betadine®) solution and allowed to dry. Area was draped with sterile dressing. Electrocautery instrument was applied to nail directly over the hematoma and held in place for 4 seconds, creating a 3 mm hole in the nail. Nail spontaneously drained 3 mL of dark blood. Patient felt immediate relief of pain and pressure. Nail was dressed with sterile dressing and finger was wrapped in gauze to prevent additional trauma. Patient tolerated procedure well without complications. Estimated blood loss: less than 5 mL.”

154 c. May have skin breakdown between digits 5. Lab: May be confirmed by KOH prep (hyphae) or culture; KOH may not be positive if skin is macerated in web spaces; spores identified on hair shafts (capitis) B. Treatment 1. Tinea Capitis a. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION i. Consider oral griseofulvin (Gris-Peg®) treat for 6-12 weeks ii. Avoid topical agents due to high incidence of treatment failure b. Lesions should be kept clean and dry 2. Tinea Corporis a. Miconazole (Monistat®) 2% cream twice daily until cleared plus 1 week, OR b. Clotrimazole (Lotrimin®) 1% cream twice a day until cleared plus 1 week OR c. Terbinafine (Lamisil®) 1% cream once daily, continue treatment for 1-2 weeks after clearing d. If not clearing in 1-2 weeks contact preceptor for further care 3. Tinea Cruris a. Counseling includes: use drying powder; wear loose fitting underwear b. Miconazole (Monistat®), clotrimazole (Lotrimin®) or terbinafine (Lamisil®) cream as above c. High incidence of relapse if also infected with tinea pedis 4. Tinea Pedis a. Treat with topical terbinafine powder for two weeks b. Change socks multiple times during the day, keep shoes dry and rotate shoes when possible, wear s

lso infected with tinea pedis 4. Tinea Pedis a. Treat with topical terbinafine powder for two weeks b. Change socks multiple times during the day, keep shoes dry and rotate shoes when possible, wear shower shoes in communal showers, use absorbent non-synthetic socks, make sure feet and web spaces are completely dry before putting shoes and socks on, use over-the-counter anti-fungal powders to prevent recurrences c. If macerated: use Aluminum acetate (Domeboro®) solution soaks 20 min twice daily and clotrimazole (Lotrimin®) or miconazole (Monistat®) cream as above If dry and scaly: For especially thick, scaly soles use Urea 10-20% cream daily to soften skin and use clotrimazole (Lotrimin®) or miconazole (Monistat®) cream as above CLINICAL NOTE: Some forms of tinea have luminescent glow when examined with Wood’s Lamp. Iridescence confirms the diagnosis, but absence does not rule out fungal infection. ACTION ALERT: Tinea capitis requires oral antifungal agent. Delay in treatment can cause permanent scarring.

155 XXVII. TINEA VERSICOLOR- Yeast infection usually of the upper trunk that causes hypo- or hyper-pigmentation of the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Usually asymptomatic 2. May have pruritus 3. Velvety tan, pink or white macules that vary in size 4. Area does not tan or appears darker than surrounding skin if affected areas are not exposed to sun 5. No visible scale but scale occurs by lightly scraping lesion with flat blade or fingernail 6. Macules may appear on trunk, upper arms, neck or groin 7. Lab: Large hyphae and thick walled budding spores “spaghetti and meatball” appearance on slide treated with KOH B. Treatment 1. Apply selenium sulfide (Head and Shoulders®, Selsun Blue®) from neck to waist, leave on for 5-15 min on one day then no treatment for 6 days 2. Repeat selenium sulfide treatment weekly x 1 month, then once monthly for maintenance 3. To prevent recurrence: ketoconazole (Nizoral®) shampoo 1% or 2% lathered on chest or back, left on for 5 min applied weekly XXVIII. URTICARIA- Rapidly expanding pruritic lesions that erupt within a few minutes of exposure to a trigger and generally resolve if the trigger is removed. May recur anytime the individual is exposed to the

TICARIA- Rapidly expanding pruritic lesions that erupt within a few minutes of exposure to a trigger and generally resolve if the trigger is removed. May recur anytime the individual is exposed to the trigger again. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pruritic red swellings that vary in size, enlarge, coalesce, and disappear over the course of several hours 2. May cause angioedema (involvement of deeper subcutaneous tissue— swelling of lips, eyelids, palms, soles, genitalia) 3. If severe reaction, consider prophylactic intubation; refer to “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis” 4. Common triggers include taking a new medication, eating a new food, insect stings, latex exposure, temperature changes, and some systemic infections CLINICAL NOTE: Inform patient that the medications treat the raised and scaly portion of the rash and the pigmentation change will take months to fill or fade. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if no improvement with topical medications after 1 month. ACTION ALERT: If there are corneal ulcerations or vesicles on the nose, immediately CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION. CLINICAL NOTE: Search for a trigger (medications, food, hot showers, exercise, col

ERT: If there are corneal ulcerations or vesicles on the nose, immediately CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION. CLINICAL NOTE: Search for a trigger (medications, food, hot showers, exercise, cold exposure, sun, freshly mown grass, etc). Avoid systemic steroids unless instructed to do so by preceptor. Steroids may temporarily improve the condition but it is likely to return with cessation of steroids.

156 B. Treatment 1. Antihistamines- diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) or hydroxyzine (Atarax®) (sedating) with better demonstrated response, but if patient must avoid sedation consider cetirizine (Zyrtec®) or loratadine (Claritin®) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR if no response initially, may require up to 4 times normal doses of second generation antihistamines to suppress urticaria XXIX. WARTS- Verrucous (cauliflower-like) papules on the skin caused by papillomaviruses. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Plantar warts can be tender with pressure and may resemble corns or calluses 2. Some warts itch, especially anal warts 3. Appearance varies from large cauliflower-like papules with multiple finger- like projections to small flat papules. May see black spots within lesion when illuminated with magnification (can use otoscope) 4. All warts have rough bumpy texture B. Treatment- Multiple options (Choose only one) 1. Liquid nitrogen may be used on any warts, including genital and anal. Freeze for 5 seconds and thaw for 20-45 seconds for 2 cycles every 2-4 weeks. Do not freeze more than 1 mm of surrounding skin. Risks: painful, may scar, cause hypopigmentation in dark skin 2. Salicylic acid may be use

ds and thaw for 20-45 seconds for 2 cycles every 2-4 weeks. Do not freeze more than 1 mm of surrounding skin. Risks: painful, may scar, cause hypopigmentation in dark skin 2. Salicylic acid may be used for common or plantar warts. Pare down wart and apply 40% salicylic acid (Mediplast®); keep area occluded for 5-6 days, then remove. Repeat until lesion disappears. Safe and effective with almost no side effects. Avoid occluding normal skin as much as possible. 3. If anal or genital warts, if wart involves a nail fold, or if wart involves a mucous membrane, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION 4. If no improvement with common or plantar warts after 3 months, refer for possible treatment with podophyllin (Podofilox®), laser, or surgery. C. Prevention 1. Virus is spread into areas of broken skin through contaminated surfaces (such as shower floors) 2. Advise all in unit to wear shower shoes and dry feet thoroughly prior to applying socks and footwear CLINICAL NOTE: Warts can be frustrating to treat because they do not resolve based on duration of treatment. Lesions sometimes resolve on their own spontaneously. Often the warts seem completely resistant but then resolve spontaneousl

ating to treat because they do not resolve based on duration of treatment. Lesions sometimes resolve on their own spontaneously. Often the warts seem completely resistant but then resolve spontaneously with the same treatments.

157 Chapter 12: Musculoskeletal Protocols Introduction to Musculoskeletal Injuries I. LYME DISEASE- See “Febrile Illness Protocols, Lyme Disease” II. RHABDOMYOLYSIS- See “Genitourinary Protocols, Rhabdomyolysis” III. GOUT- Sedimentation of urate crystals into a joint space causing severe pain. Most frequently affects base of 1st toe (1st metatarsophalangeal joint), or the knee. All individuals with gout have hyperurecemia, which causes the sediment to form. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe pain, redness, swelling, active and passive immobility at affected joint; pain peaks in 12-24 hours and resolves within days to weeks 2. Tophaceous deposits (horn-like crusted growths coming out of the skin) 3. History of kidney stones made of uric acid 4. Risk factors include chronic beer or hard liquor consumption, starvation, trauma, fatty foods or overindulgent food behaviors 5. High dietary intake of red meat and seafood B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Do not give aspirin, it will worsen the condition b. Naproxen (Aleve®) or indomethacin (Indocin®) are good first line agents c. Joint will be exquisitely tender- avoid manipulation or any pressure on affected joint, even sheets 2. CONTACT P

condition b. Naproxen (Aleve®) or indomethacin (Indocin®) are good first line agents c. Joint will be exquisitely tender- avoid manipulation or any pressure on affected joint, even sheets 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION IV. SUSPECTED FRACTURES- Acute trauma or repetitive stress that leads to a disruption in bone. Stress fractures and multi-system trauma are addressed separately. This section will discuss general management of uncomplicated fractures. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Suspicion is based on mechanism of injury plus at least one of the following: bony deformity, excessive edema, limited range of motion at suspected fracture site, crepitus on palpation 2. Exam should include assessment of neurovascular status to include distal pulses, distal capillary refill, and checking sensation, motion, and reflexes distal to fracture site (as applicable) 3. Exam should always compare injury site to non-affected side 4. Exam should include joints above and below injury site (at a minimum) 5. Reassess neurovascular status after every manipulation of the injury site B. Treatment 1. Based on location of injury 2. Immobilize injury in position found/position of comfort 3. R

(at a minimum) 5. Reassess neurovascular status after every manipulation of the injury site B. Treatment 1. Based on location of injury 2. Immobilize injury in position found/position of comfort 3. Reassess neurovascular status after immobilization 4. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain management. Recent evidence suggests NSAID use may delay fracture healing

158 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION V. DISLOCATIONS- Traumatic disruption of a joint most commonly in the fingers, shoulders, or patellae but may be seen in other joints during high velocity trauma. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Mechanism of injury describes non-anatomic movement of a joint (finger bent backwards, etc) 2. Suspicion based on mechanism of injury plus at least one of the following: joint deformity, excessive edema, and limited range of motion at suspected dislocation site 3. Exam should include assessment of neurovascular status to include distal pulses, distal capillary refill, and checking sensation, motion, and reflexes distal to dislocation site (as applicable) 4. Exam should compare injury site to non-affected side 5. Exam should include joints above and below injury site (at a minimum) 6. Reassess neurovascular status after every manipulation of the injury site B. Treatment 1. Based on location of injury 2. Immobilize injury in position found/position of comfort 3. Reassess neurovascular status after immobilization 4. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or NSAIDs for pain management 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION VI. LIGAMENT SPRAINS AND TEARS- Ligame

rt 3. Reassess neurovascular status after immobilization 4. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or NSAIDs for pain management 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION VI. LIGAMENT SPRAINS AND TEARS- Ligaments connect bone to bone. Sprains refer to unnatural “stretching” of a ligament, whereas a tear refers to partial or complete rupture. Dislocations can accompany complete ligament tears since bones are no longer being maintained in anatomic alignment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Edema and pain along ligament 2. No “endpoint” when ligament is stressed. Bony elements will continue to move in direction of stress. Examples of this include positive anterior ACTION ALERT: New or worsening pain, diminished circulation, cool or dusky digits, bleeding through splint, and/or feelings of pressure or numbness at the fracture site after fracture is immobilized are signs of constriction- either from too tight of a splint or from compartment syndrome. If patient develops any of these symptoms, IMMEDIATELY remove splint and reevaluate. If symptoms resolve, replace splint more loosely. If they do not resolve, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION. Consider emergency fasciotomy (long cuts throu

emove splint and reevaluate. If symptoms resolve, replace splint more loosely. If they do not resolve, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION. Consider emergency fasciotomy (long cuts through the skin and fascia to release pressure) if evacuation is delayed. ACTION ALERT: ANY fracture that is accompanied by a break in the skin, no matter how slight, is considered an open fracture. Open fractures are very serious due to the potential for life- and limb-threatening infections. If the skin is broken in any way (abrasion, laceration, etc) CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION.

159 drawer’s test when the Anterior Cruciate Ligament has been damaged; fingers can bend laterally at PIP or DIP joints if the lateral ligaments of the finger have been disrupted B. Treatment 1. Based on location of injury 2. Rest, ice, compress, elevate (RICE therapy) 3. Injury may or may not require immobilization 4. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or NSAIDs for pain management VII. TENDONITIS/MUSCLE STRAIN/TENDON RUPTURE- Tendons connect muscle to bone. If a muscle is poorly flexible and experiences a significant stress, the muscle fibers are unable to react quickly enough to absorb the force by stretching. This can result in inflammation where the tendon attaches to bone (tendonitis) or microtears in the muscle (muscle strain). The worst final outcome of this process is tendon rupture, when the tendon separates from the bone to which it is attached. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Patients have pain with movement of the involved muscle (active worse than passive). They may have swelling or pain where the tendon inserts (tendonitis) or in the belly of the muscle (muscle strain) 2.
