Those with more serious muscle tear or tendon rupture will have a palpable “knot” where the muscle has torn f

ain where the tendon inserts (tendonitis) or in the belly of the muscle (muscle strain) 2. Those with more serious muscle tear or tendon rupture will have a palpable “knot” where the muscle has torn free and bunched up in or near the belly of the muscle 3. Some strained muscles, particularly of the neck and back, will be associated with muscle spasms B. Treatment 1. NSAIDs or acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for 5-10 days 2. Reduce the activities that cause pain 3. Increase gentle stretching routine tailored for specific muscle group VIII. BURSITIS- Bursae are fluid-filled cushioning sacs located around bony prominences that help protect ligaments and tendons as they slide over bone. Bursitis occurs when the sac ACTION ALERT: Tendonitis, muscle strain, and muscle tear are all overuse injuries due to poor flexibility. Prevention is key! As an IDMT, advise fitness leaders and Commanders regarding workout strategies to avoid injury. All group-led workouts should meet the following criteria: 1. Begin with 5 minutes of light calisthenics (jog in place, jumping jacks, etc) 2. Complete at least 5 minutes of full body warmup stretches, holding each stretch for 10-15 seconds without bouncing 3. Com

gin with 5 minutes of light calisthenics (jog in place, jumping jacks, etc) 2. Complete at least 5 minutes of full body warmup stretches, holding each stretch for 10-15 seconds without bouncing 3. Complete planned exercise regimen 4. Cool down with slower activity to reduce heart rate followed by ten minutes of whole body deep stretching, holding each stretch for 30 seconds or two rounds of 15 seconds 5. Additionally, adding a “stretch day” into exercise regimens is recommended to increase flexibility. Yoga is effective at increasing both core strength and flexibility

160 becomes inflamed. It is frequently the result of repetitive motion with poor body mechanics or repetitive pressure, such as kneeling. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain with motion and at rest 2. Pain with active movement of the adjacent structures 3. Tenderness when the bursa is palpated 4. Localized edema confined to the bursa may be present 5. If there is erythema or heat associated with the swelling, assume that it is an infected bursa or joint and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION B. Treatment 1. Rest, ice after activity, and heat prior to stretching or activity are mainstays of treatment 2. NSAIDs for up to 14 days Neck, Back, and Torso Disorders IX. ACUTE NECK PAIN-Muscle strain and/or spasms due to position, load-bearing, or whiplash injury. For traumatic neck injury, refer to “Emergency Protocols, Neck Trauma” A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Neck pain and stiffness, often limiting the neck range of motion 2. History of holding head in same position for long periods of time, whiplash, or waking up after “sleeping wrong” B. Treatment 1. Posture modification (don’t sit/sleep like that anymore) 2. ROM exercises for 5 minutes every hour 3. Avoid sustained computer work

time, whiplash, or waking up after “sleeping wrong” B. Treatment 1. Posture modification (don’t sit/sleep like that anymore) 2. ROM exercises for 5 minutes every hour 3. Avoid sustained computer work or fine motor work 4. Sleep on back with low pillow under neck and head, thighs raised on pillows 5. Keep spine in neutral, inline position 6. NSAIDs for first 72 hours after injury. Add acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for additional pain relief 7. Cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril®) for muscle spasms and difficulty sleeping due to spasms 8. Upon waking, prior to sleeping, and at least one other time during the day, perform the following exercises: a. Warm compresses on neck and upper shoulders for 10 minutes b. Slow ROM of neck, holding each position for at least 10 seconds c. Shoulder rolls and rows without weights 9. AVOID immobilization devices (soft neck collars, etc) as long as acute fracture or ruptured disc is not a possibility 10. If symptoms do not resolve within 2 weeks, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION CLINICAL NOTE: Bursitis will typically resolve with rest in 5-10 days, but it will return if the damaging activity is resumed. It is vital to find and correct the underlying issue to

EPTOR- EVACUATION CLINICAL NOTE: Bursitis will typically resolve with rest in 5-10 days, but it will return if the damaging activity is resumed. It is vital to find and correct the underlying issue to avoid further debilitation.

161 X. COSTOCHONDRITIS- A musculoskeletal source of acute anterior chest or thoracic pain caused by irritation of the cartilage that attaches ribs to the sternum or from microtears in the muscles between ribs from forceful exhaling (coughing, retching, etc). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Able to reproduce the pain by putting pressure on the region 2. Still must complete thorough history and physical to rule out cardiogenic causes (see “Emergency Protocols, Cardiac”) B. Treatment 1. Limit activities involving load bearing, lifting, weight training, running, jumping or any activity causing pain 2. Allow 4-6 weeks for resolution 3. NSAIDs for pain; consider ketorolac (Toradol®) injection upon presentation 4. Consider cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril®) if pain is associated with muscle spasm 5. Tell patient to avoid splinting (holding arm tight to chest or taking shallow breaths to prevent pain) 6. Encourage deep breathing exercises to avoid atelectasis and ensuing pneumonia XI. SUSPECTED RIB FRACTURE- Fracture of a rib resulting from blunt trauma to the rib cage. Evaluate patient for emergent conditions as described in “Emergency Protocols, Trauma”. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain in ribs or thorax

- Fracture of a rib resulting from blunt trauma to the rib cage. Evaluate patient for emergent conditions as described in “Emergency Protocols, Trauma”. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain in ribs or thorax following trauma 2. Patient will likely be self-splinting the affected rib with their arm to prevent lung expansion B. Treatment 1. Evaluate patient at 6, 12 and 24 hours after injury to make sure no cardiopulmonary structures have been compromised 2. Limit activities involving load bearing, lifting, running, jumping or anything that causes pain 3. NSAIDS for pain; consider ketorolac (Toradol®) injection upon presentation 4. If muscle spasms are present, consider cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril®) 5. Avoid taping or splinting 6. Encourage patient to complete deep breathing exercises (use incentive spirometry, if available) to avoid atelectasis and ensuing pneumonia XII. LOW BACK PAIN (“LUMBAGO”)- Pain in the lumbar region of the back, typically following a physical stressor (such as lifting) or prolonged immobility. CLINICAL NOTE: No EKG/cardiac testing is required if the patient meets ALL of the the following diagnostic criteria: 1. Patient/examiner can reproduce the pain through movement,

prolonged immobility. CLINICAL NOTE: No EKG/cardiac testing is required if the patient meets ALL of the the following diagnostic criteria: 1. Patient/examiner can reproduce the pain through movement, palpation or by deep inspiration 2. Symptoms are NOT associated with cyanosis, edema, shortness of breath, flushing or pallor 3. Vital signs are within normal limits

162 A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Recent heavy lifting or increase in strenuous activity 2. Back pain, pressure or spasm, unilaterally or bilaterally in the lumbar region 3. May have shooting or stabbing pains radiating below the knee (sciatica) 4. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear B. Treatment 1. Limit activities that include running, jumping and load bearing 2. Avoid bed rest and immobility 3. Most patients are pain free within 6 weeks regardless of treatment 4. Encourage activity as tolerated, especially walking and gentle stretching 5. NSAIDs and/or acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for acute pain; consider ketorolac (Toradol®) injection at initial presentation for pain control 6. Avoid narcotics for pain control 7. Consider cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril®) for muscle spasms C. Prevention 1. Emphasize appropriate workout structure with calisthenics, warm up stretches, exercise, and cool down stretches (See “Action Alert, Tendonitis”) 2. Add a “stretch day” into weekly exercise routine with 20-60 minutes of focused stretching 3. Core strengthening exercises- incorporate TRX® suspension training, Crossfit®, kettlebell exercises, stabilit

d a “stretch day” into weekly exercise routine with 20-60 minutes of focused stretching 3. Core strengthening exercises- incorporate TRX® suspension training, Crossfit®, kettlebell exercises, stability ball exercises, and/or Pilates® to weekly exercise program (see Action Alert below) ACTION ALERT: If any of the following are newly present associated with back pain, further evaluation is required. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION: 1. Focal neurological deficits such as weakness or numbness that can’t be improved by changes of position 2. Weight loss 3. Fevers 4. High velocity trauma ACTION ALERT: Low back pain with bowel or bladder incontinence, numbness of the inner thighs, numbness of the genitalia, and new onset of sexual dysfunction are all predictors of paralysis. If any of these symptoms are present, place the patient in the position of comfort, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION. See “Emergency Protocols, Trauma” for more information.

163 Shoulder Disorders XIII. ROTATOR CUFF STRAIN/TEAR- Throwing injury or trauma resulting in strain or tear of one or more of the rotator cuff muscles. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of injury followed by pain and weakness 2. Pain over the lateral deltoid and exacerbated by overhead movements and while sleeping 3. Weakness in any direction of shoulder movement, but most commonly with external rotation 4. Unable to complete adduction (patient’s arms are overhead and adduction is attempted; when the adducting arm reaches 90°, it abruptly falls to the patient’s side = “drop arm” test) 5. Suspect coexisting labral tear if patient also complains of catching sensation, instability, and crepitus B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION for any labral symptoms (catching, instability, and/or crepitus) or any shoulder weakness worse than 4/5 2. Suspect rotator cuff strain if patient is able to perform all motions and has shoulder strength of 4/5 or greater in all directions 3. Treat with stretching and strengthening exercises, NSAIDs and activity restriction ACTION ALERT: Core strengthening is a critical and frequently missed component of a healthy lifestyle. Advise worko

. Treat with stretching and strengthening exercises, NSAIDs and activity restriction ACTION ALERT: Core strengthening is a critical and frequently missed component of a healthy lifestyle. Advise workout leaders and Commanders that weekly exercise routine should include frequent core training elements. Some options include: 1. TRX® suspension training exercises (or other suspension system) 2. Stability ball exercises 3. “Planks” and any of their variations 4. Pilates® 5. Balance boards/wobble boards 6. Bosu® balance board 7. Pull-ups/chin-ups 8. Ab wheel (make sure it is being used correctly- improper use will cause back strain) 9. Rowing machine Some common home workout regimens that emphasize good core strengthening principles: 10. Crossfit® 11. Insanity® 12. P90X® 13. The Firm® 14. Zumba®

164 XIV. BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY- Known as “burners” or “stingers”, injury caused by trauma to the neck or shoulder with pain radiating down affected upper extremity. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Frequently caused during participation in contact or high velocity sports such as football, wrestling, or gymnastics or by shoulder-carried load bearing equipment such as ruck sack, shoulder-slung weapons, dive gear, etc. 2. History of trauma to the neck or shoulder region 3. Radiating pain/paresthesias down arm circumferentially (all the way around the arm and not in a dermatomal pattern) 4. Injury can be followed by numbness, tingling or weakness 5. May have scapular winging and shoulder muscle atrophy if episodes have been recurrent 6. Focal neural deficits depending on which nerves are affected B. Treatment 1. Evaluate for cervical/head trauma (See “Emergency Protocols, Trauma”) 2. Limit activities that include lifting/contact until symptoms are resolved 3. Reevaluate patient daily until all symptoms are resolved 4. NSAIDS for pain 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION XV. CLAVICULAR FRACTURE- Fracture of the clavicle (collarbone) is usually caused by falling onto the lateral shoulde

mptoms are resolved 4. NSAIDS for pain 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION XV. CLAVICULAR FRACTURE- Fracture of the clavicle (collarbone) is usually caused by falling onto the lateral shoulder, but may be caused indirectly from an anterior blow to the shoulder, seatbelt injury, or fall on an outstretched hand. See “Introduction to Musculoskeletal Injuries, Suspected Fracture” at the beginning of this chapter for additional information. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain in clavicle with arm movement 2. Anterior shoulder/clavicle swelling 3. History of clavicular snapping or cracking sensation at time of injury 4. Deformity, point tenderness, edema and ecchymosis at the fracture site 5. May have associated hemothorax, pneumothorax or rib fracture 6. May be accompanied by brachial plexus injury or glenoid fracture B. Treatment 1. Treat any additional complications from the trauma (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Trauma” for more information) 2. Immobilize extremity using a sling and swath 3. Reassess neurovascular function and vital signs regularly ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there are any changes in sensation, any decreased elbow, wrist, or hand strengt

nd swath 3. Reassess neurovascular function and vital signs regularly ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there are any changes in sensation, any decreased elbow, wrist, or hand strength, or any decreased circulation (diminished pulses, delayed capillary refill, skin pallor, etc). CLINICAL NOTE: Counsel patient regarding importance of safety equipment during high- risk activities. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there is cervical pathology, limited ROM of the c-spine is present, or for any neurovascular compromise that does not resolve within the first few minutes after injury.

165 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR-MEDICATION EVACUATION a. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) initially for pain b. Avoid NSAIDs unless directed by your preceptor- recent studies show NSAIDs may impair fracture healing c. Consider narcotics for severe pain XVI. ACROMIOCLAVICULAR (AC) SEPARATION– “Separated Shoulder” due to direct trauma or fall on shoulder. Often unable to differentiate from clavicular fracture without x-rays. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of prior shoulder injury increases risk 2. Numbness, tingling or weakness in the affected limb 3. Tenderness and swelling on the AC joint with or without deformity 4. Inability to cross arm across body to touch opposite shoulder or complete shoulder exam ranges of motion due to pain B. Treatment 1. Immobilize extremity in position of comfort using a sling and swathe 2. Rest and ice therapy as tolerated 3. Reassess neurovascular function and vital signs after each movement of arm/shoulder and every 30 minutes while at rest 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION XVII. GLENOHUMERAL DISLOCATION- Displacement of the humeral head anterior to the glenoid fossa. Posterior dislocations are rare. Dislocation is frequently caused by blunt tra

IAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION XVII. GLENOHUMERAL DISLOCATION- Displacement of the humeral head anterior to the glenoid fossa. Posterior dislocations are rare. Dislocation is frequently caused by blunt trauma when the shoulder is abducted, externally rotated and arm is extended. Inferior dislocations can occur when patient has fallen from a height and tries to grab something to break the fall. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma 2. Pain, numbness, and tingling distal to injury 3. Deformity and ecchymosis 4. Anterior dislocations cause the arm to be held in a position of slight abduction and external rotation at rest 5. Do not force arm against patient’s side 6. Patient will avoid all active and passive movements B. Treatment 1. Immobilize extremity in position of comfort 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if glenohumoral fracture is suspected, if there is any neurovascular compromise, severe deformity is present, lung involvement is suspected, or if any break in the skin has occurred. ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there are any changes in sensation, any decreased elbow, wrist, or hand strength, any decre

t is suspected, or if any break in the skin has occurred. ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there are any changes in sensation, any decreased elbow, wrist, or hand strength, any decreased circulation (diminished pulses, delayed capillary refill, skin pallor, etc), severe deformity is present, lung involvement is suspected, or any break in the skin has occurred.

166 a. Consider IV narcotic meditations such as morphine or meperidine (Demerol®) b. If evacuation will be delayed, consider IV benzodiazepine such as diazepam (Valium®) c. If both medications are administered, be prepared to support ventilations due to the combined respiratory depression effect of narcotics and benzodiazepines 3. Reduction should not be attempted by the IDMT prior to x-rays unless evacuation will be prolonged AND neurovascular compromise is present a. Reduction attempt must have specific consent of preceptor and patient b. NEVER attempt to reduce a dislocated joint if there is also a suspicion for fracture XVIII. ADHESIVE CAPSULITIS- “Frozen Shoulder” is the reversible contraction of the shoulder joint capsule after immobilization or disuse. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Loss of ROM following days to weeks of disuse 2. May have history of minor shoulder injury days or weeks prior, then stopped moving arm or kept arm continuously in a sling 3. Stiffness/immobility more than pain 4. >50% reduction of BOTH active and passive ROM in all directions, but usually worse with abduction and external rotation B. Treatment 1. Active and passive movement of the joint is the mainsta

pain 4. >50% reduction of BOTH active and passive ROM in all directions, but usually worse with abduction and external rotation B. Treatment 1. Active and passive movement of the joint is the mainstay of therapy 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION XIX. BICEPS TENDONITIS AND BICEPS TENDON RUPTURE- Injuries to the biceps muscle or tendon is caused by sudden extension at the elbow when the biceps is fully contracted and loaded (doing a bicep curl and allowing the weight to snap the arm back straight). Likelihood of injury increases with age. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Anterior shoulder pain with radiation over the biceps muscle 2. Biceps Tendon Rupture--History of trauma and feeling a “pop”, followed by sharp pain, ecchymosis, and swelling (“Popeye” deformity) 3. Tendonitis- Absence of trauma, pain increases with time; history of repetitive motions ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if glenohumoral fracture in suspected, any neurovascular compromise is present, or any signs of shock. CLINICAL NOTE: Frozen shoulder can be differentiated from other rotator cuff problems because frozen shoulder will have limited active AND passive ranges of motion. Rotator cuff strai

signs of shock. CLINICAL NOTE: Frozen shoulder can be differentiated from other rotator cuff problems because frozen shoulder will have limited active AND passive ranges of motion. Rotator cuff strains and tears will have limited active range of motion but markedly improved passive range of motion.

167 4. May have history of other shoulder problems prior to development of symptoms 5. Visible/palpable deformity of the biceps muscle 6. Palpation of the biceps groove of the humerus and resisted supination will cause pain B. Treatment 1. NSAIDS for up to ten days and rest 2. If biceps tendon rupture is suspected, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION 3. Ice and compression may reduce hematoma formation and deformity Elbow Disorders XX. EPICONDYLITIS (MEDIAL OR LATERAL) - Repetitive motion injury caused by repetitive hand gripping (“tennis elbow”), handshake motions, or repetitive use of screwdrivers. Frequently develops in carpenters, construction workers, and dentists as well as racquet sport enthusiasts and golfers. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of repetitive motion tasks or the above risk factors 2. Pain and tenderness with palpation of the affected epicondyle and associated muscles of the forearm 3. Pain with resisted wrist flexion (medial epicondylitis) 4. Pain with resisted wrist extension (lateral epicondylitis) 5. Decreased grip strength B. Treatment 1. Limit activities that cause pain 2. NSAIDs- use topical diclofenac (Solaraze®), or if not availabl

Pain with resisted wrist extension (lateral epicondylitis) 5. Decreased grip strength B. Treatment 1. Limit activities that cause pain 2. NSAIDs- use topical diclofenac (Solaraze®), or if not available, use oral diclofenac (Voltaren®). If neither is available, may use other NSAIDs 3. Prescribe exercises that increase forearm strength 4. If no relief after six weeks with the above therapies, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION for specific exercises and additional therapies such as counter bracing XXI. ELBOW DISLOCATION AND ELBOW FRACTURE- Caused by direct trauma to the elbow or fall on outstretched limb. Over half of adults with elbow dislocations have associated fractures. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma to extremity or sudden “pop” while throwing 2. Inability to complete full range of motion 3. May have gross deformity, discoloration ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there are any changes in sensation, any decreased elbow, wrist, or hand strength, or any decreased circulation (diminished pulses, delayed capillary refill, skin pallor, etc) that does not resolve with change in position or decreased physician.
any decreased circulation (diminished pulses, delayed capillary refill, skin pallor, etc) that does not resolve with change in position or decreased physician.

168 4. Elbow dislocation and fracture are unlikely if full active range of motion is possible (able to fully flex and extend even if painful) 5. Some active joint movement is usually still possible B. Treatment 1. Splint in position of comfort 2. Reassess neurovascular status after splinting 3. Avoid NSAIDs unless directed by your preceptor- recent studies show NSAIDs may impair fracture healing 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND EVACUATION 5. Reassess neurovascular status after any movement of limb or patient and every 30 minutes Wrist and Hand Disorders XXII. CARPAL TUNNEL SYNDROME- A condition of median nerve entrapment as it passes through the carpal tunnel. The carpal tunnel is located on the ventral side of the angle of the wrist. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Numbness or tingling in the thumb and first fingers 2. History of repetitive motion work during the day, such as typing or using tools 3. Symptoms worsen at night and relieved by wringing hands, running warm water over hands or shaking the hand out 4. May have pain with repetitive motions or sustained positions 5. Pain may radiate up forearm 6. May complain of reduced grip strength or decreased dexterity B. Treat

er hands or shaking the hand out 4. May have pain with repetitive motions or sustained positions 5. Pain may radiate up forearm 6. May complain of reduced grip strength or decreased dexterity B. Treatment 1. Identify nerve entrapment in the extremity by checking sharp, dull and light touch sensitivity in all the fingers 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION if patient displays sensory changes or muscle atrophy 3. Splint wrist every night and during the day if daytime symptoms are present 4. Recovery may take weeks or months CLINICAL NOTE: If no history of trauma, have patient hold hands above head for 60 seconds. If numbness or tingling occur in all the digits sparing the 5th, this is suggestive of carpal tunnel syndrome. CLINICAL NOTE: If pain is in the 4th and 5th digits, consider diagnosis of “cubital tunnel syndrome”, which affects the ulnar nerve.

169 5. If symptoms at work are limiting duty, consider profile for 2-4 weeks avoiding activities causing pain. Do not give profile for “no pushups”- patient may do modified pushups on knuckles or using grip bars XXIII. SCAPHOID “SNUFFBOX” FRACTURE- A fracture of the scaphoid bone, the carpal at the base of the thumb. Usually caused by a fall on an outstretched hand. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma 2. Pain localized to radial side of wrist at base of thumb (“anatomic snuffbox”) 3. May present with continuing or worsening pain several weeks after initial injury 4. Pain with radial deviation 5. Minimal swelling and full ROM 6. Tenderness elicited when palpating the scaphoid with the wrist in ulnar deviation and slight flexion 7. X-rays are often negative for 1-2 weeks post injury B. Treatment 1. Thumb spica splint or cast 2. Consider acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain 3. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) for three days after initial injury 4. Limit activities involving pressure on the wrist 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION XXIV. DORSAL GANGLION- A cystic swelling overlying a joint or tendon sheath most commonly in the wrists or fingers. A. S

essure on the wrist 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION XXIV. DORSAL GANGLION- A cystic swelling overlying a joint or tendon sheath most commonly in the wrists or fingers. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Spontaneously occurring, painful mass on dorsal wrist 2. Lesion fluctuant, mobile, demarcated, and transilluminates well B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION 2. Consider limiting activities if pain is severe 3. Medications are not generally helpful XXV. DEQUERVAIN’S TENOSYNOVITIS- Inflammation of the tendons that extend and/or cross the thumb across the palm most commonly caused by repetitive gripping and grasping. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Thumb movements cause catching 2. No trauma history 3. Swelling at the base of thumb along anatomic snuffbox CLINICAL NOTE: Perform Finkelstein’s maneuver--Have the patient close fingers over the thumb and ulnar-deviate the wrist. This test is diagnostic, causes extreme pain and may cause the patient to lift shoulder to prevent examiner from stretching the tendons. CLINICAL NOTE: There are no documented benefits from using NSAIDs (or other analgesics) in the treatment of carpal tunnel syndrome.
lder to prevent examiner from stretching the tendons. CLINICAL NOTE: There are no documented benefits from using NSAIDs (or other analgesics) in the treatment of carpal tunnel syndrome.

170 B. Treatment 1. Restrict grasping and gripping motions 2. Ice/NSAID therapy 3. Thumb spica cast or splint 4. If symptoms persist for >6 weeks, consult physician preceptor and consider evacuation for further evaluation and possible steroid injection XXVI. BOXER’S FRACTURE- A fracture of the 4th and/or 5th metacarpal necks, usually of the dominant hand, caused by punching a solid object with a closed fist. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dorsal hand swelling, bony tenderness over the affected metacarpal bone and loss of contour of the affected knuckle (appears sunken in or depressed) 2. Tenderness and ecchymosis of the palmar bony metacarpal surfaces 3. Inability to straighten fingers or form a fist well 4. May have inability to straighten distal affected finger 5. Thoroughly evaluate for any traumatic breaks in the skin of the hand AT ALL. If the individual has sustained a “fight bite” the fracture is classified as an open fracture even if the lesions are minor B. Treatment 1. Thoroughly wash any open lesions and decontaminate wounds as much as possible 2. Splint in position of comfort 3. Reassess neurovascular status after splinting 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 5. Consid

ash any open lesions and decontaminate wounds as much as possible 2. Splint in position of comfort 3. Reassess neurovascular status after splinting 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 5. Consider antibiotics if wound is open and evacuation will be delayed Hip/Pelvis/Upper Leg Disorders XXVII. ILIOTIBIAL BAND SYNDROME- The iliotibial (IT) band originates at the lateral hip and inserts on the lateral knee at the lateral femoral epicondyle. A tight muscle at its origin causes pain and inflammation along the IT band. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dull aching or burning pain at the lateral femoral condyle radiating up the lateral thigh toward the hip CLINICAL NOTE: Have patient flex at the MCP joints while keeping the DIPs and PIPs in extension so the fingers form a 90° angle with the palm. All fingernails should be in the same horizontal plane. ACTION ALERT: Patients with open wounds associated with a fracture (no matter how minor) and/or patients with decreased sensation should be evaluated by a surgeon as soon as possible.
ated by a surgeon as soon as possible.

171 2. Risk factors include excessive running mileage, old running shoes, or running on uneven terrain 3. Focal tenderness with or without palpable clicking at the lateral femoral condyle 4. Remainder of knee exam is usually normal and pain free B. Treatment 1. Limit activities involving running and jumping 2. NSAIDs no more than 2 weeks 3. Stretching is key! Prescribe stretching exercises of the lateral thigh and strengthening of the medial thigh 4. Rehab exercises should be completed three times daily until pain free (may take several months) XXVIII. TROCHANTERIC BURSITIS- Inflammation of the lubricating sac on lateral trochanter of the proximal femur (on the point of the hip) caused by abnormalities in gait or repetitive hip flexion (climbing stairs). A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe, lateral hip pain and pain over the outer thigh 2. May cause difficulty walking and aggravated by direct pressure (such as laying on that side) 3. May have leg length discrepancy, fallen or high foot arches 4. Shoes may have unequal tread wear 5. Hip exam many be normal except for point tenderness over the greater trochanter B. Treatment 1. Correct any misalignments 2. Limit activities that cause pai

hes 4. Shoes may have unequal tread wear 5. Hip exam many be normal except for point tenderness over the greater trochanter B. Treatment 1. Correct any misalignments 2. Limit activities that cause pain 3. Apply heat for 15-20 minutes followed by stretches of the IT band and lateral hip three times daily for 2 weeks 4. Consider ibuprofen (Motrin®) or naproxen (Aleve®, Naprosyn®) for no more than 2 weeks XXIX. PELVIS FRACTURE- Disruptions of the pelvic ring, acetabulum or sacrum caused by high- energy blunt trauma to the pelvic girdle, such as vehicular and motorcycle accidents. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma 2. Abnormal positioning of lower extremity/extremities 3. Pelvic instability (gentle bilateral compression at the iliac crests- do not rock)- if any instability or crepitus is discovered avoid any further manipulation 4. Bowel or bladder incontinence 5. Vaginal or rectal bleeding, hematuria 6. Numbness or weakness distal to injury 7. Bruising of the buttocks, flanks, genitalia, lower abdomen, inner thighs, skin folds 8. Blood at the penile meatus
italia, lower abdomen, inner thighs, skin folds 8. Blood at the penile meatus

172 9. Rectal exam is required (both males and females) to check sphincter tone and palpate for bony fragments of the pelvic ring; use double glove technique to help protect finger from fracture fragments 10. High incidence of associated organ injuries and severe bleeding B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Stabilize life threatening injuries (see “Emergency Protocols, Trauma”) b. Initiate airway management, IV and O2 c. Wrap pelvis in a folded sheet, covering the lateral hips and buttocks, tighten until lower extremities are brought back into anatomic alignment, and secure with towel clamps d. Sheet wrap should improve comfort- do not over-compress e. Do not give NSAIDs or aspirin; avoid ketorolac (Toradol®) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION AND IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider morphine or meperidine (Demerol®) for pain control b. Consider IV cefuroxime (Ceftin®) and tetanus prophylaxis XXX. FEMUR FRACTURE- A fracture of the femur, usually from a high-energy trauma such as a motor vehicle accident. Femur fractures can be life-threatening and very difficult to treat due to the strength of the surrounding musculature. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of high-energy trauma (

motor vehicle accident. Femur fractures can be life-threatening and very difficult to treat due to the strength of the surrounding musculature. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of high-energy trauma (if trauma seems minor for extent of injury, suspect tumor or congenital defect) 2. Extreme pain, deformity, swelling and obviously shortened and/or rotated thigh 3. Extensive soft tissue injury, hemorrhage and shock are common B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Stabilize life-threatening injuries (see “Emergency protocols, Trauma”) b. Initiate airway management, IV and O2 c. Splint the limb in the position found unless neurovascular compromise has occurred d. If sensation is altered or evidence of bleeding, reduce the fracture with a mechanical traction device or splint 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider morphine or meperidine (Demerol®) for pain control b. Consider IV cefuroxime (Ceftin®) and tetanus prophylaxis for open fracture 3. If evacuation is delayed, monitor for development of compartment syndrome and consider emergency fasciotomy ACTION ALERT: Ominous signs include rectal/vaginal/penile bleeding, urinary incontinence, and open fractures.
monitor for development of compartment syndrome and consider emergency fasciotomy ACTION ALERT: Ominous signs include rectal/vaginal/penile bleeding, urinary incontinence, and open fractures.

173 Knee/Lower Leg Disorders XXXI. CALF STRAIN/RUPTURE- A pull or tear in the gastrocnemius muscle. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sudden onset of posterior calf pain following abrupt push-off (ie beginning a sprint or leaping) without adequate warm up 2. Pain or inability to tiptoe walk on affected side 3. Swelling or palpable “knot” in the gastrocnemius muscle (rupture) 4. Calf circumference may be greater than the opposite side B. Treatment 1. Limit activities causing pain 2. NSAIDs for no more than 2 weeks 3. Stretching and strengthening exercises tailored for calf strain 4. Exercises should be completed three times daily until pain free C. Prevention 1. Stretching is key! Injury occurs due to inadequate compliance of muscle 2. See text box “Musculoskeletal Injuries, Tendonitis” XXXII. DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS (DVT)- Blood clot that forms in a large vein of an upper or lower extremity. Blood clot can break off and travel to distant sites such as the lungs, known as “pulmonary embolism.” A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Risk factors include history of immobilization (such as airplane ride or recent lower extremity injury), bed rest, recent surgery, smoking, obesity, prior episode of DVT, use of

Signs and Symptoms 1. Risk factors include history of immobilization (such as airplane ride or recent lower extremity injury), bed rest, recent surgery, smoking, obesity, prior episode of DVT, use of oral contraceptives and pregnancy/postpartum status 2. Swelling, pain and erythema of the affected leg 3. Palpable, painful, warm cord in the lower extremity 4. Asymmetrical calf circumference B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Confine patient to bedrest b. Establish IV access, may saline lock or KVO c. Avoid aggressive palpation of limb or manipulation of limb d. Monitor patient for signs and symptoms of pulmonary emboli 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXXIII. PREPATELLAR BURSITIS– Inflammation of the bursal sac located between the patella and the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Swelling anterior to the kneecap after kneeling or falling on the knee; swelling is very superficial and located between patella and skin 2. Pain is present without motion (while knee is at rest) 3. Tenderness specifically localized to area of swelling/trauma 4. Knee ROM and strength are not limited unless due to swelling CLINICAL NOTE: If erythema and warmth are present, consider systemi

est) 3. Tenderness specifically localized to area of swelling/trauma 4. Knee ROM and strength are not limited unless due to swelling CLINICAL NOTE: If erythema and warmth are present, consider systemic cause of bursitis such as gout or infection.

174 B. Treatment 1. If fluid collection is superficial and margins are easy to identify, consider therapeutic aspiration procedure 2. Wrap knee after procedure (or instead of procedure) with compression dressing for 3 days to help reduce swelling 3. May use NSAIDs for up to two weeks 4. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) therapy three times daily for 3 days 5. On fourth day, patient can begin quad-strengthening exercises, discontinue compression dressing, and use knee sleeve if desired for comfort 6. Limit squatting and kneeling exercises for 4 weeks 7. Advise patient to use proper PPE to include knee pads to avoid re-injury CLINICAL NOTE: How to complete informed consent for therapeutic aspiration of prepatellar bursitis: 1. Type of procedure: therapeutic aspiration of prepatellar bursitis 2. Why procedure is necessary: remove painful collection of fluid from anterior knee 3. Risks of procedure: pain, bleeding, infection, risk of repeat or additional procedures, poor cosmetic outcome (scarring) 4. Risk of not doing the procedure: continuing pain and delayed healing 5. Type of anesthesia to be used: N/A 6. Patient’s statement of his known allergies and that he/she does not ha

tcome (scarring) 4. Risk of not doing the procedure: continuing pain and delayed healing 5. Type of anesthesia to be used: N/A 6. Patient’s statement of his known allergies and that he/she does not have a known allergy to latex or iodine or the medications you plan to prescribe. Example: “Patient denies food, drug, and contact allergies” or “Patient denies known allergies to latex, lidocaine (Xylocaine®), and ibuprofen (Motrin®); has taken OTC Motrin® without adverse reaction” 7. Your signature/date 8. Patient signature/date 9. Witness signature/date CLINICAL NOTE: Example procedure note for therapeutic aspiration of prepatellar bursitis: “Written consent obtained and placed in patient chart. Area was cleansed with betadine solution. Area was prepped and draped in sterile fashion. A 5 mL syringe with 18 gauge needle was inserted into the bursal sac using a shallow approach. The needle was rotated 180° so the bevel was facing the patella bone. Gentle aspiration yielded 4 mL of clear fluid. Knee was wrapped with compression dressing and patient advised to avoid all squatting, jumping, running, and kneeling exercises for 3 days. Patient tolerated procedure well. No complications. Esti

id. Knee was wrapped with compression dressing and patient advised to avoid all squatting, jumping, running, and kneeling exercises for 3 days. Patient tolerated procedure well. No complications. Estimated blood loss: less than 1 mL.” If fluid collected is turbid, murky, or thick, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND MEDICATION. Consider culture of fluid if possible and antibiotics due to probable infectious cause.

175 XXXIV. PATELLOFEMORAL SYNDROME- Overuse injury causing anterior knee pain. Occurs due to abrupt increase in strenuous exercise without appropriate conditioning. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of recent increase in exercise, especially running 2. BMI over 25 or increased load bearing 3. Patellar tenderness, crepitus and popping 4. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (see “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat Foot”) 5. May have a normal exam B. Treatment 1. Modify activities for 4 weeks and only limit those activities that cause pain a. If patient is limping, limit all running, jumping, marching b. If patient always has pain with running that occurs during run, limit running but keep other lower body exercises c. If patient has pain with stairs, limit only squats, step-ups, lunges, and running on hills and uneven terrain. Track running should still continue d. If patient has pain intermittently with running or pain occurs after running, limit sprints, long distances, and running on hills and uneven terrain. Patient may continue light running workouts 2. Ibuprofen (Motrin®) or naproxen (Aleve®, Naprosyn®) for inflammation no

running, limit sprints, long distances, and running on hills and uneven terrain. Patient may continue light running workouts 2. Ibuprofen (Motrin®) or naproxen (Aleve®, Naprosyn®) for inflammation not to exceed 2 weeks 3. Quadriceps strengthening exercises starting with isometric tightening of the quadriceps and slowly adding more strenuous exercises such as wall squats and lunges C. Prevention 1. Progressive, realistic training is key 2. Start new exercise regimen at 70% of current ability level and progress up to 100% challenge after the first two weeks 3. Multiple patients in same unit with overuse injuries such as tendonitis and patellofemoral syndrome warrant evaluation of the unit exercise regimen and intervention by the IDMT XXXV. PATELLAR TENDONITIS “JUMPER’S KNEE”- Caused by chronic inflammation of the patellar tendon due to running and jumping. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain inferior to the patellar pole worse with jumping 2. Aggravated by going up or down stairs and prolonged sitting 3. Tenderness on the inferior pole of the patella or patellar tendon 4. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal

d sitting 3. Tenderness on the inferior pole of the patella or patellar tendon 4. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat foot”) 5. May have normal exam B. Treatment 1. Modify activities to avoid those causing pain (see “Patellofemoral syndrome, above) 2. Ibuprofen (Motrin®) or naproxen (Aleve®, Naprosyn®) for inflammation not to exceed 2 weeks 3. Stretching and strengthening exercises tailored for patellar tendonitis

176 4. Quadriceps strengthening exercises starting with isometric tightening of the quadriceps and slowly adding more strenuous exercises such as wall squats and lunges C. Prevention 1. Progressive, realistic training is key 2. Start new exercise regimen at 70% of current ability level and progress up to 100% challenge after the first two weeks 3. Multiple patients in same unit with overuse injuries such as tendonitis and patellofemoral syndrome warrant evaluation of the unit exercise regimen and intervention by the IDMT XXXVI. SHIN SPLINTS/TIBIAL STRESS FRACTURE- Overuse injuries of the lower leg due to prolonged walking, running, jumping or marching. “Shin splints” generally refer to muscular derangements and “stress fractures” to bony derangements, but clinical evaluation is usually not able to discern the difference. Both conditions occur as gradual progression of activity-related pain resulting from poor body mechanics. Treatment is initially the same, but if patient fails to respond, stress fracture should be considered. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of overuse, increase in exercise regimen, or change in regimen 2. May have poor footwear or history of running on irregular

ond, stress fracture should be considered. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of overuse, increase in exercise regimen, or change in regimen 2. May have poor footwear or history of running on irregular terrain 3. May have history of knee, shin, ankle or foot injuries 4. Females experiencing irregular/absent menses are at higher risk for stress fractures 5. Shin Splints—generalized anterior lower leg pain; tenderness while hopping on the affected leg; pain escalates through the run but usually able to continue 6. Stress Fracture—localized, bony surface pain, cannot complete 10 hops on the affected leg. Pain begins within the first steps of running 7. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear B. Treatment 1. Treat underlying cause (purchase new shoes/boots, add inserts if flat-footed, etc) 2. Ice 10 minutes three times daily (avoid applying ice directly to the skin) 3. Limit activities involving running/jumping 4. Stretching and strengthening rehabilitation exercises to include heel raises, toe raises and squats (as long as activities are painless) 5. Reevaluate after 2 weeks/introduce additional rehabilitation exercises 6. If pain

hening rehabilitation exercises to include heel raises, toe raises and squats (as long as activities are painless) 5. Reevaluate after 2 weeks/introduce additional rehabilitation exercises 6. If pain persists after six weeks, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION CLINICAL NOTE: If tenderness extends into the joint space/tibial plateau or to the medial malleolus, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION. Fractures in these areas are high risk for non-union and require further evaluation. CLINICAL NOTE: Patients experiencing foot numbness, tingling or foot drop/motor deficits should be evaluated for exercise-induced compartment syndrome (see below).

177 XXXVII. EXERCISE INDUCED COMPARTMENT SYNDROME- Increased tissue pressure in a closed muscle compartment that compromises local circulation and neuromuscular function. Occurs when individuals with congenitally tight compartments increase blood flow to lower extremities (by running or marching, for example), which increases pressure within the compartment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain begins after a specific distance or time into the exercise 2. Pain does not resolve as soon as activity ceases, but does resolve with rest 10-60 minutes after stopping the activity 3. Pain during exercise described as severe and debilitating 4. May have numbness/tingling distal to pain and may complain of foot drop or foot “slapping” when pain is severe 5. Exam at rest is usually normal, but may have associated swelling/tenderness along the affected compartment 6. Check peripheral pulses and capillary refill 7. Pain/tenderness often out of proportion to exam findings 8. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat foot” ) B. Treatment 1. Treat underlying cause (purchase new shoes/boots, add inserts if flat-foot

, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat foot” ) B. Treatment 1. Treat underlying cause (purchase new shoes/boots, add inserts if flat-footed, etc) 2. Limit activities involving impact activities for 4 weeks 3. Condition is chronic and may require permanent profile or surgery XXXVIII. MENISCAL TEAR- Disruption in the horseshoe-shaped cushion (meniscus) between the femur and the tibia. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of injury involving weight bearing while twisting 2. Clicking, locking, popping and catching are common 3. Weakness or sudden “giving out” of the knee is common 4. Tenderness with palpation along the joint line B. Treatment 1. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) therapy for three days 2. Crutches for stability if needed (patient should still be able to do some weight-bearing) 3. Pain control for first 5 days: NSAIDs with/without acetaminophen (Tylenol®) 4. For severe pain, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION and consider acetaminophen/oxycodone (Percocet®) or acetaminophen/ hydrocodone (Vicodin®) 5. Avoid bracing unless necessary for pain relief or severe instability ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATIO

TION and consider acetaminophen/oxycodone (Percocet®) or acetaminophen/ hydrocodone (Vicodin®) 5. Avoid bracing unless necessary for pain relief or severe instability ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if numbness, tingling or foot drop persists for greater than 1 hour after activity is stopped, if extremity is cold to touch, or if pulses are absent. Emergent fasciotomy may be required.

178 6. Rehabilitation exercises should begin as soon as movement is tolerated; may start isometric quadriceps exercises immediately 7. Reassess patient weekly for six weeks, adding additional rehabilitation exercises each visit XXXIX. MCL/PCL/LCL/ACL SPRAINS AND TEARS- Trauma causes disruption of one or more of the ligaments of the knee that connects the femur to the tibia. Often accompanied by a meniscal tear. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of trauma, not overuse a. MCL- Caused by sudden change in direction and speed or by direct blow to the lateral knee (ie low football tackle) b. PCL- Direct trauma to the upper anterior tibia when the knee is flexed (ie sliding into a base); injury often perceived as minor and patient continues activity but later may report swelling, deep joint pain, and sensation of instability c. LCL- Less common; caused by blow to the medial knee (ie being kicked in the medial knee by an oncoming soccer player) d. ACL- Direct trauma or hyperextension while foot is planted. May be complicated by meniscal injury; athlete typically falls down grabbing the knee because pain is immediate and limiting after injury; may report sensation that the knee shifted or a

is planted. May be complicated by meniscal injury; athlete typically falls down grabbing the knee because pain is immediate and limiting after injury; may report sensation that the knee shifted or a pop inside the knee 1. Examination a. MCL- swelling unlikely unless meniscus or ACL are involved; decreased flexion and extension compared to the opposite side; no decreased passive ROM; pain/laxity with valgus stress test b. PCL- Pain or no endpoint with posterior drawer test c. LCL- Laxity with varus stress test; gross instability suggests PCL and meniscus disruption d. ACL- Swelling from hemarthrosis; sensation of instability, difficulty or inability to walk; no endpoint with anterior drawer test B. Treatment 1. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) therapy for three days 2. Crutches for stability if needed (patient should still be able to do some weight-bearing) CLINICAL NOTE: For all ligamental injuries, exam will be of limited benefit if begun more than 30 minutes after injury due to muscle spasm, swelling and pain. Observe gait as the patient enters the room. Patient must have relaxed quadriceps and hamstrings to be able to examine thoroughly. ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATI

to muscle spasm, swelling and pain. Observe gait as the patient enters the room. Patient must have relaxed quadriceps and hamstrings to be able to examine thoroughly. ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION is indicated if there is complete inability to bear weight, evidence of deep bruising (hemarthrosis) is present, joint is locked or unable to move, or there is suspected ACL/PCL/MCL/LCL involvement. Routine evacuation is indicated if there is no clinical improvement or if swelling is still substantial after 3 weeks.

179 3. Pain control for first 5 days: NSAIDs with/without acetaminophen (Tylenol®) 4. For severe pain, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION and consider acetaminophen/oxycodone (Percocet®) or acetaminophen/ hydrocodone (Vicodin®) 5. Avoid bracing unless necessary for pain relief or severe instability 6. Rehabilitation exercises should begin as soon as movement is tolerated; may start isometric quadriceps exercises immediately 7. Reassess patient weekly for six weeks, adding additional rehabilitation exercises each visit XL. ANSERINE BURSITIS– Inflammation of the anserine bursa, located on the medial knee several cm below the joint line. Usually cased by poor body mechanics. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Medial knee pain, especially at night 2. Pain specifically located in the anserine bursa (can point with one finger) 3. If pain is diffuse, consider medial collateral ligament or bony abnormality 4. Tenderness of the anserine bursa with direct palpation 5. Minor palpable, localized swelling 6. Medial collateral (valgus) stress test does not aggravate pain 7. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols,

ng 6. Medial collateral (valgus) stress test does not aggravate pain 7. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat foot”) B. Treatment 1. Apply ice to affected area 2-3 times per day 2. Avoid applying ice directly to the skin 3. Sleep with pillow between legs at night to avoid direct pressure on the bursa 4. Modify activities to exclude those involving squats and repetitive knee bending 5. Stretching and strengthening exercises tailored for anserine bursitis Ankle and Foot Disorders XLI. ACHILLES TENDONITIS AND ACHILLES TENDON RUPTURE- Damage to the Achilles tendon due to excess strain, often due to running, jumping, or sudden acceleration or deceleration. Most common when tendon becomes damaged due to trauma or inelastic due to poor stretching regimen for the posterior legs. A. Signs and Symptoms ACTION ALERT: IMMEDIATE EVACUATION should be considered for all ligamental injuries and is required if there is complete inability to bear weight, evidence of deep bruising (hemarthrosis) is present, joint is locked or unable to move, or there is suspected ACL/PCL/MCL/LCL involvement. Routine evacua

if there is complete inability to bear weight, evidence of deep bruising (hemarthrosis) is present, joint is locked or unable to move, or there is suspected ACL/PCL/MCL/LCL involvement. Routine evacuation is indicated if there is no clinical improvement or if swelling is still substantial after 3 weeks. CLINICAL NOTE: Failure to correct underlying gait abnormality will lead to further episodes.

180 1. Pain or stiffness a few centimeters above the posterior calcaneus 2. History of increased training regimen or activity after sedentary period 3. Whereas ankle sprains tend to occur when landing, Achilles tendonitis and rupture tend to occur with push-off 4. Sensation of being struck in the heel or hearing a “pop” followed by severe pain 5. Risk factors include poor flexibility, cold weather training, misalignment, poor running mechanics, increased speed training or hill work, inappropriate footwear, and leg length discrepancy 6. Unable to bear weight 7. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat foot”) B. Treatment 1. Achilles rupture is based on Thompson’s test and inability to plantar-flex; not all Achilles ruptures are painful 2. If Achilles tendon is ruptured, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 3. If no rupture is suspected: a. Limit activities causing pain b. NSAIDs; consider ibuprofen (Motrin®) or naproxen (Aleve®, Naprosyn®) for 7-10 days only c. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) therapy for 3 days d. Stretching and strengthening exercises tailored for Achilles tendo

profen (Motrin®) or naproxen (Aleve®, Naprosyn®) for 7-10 days only c. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) therapy for 3 days d. Stretching and strengthening exercises tailored for Achilles tendonitis XLII. ANKLE SPRAIN/FRACTURE - Ankle sprains are a disruption of the stabilization ligaments of the ankle. Ankle fractures occur when the distal tibia or fibula are fractured. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe sprain or fracture a. Pain, swelling, tenderness and ecchymosis b. Mild to moderate joint instability (lack of endpoint with passive range of motion or anterior ankle drawer test) c. May not be able to bear weight 2. For milder sprains, patients should be able to partially bear weight CLINICAL NOTE: Thompson’s Test- examine the Achilles tendon while patient is lying prone with feet hanging off the table. Squeeze the belly of the gastrocnemius, which should plantar-flex the foot. No movement is diagnostic of an Achilles tendon rupture. CLINICAL NOTE: Ottawa Ankle Rules define which patients require x-rays. Routine medical evacuation is indicated for x-rays are indicated if any of the following conditions are met: 1. Unable to bear weight at the time of incident 2. Unable to bea

patients require x-rays. Routine medical evacuation is indicated for x-rays are indicated if any of the following conditions are met: 1. Unable to bear weight at the time of incident 2. Unable to bear weight at the time of examination 3. Pain with palpation of the inferior or posterior edge of the medial malleolus 4. Pain with palpation of the inferior or posterior edge of the lateral malleolus

181 B. Treatment 1. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) therapy for first three days after injury 2. Crutches for stability 3. Continuous NSAIDs for pain control and anti-inflammatory effect for no more than 10 days 4. ROM exercises to reduce risk of “frozen ankle”. Exercises include circles, plantar flexion/dorsiflexion and should begin as soon as pain permits and continue until patient returns to full activity 5. Splints and braces allow for improvement in weight bearing and offer good protection against injury; patient should remove brace three to five times daily to complete ROM exercises 6. Tight-laced combat boots work well if commercial splints/braces are not available 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION if neurovascular impairment exists, or if fracture with gross deformity has occurred. Splint extremity in position of comfort for transportation unless otherwise directed by preceptor 8. If ankle has been sprained before, double the rehabilitation time XLIII. PLANTAR FASCIITIS- Inflammation where the plantar fascia attaches to the base of the calcaneus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe pain with first steps of the morning with symptoms improving with wal

I. PLANTAR FASCIITIS- Inflammation where the plantar fascia attaches to the base of the calcaneus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe pain with first steps of the morning with symptoms improving with walking 2. Risk factors include obesity, frequent high-heel wear, prolonged standing or jumping, flat feet or high arches, old footwear, and running on hard surfaces 3. Patients usually have tight hamstrings which exacerbate the pressure on the plantar fascia insertion 4. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches and shoes for unequal tread wear B. Treatment 1. Limit activities causing pain, especially running and jumping 2. Suggest frozen bottle massage to bottom of foot BID 3. Correct problems with gait or footwear 4. NSAIDs for no more than 14 days 5. Exercises include dorsiflexion of the foot and hamstring stretches before getting out of bed in the morning and after sitting for prolonged periods 6. Discourage walking bare footed or flat-soled shoe wear; patients should have cushioned footwear even when in the shower 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if symptoms do not improve after 4 weeks of conservative treatment XLIV. STRESS FRACTURE OF THE FOOT- “Marching

cushioned footwear even when in the shower 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if symptoms do not improve after 4 weeks of conservative treatment XLIV. STRESS FRACTURE OF THE FOOT- “Marching fracture” is an overuse injury of the metatarsal shaft caused by repetitive stress to the forefoot from running, jumping or marching. A. Signs and Symptoms

182 1. Gradually worsening forefoot pain that initially occurs with a specific activity, but progresses to a poorly-defined forefoot pain induced by minimal activity 2. Point tenderness along the metatarsal shaft 3. Risk factors include tight gastrocnemius, poorly fitting shoes, osteopenia associated with amenorrhea, obesity, or increased load bearing, increase in training volume or change in training surfaces 4. Check for leg length discrepancy, fallen or high arches, and check shoes for unequal tread wear (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols, Flat foot”) 5. Females with disordered eating and/or amenorrhea are at increased risk for stress fractures B. Treatment 1. Replace old or worn out running shoes and boots (life of a running shoe is six months or 500 miles, whichever happens first) 2. Identify and correct the underlying risk factors 3. Limit activities causing pain for 6-8 weeks 4. Crutches if pain occurs with walking 5. Consider acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain control XLV. PES PLANUS (“FLAT FEET”)– Congenital or traumatic (“fallen arches”) loss of the arch of the foot. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Feet display little or no anatomic arch 2. Have patient face you and stand at attenti

US (“FLAT FEET”)– Congenital or traumatic (“fallen arches”) loss of the arch of the foot. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Feet display little or no anatomic arch 2. Have patient face you and stand at attention; individuals with fallen arches will appear as if their medial ankles are close to the ground or the lateral foot is rolled out B. Treatment 1. If no foot, ankle, knee or back pain, no treatment necessary CLINICAL NOTE: Have barefoot patient face away and rise up on tiptoes. If there is no arching visible in the midfoot, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR for further evaluation. Alternatively, have the patient wet their feet on a damp towel, then step on to a piece of paper. The midfoot width in contact with the paper should be approximately ½ the width of the total midfoot. CLINICAL NOTE: if fracture is suspected, hold each toe in line with its metatarsal and load the toe by pushing it in towards the metatarsal. If the patient has soft tissue injury alone, this maneuver should not produce pain, but if a bony abnormality is present, the maneuver will cause pain. CLINICAL NOTE: Stress fractures are difficult to observe on x-ray and findings may not be visible until 2-6 weeks after the fract

n, but if a bony abnormality is present, the maneuver will cause pain. CLINICAL NOTE: Stress fractures are difficult to observe on x-ray and findings may not be visible until 2-6 weeks after the fracture occurs. Physical examination is more sensitive. Untreated stress fractures can progress to complete fractures of the metatarsal.

183 2. If patient complains of lower-body musculoskeletal issues, counsel patient regarding stretching of the gastrocnemius and foot 3. Recommend appropriate footwear- “motion control” style running shoes prevent overpronation and associated lower extremity injury 4. Consider arch supports XLVI. TARSOMETATARSAL FRACTURE (“LISFRANC” FRACTURE)- A fracture-dislocation of the metatarsals from the tarsals caused by forceful pronation of the foot. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of traumatic pronation of the foot such as stepping awkwardly in a hole 2. Pain with direct palpation of the tarsometatarsal joints 3. Dorsal and plantar ecchymosis in the midfoot 4. Pain with passive pronation of the transverse tarsal joint B. Treatment 1. Crutches; no weight bearing 2. RICE therapy 3. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION if Lisfranc fracture is suspected based on mechanism of injury and physical exam 5. Lisfranc fractures have a high rate of poor healing and severe morbidity XLVII. LEG LENGTH DISCREPANCY– Congenital condition frequently missed on initial military physicals that can lead to permanent damage of the feet, knees, hips, and back if not corr

nd severe morbidity XLVII. LEG LENGTH DISCREPANCY– Congenital condition frequently missed on initial military physicals that can lead to permanent damage of the feet, knees, hips, and back if not corrected. Frequently a contributing factor to many conditions listed above. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pattern of back and lower extremity overuse injuries 2. Uneven wear on shoes 3. History or exam suggestive of scoliosis 4. Have patient stand at attention facing away from you and observe level of iliac crests for unevenness 5. Have patient lay supine and measure from anterior superior iliac spine along medial knee to tip of medial malleolus; 0.5-1.0 cm difference is normal B. Treatment 1. For discrepancies up to 2 cm, use heel cup or wedge in shorter leg’s shoe 2. Discrepancies greater than 2 cm require podiatrist evaluation upon return to home station

184 Chapter 13: Mental Health/Substance Abuse Protocols Mood Disorders I. DEPRESSION- Depression is extremely common with an incidence of up to 30% of military primary care patients having some depressive symptoms. Depression may be the expression of genetic or developmental factors or psychological stresses. Rates of depression are especially high in combatants who have sustained relatively minor trauma such as mild concussion/traumatic brain injury. Military personnel frequently avoid medical attention for depression due to fear of career repercussions. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Mood varies from mild sadness to intense feelings of guilt, worthlessness and hopelessness 2. Difficulty in thinking, inability to concentrate, rumination and lack of decisiveness 3. Loss of interest, diminished involvement in work and recreation 4. Somatic complaints such as headache, sleep problems, fatigue, appetite change and decreased sexual drive 5. Patients appear fatigued, frequent crying, sighing, poor hygiene, missed appointments or tardiness 6. Severe depression is characterized by anxiety, social withdrawal, and suicidal ideations 7. Ask about mania episodes (see “Bipolar Disorder”) to rule out

r hygiene, missed appointments or tardiness 6. Severe depression is characterized by anxiety, social withdrawal, and suicidal ideations 7. Ask about mania episodes (see “Bipolar Disorder”) to rule out manic- depression B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others a. If you have ANY suspicion patient may cause harm, immediately assign nonmedical attendant to one-to-one direct observation b. Do not allow patient out of sight (even in the bathroom) and do not allow them access to anything potentially harmful c. Rule out any organic defect (check thyroid, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 3. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination findings and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR based on “9-symptom questionnaire” guidance below CLINICAL NOTE: The management of most psychiatric conditions will be determined by mental health professionals, not the IDMT. Administer all drugs; DO NOT give them to the patient for self-administration.
tal health professionals, not the IDMT. Administer all drugs; DO NOT give them to the patient for self-administration.

185 4. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with Haloperidol (Haldol®) 10 mg oral concentrate or 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 5. If evacuation is required a. Advise patient’s Command of the need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis will be determined by mental health provider b. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations 6. If evacuation is not required a. Have daily/weekly talks with patient; encourage patient to speak freely b. Schedule adequate time, don not appear rushed or hurried c. Increase quantity and intensity of exercise, be outdoors during hours of light as much as possible, increase water intake, regulate sleep schedule, start a multivitamin d. Avoid all substances that could alter mood, including herbs, supplements, caffeine, and especially tobacco and alcohol II. ADJUSTMENT DISORDER WITH DEPRESSED MOOD- Occurs within 3 months of the stressor and causes significant impairment in social or occupational functioning. Care is focused on rapid return to functionality and focusing on present/future r

MOOD- Occurs within 3 months of the stressor and causes significant impairment in social or occupational functioning. Care is focused on rapid return to functionality and focusing on present/future rather than the past. CLINICAL NOTE: Screen all potentially depressed patients with “9-Symptom Questionnaire”. Rate each question on a scale of 0-3, with 0 meaning not at all, 1 means several days, 2 means more than half the days, and 3 means almost every day: 1. Little interest or pleasure in doing things 2. Feeling down, depressed or hopeless 3. Trouble sleeping or sleeping too much 4. Feeling tired or little energy 5. Poor appetite or overeating 6. Feeling bad about self 7. Trouble concentrating on things 8. Moving or speaking slowly or being restless 9. Thoughts that you would be better off dead 10. Scores greater than 15, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION for further evaluation 11. Scores between 4 and 15, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION ACTION ALERT: If you believe patient may be a threat to themselves or others, patient may require physical and/or chemical restraints (sedation). Frequently reassess patient for risk of suicide or homicide. Even if a patient is not

ient may be a threat to themselves or others, patient may require physical and/or chemical restraints (sedation). Frequently reassess patient for risk of suicide or homicide. Even if a patient is not an immediate risk to self or others, depressed individuals can cause inadvertent harm to self or others through inattention and neglect. See “Suicidal Gestures/Attempts”.

Ask about mania symptoms (see “Bipolar Disorders”) B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Do not allow patient out of sight (even in the bathroom) and do not allow patient access to anything potentially harmful 3. Rule out any organic causes (check thyroid, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination findings and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR based on “9-symptom questionnaire” guidance 5. Treatment emphasis is on keeping patient engaged in daily activities and pla

he history and physical examination findings and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR based on “9-symptom questionnaire” guidance 5. Treatment emphasis is on keeping patient engaged in daily activities and planning for the future 6. Encourage relaxation techniques (journaling, creative outlets, sports, yoga, meditation) 7. Have daily/weekly talks with patient; encourage patient to speak freely 8. Scheule adequate time, don not appear rushed or hurried 9. BIPOLAR DISORDER (“MANIC DEPRESSION”)- Episodic mood shifts between mania, major depression, hypomania and mixed mood states. The ability of bipolar disorder to mimic aspects of many other major mental health disorders and its high comorbidity with substance abuse can make the initial diagnosis of bipolar difficult. A. Signs and Symptoms-Manic episode 1. Extravagance, elation, hyperactivity, over-involvement in life activities, increased irritabilit

tance abuse can make the initial diagnosis of bipolar difficult. A. Signs and Symptoms-Manic episode 1. Extravagance, elation, hyperactivity, over-involvement in life activities, increased irritability, hypersexuality, flight of ideas, easy distractibility and little need for sleep 2. Pressured speech, hyperverbosity 3. History of overspending, quitting jobs, quick (short-lived) marriages and sexual promiscuity 4. May include gross delusions, severe paranoia, and auditory hallucinations 5. Acute onset lasting several days to months 6. Manic episodes typically shorter than depressive episodes B. Signs and Symptoms- Depressive Episode 1. See “Depression” and “Adjustment Disorder” above

187 2. Bipolar patients have high incidence of attempted and completed suicide and high incidence of substance abuse 3. Must ask about mania symptoms for any patient with depressive symptoms to avoid missing diagnosis of bipolar disorder C. Treatment 1. If you have ANY suspicion patient may cause harm, immediately assign nonmedical attendant to one-to-one direct observation b. Do not allow patient out of sight (even in the bathroom) and do not allow patient access to anything potentially harmful 3. Rule out any organic causes (check thyroid, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination findings and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR based on “9-symptom questionnaire” guidance 5. Treatment emphasis is on keeping patient engaged in daily activities and planning for the future 6. Encourage relaxation techniques (journaling, creative outlets, sports, yoga, meditation) 7. Have daily/weekly talks with patient; encourage patient to speak freely 8.

HOMESICKNESS- Distress and functional impairment caused by actual or anticipation of separation from loved ones and familiar situations. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Recurrent, intrusive thoughts of home that impact work or daily functioning 2. Risk factors include little or no prior experience away from home, presently insecure home environment, young age, negative attitude towards separation, no input into the decision to be separated 3. May have behavior issues such as anger, fighting, irrational behavior, tardiness, insubordination 4. May have somatic complaints such as headaches, upset stomach, fatigue B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsy

ave behavior issues such as anger, fighting, irrational behavior, tardiness, insubordination 4. May have somatic complaints such as headaches, upset stomach, fatigue B. Treatment 1.
