GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER- Most common anxiety disorder; initial manifestations appear at age 20-35 and is slightly higher in women. The two primary components are psychological

ative Disorders V. GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER- Most common anxiety disorder; initial manifestations appear at age 20-35 and is slightly higher in women. The two primary components are psychological and somatic. Consider differential diagnosis of illegal drug and steroid use. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Symptoms of anxiety are present more days than not and are present for at least 6 months 2. Apprehension, insomnia, worry, irritability, difficulty concentrating 3. Somatic complaints that include cardiac (chest pain, palpitations, etc), GI (nausea, vomiting, gas, diarrhea, constipation, etc) and neurologic (headaches, numbness, tingling, weakness, pseudoseizures, etc) 4. Ask about supplement use- many weight loss preparations and muscle building preparations induce anxiety B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 3. Rule out any organic defect (check thyroid, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION. Patient will req

thyroid, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION. Patient will require further workup before diagnosis of anxiety can be made 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 2 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. Advise patient’s Command of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider

189 7. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations VI. PANIC DISORDER- Short lived, recurrent, unpredictable episodes of intense anxiety accompanied by severe physical symptoms. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Agoraphobia (irrational fear of public or open places or embarrassing situations where escape will not be feasible), palpitations, paresthesias (pricking or tingling feeling, such as “limb going to sleep”), nausea, dyspnea, headaches, choking, bloating, tachycardia, dizziness, claustrophobia (fear of small spaces without escape), or “smothered” feeling 2. Feeling of impending doom 3. Differentiating panic disorder from physical disorders (such as myocardial infarction) can often be made by the patient’s description of the “psychic pain” that precipitates episodes 4. Symptoms resolve completely in less than an hour 5. Ask about supplement use- many weight loss preparations and muscle building preparations induce anxiety B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self o

preparations induce anxiety B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 3. Rule out any organic causes (check thyroid, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION, patient will require further evaluation before a diagnosis of panic disorder can be made 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider 7. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations VII. OBSESSIVE-COMPULSIVE DISORDER (“OCD”)- A mental condition with predominant features of obsessions (constant recurrent intrusive thoughts) and compulsions (intense need to perform repetitive actions such as door locking, handwashi

(“OCD”)- A mental condition with predominant features of obsessions (constant recurrent intrusive thoughts) and compulsions (intense need to perform repetitive actions such as door locking, handwashing, ordering of objects, etc.) A. Signs and Symptoms CLINICAL NOTE: Panic disorder tends to be familial with onset usually under age 25. Female to male ratio is 2:1. Patients with recurrent panic disorder become demoralized, hypochondriacal (afraid that they have severe or fatal medical conditions), agoraphobic (fear of public situations from which they can’t easily escape) and depressed. Suicide attempts and major depression are often associated with panic disorder.

190 1. Irrational ideas or impulses persistently intrude into awareness 2. Actions recognized as absurd and are resisted, but anxiety is relieved only by ritualistic performance of the action or by deliberate contemplation of the intruding thought or idea 3. Compulsions are time consuming, cause significant distress, and produce shame 4. Patients are usually predictable, orderly, conscientious and intelligent 5. May be associated with tics, Tourette syndrome or eating disorders B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Examine for signs of alcohol or substance abuse 3. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION; patient will require further evaluation before a diagnosis of obsessive-compulsive disorder can be made 7. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosi

quire further evaluation before a diagnosis of obsessive-compulsive disorder can be made 7. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider 8. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations VIII. ANOREXIA NERVOSA AND BULIMIA NERVOSA- Obsession with weight loss leads to recurrent intrusive thoughts about food, weight loss, exercise, and ritualistic eating behaviors focused on secrecy and denial. Although disorders manifest themselves with regards to food and eating behaviors, source of disorders are an attempt to gain control over their environment. A. Signs and Symptoms- Anorexia Nervosa 1. Refusal to maintain body weight at or above a minimally normal weight for age and height 2. Intense fear of gaining weight or becoming fat despite being underweight 3. Distorted perception of body weight and shape 4. Amenorrhea B. Signs and Symptoms- Bulimia Nervosa 1. Recurrent episodes of binge eating and inappropriate compensatory behavior to prevent weight gain (laxative abuse, induced emesis, exces

d shape 4. Amenorrhea B. Signs and Symptoms- Bulimia Nervosa 1. Recurrent episodes of binge eating and inappropriate compensatory behavior to prevent weight gain (laxative abuse, induced emesis, excessive exercise) 2. Binge/purge cycles average at least twice per week for three months C. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Examine for signs of alcohol or substance abuse 3. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination

191 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION; patient will require further evaluation before a diagnosis of eating disorder can be made 7. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider IX. PHOBIC DISORDER- Clinically significant irrational fear of an object or situation that leads to dysfunctional avoidance of the stimulus. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Excessive fear triggered by specific object or situation such as performance anxiety, bridges, heights, spiders, water, or agorophobia (fear of not being able to get away from a situation, such as when one is in a crowd) 2. Exposure to stimulus or even mention of the stimulus evokes an immediate anxiety response and may be associated with severe panic attacks 3. The individual recognizes the fear is excessive or unreasonable 4. The fear causes significant distress and interference in daily functioning B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive his

fear is excessive or unreasonable 4. The fear causes significant distress and interference in daily functioning B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 3. Rule out any organic defect (check thyroid, examine for signs of alcohol or substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. If evacuation is required i. Advise patient’s commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider ii. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations b. If evacuation is not required i. Treatment is based on desensitization to the stimulus that causes the phobia ii. Have frequent talks with the patient iii. Acknowledge negative reactions X. DISSOCIATIVE DISORD

tion is not required i. Treatment is based on desensitization to the stimulus that causes the phobia ii. Have frequent talks with the patient iii. Acknowledge negative reactions X. DISSOCIATIVE DISORDER- A disunion of mind of which the patient is not aware. Examples include dual personality, dissociative fugue, somnambulism (sleepwalking), selective amnesia, and depersonalization. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Reaction often precipitated by emotional crisis

192 2. Repression, isolation, limited scope of attention 3. Dissociative symptoms result in reduced anxiety B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others a. Patients with dissociative symptoms typically do not desire to hurt themselves because their dissociative symptoms take away reduce their anxiety but they may act irrationally, erratically, or destructively b. If you have ANY suspicion patient may cause harm to self or others, immediately assign nonmedical attendant to one-to-one direct observation c. Do not allow patient out of sight (even in the bathroom) and do not allow the patient access to anything potentially harmful 3. Rule out any organic causes (check thyroid, evaluate for alcohol and substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evalu

s severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider 7. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations Somataform Disorders (in these disorders physical symptoms are not intentionally induced) XI. CONVERSION DISORDER- Conversion of psychic conflict into physical sensory or motor symptoms manifesting as a single, continuous or recurrent symptom. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sudden blindness or paralysis are common presentations 2. Symptoms are not intentional or for personal gain, and can not be explained by a medical condition 3. History of coping through repression and isolation 4. History of serious precipitating emotional event (combat or natural disaster) 5. May occur when others present with same symptoms (ie several members of unit were exposed to bright flash and blinded; patient not exposed but develops sudden onset blindness) 6. Often associated with other ps

when others present with same symptoms (ie several members of unit were exposed to bright flash and blinded; patient not exposed but develops sudden onset blindness) 6. Often associated with other psychopathology B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 3. Rule out any organic defect (check thyroid, evaluate for alcohol and substance abuse)

193 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION; patient will require further evaluation before a diagnosis of conversion disorder can be made 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider 7. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations XII. SOMATIZATION DISORDER- Syndrome of physical symptoms without medical basis resulting in high utilization of medical care without meeting diagnostic criteria for specific illnesses. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of long-standing medical utilization for multiple nonspecific complaints 2. Extensive workups that reveal normal exams and testing 3. Common complaints include headaches, dysmenorrhea, nausea/vomiting, bloating/diarrhea, back pain, symptoms of the sex organs, amnesia, lump in throat, dyspnea, pain in extremities

xams and testing 3. Common complaints include headaches, dysmenorrhea, nausea/vomiting, bloating/diarrhea, back pain, symptoms of the sex organs, amnesia, lump in throat, dyspnea, pain in extremities 4. History of multiple symptoms, multiple referrals, and no solid diagnosis 5. Frequently has anxiety, panic, and depression components 6. Disorder is a reflection of maladaptive coping techniques; patient uses symptoms to avoid responsibility or intimacy B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others 3. Rule out any organic defect (check thyroid, evaluate for alcohol and substance abuse) 4. Establish probable diagnosis based on the history and physical examination and CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 5. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 6. Advise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider 7. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous di

dvise patient’s Commander of evacuation and need for further evaluation; do not suggest diagnosis- diagnosis is determined by mental health provider 7. Establish nonmedical attendant for continuous direct observation of patient, even if patient does not report suicidal or homicidal ideations Personality Disorders XIII. PERSONALITY DISORDERS- Recurrent, maladaptive behavior resulting from a long history dating to childhood. Patients with personality disorders generally have low self-esteem and lack of confidence. They exhibit difficulties with interpersonal relationships or social

194 functioning and have a tendency to blame others. Most develop anxiety and depression when the maladaptive behavior fails. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. All personality disorders share common symptoms of frequent mood swings, feeling there is nothing wrong with their behavior, need to be center of attention, feeling the external world is to blame for their behaviors and feelings 2. Paranoid- Defensive, oversensitive, secretive, suspicious, hyper-alert, with limited emotional response 3. Schizoid- Shy, introverted, withdrawn, avoids close relationships 4. Obsessive Compulsive- Perfectionistic, egocentric, indecisive, with rigid thought patters and need for control 5. Histrionic (hysterical)- Dependent, immature, seductive, egocentric, vain, emotionally labile 6. Schizotypal- Superstitious, magical thinking, socially isolated, suspicious, with limited interpersonal ability, eccentric behaviors and odd speech 7. Narcissistic- Exhibitionist, grandiose, preoccupied with power, lacks interest in others, excessive demands for attention (often associated with histrionic behavior) 8. Avoidant- Fears rejection, hyper-reacts to rejection and failure, with poor social endeavors and low self este

in others, excessive demands for attention (often associated with histrionic behavior) 8. Avoidant- Fears rejection, hyper-reacts to rejection and failure, with poor social endeavors and low self esteem 9. Dependent- Passive, over-accepting, unable to make decisions, lacks confidence, poor self esteem 10. Antisocial- Selfish, callous, promiscuous, impulsive, unable to learn from experience, has legal problems 11. Borderline- Impulsive; has unstable and intense interpersonal relationships; is suffused with anger, fear and guilt; lack self control and self-fulfillment; has identity problems and affective instability; is suicidal or self-mutilating; aggressive behavior, feelings of emptiness and occasional psychotic decompensation. This group has a high drug abuse rate and up to 80% of hospitalized borderline patients make a suicide attempt at some time after treatment B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION 2. Avoid all medications, especially benzodiazepines such as diazepam (Valium®) unless prescribed by a clinician who has evaluated the patient 3. Antipsychotics such as haloperidol (Haldol®) for chemical restraint may be required for emergent conditions 4. If the

azepam (Valium®) unless prescribed by a clinician who has evaluated the patient 3. Antipsychotics such as haloperidol (Haldol®) for chemical restraint may be required for emergent conditions 4. If the patient is severely agitated or experiencing a psychotic break (disconnected from reality) administer haloperidol (Haldol®) 5 mg IM CLINICAL NOTE: IDMTs do not diagnose or treat personality disorders. They are mentioned here as a reference to recognize behavior and guide referrals.

195 Acute Stress Conditions XIV. POST TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (“PTSD”)- PTSD is the complex cognitive and behavioral effects of psychological trauma. PTSD syndrome characterized by “re- experiencing” a traumatic event, with decreased responsiveness and avoidance of current events associated with the trauma. PTSD is more common when the event is associated with a physical injury. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Hyper-arousal, intrusive thoughts, nightmares, impulsivity, hyper-alertness, startle reactions, illusions, sleep problems, flashbacks, difficulty concentrating, impaired social functioning and social withdrawal 2. Attempts to avoid thoughts or flashbacks may result in fleeing or combativeness 3. Symptoms may be precipitated or exacerbated by events that are a reminder of the original stress 4. Onset of symptoms is often weeks to months after the initial stressful event 5. Symptoms frequently arise after a long latent period B. Treatment 1. Perform a neuropsychiatric evaluation of the patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others a. If you have ANY suspicion patient may cause harm to self or others, immediatel

patient (comprehensive history interview and physical examination) 2. Assess patient’s risk for harm to self or others a. If you have ANY suspicion patient may cause harm to self or others, immediately assign nonmedical attendant to one-to-one direct observation b. Do not allow patient out of sight (even in the bathroom) and do not expose patient to anything potentially lethal 3. Establish history of exposure to a life-threatening event followed by intrusive or avoidance symptoms 4. Rule out any organic defect (check thyroid, rule out current traumatic brain injury, examine for evidence of alcohol or substance abuse) 5. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 6. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible CLINICAL NOTE: For diagnosis of PTSD, the following criteria must be met: 1. Severe traumatic event causing intense fear or helplessness (rape, capture, torture, etc) 2. Symptoms in each category of: a. Re-experiencing the event in flashbacks b. Avoidance of triggers c. “Jumpy” or on edge 3. Social or occupational impairment due to symptoms 4. Symptoms and functional impairment la

ach category of: a. Re-experiencing the event in flashbacks b. Avoidance of triggers c. “Jumpy” or on edge 3. Social or occupational impairment due to symptoms 4. Symptoms and functional impairment lasting more than one month after the traumatic event ends

196 XV. COMBAT OPERATIONAL STRESS REACTION (“ACUTE STRESS DISORDER”)- Traumatic event directly witnessed or indirectly experienced by an individual or group that leads to a maladaptive behavioral response. The Air Force takes these exposures very seriously and treats them with a comprehensive team approach called the “Traumatic Stress Response Team”. At a minimum, the team includes mental health providers and technicians, spiritual leaders, and the Airmen and Family Readiness Center. Medical records are not maintained and treatment is confidential unless patient is actively homicidal or suicidal. Treatment focuses on fostering resilience, individual responsibility and self-esteem, and unit cohesion. See AFI 44-153 for more information. XVI. SUICIDAL OR HOMICIDAL GESTURES/ATTEMPTS- Individuals become suicidal when they perceive stressors as overwhelming and permanent and they desire to escape the stressors by ending their lives. Individuals become homicidal when they perceive stressors as overwhelming and permanent and desire to hurt those whom they believe are the source of the stressors. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Start assessment by evaluating patient’s responses to the 9-symptom q

as overwhelming and permanent and desire to hurt those whom they believe are the source of the stressors. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Start assessment by evaluating patient’s responses to the 9-symptom questionnaire for severe depression (see below) 2. If you believe the patient may be at risk for harming self or others, even if they don’t report wanting to hurt themselves, assess suicide/homicide risk through the following criteria: a. Are they thinking about killing self or others (suicidal/homicidal ideation)? If they were going to hurt somebody or themselves, how would they do it (plan)? Do they wish they could (intent)? ACTION ALERT: Therapy should begin as soon as possible after the traumatic event and should be kept brief with quick return to unit and regular activities. Recognizing symptoms and early intervention after a traumatic event is the key to successful treatment. CLINICAL NOTE: Screen all potentially depressed patients with “9-Symptom Questionnaire”. Rate each question on a scale of 0-3, with 0 meaning not at all, 1 means several days, 2 means more than half the days, and 3 means almost every day: 1. Little interest or pleasure in doing things 2. Feeling down, depress

a scale of 0-3, with 0 meaning not at all, 1 means several days, 2 means more than half the days, and 3 means almost every day: 1. Little interest or pleasure in doing things 2. Feeling down, depressed or hopeless 3. Trouble sleeping or sleeping too much 4. Feeling tired or little energy 5. Poor appetite or overeating 6. Feeling bad about self 7. Trouble concentrating on things 8. Moving or speaking slowly or being restless 9. Thoughts that you would be better off dead

197 b. Do they have access to a means to commit suicide or homicide and the lethality of those means (weapons, pills, etc)? c. Do they have psychotic symptoms such as command hallucinations (“the voice in my head is telling me to kill him”), or severe anxiety? d. Is there any present or history of alcohol or substance abuse? e. Is there history of prior attempts and how close they came to completion? f. Is there family history of suicide or recent exposure to suicide? 3. The patient may make statements indicating lack of future thinking (“When I’m gone…”) 4. The patient may make statements indicating intense desire to avoid activities of daily living (“I wish I would never wake up….”) 5. The patient may have disproportionate anger to relatively minor stressors (shattering a computer monitor when the network system is slow) 6. The patient may take routine frustrations personally and then generalize them into statements of low self-worth (“I never pass the training pretest. I’m worthless to the Air Force”) 7. The patient may give away items of high personal value or seem disinterested when something of high personal value is damaged; may destroy important personal artifacts such as p

to the Air Force”) 7. The patient may give away items of high personal value or seem disinterested when something of high personal value is damaged; may destroy important personal artifacts such as pictures of family, etc. B. Treatment 1. If patient talks of committing suicide or homicide a. Initiate 24-hour 1-to-1 direct surveillance with patient in protective custody b. Remove all of patient’s belongings to a secure location c. Prevent access to potentially harmful items d. Arrange for immediate evacuation to a medical facility for specialized care e. Employ appropriate physical and/or chemical restraint if necessary, until evacuation can occur f. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 2. If patient has attempted suicide a. Render emergency treatment required to stabilize patient (treat for drug overdose, physical injury, etc. See “Emergency Protocols”) b. Initiate 24-hour surveillance with patient in protective custody c. Employ appropriate physical and/or chemical restraint if necessary, until evacuation can occur d. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with di

eillance with patient in protective custody c. Employ appropriate physical and/or chemical restraint if necessary, until evacuation can occur d. If the patient is severely agitated then subdue with diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IM; CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to use if possible 3. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION Substance Abuse XVII. OPIATE (NARCOTIC) INTOXICATION/OVERDOSE- Includes many prescription and recreational sedatives including heroin, opium, morphine, hydrocodone, oxycodone,

198 Percocet®, Vicodin®, Tylenol #3®, and Tylox®. If overdose of prescribed substance containing acetaminophen (Tylenol®) is suspected, be alert for possible severe liver damage. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dissociation, amnesia, confusion, apathy 2. Bradycardia, severe respiratory depression 3. Fixed, constricted pupils, decreased bowel sounds 4. Hypothermia B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Support respirations, be prepared to intubate for deteriorating respiratory function, administer CPR if necessary b. Administer oxygen 15 LPM by non-rebreather mask for respiratory distress, may adjust according to patient comfort as needed c. Establish IV access d. Attach EKG if available and continually monitor; use waveform capnography to monitor respirations if available e. Administer naloxone hydrochloride (Narcan®) 0.4 mg IV STAT; then, if patient is having severe respiratory distress or is not breathing, administer Narcan® 0.2 mg IV every 2 to 3 minutes until breathing and alertness are restored or until three doses have been administered NOTE: may give Narcan® at double the dose through endotracheal tube if IV access is not available 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION

ored or until three doses have been administered NOTE: may give Narcan® at double the dose through endotracheal tube if IV access is not available 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Do not perform gastric lavage or administer activated charcoal unless directed by preceptor XVIII. OPIATE WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME- Unpleasant but rarely life-threatening side effects when chronic opiate users discontinue the drug. Seen in active duty populations primarily in chronic pain patients who are taking daily medications containing narcotics such as Percocet® or Vicodin®. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Restlessness, rhinorrhea, lacrimation, myalgias, arthralgias 2. Incapacitating nausea/vomiting/cramping/diarrhea 3. Pupillary dilation, tachycardia, abnormally elevated or decreased blood pressure (decreased if severe diarrhea/emesis) 4. Increased bowel sounds, yawning, and piloerection (goosebumps) 5. Opiate withdrawal is rarely deadly, although patients will assert that they will die without treatment ACTION ALERT: Due to the short half-life of Narcan®, the dose of Narcan® may need to be repeated. If patient responds, monitor closely to determine if additional doses are needed. If the

out treatment ACTION ALERT: Due to the short half-life of Narcan®, the dose of Narcan® may need to be repeated. If patient responds, monitor closely to determine if additional doses are needed. If there is no improvement in patient’s condition after three doses of Narcan®, consider other sources of patient’s symptoms. Narcan® is of no value in treating other intoxicants such as alcohol, barbiturates, cocaine, and LSD.

199 B. Treatment 1. May treat diarrhea with loperamide (Immodium®) 2. May treat nausea/vomiting with ondansetron (Zofran®) 3. If patient is severely agitated or violent, may give diazepam (Valium®) 10 mg PO or 5 mg IM; may repeat 5 mg PO every 8 hours PRN C. Prevention 1. As an IDMT, never prescribe narcotics for chronic pain 2. Patients taking as few as 1-2 daily pills containing narcotics can quickly develop tolerance and withdrawal symptoms if withheld XIX. BARBITURATES AND TRANQUILIZER INTOXICATION/OVERDOSE- Often prescribed as sleeping pills, muscle relaxers, and anti-anxiety medications. Includes phenobarbital (Donnatal®), lorazepam (Ativan®), diazepam (Valium®), alprazolam (Xanax®). Street drugs generally classified as “downers”. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Sleep, stupor, sedation, coma 2. Respiratory depression 3. Short-term memory impairment, inability to form new memories 4. Ataxia, impaired psychomotor skills 5. New onset sleepwalking or complex behaviors such as driving when the patient is not fully awake and after which the patient has no memory B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Support respirations, be prepared to intubate for deteriorating respiratory function, admini

he patient is not fully awake and after which the patient has no memory B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Support respirations, be prepared to intubate for deteriorating respiratory function, administer CPR if necessary b. Administer oxygen 15 LPM by non-rebreather mask for respiratory distress, may adjust according to patient comfort as needed c. Establish IV access d. Attach EKG if available and continually monitor 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider administering flumazenil (Ramazicon®) if available b. Do not perform gastric lavage or administer activated charcoal unless directed by preceptor CLINICAL NOTE: Opioids have little direct effect on the heart; they usually cause death by severe respiratory depression. If adequate ventilator support is provided, the patient will likely recover, even from massive overdose. As evidenced by their contribution to multiple high-profile celebrity deaths (Michael Jackson, Heath Ledger, and Elvis Presley to name a few), prescription narcotics and sedatives are dangerous and potentially deadly. As an IDMT, avoid prescribing more than 5 narcotic- containing pills at a time. Reevaluate patient’s pain level each time

w), prescription narcotics and sedatives are dangerous and potentially deadly. As an IDMT, avoid prescribing more than 5 narcotic- containing pills at a time. Reevaluate patient’s pain level each time before refilling.

200 XX. BARBITURATE/BENZODIAZEPINE WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME- Abrupt discontinuation of prescription or recreational sedative-hypnotics produces life-threatening withdrawal syndrome. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Life threatening 2. Tremors, anxiety, psychoses, dysphoria, and seizures 3. May have complete break with reality 4. History of chronic medication or drug ingestion; identify substance taken, if possible B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access b. Attach EKG if available and continually monitor c. Cover patient’s eyes with cool damp cloth to minimize visual stimuli (this helps to stop hallucinations) d. Reassure patient frequently e. Treatment of barbiturate withdrawal syndrome is administration of a long acting benzodiazepine such as diazepam (Valium®). CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION prior to administration 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXI. STIMULANT DRUG INTOXICATION/OVERDOSE- Legal stimulants include caffeine, weight loss products, nicotine, energy drinks, and medications used to treat Attention-Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder such as dextroamphetamine (Adderall®). Illegal stimulants include cocaine, crystal meth, crack, crank, and ecs

ne, energy drinks, and medications used to treat Attention-Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder such as dextroamphetamine (Adderall®). Illegal stimulants include cocaine, crystal meth, crack, crank, and ecstasy. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Agitation, tachycardia, hyperthermia 2. Headache, psychoses, seizures, focal neurologic symptoms 3. Patient likely to become violent; agitation and violence can be abrupt and unpredictable 4. Death by cardiac arrhythmias and multi-organ system failure 5. If drug inhaled by snorting, may have ulcers, holes or burns in nasal septum 6. Consider acute alcohol withdrawal syndrome as a differential diagnosis B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access; maintain adequate oral/IV intake to prevent dehydration; cool patient to core temperature below 102°F b. Attach EKG if available and continually monitor CLINICAL NOTE: Benzodiazepines are commonly prescribed in the military due to high incidence of sleep problems, muscles spasms, and anxiety disorders. They have little direct effect on the heart; they usually cause death by severe respiratory depression. If adequate ventilator support is provided, the patient will likely recover, even from massive ove

ey have little direct effect on the heart; they usually cause death by severe respiratory depression. If adequate ventilator support is provided, the patient will likely recover, even from massive overdose. Celebrity deaths associated with tranquilizer, barbiturate, or benzodiazepine overdose include Marilyn Monroe, Judy Garland, Jimi Hendrix, and Anna Nicole Smith, to name a few.

201 c. If the patient becomes violent, administer 10 mg diazepam (Valium®) IV (only use IM if IV not feasible) d. Repeat diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IV or IM every 10 minutes after initial dose until appropriate response is reached; be prepared for respiratory arrest e. Use physical restraints and keep patient in protective custody f. Severe agitation is a predictor for cardiac arrest- review ACLS guidelines and be prepared for cardiac failure g. Cover patient’s eyes with cool damp cloth to minimize visual stimuli (this helps to stop hallucinations) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXII. HALLUCINOGEN INTOXICATION/OVERDOSE/FLASHBACK- Dextromethorphan, a cough suppressant commonly found in over-the-counter cold medicines (Day-Quil®, Ny-Quil®, Robitussin®, Pediacare®, Triaminic®, and Theraflu®, etc) is readily available and becoming a common recreational hallucinogen. Illegal hallucinogens include “magic mushrooms”, PCP, LSD, special K, and ecstasy. Ketamine is an anesthetic used in veterinary medicine and on the battlefield instead of sedatives; enables soldier to dissociate from pain of severe injury so that they can participate in their own rescue. A. Signs and Symp

ic used in veterinary medicine and on the battlefield instead of sedatives; enables soldier to dissociate from pain of severe injury so that they can participate in their own rescue. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. In general, blending of senses such as “hearing colors”, feelings of expansiveness, out of body experiences, distortion of time, euphoria 2. Unwanted effects include fear, panic, frightening imagery, intense dread 3. Frank psychoses may last for days B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Establish IV access; maintain adequate oral/IV intake to prevent dehydration b. Attach EKG if available and continually monitor c. Sedation generally not necessary; if the patient becomes violent, use physical restraints and protective custody d. Cover patient’s eyes with cool damp cloth to minimize visual stimuli (this helps to stop hallucinations) e. Reassure patient frequently 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION XXIII. MARIJUANA INTOXICATION- A controversial herb that can be smoked to produce generalized euphoria and relaxation. It is legal in some states for medicinal use to gain weight, especially in cancer patients. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Euphoria, more vivid sensations,

d to produce generalized euphoria and relaxation. It is legal in some states for medicinal use to gain weight, especially in cancer patients. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Euphoria, more vivid sensations, distortion of time 2. Impaired cognition and impaired reaction time last much longer than the “high” 3. Tachycardia, increased blood pressure, increased respiratory rate, conjunctival injection (red eyes), dry mouth, increased appetite (“munchies”) B. Treatment 1. Maintain adequate oral/IV intake to prevent dehydration 2. If the patient becomes violent, use physical restraints and protective custody

202 3. If patient becomes severely agitated, may use diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IV or IM 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION XXIV. ALCOHOL INTOXICATION AND ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL- See “Neurologic Disorders, Alcohol Intoxication” and “Neurologic Disorders, Alcohol Withdrawal”

203 Chapter 14: Poisonings, Bites, and Stings Protocols Marine Life I. GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS- Many marine life envenomations can cause paralysis, including respiratory paralysis. Be prepared to provide long-term ventilator support from the beginning of the incident through the entire evacuation. These patients can be “locked in”, meaning they are fully aware but unable to communicate, breathe, or move. If full cardiorespiratory support is provided, these patients can often have a complete recovery. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain, local skin reaction, and edema spreading from bite/sting site are common to all marine envenomations 2. Rapid progression to shortness of breath, paralysis, shock, and respiratory arrest B. Treatment 1. Basic Life Support, especially respiratory support is the most critical action following marine bites and stings 2. Marine wounds are prone to infection and poor wound healing; consider antibiotic prophylaxis 3. Treatment of some envenomations and stings call for hot water immersion, which destroys some of the toxins a. Make sure patient is hemodynamically stable before starting an immersion attempt b. Immerse the extremity in water as hot as will be tolera

or hot water immersion, which destroys some of the toxins a. Make sure patient is hemodynamically stable before starting an immersion attempt b. Immerse the extremity in water as hot as will be tolerated or at (45°C/113°F); patient may have lost sensation in affected extremity so be sure to monitor water temperature closely to avoid scald burns c. Keep extremity immersed for 30-90 minutes; refresh the hot water as needed to keep it hot II. COELENTERATES/CNIDARIA- These include jellyfish, box jellyfish, Man o’ War, soft corals, and anemones. These creatures are common in both warm and cold brackish salt water, with about a million envenomations occurring around the Chesapeake Bay and Florida Gulf Coast annually. Australian box jellyfish and the Portuguese Man o’ War are especially fatal. CLINICAL NOTE: Suspect possible Cnideria envenomation in any unresponsive patient found in surf/seawater. The Box Jellyfish (Chrionex fleckeri) can cause death in a matter of minutes, and it is the only Cnideria for which there is an antivenin (Australian species only). If Chrionex envenomation is suspected, and antivenin is available, give the antivenin immediately. CLINICAL NOTE: “Venomous” means

Cnideria for which there is an antivenin (Australian species only). If Chrionex envenomation is suspected, and antivenin is available, give the antivenin immediately. CLINICAL NOTE: “Venomous” means that a creature can attack using its toxin by biting, stabbing, or stinging. “Poisonous” means the creature must be touched, bitten, or eaten for its toxin to take effect.

204 A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Immediate burning, itching, throbbing pain at the site of the sting 2. “Tentacle prints” - linear, papular eruptions that develop into raised lines, surrounded by red, inflamed dermis 3. Weakness, nausea, headache, muscle pain, and muscle spasms 4. Lacrimation, nasal discharge, increased perspiration, changes in pulse rate B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Provide life support: airway, breathing, O2, circulation b. The respiratory system is often the most severely affected; death can sometimes be prevented by basic respiratory support c. If bronchospasm is present give albuterol (Ventolin®) 2 puffs inhaled orally with spacer every 4 hours d. Remove unfired nematocysts and rinse with salt water e. After all tentacles have been removed, rinse or soak wound in acetic acid (vinegar) f. Obtain IV access early in unaffected limb, even if it seems unnecessary at the time; give normal saline at maintenance rate unless hypotensive g. If patient is hypotensive give crystalloid such as Lactated Ringer’s solution 1-3 liters then maintenance at 125 cc/hour h. Limit patient’s movements and carry them when possible; envenomations are hastened by muscular effort push

crystalloid such as Lactated Ringer’s solution 1-3 liters then maintenance at 125 cc/hour h. Limit patient’s movements and carry them when possible; envenomations are hastened by muscular effort pushing more of the poison towards the central circulation i. Monitor airway for anaphylaxis-type reaction (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider pressure bandage (requires preceptor approval) b. Consider topical lidocaine (Xylocaine®) or benzocaine (Oragel®, Anbesol®) applied directly to wound area ACTION ALERT: Cnidarian victims often have unfired nematocysts on their skin. Avoid skin to skin contact: Wear gloves/use universal precautions. Brush off unfired nematocysts with a plastic object such as a credit card; applying shaving cream if available and scraping with credit card enhances removal. Use salt water for rinsing injury site after brushing off nematocysts. DO NOT use cool fresh water to rinse off nematocysts because this will cause them to fire.
because this will cause them to fire.

205 3. For minor Cnidarian wounds without systemic symptoms, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider topical hydrocortisone 1% cream bid (do not use with oral steroids) b. If patient does not respond to topical treatment, consider prednisone (Deltasone®)60 mg PO daily (requires physician approval) c. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) and/or ibuprofen (Motrin®) for pain d. Administer tetanus immunization if last immunization is unknown or if it was greater than 5 years ago e. Be prepared to treat delayed reactions f. Limited duty profile or bed rest C. Prevention 1. Know the toxic marine life in your area, availability of antitoxin, and creature-specific treatments prior to deployment 2. Monitor local warnings regarding movements/migrations of sea creatures; consider banning marine recreation during periods of increased creature presence 3. Wearing nylons while swimming or wading can prevent tentacles from attaching to the legs 4. Wear thick-soled shoes while swimming or wading to prevent injury to the feet III. CONE SHELLS, SNAILS, OCTOPUS, AND NUDIBRANCH (SEA SLUGS)- Most ocean species of snails, octopi, and slugs are not venomous but many are poisonous. Sea creatures with br

prevent injury to the feet III. CONE SHELLS, SNAILS, OCTOPUS, AND NUDIBRANCH (SEA SLUGS)- Most ocean species of snails, octopi, and slugs are not venomous but many are poisonous. Sea creatures with bright blue coloring tend to be venomous, including the blue ringed octopus and the blue sea slug. Cone shell snails are found in many tropical and reef environments; their shells are highly prized due to their smooth contour and lattice-like or polka-dotted decorations. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Localized severe pain, swelling, redness, and numbness, and paralysis of affected limb 2. Wounds cause significant tissue damage and necrosis 3. Pain is sometimes so severe victim may request amputation 4. Rapid progression to shock, paralysis, and respiratory failure 5. Although patient is unable to communicate or move, their sensation, pain, and awareness remains intact (“locked in”) CLINICAL NOTE: Patients often experience fever, fatigue, malaise, joint pain, and swelling intermittently for 1-2 months after Cnidarian sting. This is termed “delayed reaction.” ACTION ALERT: How to apply pressure immobilization to an extremity: 1. Apply 6-8 cm gauze pad with 2-3 cm thickness directly to bite or s

nths after Cnidarian sting. This is termed “delayed reaction.” ACTION ALERT: How to apply pressure immobilization to an extremity: 1. Apply 6-8 cm gauze pad with 2-3 cm thickness directly to bite or sting 2. Hold in place using 15-18 cm wide bandage 3. Tighten enough to occlude venous and lymphatic vessels only- pulse should still be present distal to bandage site 4. Immobilize and elevate extremity above level of the heart

206 B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Provide life support: airway, breathing, O2, circulation b. The respiratory system is often the most severely affected; death can sometimes be prevented by basic respiratory support c. If prolonged cardiopulmonary support can be administered, patients sometimes can experience complete recovery as the poison is metabolized—however this can take up to 24 hours d. If bronchospasm is present give albuterol (Ventolin®) 2 puffs inhaled orally with spacer every 4 hours e. Rinse with salt water or normal saline f. Attempt to find and remove any foreign bodies (radula) g. Obtain IV access early in unaffected limb, even if it seems unnecessary at the time; give normal saline at maintenance rate unless hypotensive h. If patient is hypotensive give crystalloid such as Lactated Ringer’s solution 1-3 liters then maintenance at 125 cc/hour i. Limit patient’s movements and carry them when possible; envenomations are hastened by muscular effort pushing more of the poison towards the central circulation j. Monitor airway for anaphylaxis-type reaction (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consi

e poison towards the central circulation j. Monitor airway for anaphylaxis-type reaction (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider pressure bandage (requires preceptor approval) – see text box above b. Consider topical lidocaine (Xylocaine®) or benzocaine (Oragel®, Anbesol®) applied directly to wound area c. If pain is severe, consider IV morphine, however this will worsen respiratory depression d. Administer tetanus prophylaxis if last immunization is unknown or greater than 5 years C. Prevention 1. Know the toxic marine life in your area, availability of antitoxin, and creature-specific treatments prior to deployment 2. Wear thick-soled shoes while swimming or wading to prevent injury to the feet 3. Counsel all personnel to avoid handling venomous creatures; make sure all personnel are familiar with local threats IV. VENOMOUS FISH STINGS- From least to most potent, these envenomations include the lionfish, scorpionfish, and stonefish. Lionfish tend to live in more tropical and subtropical areas, while scorpionfish and stonefish are more temperate. Scorpionfish and stonefish are both bottom-dwellers and most

ionfish, and stonefish. Lionfish tend to live in more tropical and subtropical areas, while scorpionfish and stonefish are more temperate. Scorpionfish and stonefish are both bottom-dwellers and most envenomations come from waders inadvertently stepping on them. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Excruciating localized pain and swelling, peaking in intensity in <90 minutes and gradually diminishing over hours to days

207 2. Syncope, weakness, nausea, and anxiety are common, followed by hypotension, respiratory collapse, and possibly death 3. Peripheral vasodilation, vomiting, diarrhea, sweating, muscle cramps 4. Lymphangitis (inguinal or axillary swollen tender lymph nodes proximal to envenomation site) 5. Puncture wounds on the soles of the feet (stonefish and scorpionfish) or on the hands (lionfish) surrounded by blue, cyanotic tissue 6. Severe envenomations can cause vesicle formation followed rapidly by tissue sloughing and excruciating pain B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Provide life support: airway, breathing, O2, circulation; as with other marine envenomations, prolonged cardiopulmonary support may be required b. If patient is hypotensive give crystalloid such as Lactated Ringer’s solution 1-3 liters then maintenance at 125 cc/hour g

SEA SNAKES- See Hydrophiidae in Snakes section of this chapter, under “Coral Snakes”.

208 VI. SEA URCHINS, CORALS, AND STINGRAYS- Although not commonly fatal, sea urchins, corals, and stingray wounds cause extensive local damage and are prone to infection. Sea urchins are small spiny creatures that live in coral reefs and at the bottom of the ocean. Corals form extensive rigid reefs that can inflict jagged knife-like wounds and abrasions with minimal contact. Stingrays live on the ocean floor in warm shallow waters; human injuries commonly occur when a wader steps on the stingray. Since a stingray tail is barbed, wounds from stingrays are typically deep puncture surrounded by jagged tissue damage. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain, bleeding, burning, stinging, swelling, and itching at the injury site 2. Wounds are prone to poor healing; secondary infection is common 3. May have urticaria, vesicles, and fever develop within a few hours after injury- these are reactions to the poison and not evidence of infection 4. Systemic symptoms are less common but include nausea, vomiting, muscle cramping and malaise 5. Death from corals and sea urchins is rare, but stingray envenomations can progress to hypotension, respiratory failure, seizures, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE

ng, muscle cramping and malaise 5. Death from corals and sea urchins is rare, but stingray envenomations can progress to hypotension, respiratory failure, seizures, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation; as with other marine envenomations, prolonged cardiopulmonary support may be required b. If bronchospasm is present give albuterol (Ventolin®) 2 puffs inhaled orally with spacer every 4 hours c. Rinse wound with salt water or normal saline d. Attempt to find and remove any retained sheaths and spines e. Obtain IV access early in unaffected limb, even if it seems unnecessary at the time; give normal saline at maintenance rate unless hypotensive f. Immerse affected limb in hot water- see “General Principles” at the beginning of this chapter h. Monitor airway for anaphylaxis-type reaction (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider topical lidocaine (Xylocaine®) or benzocaine (Oragel®, Anbesol®) applied directly to wound area b. If pain is severe, consider IV morphine, however this will worsen respiratory depression c. Administer tetanus prophylaxis if last immunization is unknown or greater than 5 years 3. For minor wounds without systemic symptoms, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Do not suture wound closed; may draw edges loosely together with Steri-Strips® or loose suture if approved by preceptor b. Significant discoloration around wound after 48 hours indicates probable retained foreign body that requires surgical debridement

209 c. Acetaminophen (Tylenol®) for pain d. Consider antibiotics for prevention of secondary infection; discuss with preceptor e. Administer tetanus immunization if last immunization is unknown or if it was greater than 5 years ago f. Be prepared to treat delayed reactions g. Limited duty profile or bed rest C. Prevention 1. Know the toxic marine life in your area, availability of antitoxin, and creature-specific treatments prior to deployment 2. Wear wetsuits and gloves when swimming or diving around reefs 3. Wear thick-soled shoes while swimming or wading to prevent injury to the feet (will not protect against stingrays or sea urchins) 4. Shuffle feet when wading in waters inhabited by stingrays 5. Counsel all personnel to avoid handling venomous creatures; make sure all personnel are familiar with local threats Arachnids and Insects VII. BLACK WIDOW SPIDER BITE- Black widows are found throughout the US and around the world, especially around human habitation near wooded areas. Bites most commonly occur when an unsuspecting human disturbs a female’s nest and threatens her egg sac. The spiders grow up to 1.5 inches long, is glossy black or dark brown, with bright red or orange “ho

most commonly occur when an unsuspecting human disturbs a female’s nest and threatens her egg sac. The spiders grow up to 1.5 inches long, is glossy black or dark brown, with bright red or orange “hourglass” on the underside of the abdomen. Black widow webs are typically irregular mesh-type in dark corners, often in clutter around houses, woodpiles, and garages. If the spider was not witnessed, always consider other possible diagnoses, especially focal infection with skin bacteria. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Positive identification of spider or history of high risk activity such as sitting on outdoor furniture, working in garage or outdoors clearing debris, gardening, etc 2. Immediate sharp, stinging pain at bite site; wound is a blanched circular patch with central puncture wound or pair of wounds and blanched region has ring of surrounding erythema 3. In approximately an hour of bite, affected limb develops unilateral diaphoresis with muscle pain and cramping 4. Can progress into systemic symptoms of diaphoresis (sweating), restlessness, anxiety, dizziness, nausea, and vomiting, and generalized peripheral vasodilation 5. Abdominal cramping and muscular rigidity involving major mus

emic symptoms of diaphoresis (sweating), restlessness, anxiety, dizziness, nausea, and vomiting, and generalized peripheral vasodilation 5. Abdominal cramping and muscular rigidity involving major muscle groups can be severe and can be indistinguishable from acute abdomen, however abdominal sounds will normally still be present with widow bite and absent in cases of acute abdomen 6. Severe envenomations may have respiratory distress/tachypnea, salivation, and hypertension- mimicking myocardial infarction, tetanus, or rabies B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Provide Basic Life Support if needed

210 b. Obtain IV access with normal saline at maintenance rate c. Clean bite site with copious soap and water d. Warm baths may provide some relief for cramping and muscle spasms e. For severe allergic reaction, see “Emergency Protocols- Anaphylaxis” 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Administer acetaminophen (Tylenol®) PO for pain; if pain is severe, consider acetaminophen/narcotic combination such as Vicodin® or Percocet® b. For severe muscle contractions/spasms, consider IV benzodiazepine such as diazepam (Valium®) or lorazepam (Ativan®) c. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or >5 years ago; consider human tetanus immunoglobulin VIII. BROWN RECLUSE SPIDER BITE- Also known as the “fiddleback” due to its distinctive brown violin shape on its cephalothorax. They are found primarily in the South, West, and Midwest United States as well as in the Mediterranean and South America. They live primarily in homes in dark quiet areas such as attics, cupboards, behind bookshelves, and in basements. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Initially painless bite that increases over the next several hours 2. Bite site begins to swell with local erythema, small punc

upboards, behind bookshelves, and in basements. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Initially painless bite that increases over the next several hours 2. Bite site begins to swell with local erythema, small puncture wounds may be visible 3. Within a few hours of bite, lesion develops “Bull’s Eye” appearance with central pallor and surrounding erythema and ecchymosis 4. Lesion may resolve over several days or become dusky and insensate, rupture, and leave a central ulceration; these lesions can develop significant tissue necrosis 5. Systemic reactions are rare but may include nausea, vomiting, fevers and myalgias (indistinguishable from sepsis) B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Obtain IV access on unaffected limb with normal saline at maintenance rate if changes in sensation, systemic symptoms, severe pain, or any signs of anaphylaxis b. Ice the bite site taking care to protect tissue since patient may have altered sensation c. Immobilize extremity and elevate (possibly for several days until lesion starts to resolve or until necrosis starts) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION for any patient with severe or symptoms, signs of necrosis, or unconfirmed diagnos

sion starts to resolve or until necrosis starts) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION for any patient with severe or symptoms, signs of necrosis, or unconfirmed diagnosis b. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or >5 years ago; consider human tetanus immunoglobulin c. Consider antibiotic prophylaxis such as dicloxacillin (Dynapen®) or clindamycin (Cleocin®) IX. BEES, WASPS, HORNETS, AND YELLOW JACKETS- These are easily recognizable flying insects that predominantly sting when threatened. Sugar, scented flowers, and sweat

211 attract bees. Meat, sugar, and fruit attract yellow jackets, which are typically found at picnic sites and garbage bins. Wasps and hornets make paper nests that are frequently attached to gutters, eaves, and the sides of buildings. All of these insects can secrete pheromones that incite other members of the hive to become aggressive and attack any creatures that threaten a nest- this behavior can lead to massive swarming attacks. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Uncomplicated local reaction a. Immediate pain and burning at the site of the sting b. Red, swollen wheal up to 5 cm develops quickly and should resolve over several hours 2. Large Local Reaction a. Exaggerated erythema and swelling (typically 10 cm) develops over 1- 2 days and resolves over 5-10 days b. Swelling crossing a joint limits movement and can be debilitating 3. Anaphylaxis a. Skin symptoms: generalized urticarial, flushing of the skin, swelling of the face and around the eyes b. Respiratory symptoms: hoarse voice, wheezing, swelling of the pharynx and epiglottis, severe shortness of breath c. Cardiovascular symptoms: lightheadedness, hypotension, shock, death B. Treatment 1. Bee stingers continue to inject venom for

ng, swelling of the pharynx and epiglottis, severe shortness of breath c. Cardiovascular symptoms: lightheadedness, hypotension, shock, death B. Treatment 1. Bee stingers continue to inject venom for several seconds after the sting- it is therefore important to try to remove the stinger as fast as possible; after 10 seconds removal is less critical because all of the venom is injected 2. Use cold compresses on sting site and elevate extreme 3. Control pain with NSAIDs 4. Control itching and swelling with cetirizine (Zyrtec®) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) PLUS high potency topical corticosteroid such as clobetasol (Temovate®) 0.05% ointment applied to sting every 4 hours until itching subsides 5. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or >5 years ago; consider human tetanus immunoglobulin 6. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION; may administer prednisone 60 mg one-time dose or with taper over 3-5 days for large local reactions 7. For systemic symptoms, see “Emergency Protocols- Anaphylaxis” C. Prevention 1. Keep trash bins cleaned regularly to reduce attractive residues 2. If wasp nests must be sprayed, do so at night when insects are less active 3. Prescreen

ncy Protocols- Anaphylaxis” C. Prevention 1. Keep trash bins cleaned regularly to reduce attractive residues 2. If wasp nests must be sprayed, do so at night when insects are less active 3. Prescreen all deployers for history of anaphylaxis and make sure they bring multiple sets of epinephrine autoinjectors (EpiPen®) X. FIRE ANT STINGS- Fire ants are found throughout the US with greatest density in the Southern states. They grow up to 1 cm in length and are usually reddish brown or reddish black in color. They are aggressive and attack with minimal provocation; like other Hymenoptera they release pheromones that can incite a swarming attack. Fire ants

212 attach with strong jaws and can deliver multiple stings within seconds. If a bite is felt, immediately crush the ant to prevent more stings. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Intense burning at the site of the sting changing to itching after 20-30 minutes 2. Red swollen wheal at sting site 3. Within 24 hours a sterile pustule (not infected with bacteria) develops at the bite site 4. Since lesions are itchy and unsightly, patient will be tempted to pick and scratch at pustules; this should be avoided because it increases risk of infection and scarring 5. Large local reactions can occur (as with other Hymenoptera stings) as well as anaphylaxis reactions B. Treatment 1. Use cold compresses on sting site and elevate extremity 2. Control pain with NSAIDs 3. Control itching and swelling with cetirizine (Zyrtec®) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl®) PLUS high potency topical corticosteroid such as clobetasol (Temovate®) 0.05% ointment applied to sting every 4 hours until itching subsides 4. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION; may administer prednisone (Deltasone®) 60 mg one-time dose or with taper over 3-5 days for large local reactions 5. For systemic symptoms, see “Emergency Protocols- Anaphy

ICIAN PRECEPTOR- MEDICATION; may administer prednisone (Deltasone®) 60 mg one-time dose or with taper over 3-5 days for large local reactions 5. For systemic symptoms, see “Emergency Protocols- Anaphylaxis” 6. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or >5 years ago; consider human tetanus immunoglobulin C. Prevention 1. Ants can not bite through fabric or nylon; therefore wear closed-toe shoes, socks, (not sandals or flip-flops), long sleeves, and long pants when working outdoors 2. Avoid placing campsites on or near ant mounds 3. Insect repellants are NOT effective against fire ants XI. CENTIPEDES, MILLIPEDES, AND CATERPILLARS- Rarely deadly, these arthropods can cause substantial irritation. Centipedes live under rocks or fallen logs and will bite when disturbed. Millipedes have a chemical arsenal of poisons including cyanide; some tropical species of millipedes can shoot their poisons over a distance of two feet or more. Millipede reactions resemble 1st or 2nd degree burns. Caterpillars can implant tiny hairs that release venom into the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain at bite site, especially with larger specimens 2. Local tissue redness and swelling at bit

ee burns. Caterpillars can implant tiny hairs that release venom into the skin. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Pain at bite site, especially with larger specimens 2. Local tissue redness and swelling at bite site, often with pruritus 3. Swollen, painful lymph nodes 4. Systemic symptoms are less common but can include headache, palpitations, nausea/vomiting, and anxiety or anaphylaxis 5. Millipede wounds typically start as brown-stained skin that progresses into blisters and sloughing of the skin

213 6. Observe patients for a minimum of four hours since delayed systemic symptoms are common B. Treatment 1. For caterpillars and millipedes- copiously irrigate the area with sterile water and dry without touching the skin (use fan or hairdryer) 2. Retained caterpillar hairs can be removed by applying scotch tape or duct tape and pulling away. If this is ineffective, use fingernail polish or rubber cement, allow it to dry, and peel away 3. Cold compresses are the historical mainstay of therapy, but recent evidence suggests that hot water immersion may actually be more soothing for centipede bites 4. For centipede bites, patients receive prompt relief if bite site is injected with local anesthetic such as lidocaine 1% (Xylocaine®); CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION for approval 5. For systemic symptoms, see “Emergency Protocols- Anaphylaxis” 6. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or >5 years ago; consider human tetanus immunoglobulin C. Prevention 1. Know the toxic arthropods in your area, availability of antitoxin, and creature-specific treatments prior to deployment 2. Wear proper clothing, gloves, and footwear when working outdoors 3. Counsel all p

the toxic arthropods in your area, availability of antitoxin, and creature-specific treatments prior to deployment 2. Wear proper clothing, gloves, and footwear when working outdoors 3. Counsel all personnel to avoid handling venomous creatures; make sure all personnel are familiar with local threats XII. SCORPIONS- Found on all continents except Antarctica, scorpions dwell in desert, arid, and tropical environments. These arthropods grab prey with their pincers and whip their tails overhead to sting with a neurotoxic poison, with symptoms peaking within 5 hours. Scorpions fluoresce under black light or Wood’s lamp, making them easy to locate in the dark. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Immediate pain at the sting site, but unlike hymenoptera stings erythema and immediate edema (“wheel and flare”) do not occur 2. Cranial nerve dysfunction includes hyper-salivation, involuntary conjugate roving eye movements or sharp chaotic nystagmus 3. Skeletal muscle dysfunction includes restlessness, twitching, jerking movements, and writhing movements of the trunk that may resemble seizure- however unlike seizure patient remains alert during movements 4. Systemic symptoms include tachycardia, vomitin

ching, jerking movements, and writhing movements of the trunk that may resemble seizure- however unlike seizure patient remains alert during movements 4. Systemic symptoms include tachycardia, vomiting, hyperthermia to 104°F, diaphoresis, hypertension, dyspnea, priapism, pancreatitis, delirium, shock, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation; as with other envenomations, prolonged cardiopulmonary support may be required b. If bronchospasm is present give albuterol (Ventolin®)2 puffs inhaled orally with spacer every 4 hours c. Cleanse wound with soap and water

214 d. Place patient on bed rest and immobilize affected limb; apply cold packs to sting site e. Obtain IV access early in unaffected limb, even if it seems unnecessary at the time; give normal saline at maintenance rate unless hypotensive f. If patient is hypotensive give crystalloid such as Lactated Ringer’s solution 1-3 liters then maintenance at 125 cc/hour g. Monitor airway for anaphylaxis-type reaction (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Take scorpion with patient for identification at treatment facility b. Administer tetanus prophylaxis if last immunization is unknown or greater than 5 years c. NSAIDs for pain; if this is insufficient, fentanyl (Duragesic®) patch/IM/IV/lollipop is the preferred narcotic since it won’t interfere with administration of antivenom 3. For minor wounds without systemic symptoms observe for a minimum of four hours to make sure no systemic symptoms occur, then CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider antibiotics for prevention of secondary infection; discuss with preceptor b. Administer tetanus immunization if last immunization is unknown or if it was greater than 5 y

TOR- CONSULTATION a. Consider antibiotics for prevention of secondary infection; discuss with preceptor b. Administer tetanus immunization if last immunization is unknown or if it was greater than 5 years ago c. Be prepared to treat delayed reactions d. Limited duty profile or bed rest C. Prevention 1. Examine all clothes and shoes prior to putting them on 2. Shake out bedrolls before entering, especially if they are on the ground 3. Shake out all camping gear and equipment prior to using 4. Reduce brush and debris in campsite to reduce habitat 5. Spraying insecticides around base of tent won’t kill the scorpions but will reduce their food supply Lizard Bites XIII. GILA MONSTER AND BEADED LIZARD- Found in the Southwestern United States and Mexico, these lizards are becoming endangered species due to human encroachment on their habitat. Gila monsters are stout black lizards with orange or yellow marbling or banding patterns and grow approximately 1.5 feet in length. Beaded lizards are also stout, grow up to 3 feet in length, are brown with bright white spots and live in scrubland, tropical forests, and pine forests. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bites usually occur from attempting to int

lso stout, grow up to 3 feet in length, are brown with bright white spots and live in scrubland, tropical forests, and pine forests. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Bites usually occur from attempting to interact with the lizard; lizard clamps down with strong jaws and is difficult to remove 2. Chewing action instills poison in the wound 3. Severe pain, burning, and progressive edema from the bite site

215 4. Systemic symptoms include generalized weakness, nausea/vomiting, shortness of breath, profuse sweating, dizziness, and numbness/tingling 5. Exam findings include tachycardia, regional lymphadenopathy, hypotension, significant local tissue trauma, retained teeth, and cyanosis/ecchymosis at the bite site B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. If lizard is still attached, remove by submerging in running hot water or pry jaws loose- be careful not to injure victim or allow rescuer to be bitten b. Keep patient calm and immobile; DO NOT allow patient to walk or run c. Immobilize bitten area and DO NOT manipulate limb; remove obvious retained foreign bodies and apply direct pressure to control bleeding d. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation; as with other envenomations, prolonged cardiopulmonary support may be required e. If bronchospasm is present give albuterol (Ventolin®) 2 puffs inhaled orally with spacer every 4 hours f. Cleanse wound with soap and water g. Obtain IV access early in unaffected limb, even if it seems unnecessary at the time; give normal saline at maintenance rate unless hypotensive h. If patient is hypotensive give crystalloid such a

r g. Obtain IV access early in unaffected limb, even if it seems unnecessary at the time; give normal saline at maintenance rate unless hypotensive h. If patient is hypotensive give crystalloid such as Lactated Ringer’s solution 1-3 liters then maintenance at 125 cc/hour i. Monitor airway for anaphylaxis-type reaction (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Anaphylaxis”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Do not give NSAIDs or aspirin for pain because these may increase bleeding from wound b. May give acetaminophen (Tylenol®) c. May consider acetaminophen with codeine (Tylenol #3®) with preceptor approval, however this will enhance respiratory depression C. Prevention 1. Know the toxic reptiles in your area, availability of antitoxin, and creature- specific treatments prior to deployment 2. Wear long pants and boots when hiking or working outdoors 3. Counsel all personnel to avoid handling venomous creatures; make sure all personnel are familiar with local threats 4. Watch venomous lizards only from a distance; these reptiles are shy and will try to hide. Do not attempt to poke or manipulate them to “get a better look” Snake Bites CLINICAL NOTE: Severed snake heads

atch venomous lizards only from a distance; these reptiles are shy and will try to hide. Do not attempt to poke or manipulate them to “get a better look” Snake Bites CLINICAL NOTE: Severed snake heads can still bite up to 60 minutes after decapitation. Venom in fangs remains active for days. Avoid handling severed heads unless necessary for patient care.

216 XIV. VIPERS (INCLUDING VENOMOUS VIPERS, COTTONMOUTHS, COPPERHEADS, AND RATTLESNAKES)- These snakes are found worldwide in warm and temperate areas; in the United States, cottonmouth, copperhead, and rattlesnakes are primarily found in the warmer regions of the Southern and Western States. Their venom is primarily hemotoxic. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Fang marks at bite site (presence of fang marks does not necessarily indicate delivery of venom) 2. Erythema, edema, warmth, and ecchymosis at the bite site and adjacent tissues within 60 minutes 3. Edema progresses up proximal extremity within hours 4. Systemic symptoms include nausea/vomiting, diaphoresis, chills, oral paresthesias, unusual taste in mouth, and fasciculations 5. Late systemic effects include bleeding, rhabdomyolysis (severe muscle cramping and pain), kidney failure, tachycardia, tachypnea, shock, altered mental status, seizures, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Bring snake/snake parts with patient for positive identification if it will not put anyone else at risk; avoid direct handling of snake heads b. Keep patient calm and immobile; DO NOT allow patient to walk or run c. Immobilize bitten area and DO

CORAL SNAKES, SEA SNAKES, BOOMSLANGS, BIRD SNAKES, AUSTRALIAN DEATH ADDERS AND TAIPANS- These snakes are found worldwide in a variety of habitats. Their venom is primarily neurotoxic.

217 A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Mild to moderate pain and swelling with weakness of the bitten extremity occurring within hours 2. Unlike viper bites, there is minimal local tissue damage 3. Cranial nerve palsies/deficits, double vision, sagging eyelids, difficulty swallowing, and hyper-salivation occurs within hours 4. Dizziness, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain may occur 5. Late systemic effects include systemic weakness, drowsiness, euphoria progressing to muscle flaccidity, respiratory distress, and respiratory failure B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Bring snake/snake parts with patient for positive identification if it will not put anyone else at risk; avoid direct handling of snake heads b. Keep patient calm and immobile; DO NOT allow patient to walk or run c. Immobilize bitten area and DO NOT manipulate limb, DO NOT apply suction devices, and DO NOT cut or incise the bite area d. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation; as with other envenomations, prolonged cardiopulmonary support may be required e. If bronchospasm is present give albuterol (Ventolin®) 2 puffs inhaled orally with spacer every 4 hours f. Cleanse wound with soap and water g. Obtain

If evacuation will be delayed, consider pressure immobilization within 30 minutes of bite d. Perform neurologic assessments of cranial nerves and strength every 15-30 minutes in order to monitor progressive neurotoxicity Miscellaneous Exposures XVI. ty Miscellaneous Exposures XVI. POISONOUS PLANTS- See “Integumentary Protocols, Contact Dermatitis” XVII. ANIMAL BITES AND RABIES- Rabies is a potentially fatal virus that is transmitted when the patient is bitten by an infected dog, raccoon, bat, skunk, fox, or rodent. The virus travels along nerves to the brain and spinal cord; once clinical manifestations occur it will universally progress to neurologic deficits, encephalitis, and death. Assume any animal that bites has rabies unless it is a pet and its vaccination status is well-documented.

218 A. Signs and Symptoms of Rabies 1. Incubation period- lasts days to months after bite; bites close to the head will have shorter incubation periods than bites on the extremities 2. Prodromal period- flu-like symptoms lasting up to a week that include nausea, malaise, anorexia, sore throat, headache, irritability, and low grade fever; percussion of muscular areas will cause mounding up of tissue at the percussion site 3. Encephalitic rabies- fear of water, painful pharyngeal spasms upon encountering a draft of air, excessive salivation, fluctuating consciousness, cardiac arrhythmias lasting 2-7 days 4. Paralytic rabies- less common than encephalitic rabies, it resembles Guillain- Barré syndrome with weakness progressing to flaccid paralysis and respiratory failure; it is different than Guillain-Barré in that it is accompanied by persistent fever, intact sensation, and bladder dysfunction 5. Coma- both encephalitic and paralytic rabies progress to coma, flaccid paralysis, and respiratory and vascular collapse B. Treatment 1. Apply direct pressure to control bleeding 2. Cleanse superficial wounds with soap and water; if tissue damage is extensive inject local anesthesia into wound

tory and vascular collapse B. Treatment 1. Apply direct pressure to control bleeding 2. Cleanse superficial wounds with soap and water; if tissue damage is extensive inject local anesthesia into wound prior to cleansing and clean with gently pressurized sterile water; remove any devitalized tissue prior to closure 3. The IDMT should not close bite wounds from cats or humans, puncture wounds, bites > 12 hours old, bites associated with crush injury, or bites on the hands or feet due to increased risk of deep infections 4.
