Survival from rabies is dependent on being vaccinated prior to the appearance of symptoms; therefore it is critical that patient be evaluated for rabies whenever an animal bite from a potential carrier occurs 5. Every effort should be made to capture/kill the animal so that it can be evaluated for presence of rabies virus 6. In the absence of the animal, the patient may be required to undergo lengthy and painful treatments 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION AND EVACUATION for further evaluation and management Chapter 15: Environmental Injury Protocols Heat Stress Injuries I. HEAT SYNCOPE- Occurs if patient stands still immediately after exertion in a hot

ND EVACUATION for further evaluation and management Chapter 15: Environmental Injury Protocols Heat Stress Injuries I. HEAT SYNCOPE- Occurs if patient stands still immediately after exertion in a hot environment. While working or exerting, the muscles help to pump blood up to the brain. By stopping suddenly, peripheral vasodilation and venous pooling are too substantial for the heart to compensate and the patient loses consciousness briefly. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Strenuous exertion in a hot environment followed by standing still 2. Brief loss of consciousness with rapid recovery as soon as the patient is supine

219 3. Normal core body temperature B. Treatment 1. Move patient to a cool environment 2. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor 3. Patient should immediately regain consciousness 4. Administer oral rehydration with salty fluids or rehydrate while eating salty foods 5. If patient does not respond to oral treatments, if they are nauseous, or if they are orthostatic, obtain IV access and give normal saline 1-2 L fluid bolus 6. Keep patient supine until asymptomatic C. Prevention 1. Educate troops regarding appropriate cool down exercises after strenuous workout 2. After severe exertion ensure troops slow down gradually so that heart rate and blood distribution can normalize over several minutes 3. Once heart rate normalizes and skin is no longer pink, recommend 3-5 minutes of stretching exercises, holding each stretch for a minimum of 30 seconds per side (See “Musculoskeletal Protocols”) 4. Many weight loss and muscle building supplements decrease the body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; discontinue use in hot conditions II. HEAT CRAMPS- Muscular cramps and spasms that occur during or after activity in a hot environment that o

body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; discontinue use in hot conditions II. HEAT CRAMPS- Muscular cramps and spasms that occur during or after activity in a hot environment that occurs due to loss of sodium chloride. Individuals that have been sweating and aggressively hydrating with water replaces fluid but not salt balance and places them at greater risk. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Abrupt onset of cramping in large muscle groups such as thighs, calves, abdomen 2. Painful muscle spasms and profuse sweating; core temperature normal or elevated, but not above 104°F 3. May progress to “heat tetany” with severe muscle spasms, hyperventilation, cramping of the upper extremities, and perioral numbness B. Treatment 1. Symptoms can continue to progress even after activity has ceased; therefore be prepared to offer life support measures 2. Move patient to a cool environment 3. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor 4. Administer oral rehydration with salty fluids or rehydrate while eating salty foods 5. If patient does not respond to oral treatments or if they are nauseous, obtain IV access and give normal saline 1-2 L fluid bolus 6.

dration with salty fluids or rehydrate while eating salty foods 5. If patient does not respond to oral treatments or if they are nauseous, obtain IV access and give normal saline 1-2 L fluid bolus 6. Work muscles with passive stretching techniques and massage 7. If patient is hyperventilating, use a partial rebreather mask delivering less than 5 liters per minute of oxygen; this will enable the patient to get adequate amounts of both carbon dioxide and oxygen and will reduce the hyperventilation C. Prevention

220 1. Ensure adequate work/rest cycles are followed and that workers in hot environments have a cool place available where they can recover 2. Ensure appropriate hydration is taking place; workers should alternate water and salty/sugary fluids (such as Gatorade®), mix Gatorade® ½ and ½ with water, or have salty snacks such as chips, nuts, or crackers available on breaks 3. Many weight loss and muscle building supplements decrease the body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; recommend discontinuing use in hot conditions III. HEAT EXHAUSTION- Fatigue due to loss of fluids and salts. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Dizziness, confusion, headache, fatigue, weakness 2. May have mild confusion that returns to normal within 30 minutes of cooling 3. Nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, profuse sweating, orthostatic hypotension 4. Ashy gray, pale, cool and clammy skin, elevated body temperature, thirst 5. Symptoms can rapidly progress to heat stroke; if any neurologic symptoms are present other than mild confusion, move to stroke protocols B. Treatment 1. Symptoms can continue to progress even after activity has ceased; therefore be prepared to offer life support measures 2. Move patie

other than mild confusion, move to stroke protocols B. Treatment 1. Symptoms can continue to progress even after activity has ceased; therefore be prepared to offer life support measures 2. Move patient to a cool environment 3. Provide life support if needed: airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor 4. Administer oral rehydration with salty fluids or rehydrate while eating salty foods if patient can tolerate 5. If patient does not respond to oral treatments or if they are nauseous, obtain IV access and give normal saline 1-2 L fluid bolus 6. Reassess vitals every 15 minutes until stable 7. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION a. Patient may require evacuation if recovery is slow b. No medications indicated C. Prevention 1. Ensure adequate work/rest cycles are followed and that workers in hot environments have a cool place available where they can recover 2. If work/rest cycles can not be followed, recommend close medical monitoring (high ops tempo environments where the mission takes priority over health). The IDMT should closely observe all individuals involved in the activity 3. Ensure appropriate hydration is taking place; workers should alternate water and salty/sugary

takes priority over health). The IDMT should closely observe all individuals involved in the activity 3. Ensure appropriate hydration is taking place; workers should alternate water and salty/sugary fluids (such as Gatorade®), mix Gatorade® ½ and ½ with water, or have salty snacks such as nuts, chips, or crackers available on breaks 4. Ensure that all troops that are new to a hot environment acclimatize to activity over several weeks 5. Ensure that all troops rehydrate fully after exertion- repeated heat stresses without full recovery of fluid status will markedly increase potential for heat injury

221 6. Many weight loss and muscle building supplements decrease the body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; recommend discontinuing use in hot conditions IV. HEAT STROKE- A true medical emergency, heat stroke is caused by elevation in the core body temperature that overwhelms the central nervous system and leads to a failure of thermoregulation. Patients have a high morbidity and mortality rate even when symptoms are rapidly recognized and treated. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Elevated core body temperature, usually over 105°F, rectal temperature is most accurate 2. Flushing, tachypnea, pulmonary crackles, altered mental status 3. Skin may be diaphoretic or dry, depending on how long it took symptoms to develop 4. May spontaneously bleed or bruise due to clotting mechanism failure 5. Rapidly progresses to seizures, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, shock, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Move patient to a cool environment b. Provide immediate life support: airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor c. Obtain IV access and give normal saline 1-2 L fluid bolus d. Initiate rapid cooling measures (any combination) until core temperature is below 102°F i.

: airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor c. Obtain IV access and give normal saline 1-2 L fluid bolus d. Initiate rapid cooling measures (any combination) until core temperature is below 102°F i. Spray unclothed patient with cool mist and use fans to rapidly evaporate water ii. Immerse patient in ice water (don’t perform on patients over age 65) iii. Use ice packs on pulse points in axillae, neck, and groin iv. Use room temperature to slightly cool IV fluids e. Reassess vitals every 15 minutes including rectal core temperature checks during and after rapid cooling 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION C. Prevention 1. Ensure adequate work/rest cycles are followed and that workers in hot environments have a cool place available where they can recover 2. If work/rest cycles can not be followed, recommend close medical monitoring (high ops tempo environments where the mission takes priority over health). The IDMT should closely observe all individuals involved in the activity if possible 3. Ensure appropriate hydration is taking place; workers should alternate water and salty/sugary fluids (such as Gatorade®), mix Gatorade® ½ and ½ with water, or have salty snacks

ctivity if possible 3. Ensure appropriate hydration is taking place; workers should alternate water and salty/sugary fluids (such as Gatorade®), mix Gatorade® ½ and ½ with water, or have salty snacks such as chips, nuts, or crackers available on breaks 4. Ensure that all new troops to a hot environment acclimatize to activity over several weeks

222 5. Ensure that all troops rehydrate fully after exertion- repeated heat stresses without full recovery of fluid status will markedly increase potential for heat injury 6. Personnel with a prior history of heat injury are at greater risk for developing subsequent heat related illness 7. Many weight loss and muscle building supplements decrease the body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; recommend discontinuing use in hot conditions Cold Stress Injuries V. TRENCH FOOT AND IMMERSION FOOT/SYNDROME- “Trench foot” occurs when wearing wet boots and socks for a prolonged period of time. Immersion foot and Immersion syndrome come from prolonged immersion of the lower extremities in water or mud. Both occur when ambient temperatures are above freezing. Nerve damage occurs when environment causes skin breakdown and tight footwear combined with immobility causes vasoconstriction. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Prehyperemic phase (before rewarming) a. Skin is white, yellow or mottled, edema is present b. Complete absence of sensation, leading to “walking on air” feeling c. Exam findings include sluggish capillary refill, loss of sensation including light touch, sharp/dull, and p

d, edema is present b. Complete absence of sensation, leading to “walking on air” feeling c. Exam findings include sluggish capillary refill, loss of sensation including light touch, sharp/dull, and proprioception, and abnormalities in gait 2. Hyperemic phase (Starts within a few hours of rewarming) a. Extremity become hot, red, painful, very swollen, and covered with petichiae b. Severe pain with any heat, movement, or touch, including pressure from sheets c. Vibratory sense usually absent, proprioception usually present 3. Vaso-spastic phase (several days after rewarming) a. Area becomes pale or cyanotic when elevated and deep red or purple and with tense edema when not elevated b. Diminished pulses, vesicles/bullae, edema, hemorrhagic blisters, skin sloughing and area may develop gangrene B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Dry patient and keep core temperature warm (treat coexistent hypothermia) while keeping extremities cool; avoid massaging or rewetting b. Recommended method is keeping extremities elevated and exposed to a cool fan; this treatment should be continued until pain and swelling has resolved (may take days) C. Prevention 1. Ensure proper fitting boots prior to dep

eeping extremities elevated and exposed to a cool fan; this treatment should be continued until pain and swelling has resolved (may take days) C. Prevention 1. Ensure proper fitting boots prior to deployment 2. Change socks 2-3 times per day in sweaty or wet environments

223 3. Recommend boots be removed completely for a minimum of 8 hours out of every 24; feet should be completely dry and warm and preferably exposed to air during those periods VI. FROSTBITE- Crystallization of water in the distal tissues due to exposure to subfreezing temperatures; most commonly affects the ears, nose, cheeks, chin, fingers, and toes. Risk factors include smoking, exhaustion, dehydration, alcohol use, and exposure to vibration. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Patient reports cold, numbness, and clumsiness of affected area 2. Skin is without sensation, grayish yellow, and hard or waxy to the touch 3. First degree frostbite- superficial, central area of pallor and anesthesia surrounded by edema 4. Second degree frostbite- large blisters containing clear fluid surrounded by erythema and edema; blisters may form black eschar but they slough off and reveal healthy tissue; no permanent tissue loss 5. Third degree frostbite- deep forming black eschar, blisters are smaller; hemorrhagic bullae present 6. Fourth degree frostbite- extends deep into muscle and bone and causes complete tissue necrosis and mummification B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, circul

gic bullae present 6. Fourth degree frostbite- extends deep into muscle and bone and causes complete tissue necrosis and mummification B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, circulation, treat hypothermia b. Do not allow frozen tissue to thaw if there is any chance of re-freezing before evacuation is complete c. Remove all wet or constrictive clothing and replace with dry, loose fitting garments d. Avoid allowing patient to walk on frostbitten extremities; if walking is necessary do not allow any rewarming to take place e. Cover frozen tissue with large dry bulky dressing and avoid manipulation or pressure on frozen part 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. If evacuation will be delayed, consider active rewarming with the approval of preceptor; do not rewarm if there is any potential for tissue to refreeze i. Rewarming is painful; consider morphine or meperidine (Demerol®) IV during rewarming process ii. Submerge injured part in warm water bath (99-102°) ideally without letting injured part touch sides or bottom of container iii. Continue to replenish warm water until skin is pliable, painful, and erythematous at most distal part of injury iv.

9-102°) ideally without letting injured part touch sides or bottom of container iii. Continue to replenish warm water until skin is pliable, painful, and erythematous at most distal part of injury iv. Allow injury to air-dry; DO NOT towel dry as this may increase pain and tissue injury v. Keep blisters intact and protect from trauma; avoid manipulation or pressure on rewarmed part; do not allow blankets to apply pressure to rewarmed part vi. After rewarming, elevated extremity, put patient on bed rest, and keep injured part open to the air as much as possible C. Prevention

224 1. Dress to maintain whole body warmth; use mittens instead of gloves as much as possible; make sure vulnerable areas such as face, neck, nose and corneas are covered; clothes should be adequately ventilated 2. Avoid perspiring in cold weather; activity should be slowed to prevent sweating 3. Keep toenails and fingernails trimmed 4. Avoid tobacco and alcohol in extreme environments, even in camp 5. Many weight loss and muscle building supplements decrease the body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; recommend discontinuing use in extreme conditions 6. Make sure tetanus is up to date prior to deployment VII. HYPOTHERMIA- Condition from exposure to prolonged cold or being immersed in cold water which results in core body temperature dropping below 95°F. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Mild- core temperature 90-95°. Tachypnea, tachycardia, ataxia, dysarthria, impaired judgment, shivering, urinating 2. Moderate- core temperature 82-90°. Slowing of pulse and breathing, loss of shivering, decreased level of consciousness, arrhythmias 3. Severe- core temperature below 82°. Pulmonary edema, coma, hypotension, bradycardia, arrhythmias, asystole B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION

shivering, decreased level of consciousness, arrhythmias 3. Severe- core temperature below 82°. Pulmonary edema, coma, hypotension, bradycardia, arrhythmias, asystole B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor b. Remove wet/cold clothing and cover with warm/dry blankets c. Moderate and severely hypothermic patients are often hypovolemic; initiate IV and administer warm saline or D5W 500 mL-2 Liters d. Avoid administering Lactated Ringer’s solution due to potential complications with potassium and lactate contents e. If above measures are insufficient, use warm packs to chest, neck, armpits and groin f. “Not dead till you’re warm and dead”- if CPR becomes necessary, follow ACLS protocols including up to three rounds of shocks and medications; if patient doesn’t respond, continue CPR until core temperature is above 90°F then renew attempts with ACLS shocks and medications per ACLS protocols 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION CLINICAL NOTE: Beware of vital signs inconsistent with temperature. Tachycardia or hyperventilation with low core temperature suggests hypoglycemia, hypovolemia, or overdose/poisoning. CLINICAL NOTE: Move ca

L NOTE: Beware of vital signs inconsistent with temperature. Tachycardia or hyperventilation with low core temperature suggests hypoglycemia, hypovolemia, or overdose/poisoning. CLINICAL NOTE: Move carefully; rough handling may precipitate ventricular fibrillation. Keep patient in horizontal position to avoid aggravating hypotension and circulatory collapse. Remember, resuscitation efforts for hypothermia victims are not considered failed until the patient core temperature is above 95˚ F and there is no response.

225 C. Prevention 1. Dress to maintain whole body warmth; use mittens instead of gloves as much as possible; make sure vulnerable areas such as face, neck, nose and corneas are covered; clothes should be adequately ventilated 2. Avoid being wet in cool or temperate environments 3. Avoid perspiring in cold weather; activity should be slowed to prevent sweating 4. Ensure troops maintain adequate hydration and nutrition 5. Avoid tobacco and alcohol in extreme environments, even in camp 6. Many weight loss and muscle building supplements decrease the body’s ability to compensate for environmental stressors; recommend discontinuing use in extreme conditions

226 Burns VIII. THERMAL AND RADIATION BURNS- Burns were previously categorized as 1st, 2nd, or 3rd degree, but now are classified by depth of injury to more accurately reflect which burns will need surgical intervention. When analyzing the burn, consider length of exposure, temperature of environment, thickness of skin at burn area, inhalation of gases at time of burn, what burned material was made of (plastics, chemicals, etc), patient age, and patient comorbidities such as diabetes or coronary artery disease. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Superficial- dry, red, painful burn; blanches with pressure; peels after several days ACTION ALERT: The following patients should be referred to a Burn Center: 1. Any deep partial thickness or full thickness burns of face, eyes, ears, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, or major joints 2. Full thickness burns of more than 5% BSA in any age group 3. Partial or full thickness burns greater than 20% BSA in all age groups 4. Burns involving more than 10% BSA (partial thickness and full thickness) in patients older than 50 or younger than 10 Additional Burn Center referrals include: 1. Electrical burns (especially lightning) 2. Inhalation injuries with burn

partial thickness and full thickness) in patients older than 50 or younger than 10 Additional Burn Center referrals include: 1. Electrical burns (especially lightning) 2. Inhalation injuries with burns 3. Any circumferential burns 4. Concurrent burn and trauma 5. Pediatric burns 6. Victims with pre-existing medical problems 7. Chemical burns threatening disfigurement or loss of function CLINICAL NOTE: As a quick rule of thumb, use the size of a patient’s palm to measure 1% body surface area (BSA). For larger burns, use the “Rule of Nines”- each of the following areas represents 9% of total body surface area: 1. Anterior of one leg 2. Posterior of one leg 3. One arm 4. Chest 5. Abdomen 6. Upper back 7. Lower back 8. Head Genitalia = 1% total body surface area

227 2. Superficial partial thickness- blisters, moist, red, weeping, blanches with pressure, painful to temperature and air 3. Deep partial thickness- blisters, variable colors from cheesy white to red, waxy dry areas, does not blanch with pressure, sensation intact to pressure only 4. Full thickness- Waxy white to leathery grey to charred and black, dry and inelastic, sensation absent or intact to deep pressure only 5. Fourth degree- Extends into fascia and/or muscle, sensation absent or intact to deep pressure only B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Ensure safety of patient and rescue team; evacuate away from disaster if indicated; carefully remove any burning material and jewelry to avoid further injury b. Airway, breathing, O2, circultion, monitor NOTE: pulse oximetry is NOT reliable in thermal burn patients due to possibility of carbon monoxide poisoning c. Treat any life threatening trauma (See “Emergency Protocols- Trauma”) d. Immediately cool wounds with sterile water or saline-soaked gauze to prevent further tissue damage e. Continually reassess respiratory rate and depth; be prepared for abrupt airway closure necessitating intubation and/or cricothyroidotomy (will be mor

e-soaked gauze to prevent further tissue damage e. Continually reassess respiratory rate and depth; be prepared for abrupt airway closure necessitating intubation and/or cricothyroidotomy (will be more likely in patients with facial burns, neck burns, cough, wheeze, hoarseness, and/or charred material in sputum or when blowing nose) f. Obtain 2 large-bore IV’s placed in unburned areas (if possible); give Lactated Ringer’s or other crystalloid according to Parkland Burn Formula g. Administer single dose of morphine up to 10 mg IV if patient’s pain is incapacitating h. Administer single dose of lorazepam (Ativan®, Temesta®) up to 4 mg IV if patient is severely anxious (do not mix in same IV line) i. Be prepared for severe respiratory depression requiring mechanical ventilation if both medications are administered; onset of respiratory depression may occur an hour or more after medication administration

228 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Confirm fluid resuscitation requirements b. Notify if morphine and/or lorazepam were administered c. Burn patients may require extremely large doses of morphine; consider administering an additional 5 mg morphine every 10 minutes until pain is no longer incapacitating (Requires preceptor approval) d. Burn patients may be extremely anxious; consider administering additional 2 mg lorazepam every 20 minutes until anxiety is no longer incapacitating (requires preceptor approval) e. Monitor for airway compromise and reassess vitals every 10 minutes for at least two hours or until patient is stabilized (whichever is longer) f. Consider nasogastric tube and urinary catheter placement ACTION ALERT: How to use the Parkland Burn Formula (using pounds): To calculate total fluids that need to be administered over first 24 hours: 1.8 × patient’s weight in pounds × percentage of body burned with 2nd or 3rd degree burns Administer half the fluid in the first 8 hours, and half the fluid over the next 16 hours EXAMPLE: 200 lb male with 2nd degree burns covering chest and abdomen (9% + 9% = 18%) 1.8 × 200 × 18 = 6480 mL in first 24 hours Fl

luid in the first 8 hours, and half the fluid over the next 16 hours EXAMPLE: 200 lb male with 2nd degree burns covering chest and abdomen (9% + 9% = 18%) 1.8 × 200 × 18 = 6480 mL in first 24 hours Fluid quantity for first 8 hours = ½ (6480) = 3240 mL administered in first 8 hours Fluid rate for first 8 hours = 3240 mL ÷ 8 hrs = 405 mL/hr or about 1 L every 2 ½ hours Fluid quantity for remaining 16 hours = ½(6480) = 3240 mL administered over 16 hours Fluid rate for remaining 16 hours = 3240 mL ÷ 16 hrs = 202.5 mL/hr or about 1 L every 5 hours CLINICAL NOTE: Fluid under-resuscitation and over-resuscitation are equally dangerous. Not providing enough fluids leads to decreased organ perfusion, kidney failure, multi-organ system failure, and death. Giving too many fluids leads to acute respiratory distress syndrome, compartment syndrome, multi-organ system failure, and death. It is therefore CRUCIAL to calculate the correct amount of fluids to give (See “How to use the Parkland Burn Formula” above). Following the formula will usually suffice; monitor urinary output and blood pressure to ensure adequate perfusion. If patient is unable to urinate within 4 hours of starting IV therapy, or

a” above). Following the formula will usually suffice; monitor urinary output and blood pressure to ensure adequate perfusion. If patient is unable to urinate within 4 hours of starting IV therapy, or at any point if patient is orthostatic, CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION to consider fluid bolus or increase in IV fluid rate.

229 g. Patients are at high risk for hypothermia; consider using warmed fluids and monitor core temperature with each reassessment 3. Debride and dress wounds a. Use a sterile dry sheet or dressing material if transport to burn center can occur in under an hour b. Apply silver sulfadiazine (Silvadene®) to all partial or full thickness burns that do not involve the face or genitalia c. Apply Polysporin® ointment to all partial or full thickness burns of the face or genitalia d. Dress all partial and full thickness burns with Xeroform Gauze® 4. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or > 5 years; consider tetanus immunoglobulin IX. CHEMICAL BURNS- Treatment of chemical burns is dependent on the type of chemical involved. A. General Considerations 1. Remove all clothing, jewelry, gear, etc that may be potentially contaminated 2. Identify substance, if possible 3. Instruct non-medical assistant to locate MSDS data sheet and follow substance-specific treatment guidelines 4. Brush away all dry particles and powders 5. Unless specified below, immediately irrigate with copious amounts of high volume, low pressure warm water 6. Irrigate all contaminated skin surfaces cont

4. Brush away all dry particles and powders 5. Unless specified below, immediately irrigate with copious amounts of high volume, low pressure warm water 6. Irrigate all contaminated skin surfaces continuously for a minimum of 20 minutes, even if “burning” sensation has resolved 7. Irrigate all eye contaminations a minimum of 30 minutes; alkali exposures will require several hours of irrigation B. Decontamination Based on Type of Chemical 1. Dry lime or soda ash- brush away; don’t get wet while particles are still present; water will increase corrosive potential and extent of injury 2. Phenol- absorb with sponges soaked in 50% polyethylene glycol; if polyethylene glycol is not available, use copious low-pressure irrigation 3. Lithium, Magnesium, and Sodium metals- Explosive when contacted with water; immerse particles in oil 4. Sulfuric acid- heats up when combined with water producing full-thickness burns; treat with soap or lime to neutralize prior to irrigation 5. White Phosphorus- burns in contact with air and can produce gas from patient contamination; brush dry particles away and then gently cleanse with solution of soap and water (phosphorus pentoxide is the off-gas. When hyd

urns in contact with air and can produce gas from patient contamination; brush dry particles away and then gently cleanse with solution of soap and water (phosphorus pentoxide is the off-gas. When hydrolyzed,(exposed to mucous membrane) it takes on the oxygen from the water and becomes phosphuric acid…making the off-gas extremely dangerous) C. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Ensure safety of patient and rescue team; evacuate away from disaster if indicated; wear appropriate PPE based on contaminant to avoid injury during rescue effort

230 b. See “Environmental injury protocols, thermal burns” for burn treatment guidelines D. Prevention 1. Be familiar with hazardous chemicals and their treatments in each work area 2. Ensure up-to-date MSDS safety sheets are immediately available in each work area where hazardous chemicals are used 3. Ensure toxin-specific treatments are available in work area where hazardous chemicals are used (example: polyethylene glycol in work areas using phenols) X. ELECTRICAL INJURIES- Severity of wound determined by type and amount of current exposure, duration of exposure, and whether or not current crossed vital organs. Injury patterns involve three aspects: direct effect of electricity on the functioning of the body, burn injuries associated with exposure, and mechanical trauma to the body due to muscle contractions (like the injuries seen in seizure patients) or falls. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. History of witnessed electrical injury or suspicion based on location patient is found is sufficient to consider electrical injury 2. Expose patient; observe for any entrance and exit wounds 3. Size of electrical wounds DOES NOT indicate severity of injury; small wounds often indicate deep burns

ent to consider electrical injury 2. Expose patient; observe for any entrance and exit wounds 3. Size of electrical wounds DOES NOT indicate severity of injury; small wounds often indicate deep burns and severe electrical effects 4. Assume multiple organ involvement 5. Assume head and neck trauma from seizure-like activity; assume major trauma from falls or being thrown by high voltage 6. Fixed, dilated pupils are a result of paralysis of the ocular muscles and NOT necessarily an indication of severe brain injury B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Ensure safety of patient and rescue team; evacuate away from disaster if indicated b. For multiple electrocution casualties, treat THOSE REQUIRING CPR FIRST (normally these patients would be considered “expectant” or “deceased” using standard mass casualty triage) c. Airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor d. Be prepared for prolonged administration of CPR; patients are more likely to recover from electrical causes of cardiopulmonary arrest than other causes e. Treat any life threatening trauma (See “Emergency Protocols- Trauma”) f. Immediately cool wounds with sterile water or saline-soaked gauze to prevent further tissue damage g.

est than other causes e. Treat any life threatening trauma (See “Emergency Protocols- Trauma”) f. Immediately cool wounds with sterile water or saline-soaked gauze to prevent further tissue damage g. Continually reassess respiratory rate and depth; be prepared for abrupt airway closure necessitating intubation and/or cricothyroidotomy (will be more likely in patients with facial burns, neck burns, cough, wheeze, hoarseness, and/or charred material in sputum or when blowing nose) h. Obtain 2 large-bore IV’s placed in unburned areas (if possible)

231 i. Victims of electrical burns are similar to patients with severe crush injuries and often need massive fluid resuscitation; DO NOT use Parkland Burn Formula® since this is likely to grossly underestimate needed fluids; DO NOT use any fluids containing potassium (such as Lactated Ringer’s) since this will increase potential for cardiac arrhythmias and renal failure j. Administer 2 Liters normal saline if available or may use normal saline; bolus the 2 Liters over one hour k. Decrease fluid rate to 500 mL per hour and switch fluids to D5 ½ NS or as directed by preceptor after first hour l. Administer single dose of morphine up to 10 mg IV if patient’s pain is incapacitating m. Administer single dose of lorazepam (Ativan®, Temesta®) up to 4 mg IV if patient is severely anxious (do not mix in same IV line) n. Be prepared for severe respiratory depression requiring mechanical ventilation if both medications are administered; onset of respiratory depression may occur an hour or more after medication administration 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Confirm fluid resuscitation requirements b. Insert indwelling urinary catheter to measure urinary output; urine ou

ter medication administration 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Confirm fluid resuscitation requirements b. Insert indwelling urinary catheter to measure urinary output; urine output must be 100 mL per hour or greater in the first two hours of resuscitation; if it is not; discontinue IV fluids c. Notify if morphine and/or lorazepam (Ativan®, Temesta®) were administered d. Monitor for airway compromise and reassess vitals every 10 minutes for at least two hours or until patient is stabilized (whichever is longer) e. Consider nasogastric tube placement f. Patients are at risk for hypothermia; consider using warmed fluids and monitor core temperature with each reassessment 3. Administer tetanus toxoid if last immunization is unknown or > 5 years; consider tetanus immunoglobulin C. Prevention 1. Work with safety monitor; perform regular shop visits and correct any electrical safety hazards 2. Ensure all safety equipment is well maintained (no holes in electrical gloves, etc) Altitude Illnesses CLINICAL NOTE: The onset of altitude illness can occur as low as 6,000 feet. Twenty percent of cases occur when ascending to greater than 8,200 feet in less than one day. As

s, etc) Altitude Illnesses CLINICAL NOTE: The onset of altitude illness can occur as low as 6,000 feet. Twenty percent of cases occur when ascending to greater than 8,200 feet in less than one day. Ascents should be slowed to less than 2,000 feet per day when moving over 8,000 feet.

232 XI. ACUTE MOUNTAIN SICKNESS (AMS)- Common syndrome caused by decreased oxygen availability to the tissues due to higher altitudes and compounded by inadequate acclimatization. NOTE: acute mountain sickness is the early stage of high altitude cerebral edema (HACE). Prompt recognition and treatment of acute mountain sickness is necessary to avoid potentially fatal progression to HACE. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Risk factors include flying directly to higher altitude (such as ski resort cities), heavy exertion before acclimatization is complete, rapid ascent greater than 2,000 feet per day above 8,000 feet, and residence at low altitude 2. Headache within first 24 hours of reaching altitude 3. Fatigue, nausea, dyspnea, sleep disturbances, dizziness, mild shortness of breath with exertion 4. Check for pulmonary crackles and complete a full neurologic exam B. Treatment 1. Mild cases should resolve spontaneously within 48 hours of reaching altitude 2. Symptoms should be easily reversed with administration of supplemental oxygen; if oxygenation does not improve symptoms, consider alternate diagnosis 3. Limit exercise or activity for 48 hours to aid with acclimatization 4. Patient must b

h administration of supplemental oxygen; if oxygenation does not improve symptoms, consider alternate diagnosis 3. Limit exercise or activity for 48 hours to aid with acclimatization 4. Patient must be monitored closely for progression to HACE over subsequent 48 hours 5. Headaches may be treated with aspirin or ibuprofen (Motrin®) 6. Nausea and vomiting may be treated with ondansetron (Zofran®) 7. Consider descending to lower altitude 8. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- CONSULTATION 9. Consider dexamethasone (Decadron®) or acetazolamide (Diamox®) per preceptor 10. Consider staying at higher altitude during the day and sleeping at lower altitude C. Prevention 1. Proper mission planning should include gradual ascent and time to acclimatize at each change in elevation 2. Monitor new arrivals over first 48 hours 3. Proper hydration 4. Nutritious diet 5. Avoid vigorous exertion in first few days at new altitude 6. Alcohol, tobacco, sleeping medications, and many weight loss and muscle- building supplements interfere with the body’s ability to respond to altitude stress; therefore all of these should be discontinued prior to journey to altitude XII. HIGH ALTITUDE CEREBRAL EDEMA (HACE)- Encep

supplements interfere with the body’s ability to respond to altitude stress; therefore all of these should be discontinued prior to journey to altitude XII. HIGH ALTITUDE CEREBRAL EDEMA (HACE)- Encephalopathy (brain swelling) that causes neurologic dysfunction and can be fatal. Symptoms are usually preceded by symptoms of acute mountain sickness. Prompt recognition and treatment is critical. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Severe headache, irritability, ataxic (stumbling) gait, drowsiness, confusion, hallucinations, and coma progressing to death

233 2. Severity of illness often overlooked; can be mistaken for fatigue or exhaustion; patients will often minimize the severity of their symptoms and go somewhere alone and quiet 3. Stumbling gait is usually first symptom 4. Impaired coordination with finger-nose and heel-shin testing is common B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Prompt recognition is critical while patient is still ambulatory! Immediate descent is the key to survival b. Recommend bed rest, but if patient is in isolated environment and ambulatory, patient may assist in their own evacuation in order to make descent more rapid c. Airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor; O2 administered at 2-4 LPM via nasal cannula is usually sufficient and can be lifesaving as long as HAPE is not present d. If not ambulatory, obtain IV access and administer normal saline at maintenance rate 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Hyperbaric therapy if immediate descent is not possible C. Prevention 1. Proper mission planning should include gradual ascent and time to acclimatize at each change in elevation 2. Monitor new arrivals over first 48 hours 3. Proper hydration 4. Nutritious diet 5. Avoid vigorous exertio

planning should include gradual ascent and time to acclimatize at each change in elevation 2. Monitor new arrivals over first 48 hours 3. Proper hydration 4. Nutritious diet 5. Avoid vigorous exertion in first few days at new altitude 6. Alcohol, tobacco, sleeping medications, and many weight loss and muscle- building supplements interfere with the body’s ability to respond to altitude stress; therefore all of these should be discontinued prior to journey to altitude XIII. HIGH ALTITUDE PULMONARY EDEMA (HAPE)- The abnormal collection of fluid in the lung due to a breakdown in the lung’s blood-gas barrier from exposure to decreased atmospheric oxygen levels. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Onset is usually subtle with mild nonproductive cough, mild shortness of breath, and difficulty walking uphill occurring within first week of being at new altitude (similar to all persons trying to acclimatize) 2. Progresses to dyspnea at rest, which is how the IDMT can tell when interventions are necessary; pulse oximetry readings typically 10 points below expected value for altitude; patients typically look better than expected ACTION ALERT: At first sign of ataxia or mental status change, descent sho

; pulse oximetry readings typically 10 points below expected value for altitude; patients typically look better than expected ACTION ALERT: At first sign of ataxia or mental status change, descent should be started. Do not delay descent while considering alternative diagnoses or attempting to reach preceptor.

234 3. Approximately 50% of cases have signs and symptoms of acute mountain sickness 4. Tachycardia, tachypnea, low grade fever, and crackles initially in the right middle lobe (listen at right lateral mid-chest) and progresses to all lobes 5. Untreated, can progress to cough productive of pink/frothy sputum, frank hemoptysis, cyanosis, neurologic dysfunction, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Bed rest, remove from cold environment b. Prompt recognition is critical! Patient’s initial symptoms will be subtle c. Airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor; provide enough oxygen to maintain O2 saturation greater than 90% by pulse oximetry d. Have patient exhale through pursed lips on expiration to increase expiratory pressure in lungs e. If patient is unconscious, consider EPAP (expiratory positive airway pressure) or CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) if equipment available to do so f. If not ambulatory, obtain IV access and administer normal saline at maintenance rate 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Hyperbaric therapy if immediate descent is not possible Barotrauma XIV. BAROTRAUMA- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure change

e 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Hyperbaric therapy if immediate descent is not possible Barotrauma XIV. BAROTRAUMA- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure changes relative to gas-containing structures of the body. Barotrauma can affect the lungs, ears, sinuses, GI tract and tooth fillings. Most barotrauma-related injuries resolve spontaneously and require only symptomatic, outpatient treatment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Ear fullness and pain during descent 2. Patient may complain of tinnitus, which may be debilitating 3. Middle ear hemorrhage and TM rupture is possible 4. Decreased TM mobility 5. Positive vestibular tests (Romberg, nystagmus) if oval window is affected B. Treatment 1. Have patient attempt autoinflation by swallowing, yawning or Valsalva maneuver 2. Administer systemic decongestants such as pseudoephedrine (Sudafed®) 3. Administer topical decongestants such as oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin®) 2 sprays into each nostril, BID no more than 3 days 4. Administer analgesics for pain such as acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or ibuprofen (Motrin®) XV. BAROTRAOUMA, SINUS- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure changes relative t

ore than 3 days 4. Administer analgesics for pain such as acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or ibuprofen (Motrin®) XV. BAROTRAOUMA, SINUS- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure changes relative to gas-containing structures of the body. Barotrauma can affect the lungs, ears,

235 sinuses, GI tract and tooth fillings. Most barotrauma-related injuries resolve spontaneously and require only symptomatic, outpatient treatment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Most often affects the frontal sinuses 2. Mild pressure, pain, and tenderness 3. Hemorrhage, epistaxis B. Treatment 1. Have patient attempt auto-inflation by swallowing, yawning or Valsalva maneuver 2. Administer systemic decongestants such as pseudoephedrine (Sudafed®) 3. Administer topical decongestants such as oxymetazoline hydrochloride (Afrin®) 2 sprays into each nostril, BID no more than 3 days 4. Administer analgesics for pain such as acetaminophen (Tylenol®) or ibuprofen (Motrin®) XVI. BAROTRAUMA, DENTAL- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure changes relative to gas-containing structures of the body. Barotrauma can affect the lungs, ears, sinuses, GI tract and tooth fillings. Most barotrauma-related injuries resolve spontaneously and require only symptomatic, outpatient treatment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Occurs when a patient with infected tooth or recently repaired tooth is subjected to pressure changes 2. Tenderness with percussion (tooth rake) B. Treatment- See “Dental Protocols, Barot

Symptoms 1. Occurs when a patient with infected tooth or recently repaired tooth is subjected to pressure changes 2. Tenderness with percussion (tooth rake) B. Treatment- See “Dental Protocols, Barotrauma- Dental” XVII. BAROTRAUMA, GASTROINTESTINAL- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure changes relative to gas-containing structures of the body. Barotrauma can affect the lungs, ears, sinuses, GI tract and tooth fillings. Most barotrauma-related injuries resolve spontaneously and require only symptomatic, outpatient treatment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Caused by swallowing air during a dive 2. Cramps, abdominal fullness, pain, belching and flatulence 3. Visceral organ rupture is characterized by intense pain, positive rebound tenderness, and guarding B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Aggressive fluid resuscitation b. Do not give anything PO—no oral medications, fluids, etc. c. Keep patient in position of comfort and minimize abdominal exams/manipulation 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Initiate broad spectrum antibiotics IV per preceptor instructions and availability b. Immediate surgical consultation XVIII. BAROTRAUMA, PULMONARY- Barotrauma is

AN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Initiate broad spectrum antibiotics IV per preceptor instructions and availability b. Immediate surgical consultation XVIII. BAROTRAUMA, PULMONARY- Barotrauma is tissue injury resulting from pressure changes relative to gas-containing structures of the body. Barotrauma can affect the

236 lungs, ears, sinuses, GI tract and tooth fillings. Most barotrauma-related injuries resolve spontaneously and require only symptomatic, outpatient treatment. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Caused by expanding gas during dive ascent or rapid decompression at altitude 2. Alveolar rupture/pneumothroax causing dyspnea, chest pain and decreased breath sounds 3. Pneumomediastinum—Sensation of fullness, neck pain, pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, coughing and hoarseness with subcutaneous emphysema (“Rice Krispies®” under skin) 4. Positive Hamman’s sign (mediastinal crunch or rasping that occurs with the heartbeat) 5. May result in arterial gas embolism B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask b. Patients displaying neurologic signs or those with pulmonary/GI complaints should be immediately evacuated to a recompression chamber for treatment 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Patients displaying neurologic signs or those with pulmonary/GI complaints should be immediately evacuated to a recompression chamber b. If recompression will be delayed, consider other compression options such as repeat dive 3. Altitude restriction during evacuation should

should be immediately evacuated to a recompression chamber b. If recompression will be delayed, consider other compression options such as repeat dive 3. Altitude restriction during evacuation should be considered

237 Chapter 16: Occupational Exposure Protocols I. LEAD POISONING- Leaded paint was commonly used in paint products distributed in the U.S. before 1960 and is still used in developing countries. Exposure occurs during remodeling projects or accidental ingestion of chipped paint. Some ceramic glazes also contain lead and pose an ingestion hazard when used serving food or drink (particularly when used with acidic food products like fruit juice, tomato products and wine from leaded crystal). In manufacturing, lead is used in glass making, bronzing, welding, brass manufacturing, and leaded gasolines. Military occupations that may involve exposure to lead include plumbers, electricians, mechanics, shipbuilders, printers, police officers, steel workers, and firing range instructors. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Acute poisoning- abdominal pain, constipation, tremors, mood changes, joint pain, muscle aches, headache, anorexia, difficulty concentrating, decreased libido, short-term memory loss 2. Chronic exposure- Personality changes (anxiety and hostility), headache, myalgias, fatigue, irritability, insomnia, anorexia, difficulty concentrating, and short-term memory loss, decreased sperm count

hronic exposure- Personality changes (anxiety and hostility), headache, myalgias, fatigue, irritability, insomnia, anorexia, difficulty concentrating, and short-term memory loss, decreased sperm count 3. Chronic low-level exposure- cataracts, hearing loss, tooth loss, kidney cancer 4. Patients develop anemia because lead interferes with the body’s production of hemoglobin 5. Diagnosis is made by history of exposure and increased lead levels in the blood. B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION (Acute poisoning) a. Eliminate source of contamination b. Check others in work area for similar symptoms 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Perform gastric lavage and irrigation of the digestive tract using polyethylene glycol if directed by preceptor b. Lab: CBC, lead levels, CHEM 7, peripheral smear c. Patients with lead exposure require heavy metal chelating drugs; this therapy should not be administered by the IDMT 3. PREVENTION a. At risk populations should be screened for lead annually b. Tobacco products can contain low levels of lead; recommend smoking cessation c. Personnel should use appropriate PPE while at work and change clothing and footwear prior to returning home

annually b. Tobacco products can contain low levels of lead; recommend smoking cessation c. Personnel should use appropriate PPE while at work and change clothing and footwear prior to returning home d. Personnel should shower prior to sleep e. Personnel should not be allowed to eat or smoke in or near their work area; all personnel must wash hands prior to eating or smoking to reduce chance of ingestion II. CARBON MONOXIDE POISONING (CO POISONING)- Carbon monoxide is primarily released as a byproduct of incomplete combustion. Carbon Monoxide is odorless, tasteless, and has twice the affinity for hemoglobin than oxygen. This characteristic poses a serious hazard to exposed individuals because it tightly binds the hemoglobin and thus prevents

238 hemoglobin from carrying oxygen to the tissues. Sources include acetylene gas, automobile exhaust, fossil fuel combustion, furnace gas, illuminating gas and marsh gas. Exposures most commonly occur when motorized machines, electrical generators, or gas heaters are used in closed environments such as vehicles in a parking garage or warehouse, kerosene heaters indoors, or charcoal grills in the garage. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Variable depending on length of exposure and concentration of the inhaled gas 2. Headache, exertional shortness of breath, dizziness, nausea/vomiting, drowsiness, dry mouth 3. Bright red/rosy cheeks and lips, bounding pulse, hypertension, tachypnea, dyspnea 4. Often mistaken for viral syndrome- key differences are drowsiness and pupil dilation 5. Progresses to confusion, syncope, visual disturbances, muscle fasciculations (twitching), seizures and coma (Death occurs when carboxyhemoglobin blood levels are >60%) B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, 100% O₂ via tight fitting aviator’s mask, monitor b. Hyperbaric O₂ if available 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION C. Prevention 1. Smokers have up to 20% carboxyhemoglobin leve

ing, 100% O₂ via tight fitting aviator’s mask, monitor b. Hyperbaric O₂ if available 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION C. Prevention 1. Smokers have up to 20% carboxyhemoglobin levels at rest; recommend smoking cessation 2. Check CO levels in shops or living areas where CO is suspect and ventilation is poor 3. Install CO detectors in living/working areas III. BENZENE- Benzenes include toluene, xylene, benzol, and toluol. These toxins are common in household items such as plastic adhesives and glue. They are also found in industrial fluids such as JP-8, Avgas, etc. These compounds are also frequently abused by “huffing” due to the euphoric effects at lower doses. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Acute and chronic exposure (ingestion, inhalation, or absorption) can all cause dizziness, weakness, headache, euphoria, nausea and vomiting, ventricular arrhythmias, ataxia, blurred vision, paralysis and seizures 2. Acute exposure often presents with fever up to 104°F 3. If route was inhalational, may have evidence of pulmonary injury such as coughing, choking, gagging, tachypnea, dypsnea, cyanosis, wheezing, nasal flaring, or grunting respirations 4. Chronic, long term exposures

inhalational, may have evidence of pulmonary injury such as coughing, choking, gagging, tachypnea, dypsnea, cyanosis, wheezing, nasal flaring, or grunting respirations 4. Chronic, long term exposures cause aplastic anemia, hypokalemia, leukemia and CNS depression B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Decontaminate patient with water and prevent aspiration of vomitus b. Airway, breathing, circulation, O2, monitor

239 c. Initiate IV with normal saline at maintenance rate; bolus as necessary d. If seizures occur, administer lorazepam (Ativan®) 4 mg IV/IM or diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IV e. Keep patient NPO 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. For severe anemia, consider walking blood bank/transfusion b. Replace K (give “banana bag” – 10 mL liquid multivitamins + 100 mg thimine + 1 mg folic acid mixed in 1 Liter normal saline) if available C. Prevention 1. Personnel should use appropriate PPE while at work and change clothing and footwear prior to returning home 2. Personnel should shower prior to sleep 3. Personnel should not be allowed to eat or smoke in or near their work area; all personnel must wash hands prior to eating or smoking to reduce chance of ingestion IV. HYDROCARBONS (PETROLIUM DISTILLATES)- Poisoning results from ingestion or inhalation that can result in aspiration pneumonitis. Hydrocarbons include asphalt, benzenes, gasoline, kerosene, lubricating oils, paint thinner and tar. Ingestion or inhalation occurs most often while attempting to siphon a liquid hydrocarbon from one container to another. These compounds are also frequently abused by “huffing” due to th

nner and tar. Ingestion or inhalation occurs most often while attempting to siphon a liquid hydrocarbon from one container to another. These compounds are also frequently abused by “huffing” due to the euphoric effects at lower doses. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Initially, patients cough, choke, and/or vomit 2. Patients complain of epigastric burning sensation, diarrhea and pneumonia if aspiration has occurred 3. Aspiration pneumonitis causes hypoxia and respiratory distress 4. For vapor inhalation, patients complain of chest pain/burning, headache, nausea, weakness, CNS depression, confusion, dyspnea, tachypnea and rales B. Treatment 1. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- EVACUATION 2. Gastric lavage is usually not necessary for accidental ingestion 3. Provide supportive care for pulmonary edema, O₂ and respiratory support as necessary C. Prevention 1. Ensure proper equipment for siphoning is present where needed 2. Personnel should use appropriate PPE while at work and change clothing and footwear prior to returning home 3. Personnel should shower prior to sleep 4. Personnel should not be allowed to eat or smoke in or near their work area; all personnel must wash hands prior to eating or s

wear prior to returning home 3. Personnel should shower prior to sleep 4. Personnel should not be allowed to eat or smoke in or near their work area; all personnel must wash hands prior to eating or smoking to reduce chance of ingestion ACTION ALERT: Ventricular fibrillation can occur quickly due to hypersensitive myo- cardium. EPINEPHERINE SHOULD BE AVOIDED unless directed by your Preceptor.

240 V. ORGANOPHOSPHATE POISONING- Organophosphates include many pesticides, insecticides, herbicides and chemical weapons (nerve agents and defoliants). In addition to exposures to weaponized nerve agents, deployed personnel may also be exposed due to unregulated use/distribution of these products in under developed countries. Organophosphates cause extreme stimulation of secretions and smooth muscle. Poisoning can occur through inhalation, absorption or ingestion. NOTE: This section covers organophosphate poisoning in general and does not review standing orders/instructions for response to casualty exposure from chemical weapons. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Excessive salivation, lacrimation (tearing), sweating, and rhinorrhea 2. Nausea, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, urination, vomiting 3. Increased pulmonary secretions, frothing from the mouth 4. Blurred vision, miosis, slurred speech, confusion, headache 5. Difficulty breathing, seizures, coma, and death B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, monitor, consider endotracheal intubation b. Remove clothing, brush off dry chemical and flush with copious amounts of water c. Use M-291 kit, 0.5% hypochlorite solution, or

a. Airway, breathing, O2, monitor, consider endotracheal intubation b. Remove clothing, brush off dry chemical and flush with copious amounts of water c. Use M-291 kit, 0.5% hypochlorite solution, or soap and water to decontaminate skin d. Initiate large-bore IV, bolus 1 Liter normal saline or Lactated Ringer’s e. Administer atropine 5 mg IV/IM for adults; repeat every 5 minutes until excessive secretions stop; may require HUNDREDS of mg of atropine over several days 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION a. Consider pralidoxime (2-PAM®) 1-2g IV over 15-30 minutes if directed by Preceptor b. Consider diazepam (Valium®) 5 mg IV if seizures occur or if directed by Preceptor C. Prevention 1. Wear PPE when handling/disposing of known organophosphates 2. Detectors include: a. M256A1 chemical agent detector kit NSN 6665-01-133-4964 b. Improved Chemical Agent Alarm (ICAM) NSN 6665-01-199-4153 c. M-8 paper NSN 6665-00-050-8529 d. M-9 paper NSN 6665-01-049-8982 e. M-272 chemical agent water testing kit NSN 6665-01-134-0885 3. Healthcare workers should wear appropriate PPE when decontaminating patients 4. Personnel should use appropriate PPE while at work and change clothing a

agent water testing kit NSN 6665-01-134-0885 3. Healthcare workers should wear appropriate PPE when decontaminating patients 4. Personnel should use appropriate PPE while at work and change clothing and footwear prior to returning home 5. Personnel should shower prior to sleep 6. Personnel should not be allowed to eat or smoke in or near their work area; all personnel must wash hands prior to eating or smoking to reduce chance of ingestion

241 VI. RADIATION SYNDROME- Radiation is the process in which energy is emitted from one source and absorbed by another. Radiation is classified as either ionizing or non-ionizing. Ionization is the removal of electrons from an atom. Ionizing radiation is any high energy radiation or any particle having the potential energy to remove electrons from another atom. Examples of ionized radiation include alpha, beta, photon, neutron and x radiation. Ionizing radiation is measured in rads, Becquerel (Bq), grays (Gy), and Sieverts (Sv) Low energy radiation [non-ionizing, or radio frequency radiation (RFR)] does not have enough energy to produce ions in other substances, but it does have enough energy to cause vibration, heat, and coagulation in protein. Examples of non-ionizing radiation include radio waves, microwaves, infrared waves and ultraviolet waves. RF radiation is measured in MHz. Typical injuries from non-ioninzing radiation sources include skin and ocular burns. A. Signs and Symptoms 1. Hematopoietic Syndrome a. Exposure of 100-500 rad b. Nausea, vomiting and fatigue c. Leukopenia (50% drop in 24 hours =lethal dose) d. Can progress to death within a few weeks to months 2. Gastr

ms 1. Hematopoietic Syndrome a. Exposure of 100-500 rad b. Nausea, vomiting and fatigue c. Leukopenia (50% drop in 24 hours =lethal dose) d. Can progress to death within a few weeks to months 2. Gastrointestinal Syndrome a. 600-1200 rad b. Nausea, fever, vomiting, fatigue, diarrhea and abdominal cramps c. Diarrhea may be bloody d. Cardiovascular collapse, kidney failure and shock from fluid shifts e. Can progress to death within 2 weeks 3. Neurovascular Syndrome a. >1500 rad b. Nausea, Vomiting, neurologic deficits within 1 hour c. Diarrhea, ARDS, and prostration within 4-5 hours d. Death within 2 days 4. Chronic exposure a. Thyroid cancer, leukemia, growth retardation, cataracts b. Infertility, fetal abnormalities B. Treatment 1. IMMEDIATE ACTION a. Airway, breathing, O2, circulation, monitor b. Decontaminate if alpha/beta particulates are suspect c. Treat all traumatic injuries (See “Emergency Medicine Protocols, Trauma”) 2. CONTACT PHYSICIAN PRECEPTOR- IMMEDIATE EVACUATION 3. Provide supportive care as necessary C. Prevention 1. Do not become a casualty. Ensure radiation-emitting equipment is powered down 2. Wear proper PPE when required 3. Operate within safe working distances

e supportive care as necessary C. Prevention 1. Do not become a casualty. Ensure radiation-emitting equipment is powered down 2. Wear proper PPE when required 3. Operate within safe working distances as defined by the Technical Order appropriate for the weapon system

242 4. The IDMT should familiarize his/her self with the hazards associated with the assigned weapon system. Antenna arrays, radar, x-ray equipment, aircraft antennas, and anti-armor munitions (not to mention nuclear weapons) all emit radiation capable of injury or death. Understanding the occupational risks in the workplace is the first step in appropriate patient care 5. See HMTF BEE for site-specific information on workplace hazards

243 Chapter 17: Basic Laboratory Information I. MACROSCOPIC URINE ANALYSIS (URINE DIPSTICK) - A rapid and non-invasive test that assesses the genitourinary system, hydration status, and metabolic abnormalities. A. Specimen Handling 1. Sample should be fresh, well mixed, and room temperature for examination 2. Unless otherwise specified, patient should be instructed in proper cleansing technique and collect clean midstream urine 3. May use same urine sample for urine HCG sampling, but may not use with gonorrhea/chlamydia testing since G/C sample should be from first portion of void and patient should not cleanse beforehand B. Color (normal: pale yellow, clear) 1. Red/brown- In young, healthy active duty population usually indicates blood. Red urine may also come from ingestion of food dyes, beets, or fava beans. IDMT should consider whether blood is coming from a leaky kidney or from trauma to the urinary system somewhere between the kidney and the urethra. Common causes include kidney stones, rhabdomyolysis, and pyelonephritis. Common drugs that may cause this color change include metronidazole (Flagyl®) and nitrofurantoin (Macrobid®) 2. Orange- May be related to taking bladder ana

stones, rhabdomyolysis, and pyelonephritis. Common drugs that may cause this color change include metronidazole (Flagyl®) and nitrofurantoin (Macrobid®) 2. Orange- May be related to taking bladder analgesic phenazopyridine (Pyridium®) used in treating urinary tract infections. May indicate metabolic conditions such as diabetes or hypothyroidism. May be related to ingestion of B-vitamins, beta-kerotenes (carrots), blackberries, beets, or rhubarb 3. Green- may indicate food dyes, drinking mouthwash, eating asparagus 4. Black- ingestion of stimulant laxatives such as senna (in Ex-Lax®), melanoma in the bladder C. pH- (normal: 5.5-6.5; range 4.5-8.0) 1. Alkali (high pH)- may indicate low carb or vegetarian diet, aspirin overdose, systemic alkylosis, or urinary tract infections by most common bacteria 2. Acid (low pH) may indicate high protein diet or starvation, diarrhea illnesses, diabetes, or systemic acidosis D. Specific Gravity (normal: 1.002-1.030g/mL) a. Low specific gravity- excessive water intake, kidney damage b. High specific gravity- dehydration, metabolic abnormalities E. Leukocytes/white blood cells/WBC (normal: none) 1. Urine should be sterile (without bacteria or white b

ve water intake, kidney damage b. High specific gravity- dehydration, metabolic abnormalities E. Leukocytes/white blood cells/WBC (normal: none) 1. Urine should be sterile (without bacteria or white blood cells) 2. Presence of white blood cells usually indicative of contaminated sample (poor clean catch technique) 3. White blood cells may also be present in patients with urinary tract infection, but their presence is not diagnostic 4. False positive caused by trichomonas or dyes that stain urine red F. Leukocyte Esterase (normal: none) 1. Comes from damaged or destroyed white blood cells 2. Indicates possible urinary tract infection G. Nitrites (normal: none)

244 1. Come from presence of pathologic bacteria converting dietary nitrates to nitrites; especially in presence of leukocyte esterase, is diagnostic for urinary tract infection 2. False negative can occur if urine has not been in bladder long enough for bacteria to cause conversion (patient provides sample too soon after last void) H. Urobilinogen (normal: 0.2-1.0 mg/dL) 1. Elevated in some liver diseases, hemolytic anemia, malaria, and mononucleosis 2. Low in some liver diseases I. Blood (normal: none) 1. Measures intact hemoglobin only. Positive result caused by trauma, infection, inflammation, kidney stones, vascular red blood cell damage (intravascular hemolysis) 2. Red, bloody appearance of urine in the absence of +blood on urine dipstick indicates probable presence of myoglobin; occurs in rhabdomyolysis, crush injuries, and electrocution J. Bilirubin (normal: none) 1. Elevated in liver disease, and different forms of hepatitis or gall bladder disease. May also be caused by pancreatitis. 2. False positives seen in patients taking high doses of vitamin C K. Protein (normal: none) 1. Kidney damage such as pyelonephritis or nephrotic syndrome destroys the filter ability of the k

eatitis. 2. False positives seen in patients taking high doses of vitamin C K. Protein (normal: none) 1. Kidney damage such as pyelonephritis or nephrotic syndrome destroys the filter ability of the kidney and allows proteins from the blood to leak into the urine 2. May also be caused by electrical injury, gout, or low blood potassium 3. Drugs that may cause elevations in urine protein include aminoglycoside, penicillin and sulfa antibiotics and NSAIDs L. Ketones (normal: none) 1. May indicate high fat/high protein diet, starvation, alcoholism, or metabolic diseases such as diabetes or hyperthyroidism 2. Drugs that cause ketones in the urine include antibiotics used for treatment of TB such as isoniazid M. Glucose (normal: none) 1. Most associated with diabetes, but may be associated with other metabolic abnormalities of the adrenal gland or thyroid gland or diseases of the liver 2. May be seen in pregnancy (suggestive of diabetes of pregnancy) 3. May be caused by multiple classes of antibiotics and steroids 4. False positives seen in patients taking high doses of vitamin C and when urine has high specific gravity and/or ketones II. BASIC METABOLIC PANEL (“BMP”, “CHEM 7”)- A blood

ntibiotics and steroids 4. False positives seen in patients taking high doses of vitamin C and when urine has high specific gravity and/or ketones II. BASIC METABOLIC PANEL (“BMP”, “CHEM 7”)- A blood test that gives basic information about the current status of the kidneys, blood sugar, electrolyte balance, and acid/base balance. Abnormalities and combinations of abnormal results can help explain abnormalities found on physical exam or in vital signs. A. Sodium (normal 135-145 mEq/dL) 1. In the body a. Positive ion (cation), 85% found in blood and lymph

245 b. Indicates volume status of the patient c. Regulated by kidney and adrenal gland via aldosterone d. Used in nerve impulse transmission and water balance regulation 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: dehydration, steroids, contraceptives, licorice, overhydrating with IV normal saline b. Decreased in: excessive sweating/sweating and replacing water without replacing salt, blood pressure meds, heart failure c. Sodium falls about 1.6 mmol/dL for every 100 mg/dL increase in glucose B. Potassium (normal: 3.5-5.3 mEq/dL) 1. In the Body a. Positive ion (cation) regulated by renal excretion b. Critical for nerve impulse transmission and muscle contraction 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: tissue damage, rhabdomyolysis, dehydration, dietary intake, blood pressure medicines b. Decreased in: low dietary intake, prolonged vomiting or diarrhea, hyperglycemia c. Often artificially elevated due to poor handling of sample causing lysis of red blood cells (sometimes from prolonged fist clench during draw, or tourniquet on too long during draw) C. Chloride (normal: 98-107 mEq/L) 1. In the Body a. Negative ion (anion) maintains normal acid-base balance b. Increased levels cause acidosis, decrea

ing draw, or tourniquet on too long during draw) C. Chloride (normal: 98-107 mEq/L) 1. In the Body a. Negative ion (anion) maintains normal acid-base balance b. Increased levels cause acidosis, decreased levels cause alkylosis 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: renal failure, nephrotic syndrome, dehydration, overtreatment with IV normal saline, salicylate (aspirin) toxicity b. Decreased in: vomiting, diarrhea, overtreatment with blood pressure medications, excessive sweating/sweating and replacing water without replacing salt, laxative overuse, burns D. Carbon Dioxide/Bicarbonate (CO2/HCO3-) (normal: 21-28 mEq/L) 1. In the Body a. Carbon dioxide is a waste product from oxygen consumption by tissues, but in the body is found primarily in the form of bicarbonate ion b. Levels are primarily regulated by the kidneys by urinating out bicarbonate, but also by the lungs through respiration 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: severe vomiting, hypoventilation b. Decreased in: chronic diarrhea, starvation, diabetic ketoacidosis, shock E. Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) (normal: 10-20 mg/dL) 1. In the Body a. Urea is formed in the liver from ammonia, which is a byproduct of protein metabolism
tarvation, diabetic ketoacidosis, shock E. Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) (normal: 10-20 mg/dL) 1. In the Body a. Urea is formed in the liver from ammonia, which is a byproduct of protein metabolism

246 b. BUN is urea moving from liver where proteins were broken down to the kidneys, where urea is excreted c. Measures both liver’s ability to break down protein and kidney’s ability to secrete and filter 2. Interpretation a. Elevated in: impaired renal function (shock, dehydration, etc), urinary tract obstruction, large protein breakdown such as supplement users; starvation due to muscle breakdown b. Liver failure (can’t break down protein), malabsorption/malnutrition F. Creatinine (normal: 0.4-1.5 mg/dL) 1. In the Body a. Breakdown product of skeletal muscle contraction b. Is produced at a constant rate based on muscle mass of individual c. Easily excreted by kidneys d. Primarily reflects filter capability of the kidney; if produced at a constant rate, should be filtered at a constant rate unless filter is broken 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: kidney disease, pyelonephritis, rhabdomyolysis, urinary tract obstruction, massively increased muscle mass b. Decreased in: decreased muscle mass, debilitation G. Glucose (normal; 65-110 mg/dL) 1. In the Body a. Comes from carbohydrate digestion and conversion of glycogen to glucose by liver b. Controlled by pancreatic hormones glucago

cle mass, debilitation G. Glucose (normal; 65-110 mg/dL) 1. In the Body a. Comes from carbohydrate digestion and conversion of glycogen to glucose by liver b. Controlled by pancreatic hormones glucagon (raises blood sugar by increasing glycogen to glucose) and insulin (drops blood sugar by enabling sugar to cross from bloodstream into cells) 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: Severe stress (infection, burns, surgery), pancreatitis, steroid therapy or abuse, diabetes mellitus b. Decreased in: insulin overdose, starvation, hypothyroidism III. COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT (“CBC”)- Measures the numbers of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets in the blood as well as some indicators of the health of these cells. A. Red Blood Cell Count (“RBC”) (normal: males 4.6-6.2, females 4.2-5.4) 1. In the Body a. Total number of red blood cells in an mL of blood b. Formed in bone marrow and have a lifespan of 120 days c. Females tend to have lower counts than males partly due to female menstruation d. Counts 10% or more below normal are called “anemia” 2. Interpretation a. Increased in severe diarrhea, burns b. Decreased in: anemia, hemorrhage, blood transfusion with incompatible blood, severe

d. Counts 10% or more below normal are called “anemia” 2. Interpretation a. Increased in severe diarrhea, burns b. Decreased in: anemia, hemorrhage, blood transfusion with incompatible blood, severe reactions to drugs B. White Blood Cell Count (“WBC”, “Leukocytes”) (normal: 5,000-10,000) 1. In the Body

247 a. Fights infections throughout the body b. Lymphocytes primarily attack viruses c. Neutrophils primarily attack bacteria d. Eosinophils attack parasites and are present in allergies 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: infection, trauma, stress, hemorrhage, cancers, dehydration, steroid use b. Decreased in: drug toxicity (chemotherapy), overwhelming infection, dietary deficiency such as B12 or iron, enlarged spleen (spleen pulls WBCs out of blood stream) C. Hemoglobin (“Hgb”) (normal: males 13.5-18 g/dL, females 12-16 g/dL) 1. In the Body a. The oxygen and carbon dioxide carrying component of a red blood cell b. The oxygen carrying capacity is determined by the hemoglobin concentration, rather than the RBC count 2. Interpretation a. Increased in severe diarrhea, burns b. Decreased in: anemia, hemorrhage, blood transfusion with incompatible blood, severe reactions to drugs D. Hematocrit (“Hct”) (normal: males 40-54%, females 38-47%) 1. In the Body a. The percentage of total blood volume that is made up by the RBC’s b. Normally about 3 times the hemoglobin concentration 2. Interpretation a. Increased in severe diarrhea, burns b. Decreased in: anemia, hemorrhage, blood transfusion

ume that is made up by the RBC’s b. Normally about 3 times the hemoglobin concentration 2. Interpretation a. Increased in severe diarrhea, burns b. Decreased in: anemia, hemorrhage, blood transfusion with incompatible blood, severe reactions to drugs E. Platelets (“thrombocyte count”) (normal: 150,000-400,000) 1. In the Body a. Essential for blood clotting b. Survive 7-10 days c. 75% usually in blood stream, 25% in liver and spleen 2. Interpretation a. Increased in: some cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, iron deficiency anemia b. Decreased in: enlarged spleen, hemorrhage, hemolytic anemia, infection IV. TESTING FOR SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED INFECTIONS- This section reviews the common laboratory tests used to screen for different genital infections. Note that many genital infections, including Molluscum contageosum and genital warts, have no laboratory tests associated with them. Many infections, such as chancroid and herpes, can only be tested if active lesions are present. Patients often come in requesting sexually transmitted infection checks “for everything” after unprotected sexual encounters. It is CRITICAL to educate them that although there are lab tests available for some infection

in requesting sexually transmitted infection checks “for everything” after unprotected sexual encounters. It is CRITICAL to educate them that although there are lab tests available for some infections, there are many more infections than those that have suitable screening tests. Clinic visits for infection checks are NOT a substitute for condom use or abstinence. A. Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) (blood)

248 1. Blood test 2. Screening test is usually rapid HIV or HIV enzyme immunoassay 3. If patient is having upper respiratory symptoms, fever, or other signs of HIV seroconversion, order HIV RNA test instead (check with local lab) B. Gonorrhea (urine or probe swab) 1. Multiple collection methods possible (test includes chlamydia) a. Can be tested on “dirty void” of first morning urine sample- have patient collect the very first urine produced and don’t cleanse beforehand, rather than the more common collection method of midstream clean catch b. Can be tested on dirty voids later in the day, but higher likelihood of false negative test c. Can be tested by urethral swab or endocervical swab; but higher likelihood of false negative test if patient has voided recently 2. If ordering urinalysis for the same patient, be aware of logistical complications to collect both specimens a. Performing swab test before urine analysis may introduce blood into the urine sample b. Performing urinalysis before gonorrhea swab may wash away infected sample c. Performing both urinalysis and gonorrhea on urine requires patient void dirty urine into one cup and clean midstream urine into another cup; has hi

fore gonorrhea swab may wash away infected sample c. Performing both urinalysis and gonorrhea on urine requires patient void dirty urine into one cup and clean midstream urine into another cup; has high risk of getting samples mislabeled even if performed correctly C. Chlamydia (urine or probe/swab) 1. Multiple collection methods possible (test includes gonorrhea) a. Can be tested on “dirty void” of first morning urine sample- have patient collect the very first urine produced and don’t cleanse beforehand, rather than the more common collection method of midstream clean catch b. Can be tested on dirty voids later in the day, but higher likelihood of false negative test c. Can be tested by urethral swab or endocervical swab; but higher likelihood of false negative test if patient has voided recently 2. If ordering urinalysis for the same patient, be aware of logistical complications to collect both specimens a. Performing swab test before urine analysis may introduce blood into the urine sample b. Performing urinalysis before chlamydia swab may wash away infected sample c. Performing both urinalysis and chlamydia on urine requires patient void dirty urine into one cup and clean mids

e sample b. Performing urinalysis before chlamydia swab may wash away infected sample c. Performing both urinalysis and chlamydia on urine requires patient void dirty urine into one cup and clean midstream urine into another cup; has high risk of getting samples mislabeled even if performed correctly D. Trichomonas (wet mount of vaginal secretions) 1. For females, performing a wet mount on vaginal secretions may show mobile trichomonads

249 2. Culture of penile discharge or vaginal discharge is the test of choice, but these tests are not always available and take 7 days to get a result 3. New GC commercial testing kits are being developed that will include trichomonas, but these are not yet available in the US E. Syphilis (blood) 1. Rapid plasma reagin testing is most commonly used in the military 2. RPR testing is relatively quick and inexpensive, but has high false positive rates; positive tests should automatically be confirmed by treponemal testing; if a positive result comes back to the IDMT, double check to make sure it has already had treponemal testing F. Herpes (genital lesion culture) 1. Although blood tests do exist, blood testing is primarily used to identify high- risk carriers of herpes (such as pregnant women during routine prenatal visits) 2. At this time, there are no recommended routine screening tests for the asymptomatic patient requesting sexually transmitted infection screening 3. For patients with active lesions, unroof a vesicle and collect fluid on viral culture swab for diagnosis G. Hepatitis (blood) 1.
