Hepatitis B and C are transmitted through blood and genital fluids; a screening blood

th active lesions, unroof a vesicle and collect fluid on viral culture swab for diagnosis G. Hepatitis (blood) 1. Hepatitis B and C are transmitted through blood and genital fluids; a screening blood test is available 2. Hepatitis A is primarily a foodborne illness (fecal-oral transmission) but is often included in sexually transmitted infection panels due to other potential for fecal-oral spread H. Human papillomavirus (cervical swab; “Pap smear”) 1. Different serotypes of human papillomavirus are responsible for genital warts and for triggering cervical cancer 2. No good screening test exists for males; no good screening test exists for any of the serotypes associated with genital warts 3. Cervical swab specimens are tested for abnormal cells (“Pap smear”) and if abnormal cells are identified, specimen is evaluated for presence of HPV 4. Although HPV infections can progress to invasive cervical cancer, in adolescent patients pap screening is no longer recommended due to the high incidence of spontaneous resolution of the infection; pap screening is now recommended starting at age 21 I. Common Order Sets (every preceptor is different) 1. Asymptomatic male screening for sexually tr

dence of spontaneous resolution of the infection; pap screening is now recommended starting at age 21 I. Common Order Sets (every preceptor is different) 1. Asymptomatic male screening for sexually transmitted infection a. HIV b. RPR c. GC d. Hepatitis B/C or hepatitis A/B/C e. Viral culture if lesion is present f. Bacterial culture if lesion is present 2. Asymptomatic female screening for sexually transmitted infection a. HIV b. RPR c. GC d. Hepatitis B/C or hepatitis A/B/C

250 e. HCG f. Pap smear if over age 21 g. Viral culture if lesion is present h. Bacterial culture if lesion is present 3. Male presenting with dysuria or urethral discharge a. HIV b. RPR c. GC d. Hepatitis B/C or hepatitis A/B/C e. Urinalysis (see notes about collection of GC and urinalysis) f. Viral culture if lesion is present g. Bacterial culture if lesion is present 4. Female presenting with vaginal discharge a. HIV b. RPR c. GC d. Hepatitis B/C or hepatitis A/B/C e. HCG f. KOH g. Wet Prep h. Viral culture if lesion is present i. Bacterial culture if lesion is present

269 Attachment 5: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM GUIDE SUBJECTIVE PATIENT IDENTIFICATION- Age, sex, race, occupation, and/or status. CHIEF COMPLAINT (CC)- Use patient’s own words if possible, include duration of this episode. HPI Provocative/Palliative? (What makes it better/worse?) Quality? (Sharp, dull, ache, throbbing, etc.?) Region/Radiation? (Where does it hurt and does it radiate?) Severity/Strength? (1 to 10 compared to what? and/or limitations?) Time/Treatment? Started when? Had it before? When? Duration? Treatment (self/MD Rx'd)? Medications, dose, frequency, duration? Tx/medication giving relief? HPI with injuries- What was the patient doing prior to the injury? What was the patient doing at the time of the injury? What did the patient do and what was he/she capable of doing immediately after the injury? How did the patient land? Mechanism of injury? What can the patient do now compared to at the time of injury? ASSOCIATED SYMPTOMS- OPQRST all positive responses and include them in the HPI. OTHER PROBLEMS- Active on going problems being treated, or being followed without treatment, not related to the "Chief Complaint". PMH- (Past Medical History) Any significant medical histor

n the HPI. OTHER PROBLEMS- Active on going problems being treated, or being followed without treatment, not related to the "Chief Complaint". PMH- (Past Medical History) Any significant medical history (i.e. childhood illnesses, lost time from school/work, surgeries, hospitalizations, injuries, blood transfusions, etc.). Be thorough and concise. EXAMPLE: Appendectomy age 8, May 1962, ruptured, received transfusion, hospitalized 6 weeks, no complications, no sequelae. CURRENT MEDICATIONS- Include meds frequently over looked, aspirin, birth control pills, vitamins, etc. IMMUNIZATIONS- Types, current/noncurrent. ALLERGIES- Drugs? Insects? Food? Other? Address each separately; include manifestation of symptoms, duration, previous treatment, etc. HABITS- Alcohol? CAGE +/-? Tobacco? Caffeine? Sleep? Diet? Other? Address each separately; include pertinent history, amount, etc.

270 HOBBIES- What type and the tools, chemicals, etc. used for the hobby? RELIGIOUS PREFERENCE- Include if it impacts care to be given. LIVING CONDITIONS- Dorm, house, apartments, alone, with others, etc.? EXPOSURES People; family, coworkers, roommates, etc. Things; environmental, occupational, etc. FAMILY HISTORY- Mother, MGM, MGF, Father, PGM, PGF, Siblings? If living include age and state of health. If deceased include age at death, cause of death. Also, include any history of cancer, diabetes, hypertension, thyroid, heart, lung or kidney disease and mental disorders prior to age 55. If adopted, state “Adopted, Family History unknown.” ROS- (Review of Systems) Include negative responses in the organ system being evaluated and 1-3 questions from each organ system geared towards identifying life threatening illnesses OBJECTIVE VITAL SIGNS: B/P, Temp, Pulse, Respirations, O2 saturation if indicated, visual acuity, height, weight GENERAL IMPRESSION: Well developed? Well nourished? Oriented to person, place, and time? No acute distress? Appears __________________. (Fatigued, restless, uncomfortable, etc) Head: Normocephalic with even hair distribution, no masses or swelling. Mastoid

ted to person, place, and time? No acute distress? Appears __________________. (Fatigued, restless, uncomfortable, etc) Head: Normocephalic with even hair distribution, no masses or swelling. Mastoid nontender. TMJ: TMJ nontender no crepitus; Full range of motion without pain, popping, or locking. Eyes Visual acuity- right, left, bilateral, uncorrected AND corrected. Visual fields by confrontation = to examiner. PERRLA, EOMI without nystagmus. Eyes, Eyelids, Eyebrows- symmetrical without edema. Conjunctiva- moist, not injected, no cobblestoning. Sclera- no icterus or hemorrhage. Lacrimal apparatus-without edema or regurgitation. Cornea and Lens- without opacities. Funduscopic- Disc margins sharp, no hemorrhage or exudate. No photophobia. Ears Auricles (or pinna)- symmetrical, nontender. External ear canals (EAC)- no inflammation, discharge, or obstruction. (cerumen present?)

271 TM's- pearly gray and translucent, handle of malleus, umbo, pars tensa, pars flaccida , cone of light anterior/inferior, TM mobile with Valsalva. Able to distinguish two syllable whispered words. Weber- equal bilaterally (or without lateralization). Rinne- AC>BC bilaterally. Nose Nose is midline without lesions, tenderness, or deformity. Septum- midline. Nares- patent. Turbinates- pink and moist; no polyps, edema, or discharge. Sinuses- nontender with palpation and percussion. Negative head tilt. Throat Lips and buccal mucosa- pink and moist without lesions Tongue- midline no masses Teeth- intact and nontender to teeth rake, no appliances. Gums- no gingivitis Tonsils- Grade , without exudates. Neck Supple. Nontender spinous processes and paravertebral muscles. No stepoffs. Full active range of motion and strength 5/5 with flexion, extension, rotation, and lateral bending. No pain with movements. No lymphadenopathy in occipital, pre/post auricular, submental, submandibular, tonsillar, anterior /posterior cervical, or supra/infraclavicular locations. Trachea- midline. Thyroid- nontender no masses. Respiratory Chest symmetrical, no bruising, retractions, crepitus, or tenderness. R

r, anterior /posterior cervical, or supra/infraclavicular locations. Trachea- midline. Thyroid- nontender no masses. Respiratory Chest symmetrical, no bruising, retractions, crepitus, or tenderness. Respiratory excursion symmetrical. Tactile fremitus = bilaterally in all fields. Resonant to percussion. Diaphragmatic excursion cm bilaterally. Breath sounds clear bilaterally in all fields. No wheezes, rales, or ronchi. No bronchophony, egophony, or whispered pectoriloquy. Cardiovascular Skin warm and dry. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds. No lifts or heaves. PMI not displaced. EXAMPLE: “Dorsalis pedis pulse 2+ bilaterally.” Rate and rhythm regular (RRR), without murmurs.

CN-IX, X- swallowing and Gag reflex intact. Uvula rises midline. CN-XI- head movements and shoulder shrug symmetrically strong, against resistance. CN-XII- tongue midline, no fasciculations. Motor- no atrophy, weakness or tremors. Strength 5/5 (and where checked). Cerebellar- point to point and rapid alternating movements (RAM)- speed and rhythym fluent. Negative Romberg. Gaits- heel-to-toe, heel walk, toe walk, and normal gait- no limp, hesitation or loss of balance. Sensory- bilaterally intact to; light touch, pin prick, two-point discrimination, hot/cold, proprioception, and stereognosis. Identify where checked. Upper extremities? Feet? ) (draw diagram with plus signs). No ankle clonus. Plantar reflex- toes curl. NOTE: If documented elsewhere, tests do not need to be redocumented in this section. Full active ROM with 5/5 strength with flexion, extension, internal/external rotation, abduction, and adduction. No pain with movement or against resistance. GUIDE TO LOCATION AND DIRECTION WORDS: Anatomic position- standing, facing forward, arms at sides, palms facing forward Anterior- on the front side of the body when in the anatomic position Posterior- on the back side of the body when in the anatomic position Right/Left Lateral- towards the right/left side Transverse/horizontal plane-Describing a cut through the body that is parallel to the ground Ipsilateral- same side Contralateral- opposite side Superficial- towards the surface Deep- away from the surface Superior- towards the head Infer

a cut through the body that is parallel to the ground Ipsilateral- same side Contralateral- opposite side Superficial- towards the surface Deep- away from the surface Superior- towards the head Inferior- towards the feet Dorsal- towards the spine, top of foot, or back of hand Palmar/volar- towards the palm of the hand Plantar- towards the sole of the foot Proximal- towards the center of the body Distal- away from the center of the body Midclavicular line- runs vertically down the anterior side of the body passing through clavicle’s midpoint Midaxillary line- runs vertically down the lateral side of the body passing through the apex of the axilla Midpupillary line- runs vertically down the face through the midpoint of the pupil

t to fill collection tube halfway with normal saline For yeast: “KOH Prep” -Use wooden cotton-tipped applicator along vaginal wall -Place specimen in ordinary blood collection tube -Use saline bullet to fill collection tube halfway with normal saline -Add several drops of KOH solution For Pap Smear -Thin prep container/solution -Use flat spatula around outside of cervix, larger side towards os, swish in solution -Use cytobrush inside cervix, may cause trace spotting, swish in solution

277 Attachment 7: Biological Agents with Potential for Weaponization VII. CATEGORY A AGENTS- These organisms are hearty with good survivability in multiple environments. They can be easily released or spread person to person, likely to cause high degree of social disruption, and require special public health preparedness. A. Veriola Major (Smallpox) B. Bacillus anthracis (anthrax) C. Yersinia pestis (plague) D. Clostridium botulinum toxin (botulism) E. Francisella tularensis (tularemia) F. Filoviruses (Ebola, Marburg) G. Arenaviruses (Lassa, Junin, etc) VIII. CATEGORY B AGENTS- These agents are moderately easy to spread but generate less morbidity and mortality than category A agents. A. Coxiella burnetti (Q fever) B. Brucella species (brucellosis) C. Burkholderia mallei (glanders) D. Burkholderia pseudomallei (meliodosis) E. Chlamydia psittaci (psittacosis) F. Rickettsia prowazekii (typhus fever) G. Alphaviruses (eastern equine encephalitis, western equine encephalitis, Venezuelan equine encephalitis) H. Ricin toxin (found in castor beans) I. Clostridium perfringens toxin J. Staphlococcus enterotoxin B K. Salmonella species L. Shigella dysenteriae (shigellosis) M. Escherichia coli

ine encephalitis) H. Ricin toxin (found in castor beans) I. Clostridium perfringens toxin J. Staphlococcus enterotoxin B K. Salmonella species L. Shigella dysenteriae (shigellosis) M. Escherichia coli O157:H7 N. Vibrio cholera O. Cryptosporidium parvum IX. CATEGORY C AGENTS- Agents that could be weaponized due to easy availability and significant morbidity and mortalty. A. Nipah virus B. Hantaviruses C. Tickborne hemorrhagic fever viruses D. Tickborne encephalitis viruses E. Yellow fever F. Multiple drug resistant tuberculosis

278 Attachment 8: Chemicals with Potential for Weaponization I. NERVE AGENTS- Organophosphates that are colorless, odorless, tasteless, and nonirritating to the skin. They cause severe secretions (tears, runny nose, drooling), stimulation of smooth muscle (diarrhea, urination, vomiting), and inhibition of skeletal muscle, which paralyzes the diaphragm and causes respiratory failure. They are used commercially as insecticides. A. Sarin B. VX C. Tabun D. Soman II. TOXIC ASPHYXIANTS- These agents are colorless; cyanide smells like almonds. They cause respiratory depression from severe lactic acidosis. Venous blood turns red because hemoglobin is unable to deposit oxygen in the tissues. They are used commercially to fumigate ships and buildings and to clean metals. A. Cyanide B. Arsine (arsenic trihydride) III. PULMONARY IRRITANTS- Common industrial chemicals and household cleaners that cause direct pulmonary tissue injury and pulmonary edema. Irritation also occurs to the skin and eyes. A. Phosgene B. Chlorine C. Ammonia D. Diphosgene IV. BLISTER AGENTS (VESICANTS)- These agents interfere with DNA synthesis and are carcinogenic, in addition to being directly toxic. Symptoms include bl

. Phosgene B. Chlorine C. Ammonia D. Diphosgene IV. BLISTER AGENTS (VESICANTS)- These agents interfere with DNA synthesis and are carcinogenic, in addition to being directly toxic. Symptoms include blistering of skin and lungs and eye burns. Sulfur mustard and Lewisite have no industrial use, but the other agents on the list are used for cancer therapeutics. A. Sulfur mustard B. Lewisite C. Nitrogen mustard (mechlorethamine) D. Cyclophosphamide E. Alkyl sulfonate (busulfan) V. HALLUCINOGENS- Renders soldiers unable to fight due to hallucinations A. Quinuclinyl benzilate (QNB) B. LSD
